She had been the one smiling for the cameras while shaking hands with the Minister and Chief Warlock, Tiberius McLaggen. She had played her role and pretended not to feel guilty about it. Pretended that no one sneered at her back. Not that it mattered. Laws, even ones as clear as the Pro-Werewolf articles, were easy to circumvent with moderate effort. The Ministry made them difficult to enforce. Unless explicit proof was provided, discrimination accusations were deemed hearsay or speculation, and thus were considered damaging to someone's reputation. It was something that kept a lot of the cases out of the ears of the Wizengamot and within their small committees where they either settled or dropped the cases for various reasons. Instead of evicting a person because of their status and registration, they could be evicted for contamination concerns. Or because they didn't have a regular source of Wolfsbane. Or for any number of reasons. Quietly, the Wizengamot hadn't passed the only beneficial aspect of the law, which would have made the Ministry responsible for providing Wolfsbane to all lycanthropesÑfor free. That would have been too right, made things too easy for people treated as sub-human. And that was why Hermione brewed as much as she could each month. Each vial truly made a difference. Unlike the fake laws, pretend tolerance, and false smiles for the media they tried to control, brewing was the real solution. And required real work. Hermione would do more if she could, but she had limits. Padma's patientsÑwho hadn't answered the call of the Death Eaters who promised a better life under their regimeÑwere appreciative of the potion that allowed them to work and live normally. Which was all that mattered. "The full moon is tomorrow." Padma gave Hermione a long look before she prepared for her next group, waving her wand to neutralise the scent of other wolves from the air. They were especially sensitive in the days before the full moon. "Do you want someone to stay?" Hermione counted the leftovers. She usually made forty. Today there were thirty left and Padma had two more group sessions of no more than six wolves in each, which was right around the normal. "I'll be home by moonrise. My wards are strong and there won't be any issues." "I know, but if you want company, I can stay." Her brown eyes lit up. "You can help me pick flowers for the wedding." There was an entire list of things Hermione wanted to do more. She chuckled at Padma's rare show of blatant enthusiasm. "I have a vegetable garden and a working knowledge of flowers as they pertain to pollinating, but I'm no expert outside of what's aesthetically pleasing." "I know, but Neville is busy with his students, Parvati has wild ideas, and Cho will be busy." "And Blaise? He's your fiancŽ after all." "Blaise Zabini? Picking floral arrangements. Willingly?" Padma laughed and Hermione joined in. The mental image of a bewildered Blaise deciding between lilacs, amaryllis, and carnations was hilarious. "Admittedly, he has good taste, but he's been threatening elopement so often I feel this would drive him over the edge." "Fear of your grandmother alone will keep him in line." "She has a Bat-Bogey hex that makes Ginny's look amateur." They both laughed and returned to their tasks, dropping into silence to focus. Soon, Hermione disrupted it. "Are you coming tonight?" Every other Friday, they gathered at her house. Witches only. "Yes. Parvati, too, and is it okay if I bring Cho?" "That's fine." Hermione didn't feel one way or the other about Cho Chang, but she was Padma's best friend and was slowly becoming a regular at their events. Much to Pansy's annoyance. "PadÑoh, Hermione, you're here." Susan popped her head into the room, looking harried, yet perfectly composed. "Great. I know you're not floating this week, but I need help with a patient Auror and Task Force versus Death Eaters in a skirmish in Chesterfield. One dead, one missing, two critical, and six injured." There was a pauseÉ then they sprang into action, but Padma stopped herself. "I have a Group Session in fifteen minutes. I can't leave a room full of werewolves together the night before a full moon for any length of time." No, she absolutely could not. A fight would likely break out. Padma unclipped her potions holster and put it around Hermione's waist. "Any wolf bites, Susan?" "No." Hermione summoned her trusty beaded bag and exchanged an equally relieved look with Padma. Greyback hadn't been set free. Yet. While not the full moon, there was always an alarming number of people bitten either just before or after. Almost as if it were on purpose. Hermione pulled out her vial of dittany. "Is Harry out there?" "No, he wasn't there, but I suppose he'll be around soon, along with Malfoy. The fatality was a Task Force member." She paused in her efforts to find the potions in her bag. "Killing Curse?" "Yes," Susan said. "The most critical is a dagger wound laced with poison. The same one you cured Molly Weasley from." Hermione's eyes widened, then she doubled her efforts, snatching her wand while on the move. She barely said goodbye to Padma before hurrying out with Susan, who walked as she talked. "It's been thirty minutes since he was stabbed. I called someone from Plant Poisoning up to assist." Good move, but that was to be expected, Susan was an excellent planner. "They brought the blade in case it's not the same poison they've been using. Do you have the antidote?" Hermione handed Susan her bag while she put her hair up in a messy bun. "One vial, but there are more in my office, if you don't mind." "Of course." "Anything else I need to know?" "He's young." Very young, as it turned out. Barely out of Hogwarts and not-so trained. Yet another story of the life of the underfunded Auror's Office and Task Force team. He was tall, broad, blond, and close to death. Hermione couldn't tell which posed more of a threat: the dagger wound or the poison. That was about as far as she got when she shed her sweaterÑso she wouldn't ruin another oneÑand got to work. Hermione, who didn't wear a uniform or a badge, had no time for names or introductions. The other Aurors in the room must have recognised her from both her own fame and her lunch visits with Harry because no one stopped her. Also likely because the injured wizard was frothing at the mouth, bleeding from a chest wound, and hallucinating about a dead relative. The poison had a firm grip. It took two uninjured Aurors, a Mediwitch, and Hermione literally sitting on his legs to hold him down long enough for Susan, who had returned just in time, to get the first round of antidote down his throat. Then something for pain. Then Blood Replenishing. Then Dreamless Sleep. He would need it. It wasn't long before she had his bloody robes torn open, blood on her gloved hands and splattered on the floor around her. Susan followed her every move, making expert seals with her wand as Hermione carefully dripped dittany into the wound, closing as much of it as she could. They worked in silence, so familiar with each other that Hermione knew what Susan would do and what she needed before she uttered a request. And vice versa. Hermione felt the cooling sensation tingle her skin, vanishing the sweat from her brow. "Thanks." "Anytime." Susan returned to her task, remaining silent until the gashes were closed. He would have a scar, but he wouldn't die from the wound. With this poisonÉit was too soon to tell. "I'll run diagnostic charms for any internal damage." Hermione stood back while Susan worked, removing her gloves and using her wand to cleanse the patient's body of blood and dirt. Susan finished her charms and reached for the enchanted parchment with the results. She winced, but if Hermione knew anything about the witch, it was that Susan wasn't as much of a pessimist as she should have beenÑgiven all the relatives she'd lost to Voldemort. "The poison's pretty advanced, but the dagger missed everything vital. If we can keep him stable through the twelve antidote rounds, he should live." A brunette Mediwitch peeked in and looked at Hermione. "Harry Potter would like a status update on this patient." "Critical, but stable." "Healer Bones, Harry and the Task Force leader would like to speak to you about the deceased Task Force team member. They need to inform his family." Task Force leader? What an odd way to address Draco Malfoy, given the informal way she referred to Harry. "Right, of course." Susan placed the charmed parchment on the table and sighed. The excitement of their success with one patient quickly turned into a reminder of a failure and loss of life. There was nothing she could have done, but still, it never got easier. When she passed, Hermione rested a hand on her shoulder. Susan nodded in return. "See you tonight, yeah?" "Who's turn is it to bartend? If it's Pansy, hell no. She's heavy-handed and I like to not be miserable for the next week." Hermione burst out laughing; she had a point. "No, it's Ginny's turn." It showed every bit of her weariness from an obviously long day, but Susan smirked. "I'll be late, but I'll be there." Ginny had three kids under the age of eight. So, in essence, she had three jobs. Her first was Mum, wife, short-order cook, and peacekeeper (the four positions were intertwined and paid exactly the sameÑabsolutely nothing), her second was Quidditch reporter (that paid exceedingly well), and her last (but second-most important job) was barmaid. Like an excellent barmaid, Ginny had a stiff and suspiciously fruity drink waiting for Hermione when she finally made it home. "Oh, thank goodness." Without uttering a greeting, she drank it in three gulps, noting with a grimace that the liquor hadn't burned like it should, which didn't bode well for the rest of her evening. Or her morning tomorrow. She hadn't brewed any hangover potion in months. But that didn't stop Hermione from placing the empty glass down and nodding for another. It was their Friday night ritual, one that had begun out of necessity for Ginny, who needed a few hours away from her kids for the sake of her sanity. Harry kept them on Friday nights and she kept them on Saturdays when he wanted to go out with his mates. Over the years, their ritual had grown. Expanded. Now, it included extra people. Luna, when she wasn't travelling the world for work. Daphne, Padma, Susan, and ParvatiÑwhen they weren't busy. And Pansy, but only when she promised to play nice with their latest addition: Cho. Pansy didn't come often. "That bad?" Ginny asked. Hermione sat on the barstool, placing her elbows on the white granite, head bowed in her hands. "I went to drop off Wolfsbane and ended up helping out after the ambush." "Ah, Harry told me about that. One dead and one missing. Stan Mathers. He was just getting home from breaking the news to his parents when I was preparing to come here. He said you saved a young Auror who got stabbed with a poisoned dagger." She nodded. "Same poison as the one that damaged your mum's hands. They've managed to fuse it into the blade itself. Nasty bit of magic. Unnatural. He almost died." Hermione frowned. "Susan said Malfoy took it to the Department of Mysteries for them to run tests. I was too busy saving the Auror's life, but IÑI don't even remember his name." "Alan Cottleback." When Hermione eyed her, Ginny shrugged, her answer the same for everything that concerned Auror business. "Harry told me. Also told me to say thank you." "I was doing my job." "On your holiday." Hermione shrugged and accepted the drink Ginny offered. "Did you miss dinner with your parents?" It was a brave question. Ginny knew exactly how little Hermione wanted to discuss her parents. And yet she brought them up whenever the opportunity arose. Hermione gulped her drink down without uttering a response. Her silence would have to do. She wasn't a heavy drinker, but tonight she needed the reprieve. "I didn't," she confessed with a sigh. "We had dinner the night before last, but it didn't go well." "Like normal then." Ginny reached across the bar and patted her on the top of her lowered head in a dry, compassionate way that made Hermione chuckle. It took little convincing for her to talk about the visit. The conversation with her mum had remained on her mind since she'd made an excuse to leave before dessert. It warped and replayed like a scratched record. By the time Hermione finished, Ginny was cringing. "You definitely need another." Then she dipped out of sight behind the island and re-emerged with a fresh bottle of Ogden's before beginning her complicated mixing process. Hermione observed, but could never comprehend. Having inherited Molly's love for cooking, Ginny was at home in any kitchen. She'd helped with the layout and didn't comment whenever Hermione rearranged during bouts of stress. The stone flag flooring, white walls, ceiling lights, island's granite (that didn't match the treated wood on the rest of the countertops), and distressed wood beams that ran into the sitting room could be attributed to Ginny's ideas. Hermione couldn't even take credit for the sage green cabinets mixed with open shelves that ran along the wall with the stove, or the butler sink beneath the window that overlooked her garden and the land beyondÑeven those had been part of Pansy's vision. The only thing she could say was her idea alone was the windowsill lined with pots of regularly used herbs. It didn't matter. The space suited Hermione, which was great because when she wasn't brewing potions, working through her infinite reading list, overseeing patients, or gardening, she was cooking. After a few small fires and failures, she'd found a new hobby. "Time for a different topic." Hermione rested her chin in her hand. "How was your day?" Ginny's answer was a sarcastic glance followed by an amused chuckle. "I'm happy it's Friday. Before I left, Lily bit James because he kept putting his hands in her face after she told him to stop. Al still hates Nursery School and is already upset about returning on Monday. In conclusion, there are a lot of tears and hurt feelings happening at my house right now." She grinned. "Harry's thriving." Or crying with them. "Fun times." Ginny's mouth quirked into a fond smile; chaos and all, she obviously wouldn't have it any other way. "Indeed." After shaking everything in the ice-filled tumbler one last time, she flashed a smile that spelled intent. "Any plans tomorrow?" "No." She already knew what Ginny wanted. "I'll probably spend the day gardening with Al." "Thanks. I'll bring him by in the morning." Truthfully, they didn't need to ask, but every other week one of them would. And Hermione let them. At five, Albus was the shiest of the three Potter kids, scared of anything too large, and tended to melt into the background when mixed in with his more boisterous, chaos-inspiring siblings Ñand cousins. Despite being anxious to the point of tears around strange adults, in small, controlled environments, Al thrived. After daily tantrums and tears, Harry and Ginny realised that the sensory overload of his siblings exhausted him. They asked her to keep him every other SaturdayÑjust so he could get awayÑwhile they worked at home to give him the peace he needed between visits. He seemed happier during his visits, more inclined to talk, laugh, joke, and ask an array of random questions while he played with the chickens or helped pull weeds. Al was an excellent weed-puller. He enjoyed the quiet of the open pasture behind her gate and the walks they took on sunny days, each time venturing farther from the house and closer to the forest he feared. One day, they would make it. One day, he would stand at the edge of the forest and realise there was nothing to fear. Hermione would be right there, holding his hand when he decided to take his first steps in. When he stopped being afraid. "Where's Luna?" Hermione asked. Ginny poured the mixture into the glass and pushed it towards her. "Argentina." "I thought she was supposed to return today." "She had an issue with her Portkey, so she'll be back tomorrow." "Ah." Hermione nodded. "And everyone else? Susan already told me she would be late." "Parvati should be here soon. Padma's running late because she's narrowing down wedding venues with Blaise. She's bringing Cho. Pansy is upstairs deciding if she's going to be a bitch or not while measuring for your clawfoot tub." "She found one?" "Yes, she did," Pansy announced from the bottom of the stairs across the sitting room. The room was open to the kitchen, giving her the perfect sight of the drink in Hermione's hand. She gasped dramatically. "You chits started without me?" Ginny rolled her eyes so hard her head went with the motion. "Oh, for fuck's sake." She picked up the metallic shaker and gave it a shake; ice against metal rang out in the quiet room. Then, she poured a second glass as Pansy sat on the barstool next to Hermione. "Why do we tolerate you?" "Call it a matter of good taste." Ginny narrowed her eyes. "There's a compliment in there somewhere." The true answer to Ginny's question was simple in its complexity. She'd given Pansy a chance. Not as an act of compassion or forgiveness, but rather as a favour to Theo that she almost declined simply because Hermione had never liked Pansy. But they were adults, and she understood that even bullies were humans with the capacity to grow up and become better people. She'd never included Pansy in that thought before because, at the time, she hadn't given the witch a thought in years. Not since she'd shouted for someone to grab Harry so he could be offered to Voldemort to spare them all. He'd ended up doing just that on his own anyway. Hermione had principles and a strong sense of justice, but it wasn't stronger than her desire to help someone obviously in need. And Pansy had been in need. The first time Hermione had seen Pansy since the war was in St. Mungo's when Theo had called her into his office to examine the battered witch. He'd waited outside. With only the clothes on her back, she'd left her arranged marriage to a German wizard from a powerful wizarding family and was subsequently burned off her family tree and financially cut off. The black eye, busted lip, and bruises had come from being hexed by her mother. When Hermione had tried to heal her, Pansy had laughed and said, "No thank you, I'd like to wear them like a crown. I'm finally the ruler of my own destiny." The remark had stayed with Hermione. Months later, when she'd mentioned to Theo that she was finally preparing to start designing her house, he'd asked her to hire Pansy. To give her purpose. Focus. A chance. Pansy had no experience outside of decorating Theo's office, a nasty attitude that was likely a defence mechanism, and she was almost as stubborn as Hermione. But she thought about their initial meeting, the words that had been laced with a strong desire to better herself, and a time not too long before that, when Hermione had needed purpose as well. So, she had agreed to pay her to design one room: the kitchen. The project had been gruelling for them both, due to their massive personality and style clash, but they'd found common ground over the sage green paint ultimately used on Hermione's cabinets. It tentatively grew from there. Pansy talked about her miserable life under the thumb of her ex-husband's family while Hermione listened and shared bits of her own struggles: the reason behind her departure from the Ministry and why she'd become a Healer. The more Pansy learned about her, the more she stopped making bold, grandiose suggestions, instead switching to favour those more in line with Hermione's simple style. When the project was completed, she caught Pansy blinking back tears, proud of herself and her own accomplishments. And like Neville when she'd grown her first batch of oblong tomatoes, Hermione paid it forward by not judging her. Letting her cry while not speaking a word about it. Only celebrating how far she'd come and how far she would go. Not long after, Hermione suggested Pansy to Hannah Abbott, who had just taken over The Leaky Cauldron that needed updating. She'd agreed and the business Pansy hadn't planned on having exploded. But despite her busy schedule, she'd kept Hermione's house a priority as they slowly workedÑand arguedÑfrom room-to-room, turning her cottage into a home. "Who all is comingÑand you better not say Cho fucking Chang?" Ginny and Hermione remained comically silent. Pansy's glare spoke volumes. "Fuck, I'm leaving." "I'm certain you can tolerate her for a few hours." "I can tolerate a lot of thingsÑthe both of you, for starters." "TouchŽ." Ginny grinned. Instead of heading towards the Floo, Pansy approached them, rolling her eyes. "Padma is marrying one of my closest friends and Parvati is hilarious. Susan is tolerable, I suppose. I don't know her well enough except to say that, for a Hufflepuff, she has a glorious resting bitch face. Granger and I signed that armistice, and I suppose, Weasley, you've got a certain charm and a talent for hexes that the recovering bitch in me can respect. However, I draw the bloody line at Cho." "Recovering?" Hermione cocked a brow then grinned at Pansy's scowl. Meanwhile, Ginny pulled out another glass and poured the remainder of the shaker into it. "I've been married to Harry for ten years and I'm still Weasley?" "Basically." Pansy brought the glass to her lips, drinking slowly. "Oh, I've just been reminded why I tolerate you. You make excellent drinks." "Parenthood has taught me well." Ginny dramatically curtsied and they all laughed. "How was dinner with your parents?" Pansy arched an eyebrow. It was a topic they had discussed while breaking down the barriers between them. Hermione sighed. Pansy and Ginny exchanged pointed looks. The former pursed her lips and exhaled. Even though she wasn't the comforting type, not in the least, she managed an awkward pat on Hermione's hand. "Looks like I'm staying after all." Hermione felt good. Better than good, she felt absolutely splendid. Thanks to Ginny's feel-good concoctions of excellence, both the day and the conversation with her mother were all but a distant memory. Now she was splayed on the sofa, body warm and pliant from the alcohol; her legs were stretched out until they reached the empty spot Pansy had just vacated to make them both another drink. Something different because Ginny had decided it would be an excellent idea for her to catch up with Hermione, and did just that by knocking back a few of her mystery drinks just after the others had arrived. Now, she looked as regret-free and happy as Hermione felt, and she smiled lazily at her friend, who was currently lost in her own world. Ginny's hips swayed to the soft music coming from the Wireless as she waved her arms. Her eyes were closed and her red hair was freed from its ponytail, moving with her easy shimmies. Susan, who was already pissed and asleep on the floor, was closer than she knew to being precariously stepped on by the dancing witch. Oh well. A slightly flushed Pansy returned with her third and Hermione'sÉ Well, she'd lost count. Upon first taste, this drink didn't seem as strong as the others, but it did have a bite. "It's straight Firewhisky because I couldn't be bothered to make fancy drinks for someone as smashed as you are, love." The term of endearment alerted Hermione to the state of Pansy's sobriety. Or lack thereof. When Pansy attempted to elegantly sit down, she missed her landing and lost a bit of her drink to her hands. "Damn you gravity. Fickle bitch." Laughter rang out from Padma and Cho, who were on the two-seater talking about Padma's wedding plansÑa common topic at their gatherings since she'd gotten engaged to Blaise Zabini at Christmas. She didn't mind. In fact, she was looking forward to their wedding next year in India. Their inebriated giggles were drowned out by Parvati's. She was a bit of a lightweight, and nearly bombed after only one of Ginny's mixes, which made her tongue loose and her voice louder than usual. She sat on Hermione's coffee table, in black leggings and a bright pink shirt, while facing her sister and close friend, legs folded with her still unfinished first drink in her hand. Conversation continued after their laughter subsided. Hermione listened, quiet and smiling, not catching every joke or sentence because the alcohol had loosened her to the point where she didn't care that Parvati was sitting on her sled coffee table. She and Pansy had argued about the table for two weeks; even though it didn't fit with the country modern theme of the space, Hermione loved it. Ginny was bopping along in the corner when Cho asked, "How many guests have you and Blaise decided on?" Padma took a long drink and gave her a look that spoke of how overwhelmed she was about the overall processÑa sentiment she'd expressed to Hermione during their last lunch together. "It'll be at least a hundred people from my side and my parents' entire villageÑ" "Loads of family," Parvati blurted out, extending an arm as if the stretch would quantify a number. "I'm planning on not being single by then. Merlin, if I hear," her face changed as she mocked one of her numerous relatives, "'When are you going to find a good man to marry?' one more time, I'm going to launch myself at the sun!" While Parvati commiserated on single life, Pansy gave her an insincere air pat with one hand and sipped her drink with the other. "There, there," she drawled like some louche aristocrat. The Firewhisky dulled Hermione's ability to suppress her reaction to anything she found remotely funny, so she barked out a laugh. Then blushed and apologised. Parvati glared at her but there was no heat in it. "You'll need to find a date, too, Hermione. Good luck with that, mate. Your standards are far higher than mine." "And your standards aren't, Parvati?" Cho asked innocently, but there was mischief in her eyes. Susan rolled over onto her back and started snoring loudly. Bless. "I'll settle for breathing with reasonably decent hygiene. I don't ask for much." Pansy rolled her eyes and Cho agreed. Padma, too. "Lies!" Ginny yelled over the Wireless, but kept dancing like she hadn't said a thing. And while Parvati pouted, everyone laughed because they were familiar with her song and dance. Regardless of what she said, Parvati's standards were not low. In fact, they were probably higher than Hermione's. In a way. She'd never had a steady boyfriend, only a long string of casual flings that ultimately hadn't turned into anything more permanent. The reason why had been solely because she fancied unattainable menÑwhether physically, emotionally, or both. And when they began to show interest and talk of something more, when they began to chase her rather than the other way aroundÉ Well, her interest in them practically vanished. Hermione was familiar with the thrill of the hunt, with chasing something she wanted until she'd caught it, but she'd never known someone to win a race, collect their trophy, look at it, and toss it in the rubbish quite like Parvati. Over the years, she'd wondered if Parvati had even wanted those unobtainable men in the first place, or if the idea of them being just out of reach was the draw. But now that she was sozzled and overthinking every little thing, Hermione found herself sipping her Firewhisky and contemplating the idea that perhaps Parvati didn't know what she wanted. Or maybe she did and was too scared of the additional stress and responsibilities that came along with twining her life with someone else's. Hermione, secretly, could relate. Padma had done it so easily in the last six years she'd been with Blaise. Hermione considered flat out asking her just how she'd built something out of nothing. It couldn't have been easy; they had so much working against them right from the start, yet they fought each battle again and again with a better strategy and growing resilience. There were so many people that doubted they would make it one month, much less a year. Or six. Hermione had been a doubter. They were such a bizarre pair, right from the start, both cagey about the details that had led to their coming together. Alcohol, if she had to guess. Hermione liked when things made sense, when things were analysable, and they were neither. Padma wasn't as electric as her sister, not nearly as bold or outgoing. She knew how to have fun, but was a bit uptight. Like Hermione, she preferred a good book or foreign film over going out. They shared a love for hard work and a passion for helping people. Blaise wasÉ well, he was the sort of person people paid attention to because he never did anything that was expected of him. He was laid back, charismatic, and open about things that didn't matter but private about those that did. In fact, no one had any idea they were together until some unfortunate soul tried to accost Padma during one of their group outings. Hermione was still trying to calculate how he'd gone from sitting next to Theo to punching some drunken wizard in the face. They'd all been all thrown out of the bar in the process. Nevertheless, that had been how everyone found out, and also how they'd all learned that neither of their families approved of the match. Padma's family didn't approve because Blaise wasn't Indian, traditional, or interested in having a large familyÑsomething Padma wasn't interested in either. Hermione had expected Blaise's mother to disapprove because of Padma's blood status, but was surprised to learn that it stemmed from the simple fact that she wasn't rich. Interesting. But it seemed that the more their families tried to tear them apart, the tighter Blaise and Padma held on. They outlasted the disapproval and became stronger for it. Indomitable. Padma blossomed with Blaise at her side, becoming a more confident version of herself, certain of her worth in every facet of her life. She supported his career that kept him away at times and he celebrated each of her successes. On the occasions when she failed, he was still there supporting her. Encouraging her. Cheering her on in that subtle way of his. Hermione sipped her drink and considered the possibility that she had been wrong about themÉ Perhaps they made sense after all. "Who did Blaise pick as his groomsmen?" Pansy rolled her eyes at Parvati, who was finishing her first drink and moaning pitifully to herself about her single life. Ginny was doing a strange version of The Robot. The music was all wrong for her dance moves, but Hermione tilted her glass at her friend and supported her life choices. Like a good friend. She hiccupped. Susan rolled over onto her side; her snores were louder than the music. A silencing charm would do, but Hermione had no idea where her wand was. Probably for the best. Pansy gave her a look out of the corner of her eye, which made her grin. With all of her teeth. "Oh, good gods, Hermione, stop smiling!" Parvati exclaimed. "You look like you've trapped a bug." There was another riotous round of laughter that she joined in on. Ginny, sometime during the fit of giggles, drifted over and flopped down next to Hermione on the sofa. She'd barely had time to move her legs. "Who did he choose?" Ginny asked with a bit of a slur. Her cheeks were bright red from her dancing and drinks combined. Padma, who had long since kicked off her shoes, tucked her feet alongside her while leaning on Cho. "Theo and Draco." Meanwhile, her choices for bridesmaids had been obvious: Parvati and Cho. Hermione would be attending happily as a guest. Besides, Harry and Ginny's chaotic affair had made her seriously question if she wanted a ceremony at allÉ should she ever find someone. Or muster the energy to look for that matter. At Blaise's choices, Parvati lifted her head, a gleam in her dark eyes. "He picked Draco? Excellent." "I'm confused." Cho didn't slur her words; she was the most sober person in the room, as she didn't drinkÑone of the many reasons Pansy couldn't stand her. "Just how buckled are you?" "Yes." Ginny almost choked while Parvati smiled at the inside of her cup before cackling with glee. "This is fantastic news." "Why are you so excited about this? Draco Malfoy is a massiveÑ" Cho looked in Pansy's direction and blushed. She was talking about a friend of Pansy's who they all knew she was extremely protective over. "Wanker? Prat? Arse?" Pansy shrugged, completing her sentence. "I've called Draco almost every version of the word I know at some point in our lives. Or in the last month. Or week." Pansy cut her eyes at Parvati. "The question remains: why are you so excited?" Which seemed to be the question of the hour, judging by the look on everyone's face. Parvati glanced around at each of them, more and more aghast by their lack of understanding. "Draco Malfoy will be wearing traditional Indian groomsmen robes." She spoke slowly like they were missing the point. And Hermione probably was because nothing the witch said made any sense. "He will be wearing Indian robes." Padma chuckled knowinglyÑsome twin thing Hermione never understood. The rest of them were still lost. "Indian! Robes!" "I'm so lost right now," Cho said softly, more to Padma than anyone, but they all heard her anyway. Hermione giggled. The witch's confusion was so loud she almost looked regretful for asking the question that had brought her to that point. "Indian! Robes!" Parvati emphasised each word with a sharp cutting motion of her free hand. "Yes, yes, we get it," Pansy huffed. "Get to the point, for fuck's sake!" "Are you all fucking kidding me right now? The man is bloody gorgeous." Parvati brought her hand to her chin, pursing her lips. "Well, not accounting for his personality, of course." "That's a pretty large thing to not account for." Cho glanced at Pansy, who nodded her approval. "He's an arse." Parvati pushed her braid off her shoulder. "That's incredibly fair. Witch Weekly asked me to interview the top ten Wizarding World Bachelors last week and Malfoy ranked number one." His mum had to force him to attend the interview, but bloody hell is he fit. Have you lot seen him?" Ginny and Cho exchanged shrugs. They hadn't. Padma and Pansy obviously had. As the only person who didn't answer, Hermione suddenly found all eyes on her. Parvati looked expectant. "UhÉ no," Hermione said. "Haven't seen him since his trial." "Just how the hell have you managed that, Hermione?" Parvati scoffed. "You have lunch with Harry. They work together!" "I just haven't and it's not like they're chummy. I imagine they schedule their loo breaks to avoid seeing each other for a second longer than necessary." The room erupted in laughter, despite Hermione being deadly serious. She shrugged and started to take another sip of Firewhisky when Ginny made a slow grab that she'd seen coming a kilometre away. Ginny's success at stealing it could be completely blamed on Hermione's poor motor control and the fact that the room had a nice hazy glow to it. The joke ended up being on Ginny, though, who blanched at the taste with her first sip, looking as if she'd swallowed molten lava. She turned wild eyes on Hermione. "How are you drinking this straight?" Her shrug was lazy at best. "It's spicy?" "She's blitzed," Pansy cut in. Hermione started to argue, but lost both the words and the will. Next time, she vowed while nodding at nothing. Then she chuckled at herself and leaned forward, which was actually to the side because her head touched Ginny's shoulder. Ah, definitely smashed then. Marvellous. Parvati brought the focus back to herself. "Of course she is, but that doesn't matter because Draco Malfoy will be wearing traditional Indian robes and I appear to be the only one who recognises it for the gloriousness that it is." Dramatically, she pointed at them all. "I'm ashamed of you all!" "I, for one, don't see him that way because I've known him all his life." Pansy gave a lazy wave of her hand and sipped her drink. She crossed her legs and leaned back on the sofa. "Also," She grimaced, "been there and tried to do that Fifth Year. It was a terrible mistake we agreed never to speak of again." Then she got up, plucked the glass from Ginny's hand and wobbled into the kitchen to pour more Firewhisky. Hermione smiled in excitement. Parvati looked up, appearing to contemplate Pansy's point. "You know what? I'll allow that. He was still pointy then. Now, however, I'd give part of my salary for the opportunity to climb him like a tree." Ginny made a high-pitched noise, her lips pursed and eyes squinted. "Pretty certain that's called prostitution." "It's a bit illegal," Hermione pointed out. "A bit?" Cho and Padma said simultaneously. Then they broke out in giggles with the rest of them joining in quickly. Susan continued snoring. Parvati, meanwhile, was glaring at them all, but none of them were threatened by her looks because she was too busy trying not to smile. "Okay, poor choice of words, but the fact remains. He's still a bit pointy, gorgeous, andÑ well, according to the rumours, he has started to date again afterÉ you know." And they did. The witch cringed at her own insensitivity. Not because she felt particularly bad about her wordsÑshe would have said it regardlessÑbut rather because Pansy was there. She hadn't been Astoria's friend, but she was Daphne'sÉ and she was well within earshot. But when the witch returned less than a minute later, she handed Ginny her glass and ignored the way Hermione sulked at being cut off before she coolly levelled Parvati with a look. "Don't stop on my account." Pansy sat down and sipped her Firewhisky. "Seriously. It doesn't offend me because it's a fact: Draco is widowed. We all knew it was coming. The surprise was how long Astoria lasted." Ginny refused to share her drink, no matter how much Hermione pouted. "Narcissa is planning to use this season to find him a wife." After running a hand over her still perfect hair, she gave a very matter-of-fact shrug. "Purebloods who are widowed as early as he has been don't typically wait long to remarry, especially when they have young children. It's a witch's duty to raise the children, regardless if they are hers or not." "Seems cold." Cho pushed Padma's hair from her face. Pansy shrugged with an indifference that came from growing up in that world. "We all know that we'll never marry for love unless we're okay with living without a family. WellÑ" She offered the now nodding future Mrs Zabini a meaningful look. "The Zabini's aren't traditional, so they don't count. The Greengrass family aren't as traditional, but they didn't forgive Daphne for marrying Dean until Astoria died. In fact, they still act like he doesn't exist, even though they're about to have a baby. My family was far stricter." Ginny, who had been as quiet and thoughtful as Parvati, spoke up. "Do you regret leaving?" Hermione's eyes were suddenly too heavy to stay open so she allowed her head to rest on the back cushion. The room swayed as if she were on a boat in the middle of the ocean. Still, when Pansy answered, Hermione heard it loud and clear in the haze of her intoxicated mind. "I only regret not leaving sooner." And because she was always so bloody maudlin whenever she drank Firewhisky, the last thing Hermione heard before she drifted to sleep was: "I'll never be able to repay Theo, or even Firewhisky-soaked Granger here, for how they helped me figure myself out after, but I'll never forget it either." If you can't pay it back, pay it forward. Catherine Ryan Hyde Chapter End Notes A/N: Hermione in the books didn't have a huge friend following, I think she would rectify that. She deserves it. Now how everyone is connected is explained throughout the fic. Art for this chapter: Hermione Watching Her Dad Paint by meriyart Chapter3:LeaveOut All TheRest Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Three Leave Out All The Rest March 21, 2011 The three keys to gardening were good planning, forethought, and strategizing. Which made it the ideal hobby for someone like Hermione. It started as an assignment from her immensely annoying therapist, then became an outlet for her frustrations when everyone walked on eggshells around her. But after many failures, a breakthrough in therapy, and dedicating time to self-discovery, Hermione began to understand that gardening was more than digging holes, sticking plants in the ground, and keeping things properly watered. It was about making connections. Ones between science, art, and biology. She found herself fascinated with the harmony of everything: arranging plants in their surroundings until they thrived. Experimentation was another gardening concept Hermione had yet to master, but that was due to her own issues. Experimentation involved testingÑand failure. She'd done enough of both and was no longer interested in doing more of either right now. Hermione wanted to live simply, maintain the status quo, and perfect the process before trying anything new. Which brought her back to the core of who she was: researching and learning. But now she was driven by a desire to better both herself and the world. At least in a capacity that actually made a differenceÉ And didn't nearly kill her in the process. Instead of laws, she studied climate and weather patterns in her area and researched the purpose of indoor sowing. Instead of being dragged into the machinations of the most powerful at the Ministry, who only used her to make themselves look better, Hermione studied and tested soil for the right pH balance, and mastered the art of fertilising. Instead of politics and learning which members of the Wizengamot to avoid or approach, she familiarised herself with the correct wards to deter the wrong sort of wildlife. She built a greenhouse that held everything she needed. And instead of doing the work of six people, Hermione did the job of one: combining all of her knowledge to use, cultivate, and till the earth in preparation for new growth. It was exhilarating. Never dull, always therapeutic. A practical hobby for a practical person. Gardening taught her many lessons. All growth started with a seed; what she did with it was up to her. Losing plants taught her the value in all life. She understood the importance of every step involved in nourishing something until it grew healthy and strong. Everything she grew had a purpose: herbs for potions, fruits and vegetables for consumption. Every process was important: care and fertiliser to maximise output. And certain ingredients were vital, regardless of what she was growing: sunshine, water, time, and patience. But gardening also taught her to watch out for weeds. They were hard to define, much like people. Some were harmless, blending into their environment and living alongside the intended plants. In rare cases, they could even be considered beneficial. But others were destructive, and she made certain to pull them up as soon as she could. If she didn't, they could spread and grow stronger, smothering the life out of the planted seedlings. Weeds could impoverish the soil by depleting nutrients to strengthen themselves. One such weed was waiting for Hermione in her home office. Tiberius McLaggen. Hermione couldn't wait for someone to pull him out by the roots. Following the end of the war, the quiet depowering of the position of Minister had created a power vacuum unlike anything the Wizarding World had ever seen. Chaos was the reason it went unnoticed, and every high-ranking Ministry official who hadn't been associated with Voldemort rushed to fill the void. One of those open positions had been Chief Warlock, the head of the Wizengamot. The man who filled it was now standing in her office, eyeing her table of sows that were almost ready to be planted. His presence wouldn't have been an issue had the changing of their government structure not made him the most powerful wizard in the country. Because he was as crooked as they came. His presence solidified this. Tiberius usually sent Cormac following her refusals, which was always an illuminating experience. Especially when he kept his hands and thoughts about her figure to himself. Still, Cormac was easy to handle. But Tiberius? Outside of his penchant for bribing to get his way, she didn't know enough about him to decide one way or another. "Chief Warlock," Hermione greeted from her doorway. He was in his late fifties yet looked younger. Like Cormac, he was tall, broad, and imposing. His brown hair was just as curly, but his eyes were as different as his presence. Cormac leered and flirted, but Tiberius was sharp. Tiberius was the sort who had a reason behind everythingÑincluding his attire. He donned official robes that signified his position of power. The pleasant expression on his face was as fake as a copper Galleon. "Ah, Miss Granger." He clasped his hands together. "I was wondering when you would turn up." Her plan had been to not turn up until after he left through the Floo he had entered from. However, after thirty minutes of loitering in the vegetable patch, Tiberius showed an aggravating level of persistence. In that regard, he was like his nephew. "Your office is quiteÉ lively." It was spacious and mostly tidy; cosy but about as professional as Hermione could stand. Pansy was chomping at the bit to decorate it, but for now the office was a mishmash of furnishings that accentuated white walls and oak floors. On the table next to her sows were individual pots with her troublemakersÑdittany and molyÑwhich refused to sprout and needed more attention. "Thank you." She inclined her head slightly. "My office hours don't begin for another hour." "My apologies, I was unaware I needed an appointment." "Regardless of your position, I have a schedule I keep and a meeting in an hour so please be brief." She crossed the room to her desk, taking her seat and gesturing to one across from her. "Are you here for a consultation? I'm not accepting new patients at the moment, but I can always direct you to one of my peers. That is, if I know what sort of care you need." He declined the seat, further indication that he was there for a specific purpose. "I'm not here for a consultation. This is a friendly visit." Hermione wasn't sure if Tiberius' was being friendly, menacing, or a little of both. "I wasn't aware we were friends." Tiberius' smile turned cold. Definitely menacing. "My nephew has been singing your praises since Hogwarts. He continues to do so after meeting with you regarding your ongoing rejections of the Ministry's offers." "I see." Keeping the distaste off her face was a struggle. "That doesn't make us friends. Acquaintances at best." "Regardless, he firmly believes you will change your mindÑ" "Then it's clear he doesn't know me at all." Like chess opponents, they tried to figure out each other's next move. Hermione was drawing blanks. War hero status aside, she was a small cog in a large machine. His presence broke all kinds of established rules, as did her apathy about it. "A bit of advice, Miss Granger. When the Chief Warlock takes time out of his extremely busy schedule to pay you a visit, you should at least pretend to look happy about it." "I'll remember that." She opened the folder on her desk in preparation for her next meeting, folded her hands on top of it, and leaned in. "You want deference, but call this a friendly visit? I'll confess I'm perplexed. What are you here, Chief Warlock?" Tiberius said nothing at first, moving from the vegetables to the eucalyptus plant. "Before I knew of your dedication during your time with the Ministry, I thought Cormac was exaggerating his tales of your intelligence. Now I know he was telling the truth. You're smart enough to gauge my reason for coming here." "I can." "The past work you did before your unfortunate departure was most impressive. I wanted to personally see if you had carried that success to your next position." He gestured to the office around him. "It seems that you have." "You've been watching me." "Watching is such a harsh term." His evasive response reminded her of his nephew. For all the wrong reasons. "I prefer to think of it as following your illustrious career." That gave herÑwell, not an idea, but something that needed confirmation. "Ah, so you're behind the job offers from the Department of Magical Law Enforcement. Not Hestia." "If I am?" His eyes lingered on the troublemaker plants then shifted to her. "The department under Hestia Jones is having an image crisis that even promoting the famous Harry Potter to the Head Auror has been unable to cure." That was what happened when a dirty wound was left unattended: it festered until the entire limb had to go. "The public's trust in those entrusted to uphold law and order is failing. It's a problem Harry Potter doesn't care about." Hermione moved her finger, using wandless magic to lift the kettle spelled to keep warm. Another flick of her wrist and it poured the steaming liquid into her empty teacup. Tiberius watched as if she was supposed to have a different reactionÑone that didn't leave her at ease enough to drink tea in the presence of the most powerful wizard in the country. Only after taking her first sip did she address the now glaring Tiberius. "One could argue Harry has more than the public's trust to worry about. You all seem rather keen on sweeping the Death Eaters under the proverbial rug. An Auror is missing, several others were injured just days ago, and yet there was no mention of it in the papers. No awareness for the public. No issued warnings." "We felt there was no need to alarm the public." "So, you're controlling the news outlets." Hermione leaned back in her chair. "This feels familiar." Tiberius bristled. "That's not an accurate statement, Miss Granger. We've merely requested they run the story next week so it doesn't interfere with the investigation." "What investigation? From what I hear, you're not allowing them to send a team after Mathers." Tiberius placed his hands behind his backÑa comfortable and superior position. He wasn't threatened by her and felt the need to show it. Interesting. "You're very informed for someone who has no inclination to return to the Ministry." "I don't." "Shame." He tsked. "Harry Potter could use you at his side. Draco Malfoy could, too." Keeping her recoil internal, Hermione took another sip. "Why put them into positions of power if you don't believe they can do their jobs?" "Their talent wasn't the reason they were placed in their current positions." That much Hermione knew. "Harry Potter is the Boy-Who-Lived, defender of all that is good and just. Draco Malfoy is redemption in the form of a man who turned on those his family used to align themselves with. Rivals and enemies to allies. Their partnership is poetic." What utter tripe. "Apologies for my brusqueness, but this isn't a theatrical performance, Chief Warlock. This is real life with far-reaching consequences for your actions, or lack thereof. They may be qualified for the positions, butÑ" "We each have a role we must play to ensure the preservation of our government and life." Preserve was an interesting way to phrase it when there was still so much that needed to be changed. So much that was still wrong. So much that was corrupted, tainted by their mishandling. "All of us must do our part, including you, Miss Granger. People respect you. They remember your efforts in the war. The Ministry would benefitÑ" "Or, rather than using me as a draw to complete your trifecta to tote around as proof of your dedication to the fight against Death Eaters, you could actually do your job and provide the funding the Auror's Office and Task Force need to clean up the mess created by the Wizengamot's ignorance. They're underfunded, not nearly trained enough, and spread thin. Yet you're constantly giving them more responsibilities." "The Ministry has many duties. We requested the Auror Department and Task Force to perform theirs so we can dedicate our efforts to restoring our economy andÑ" "I'm aware of your duties to the economy." Hermione folded her arms. "But where do the people rank on that list?" "People benefit from economic growth and stability." "I'm talking about right now. Economic growth takes time to trickle down. The wolves, the centaurs who are losing their lands, and those with homes and businesses outside Diagon AlleyÑwhat about them? Meanwhile the rich get richer and people grow more disenchanted by the day." Hermione's stare sharpened like a blade. "Speaking of, how is business in Diagon Alley?" He would know; he owned every business and building. Except the joke shop. That the Chief Warlock was allowed to manage his business and real estate all while passing laws that directly benefited him was unconscionable. But the lack of precedence left them without any applicable laws to contest the corruption. So while it wasn't illegal, the blatant disregard of ethics was appalling. It exposed a massive hole in the way things were run. A hole the Wizengamot had no interest in filling. And people noticed. "Business is not what I'm here to discuss with you today, Miss Granger. My nephew hasn't been successful in persuading you to change your mind regarding our repeated offers, so I thought I would come here to negotiate terms, salary, and further compensation upon measurable success." He paused. "Things that no one else needs to know about." Now that he was showing exactly who he was, devoid of the public mask, Hermione shed hers. Or, more like ripped it off. "I don't want anything you can provide me. I cannot be bought. Your problems are larger than me and my so-called influence. I'm not interested in being the Ministry's puppet ever again." "I suppose you are part of the group that wants to unseat the Wizengamot." He wasn't correct, as she had no direct dealings with the restoration movement, but he wasn't wrong either. "We know they exist," he said in response to her silence. "They would support you, too. That is, should you return." Ah, well that explained it. They wanted her under their thumb. "I have no intentions to return. Not for you. Not for anyone. Perhaps you should focus less on bribing me, less on trying to scheme in order to quiet the complaints, and more on doing your job. Protect the people. Help them. Give the Aurors and Task Force a fighting chance with the funding and time they need toÑ" "There are over a hundred AurorsÑ" "I'm aware." She gave him a dark look. "I used to track that sort of thing. But they are stretched thin trying to keep up with regular crimes committed by desperate people who haven't recovered from the war. There is also the matter of Death Eaters in the countryside that hide in plain sight and pick them off one group at a time. Not to mention, they are working to find their base of operations. There aren't enough people to do what you're asking of them." "The MinistryÑ" "I won't discuss the rising increase in popularity of the Death Eaters' cause." Hermione was in no mood for excuses. "While they agree Voldemort was a megalomaniac with a severely flawed philosophy, the general consensus is that a regime change is a far better option than what they have right now." The expression on Tiberius' face tightened, his anger barely restrained. He wasn't used to being spoken to in this manner. Well, respect was earned. "I think you've forgotten who I am." "I know exactly who you are. Your robes show the power you're happily wielding." No matter how unqualified. "Nevertheless, I don't work for the MinistryÑ" "We make rules that affect you." "True, and not in all the good ways." His jaw worked as he continued to keep his temper in check. "And your department is funded by the Ministry. " "Correction: was. The start-up was for reasons you already know, but now we're self-funded through our research grants in a hospital that's privately owned. Not only do you have no power over my job, you also hold no power in my home." Hermione allowed her words to hang in the air as she took another sip of tea. Peppermint with no sugar or honey. Instead of lashing out, Tiberius did something odd. He smiled and looked genuinely pleased. Positively electrified. Engaged and intrigued by her. He actually looked a bit mad. "You have so much fire in you, Miss Granger. So much passion and brilliance. You're just what we need. You should consider returning and putting your skills to good use." "As I've stated many times over, I have no interest in returning to the Ministry. I'm not playing anyone's game." "Is this about the Ministry's handling of your incident?" The casual way he spoke of something so deeply personal made her flinchÑvisibly. It set her on edge. "Yes, most definitely, but also no." It was the best answer she could think of. "Oh?" "I'm aware of my own failings in that regard. I didn't prioritise myself, and I put my trust in an institution that didn't care if I lived or died. An institution that just wanted me to get my work done and continue spouting praises for the Ministry until I was blue in the face. Quite literally." Unfortunately, she wasn't exaggerating. At the height of Hermione's career, while working extremely long hours and not tending to herself, she had an incident of light-headedness one dayÉ and then nothing. Someone found her convulsing on the floor and rushed her to St Mungo's. A week later, she woke up with no memory or knowledge of just how close to death she had come. All she could recall were the worried faces of her friendsÑand later, her parents. She struggled with the fact that she'd lost memories and an entire week to seizures that had left her magic erratic, body weak, and her mind distressed and unable to string together complicated thoughts. Seizures that would return if she didn't monitor her stress. Her job wanted her back two days after she had woken up. The request sparked her decision to leave. Her health was a gamble they were willing to take for their self-proclaimed greater good, and that left a sour taste in her mouth. "I will admit that the handling could have been better." Tiberius approached the topic carefully, like the politician he was. "HoweverÑ" "There is nothing left to discuss. Please consider this my refusal of all offers extended, both past, present, and any you deem necessary in the future. You are more than welcome to see yourself out." Halfway out the door, she heard him. "You should reconsider, Miss Granger. I know how much you enjoy making a difference. You could help thousands." "No, thank you. I'd rather do it my own way." March 22, 2011 Hermione was reading through the mystery patient's file for the third time when Narcissa Malfoy stepped out of the Floo. Interesting. She wasn't the last person Hermione expected to see, but she was close. That position belonged to her son. If it meant anything, she looked equally as surprised to see Hermione. Theo was far sneakier than Hermione had realised. He hadn't told either of them the complete story. But before she could speculate or speak to Narcissa, who failed to suppress her shock, the Floo burst to life again as two security wizards stepped out. They took their places on either side of their charge, folding their arms in an attempt to look intimidating with matching black robes and deep frowns. Like Hermione was a threat. She almost laughed. Narcissa stood out from her guards in lavender robes with silver accents, but Hermione spotted the mismatched gold band on a plain necklace. Her makeup was as perfect as her coiffed blonde hair. Dressed to impress, Hermione wondered if she might have prepared differently had she known the identity of the Healer she was scheduled to meet. When she was younger, she knew the answer, but now it wasn't as clear. Hermione gestured to the chair. "Please, do take a seat." Addressing Narcissa's security wizards, she said, "You both are welcome to wait outside the door." Narcissa sat with her hands properly placed in her lap and her back straight; her guards remained rooted in their spots. Hermione stared at each of them, but they returned her pointed glare. The moment she was about to open her mouth and tell them to leave again, Narcissa raised one finger and motioned towards the door. The last one shut the door. And then they were alone. Hermione allowed her eyes to roam before settling back on Narcissa. This was the part where she would usually ask the patient to tell her about themselves and what they sought to accomplish under her care, but today, she waited. There was no need for formalities; they knew each otherÑeven if it was barely well enough to identify the other on the street. Or inside her home during a war. Too proper to turn her head or nose up, Narcissa scanned everything in her periphery. It didn't matter, Hermione knew what she was seeing. They were in her home, after all. On the walls, surrounding her white bookshelves, her accolades hung in rows. Narcissa stared at the frames, scanning each as if she found it impossible that someone as young as Hermione had accomplished so much. But she was thirty-one with an Order of Merlin. When Narcissa saw it, she stopped trying to scrutinise. It did nothing to help convince Hermione to accept her as a patient. This was business, nothing personal. "Theodore did not inform me that you were the Healer he had arranged." "I surmised as much." Hermione glanced down at the file in front of her. "When you requested the best"Ñshe raised her eyes to meet Narcissa's sharp gazeÑ"you should have been more specific if a certain blood status was a prerequisite." "My request was accurate." Narcissa delicately patted her hairline with a silk, embroidered handkerchief. Hermione noted the sweat before she dabbed it away. Just seeing one symptom was enough to inform her that her current potions were not working. Pity. Narcissa seemed aware of her scrutiny, and brought her hand back down as she narrowed her eyes. "You could be a troll as far as I am concerned, Miss Granger. However, if you are indeed the best, then I am in the correct office." "Very well." Silence wasn't uncommon during consultations. Some patients struggled to accept their need for help, but the one that fell between them was different. Heavier. It coiled around Hermione, reaching into her stomach to settle in a hard knot of history. There was so much of it, and it was complicated. Yet she was asking for Hermione's help. The irony was not lost on her. Years in therapy had taught her that healing from trauma wasn't just physical or mental. It was about taking charge of her personal liberation from the state of victimisation by not allowing herself to let past trauma interfere with her present and future. She'd already forgiven Narcissa and her family. Hermione had let it go years ago, and refused to go back to that mindset. She hadn't forgiven them for their sake; she'd forgiven them for hers. She knew that she couldn't grow if she held on to every grudge; couldn't fly if she allowed herself to stay grounded by every weight in her past. And Hermione very much wanted to do both. Forgiveness wasn't an action, it was a choice. It wasn't easy, but Hermione decided not to allow hate to cloud her judgement, and that granted her the peace of mind she needed to be objective about the witch in front of her. Detached enough to consider the facts of the assignment and not the patient. The truth was she couldn't accept her as a patient, no matter the angle. Hermione had rules about patients she knew in any capacity. She mentally glared rusty daggers at Theo because he had known that all along yet he had still suggested she keep an open mind. "Would you care for tea?" Hermione politely gestured to the kettle on her desk, charmed to stay warm with a blend she provided during all of her initial consultations. "It's a blend of lemon balm, kava, and valerian root. It's good to calm the nerves." "My nerves are perfectly calm, thank you." "Mmm." Hermione saw the subtle eye movements and tension in her shoulders that spoke to stress, but she didn't want to make assumptions about a woman she hardly knew. Hermione doubted Narcissa Malfoy was the same. That was impossible. Losing her husband and way of life had changed her, made her retreat. She'd disappeared from both London society and the country itself, living in an undisclosed part of France until recently. And yet, she had been busy in her self-imposed exile. Two years after her husband's death, Narcissa had published a tell-all Hermione had never bothered reading, but Andromeda read it roughly six months after its release. It accurately detailed her life growing up in the Black household with an honest retelling of Andromeda's and Sirius' exits from the family. She wrote of her marriage, her son (without many details, as he wished for privacy), the events that led to Voldemort living in her home, and every bit of suffering that followed up until her betrayal the night of the Battle of Hogwarts. Andromeda cried when she'd read Narcissa's words to Lucius about those final few moments when he'd hid her and Draco away before the Death Eaters attacked. She cried harder when Narcissa detailed watching the Manor burn. Narcissa wrote about it all: the emotions she felt, the pain, the parallels between her fate and the Manor's, destined to be both consumed and preserved by the never-ending magical fire. It was hailed as a poignant and harsh, yet honest tale of Narcissa's journey on the wrong side of the war. The bestseller propelled her into stardom after a brutal war. Hermione wondered if Narcissa carried the same prejudices that had been instilled in her from birth or if war had taught her that there was a better way. Bigotry was hard to notice in oneself, and harder still to change, but it could be done. Maybe change had already taken root. Hermione wouldn't know because Narcissa was a private and proud woman. But only of what she had created, overcome, and achieved. Exactly in that order. The rest was too murky for Hermione to tackle with a patient she didn't intend on acquiring. She waited out the silence by sipping her own teaÑgreen with a twist of fresh lemon from her greenhouse. A quick glance was all the other woman received before Hermione continued detailing notes for Roger Davies, whom she intended on passing Narcissa's case along to. He would enjoy the challenge. "I thought you would ask more questions, Miss Granger." "What would you like me to ask?" Hermione rested her elbows on the arm of her chair, relaxing as she tapped her fingertips together and looked the blonde witch straight in the eye. "I've read your file. Three times." "Perhaps you might inquire about my current condition." "I don't specialise in your condition, but from what I've observed, the elixirs and potions they have prescribed you are either not working, not the correct combination, or you aren't taking them consistently." Then she waved her hand and the kettle lifted off the table, pouring hot tea into the glass teacup in front of her. Narcissa hesitated for a moment before picking up the cup and taking a sip. "I've noticed that you are currently experiencing symptoms. Sweats, mainly, but if I were to run diagnostic charms, I would likely find your pulse and blood pressure elevated. You've taken particular care of your makeup, likely because it covers the fact that you aren't sleeping well, and you experience daytime drowsiness. I've poured tea for you because you don't seem to trust yourself. Have you dropped anything recently due to tremors? Have you ended up in a place not remembering how you got there? Have you mixed up people's identities? Even infrequently?" Hermione noticed the slight twitch in Narcissa's jaw that confirmed her observations and provided answers. "That's the nature of your disease. I don't have any questions about what I already know." Narcissa sat the teacup on the saucer. "It seems Draco did not exaggerate in any of his childhood accounts of your intelligence." "It's my job to be observant." Hermione suppressed her chuckle. They took tea together, but neither filled the silence for moments on end. "I know what you must think of me coming here asking for your help after what happened between you and my sister." Her tone was so matter-of-fact that it prompted Hermione's candid admission. "To be quite honest, until you came through my Floo today, I barely thought about youÉ Well, unless your other sister mentioned you." It wasn't often. And for good reason, judging by the stony look on Narcissa's face. Over the years, Andromeda seemed keen on reconnecting with her younger sister, but it hadn't happened. She'd spoken of it, wrote letter after letter, yet hadn't sent a single one. Hermione wondered if finding out about Narcissa's illness would change things, but it wasn't her place to deliver that information. "With that being said, I know you aren't here to discuss the past, and neither am I. I'd like to leave it behind us." Hermione tried to steer them back into the realm of professional conversation. "This is your time to speak about your goals and motivation for seeking treatment." "As I decline, my condition will require constant care." It sounded like Narcissa had accepted her disease, which was honestly better than most patients in her predicament. Hermione made a quick note to Davies. "I'm aware, but why specifically me?" "You are the best, according to Theodore. I trust his judgement. I cannot change my fate, but it appears that with the proper care, I will be able to buy myself time. IÉ" When Narcissa folded one hand over the other on Hermione's desk, she seemed to strip away all pretences and pride, highlighting the reality of her condition and her reason for seeking such specialised care. At such a high price. "I'm not finished preparing myself and my family." Narcissa's voice softened. "I also would prefer that my grandson not lose his mother and grandmother so close together. He's just a boy." An odd feeling rose when Hermione actually allowed herself to think about the fact that Draco MalfoyÑof all peopleÑhad become a husband, father, and widower by thirty. And she was stillÉ Single. No prospects. No children of her own. Not that she was complaining about her circumstance, but it was a jarring comparison. She dusted away the thought. "And Draco. He isÉ not ready to be alone." Complex emotions warred across Narcissa's features. Hermione quietly summoned a box of tissue from the table where her stubborn plants were fighting against nature itself. Hermione wanted to tell her that even though she'd spent her life protecting her son, she couldn't prevent the inevitable. But she kept her thoughts to herself, firmly locked away while Narcissa pulled herself together. "I would like to see him settled and remarried before IÉ Well, sooner rather than later. He complies with my requests to take marriage meetingsÑ" Funny name for a date, Hermione thought sarcastically. "But I know he is stalling. Biding his time. My son is an intelligent man but he's more stubborn than practical. He likes to control the things he can, and he thinks he can control this by waiting." Hermione nodded along, half-listening, still distracted by that bothersome piece of lingering mental lint. She knew Malfoy's father had been killed. Everyone knew and had mixed feelings about whether or not Lucius Malfoy had redeemed himself in death. It was such a grey area that Hermione vowed never to broach the subject; it wasn't her place. Hermione also knew Draco's marriage to Astoria Greengrass had been finalised the year his mother had published her book. Hermione heard about the birth of his son, Scorpius, before Al's birth in a roundabout way: the announcement had been splashed all over the society papers Hermione used as compost. Daphne had become a part of her circle of friends after eloping with Dean, and she talked of her nephew from time to time, but mainly with Ginny because Albus was his age. When Astoria died last November, Daphne turned up at Hermione's house at three in the morning the day of her funeral. She was in tears over what flowers to bring. Hermione had given her a pot of gladiolus from her greenhouse, told her to plant them by her graveside, and quietly spelled them to remain in a state of stasis. She'd never met Astoria, but from Daphne, she knew of her strength and sincerity. The flowers seemed appropriate, but the act was done for a friend who was mourning. Not for Draco Malfoy's deceased wife. And while Hermione knew all of this, she had never spared a moment to analyse what any of that meant as it pertained to Malfoy or his state of being. His job. His role as a father and son. The threats against his family. Hermione never once thought about any of those events as something that had occurred in his lifeÑincidents that could and would define him. But they had. "You can't make him prepare," Hermione said in an attempt to firmly push the thoughts away before they could completely crystallise. "It has to be a choice that he makes on his own. One that only he can make." "Perhaps." Narcissa looked grim. "But I would like the time to try. For both of their futures. It is only proper that he marry to provide a mother for Scorpius, which is my goal while I am still alive." She looked as if she were trying to find something in Hermione's expression, and when she found whatever she was searching for, she rose to her feet, smoothing her robes with firm strokes. Her eyes widened slightly when Narcissa gave her a cold look. "It appears that I will not find the extra time I need under your care." Hermione raised a single eyebrow in response. "Just as you are observant, Miss Granger, I am as well. I have not lost myself just yet." Hermione rested back in her chair with a blank look on her face. "I have seen enough Healers in the last year to know that, had you wished to accept me as a patient, this consultation would have gone very differently. You would have done your own diagnostic charms and compared them to the prior readings." She wasn't wrong. "You would have explained why your care has been described by many as exemplary, and by now, we would be reviewing parchments with a more detailed layout of your treatment plans." There was no need to mince words; she'd never been good at it. "You're correct." Hermione stood and walked around her desk, approaching as the other witch watched her every move. Narcissa certainly hadn't appreciated the rejection. No matter, Hermione didn't like the fact that she'd been put in this position in the first place. It was a moot point, but that didn't mean she would be rude. Now, standing in front of Narcissa, she couldn't help but make comparisons between them. While the older witch was put-together, even after her episode during the consultation, Hermione was not. Her hair was pulled back into a rushed bun, and she wore faded jeans, a long-sleeved grey shirt, and ankle-high Wellies. Not professional, but she had been checking on the outdoor herbs after a night of rain when she'd remembered the appointment. There was still dirt on one of her knees, but she made no move to brush it away. Instead, she stood straight under Narcissa's scrutiny. "I'm referring you to Healer Davies. He's excellent and will be willing to accept the terms of your contract." With a wave of her hand, the door opened and the guards filed in. "He would be the best to handle your specific needs." Narcissa bristled. "Might I have a reason as to why you are refusing to accept me?" She held up a hand in gesture for her to wait. "I have answered my own question. Of course, it has to do with our history on opposite sides of the war." "If that were the case, it would be my right to make that decision." She tilts her head. "Don't you agree?" A flash of something passed over Narcissa's face, frustration or shame. She couldn't tell which, but she exhaled and didn't argue. "I do." Hermione noted the reluctance in her voice, but knew that it came from a place of pride. Honesty wasn't easy for everyone. "However, that simply isn't the truth. Regardless of the past, I don't work with patients that I know in any capacity due to the involved nature of the care that I provide. It's a rule of mine that's well-established, and I'm perplexed as to why Theo referred you to me knowing our history." "Theodore has his own motivations." That was something she could agree on. What the motivation was, she wasn't certain, but it had to be large if he thought she would break her rules and treat Narcissa for him. Hermione thought about asking if she knew Theo's end game, but the older witch likely wouldn't tell her without a price. "I suppose he does. However, I don't need his approval to deny you," Hermione said bluntly. "I'll see to it that Roger gets your file and schedules an appointment with you as soon as possible. I wish you the best of luck." "I thank you, Miss Granger," Narcissa said thinly, "for nothing more than wasting my time." Hermione never learned to cook with magic. Even under Mrs Weasley's tutelage, she had never been able to master the craft. Molly said she lacked the driveÑa phrase that had never before been used to describe Hermione Granger. But she had a point. The issue wasn't a lack of interest, it just never felt natural. Perhaps it was due to years of eating her mum's failures and triumphs; still, Hermione found very little joy in food that was too perfect. Something about a meal coming out a bit oblong or a touch too darkÑone she'd made with her own handsÑwas more appealing than one that was flawless thanks to the aid of magic. Ron thought it was a pity that she'd never learned, but he didn't mention it again when she suggested that he should join Harry in taking lessons from Molly if he wanted magically-cooked meals. Not long after moving into her home, Hermione was in a bookstore in Godric's Hollow, on the hunt for a book to help with her struggling plant. She walked down the wrong aisle and came face to face with a row of Muggle cookbooks. Impulsively, she'd purchased one that had simple in the title. It came with a free bookstand, and Hermione left happy with her decisionÉ Until a month later when she attempted Shepherd's pie and needed to air out the smoke and char from her kitchen. After that, she started from scratchÑeggs and boiled potatoesÑand grew from there. Improved. Until she was ready to try recipes out of the book again. Cooking was like potions: if she followed the recipes verbatim, she wouldn't have any issues. And while that wasn't always true, she still used her skills in brewing to get better. The first successful meal Hermione had madeÑthat her friends genuinely enjoyedÑwas Beef Wellington. As they ate and raved, Hermione felt a sense of accomplishment that waving her wand to cook couldn't replicate. Following her appointment with Narcissa, HermioneÑnow far more proficientÑdidn't have the time needed to recreate her first successful dish for dinner that evening, so she opted for something simple: Coq Au Vin with roasted new potatoes and a salad made with homegrown spring greens. She'd just set the warming charms on her meal and started prepping the salad when Harry stepped out of the Floo.
