Ginny had taken the kids to Shell Cottage that morning for the weekend so they could spend time with their older cousins, Louis and Dominique, who hadn't gone off to Hogwarts yet. Harry brought over a Pinot Noir and a fresh bottle of Ogden's. They'd finished the last one the previous Friday, which had made Saturday rough. Al hadn't minded just lying in the conservatory after their walk towards the forest because, even after a hangover potion, Hermione hadn't been able to do much else. "Hey, it smells great. Do you need any help?" Harry was always willing to assist, but Hermione sat the knife down and shook her head. "I'm putting the last bit into the salad, so no." She grinned and accepted both his embrace and the wine she put in the chiller. The Ogden's went under the island. "We're just waiting for everyone else." "Who all is coming?" "Ron and Pansy." Hermione laughed when Harry rolled his eyes at first. The two constantly argued. But then he smirked because he enjoyed the bickering. "She promised to behave." "I'll believe it when I see it." "Fair point." Hermione shrugged. "How was your day?" It would be rude if she didn't ask, even though she already knew the answer. Harry was promoted to Head of the Auror Office after his predecessor had gotten so fed up with Draco Malfoy that he'd retired eight years early. Harry had no illusions about the reason he'd been promoted. He was, after all, The Boy Who LivedÉ Twice. And the Ministry used Harry the same way they had once tried to use her: as a symbol and a prop; a promotional tool wielded to maintain the public's trust. Unlike her, Harry accepted the role for his own reasons, believing the political motivations for his promotion wouldn't negate the good he could accomplish for the wizarding world and his family. The threat was all too real. His new position came with an office, a generous salary, and the tall task of collaborating with the Terrorism Task ForceÉ and Draco Malfoy. With the public's approval of the Ministry at new lows after a Death Eater attack in December levelled an entire wizarding village, the pressure to make instant, measurable progress was staggering. But despite the heavy odds, underfunding, and general chaos, they'd had successes and captured a handful of high-ranking Death Eaters in the last two months. However, that wasn't enough to appease the Wizengamot. Things had been tense. It didn't help that the two men at the centre of it all could barely stand each other. Years of therapy had equipped Harry with better coping skills, addressed his childhood trauma, and made peace with the long list of losses he'd endured along the way. He was calmer now, able to focus, smiled more, and was harder to angerÉ But he hadn't gotten past his old grudge. At least, not completely. There was something about Draco Malfoy that woke up the fifteen-year-old inside of Harry that wanted to punch him in the face. Regularly. Repeatedly. Harry ranted about him often enough that Hermione would complete several mental tasks and aimless mind-wandering while Harry let it all out. It never failed that whatever she did, whenever she returned to him, he would still be complaining. Today was no exception. "My day was normal in that Malfoy was being an utter bastard." Harry threw up his hands. Hermione started the timer on her watch. She wanted to know if he would break his own rant record this time. "Remember that raid I told you about?" She nodded mechanically. After an exhaustive search, Malfoy had located the Welsh hideout for the Lestrange brothers at the end of last year. Then, he'd recruited a wizard to infiltrate their ranks. Two weeks ago, that spy had reported back that there would be a meeting with the highest-ranking Death Eaters, but the date was not yet known, only that it would be before the end of May. From what Harry had disclosed, it seemed like they could end it all during this raid. Everyone had been discreetly preparing. Curse-Breakers were slowly being pulled off assignment to examine evidence and dark objects found that would assist the prosecution. Hit Wizards and Magical Law Officers were being pulled in to grow their numbers. But they hadn't had the time or capacity for the training needed to make them a more unified front. "YeahÉ Malfoy's dismissed every team lead I've suggested without any reason beyond thinking they're incompetent, but wouldn't suggest Aurors that he approves of because that's my job." Hermione kept her flinch inward. She could hear those words coming from MalfoyÑwell, the sixteen-year-old version of him. And while Harry was a lot of things, he wasn't a reliable source when it came to Draco Malfoy. However, if Hermione were to pass judgement on his character based on Harry's complaints and the bits she had heard about him, she would say he was still the same bastard he'd been during school. No matter how incredibly fit Parvati found him. "Every plan I've designed around entry points into the Manor, he's rejected. He called them simple and said that I'll get everyone killed because my freakishly good luck only extends to me." Privately, Hermione wondered if it was too early to open the Ogden's. For him. "Oh!" Harry snapped his fingers. "And then, I recommended Peruvian Instant Darkness Powder and Malfoy said no, because it's too messy." He wasn't wrong. "I brought in a Wards Expert, but he found someone elseÑa purebloodÑto do it." Harry balled his fistsÑone of the several anger management tips he'd learned over the years. "I just hate that when we meet with the Wizengamot, I have to pretend like everything is just fine. Pretend I'm not working with the biggest wanker I've ever known! And I have to act like a fucking professional when all I want to do is toss him out my window every single time I see his ferret face!" Harry took two deep, cleansing breaths, a technique he'd picked up from Ginny's Lamaze classes. Then he smiled. "That felt good. Better out than in." "True." Hermione stopped her watch, hoping he didn't realise that she was timing his Malfoy.centred rants. He hated when she did that. The record had been six minutes and thirteen secondsÑset the day of their first meeting as heads of their respective departments. They'd nearly come to blows. Today, he hadn't even been close: one minute and thirty-seven seconds. Hermione hoped she wouldn't start another rant with her perspective on the matter. She'd hate to have to restart the clock. "While I don't entirely disagree with MalfoyÉ" At the betrayed look on her best friend's face, she raised both hands. "Hear me out, Harry. He has a point about Peruvian Instant Darkness Powder. It's a hindrance that will only cause more injuries through friendly fire. Besides, last I heard, the Hand of Glory was locked away in the Department of Mysteries. I doubt anyone will approve its usage given its tendency to wind up in the wrong hands." "I didn't think about it like that." Harry rolled his eyes. "If he'd said it like that, I wouldn't have argued so hard." She didn't believe him, but was about to change the subject to Tiberius' visit when Pansy arrived, wearing a long-sleeved turquoise bohemian dress and a positively thunderous expression. If Hermione were a betting person, she'd wager her entire Gringotts vault on the possibility that she was the source of Pansy's ire. And she would be right. Pansy started yelling before Harry could greet her. "I can't believe you rejected her as a patient!" There were moments Hermione reacted too quickly, but far more often, she reacted too slowly. She was human and constantly found herself balancing between extremes. Today Hermione was careful, approaching the island where Harry sat in wide-eyed silence while Pansy vibrated with indignation. Though she had several questionsÑHow? What? When? Who?ÑHermione cast them aside to focus on Pansy before her anger left scorch marks on everything. "This is about Narcissa Malfoy." It was an overly calm, rhetorical statement designed to distract Pansy. And it worked. Her eyes went wide as she sputtered like a fish out of water; her mouth opened and closed just as fast. Before she could get her second wind, Hermione rested one hand on the granite. "I have theories about how you managed to find out so quickly, or why you're involved in this matter to begin with, but I won't deny that I rejected her as a patient." "Why? And don't waste your breath spouting your Healer rubbish about not being able to be objective because you know her. I wasn't born yesterday, Granger." "I don't tell you how to do your job, so you don't get to tell me how to do mine." Harry cleared his throat. "How about I justÑ" "Harry." Without taking her eyes off Pansy, Hermione raised her hand. "Stop talking." "Okay." "Don't talk to him like that!" Pansy cut her eyes to Harry, who looked just as surprised by her defence. "What theÑyou've got me defending Potter, for fuck's sake! You owe me a drink when I stop being mad at you. Shite, that didn't even feel right." Hermione examined Pansy with a probing look she hated. In fact, she probably would have hissed like a cat had they not had an audience. "I'm surprised you're the one arguing on her behalf and not her actual son." "Draco would sooner choke on his pride and die than ask for help from anyone. It's not his way. Control issues galore." Pansy rolled her eyes. "Besides, their relationship is strained at best and he has enough problems on his hands. The threats. Work. I'd say Scorpius, too, but he's not too involved in child-rearing, no matter his opinion on the matter. That's Narcissa's job now that Astoria's gone." Hermione recalled that bothersome thought before firmly locking it away. "Why do you care?" "Narcissa has been more of a mother to me than my own, and that was before she burned me." Pansy looked away, touching her hair, seemingly uneasy with her own candidnessÑ especially around Harry, who looked intrigued. "As soon as she heard I'd left my marriage, she gave me a chance to get away from it all until I was ready to stand on my own." She spoke of Narcissa like Hermione spoke of Mrs Weasley. "When she told me about her illness and Theo said he was going to ask you to take her case, I was relieved because I knew she would be in the best hands. I hoped she might live as long as possible. While I don't exactly agree with how rigid she is with his schooling, Scorpius needs stability. I've seen how dedicated you are to your patients. I thoughtÉ" She cleared her throat. "Well, obviously I thought wrong." "Have you seen me with my patients? Because I don't believe you have. I become a part of their lives for the length of the assignment. I monitor everything from their meals to their family situations, and should anything negatively affect their recovery, I rectify the situation. I grow the ingredients for their potions in my greenhouse, and what I can't find, I acquire, no matter how specific." "IÑ" "It takes time and effort and a certain finesse that's not typical of any Healer out there. I'm not just their Healer. I don't simply wave a wand, feed them potions, and make them better. I look out for their physical, mental, and emotional health. I help their families adjust, because most forget how much of a difference a supportive and knowledgeable family can make when it comes to a patient's care. Narcissa's family situation is convoluted at best. Not to mention the fact that I don't even specialise in her disease." Pansy folded her arms across her chest. "I know that. All of that." "Then you should understand why I won't take her case." For a moment, her blue eyes were unguarded and open. "She just wants time, Hermione." "Roger will beÑ" "Davies? That pompous prat? He'd soonerÑ" "It's not about Roger's personality. It's about his ability to do his job objectively. Narcissa and I have history, Pansy. That's like asking Harry to take care of her. It'sÑ" "I'd do it," Harry interrupted with a casual shrug. She and Pansy turned sharply. "What?" Harry ran a hand through his perpetually unruly hair before brushing it back over his infamous scar. "We've been writing for years. Not often, but a few times. Her letters come to Grimmauld Place." Pansy gaped at him. "I didn't know this." "I think she meant to keep it private. We had tea there when she returned to the country. It was right before Malfoy took the position. Andromeda was supposed to join us, but she declined at the last minute." Sometimes Harry caught her by surprise with the things he kept to himself. "You'd help her?" Pansy stared at him intensely. "Really?" "Yeah." He looked down at his hands. "At one point, she helped us all." Hermione's favourite room in her house was the conservatory. It was a glass-paned addition located just off her kitchen with pitched ceilings that gave her a view of the beautiful land around her home. It also served as a reminder that she was part of the natural order. From any spot, Hermione could see the world beyond her vegetable patch and greenhouse, the separate cobblestone walk that led from the steps to the fence, the field, and the trees in the distance that divided the end of her property and the start of the dense forest. But when simply looking wasn't enough, there was a door that opened up to that world. Pansy had spent the better part of winter turning it into an oasis with creative lighting, floor to ceiling trellis in each corner for climbing roses, decorative rugs that kept the stone floor warm, and a small jungle of plants and flowers elegantly arranged around the room. The lounge area was in the centre of the room, with a dark resin wicker sofa, settee, and two matching chairs, all with plush, cream cushions. They artfully surrounded a glass top table lined with candles that were spelled to turn on whenever someone entered the room. To the right, just beyond the lounge area, was a reading nook tucked off with lamps and a comfortable chaise large enough for two people. It wasn't uncommon for her to fall asleep in the chaise under a blanket while reading a book. Or while gazing at the stars. To the left of the lounge was an eating area with creative lighting for when it got too dark. Hermione's original dining tableÑa circular glass table with six chairs she'd been too sentimental to give upÑserved as the focal point of the area. Hermione frequently had dinner out there with guests. Or alone. Tonight, the four of them sat comfortably, eating and chatting under floating lamps that lined the stone outer wall of her home. The sun had dipped behind the trees as purple dusk began its mission to take over the sky and prepare it for nightfall. The stars would be making an appearance soon, too, and it was forecasted to be clear enough for them to enjoy the sight. While Ron and Harry talked and joked around like always, Hermione drifted in and out of their conversation. They grew more animated as dinner progressed and their lips loosened over the lager Ron brought with him. Neither were keen on the wine she and Pansy drank. As usual, Ron sat too close. Close enough for Hermione to feel his thigh brush against hers. Close enough for her to catch a small whiff of the scent she often associated with him. Hermione knew what he was doing, the goal he was trying to accomplish. Ron wasn't nearly as subtle as he thought, especially when he rested his hand on the back of her chair while talking to Harry. He wanted her to let him back in, and he would keep trying, bit by bit, until she did. But more than stubborn, Hermione was uninterested. So, when his fingers absently brushed against her hair, she scooted away, shutting him out while Pansy glared at him. And Hermione watched her. Pansy had let the earlier conversation lapse with Ron's arrival, but she was well aware the other witch was plotting. Pansy was more tolerable than expected. Tactfully amicable. Pansy would play nice and bide her time like a coiled snake, waiting for the perfect moment to strike. Hermione was no one's prey. The wards alerted Hermione to the arrival of an unexpected guest. "Percy's here." Hermione nudged Ron with her elbow. "Okay?" Ron was confused. "And who is that?" Pansy asked. "Percy is one of Ron's brothers. IÑ" "Oh, right." Ron jumped from his seat and headed towards the door. Not paying attention, he nearly clipped the settee, but recovered. "He bought tickets to tomorrow's Cannons game and I told him to bring them here." "The tickets weren't free?" Pansy asked with a bemused look. "As if I'd pay one Knut to see them lose." Ron looked both outraged and insulted, but he ended up sputtering like an engine that failed to start and vanished into her house after giving Pansy a deathly glare she laughed at. Harry and Hermione chuckled into their respective drinks and exchanged knowing looks. Pansy wasn't wrong, but neither would even so much as hint at that to the lifelong fan. "As much fun as this has been." Pansy finished the rest of her wine and stood, pushing her chair in. "I have reached my Weasley quota for one day. The addition of another simply will not do." Hermione would have explained, but thought it better for her to find out on her own. "Percy's different," Harry said. "Does he have table manners?" It was a serious question. "In a manner of speaking," he answered cryptically. Hermione laughed, resting her hand on her cheeks, warmed from the wine. "In a manner ofÑ" Pansy blinked at him incredulously. "Haven't I suffered enough? OfÑ" "Good evening." Percy's polite yet posh baritone floated from the doorway before he approached the table with Ron. Everyone turned. That was the sort of presence Percy had developed over the years. He'd always been different, but over time, he'd grown out of his desperate need to prove he was better than his family. Percy had become a man who knew exactly who he was, where he'd come from, and what he was worth. As Head of the International Magical Office of Law, Percy walked with a pride that reflected all he'd learned and experienced. Hermione couldn't help but notice that the two brothersÑin addition to being contrary in personalityÑwere also visual opposites. Ron had made an effort tonight with dark jeans, a white shirt, and brushed hair. Taller than all of his siblings, he moved with a swagger of self-assurance, like someone settled without a care in the world. Percy, on the other hand, possessed the ease of a seasoned diplomat. Dressed casually in grey tailored trousers, a matching waistcoat, and a white and purple chequered shirt, the only thing missing was his usual bowtie. Hermione had never seen Pansy look so confused. "You're a Weasley?" "I am." Percy was taken aback, but recovered smoothly. "And you areÉ" When Pansy didn't respondÑtoo busy blinking like her brain had short-circuitedÑHermione stepped in to help. "This is Pansy." Percy's blue eyes briefly slid to Hermione. "Ah." He took another step, courteously extending his hand. "And your surname?" Finally, Pansy remembered herself. She looked at his hand, then at him. "I'm between surnames right now." Harry almost choked on his drink. Ron, who had returned to his seat during the introduction, slapped him on the back. Percy suppressed a smirk of his own, but didn't retract his hand, maintained eye-contact with a determined look on his face. Hermione thought she'd have to intervene, but Pansy accepted his hand. "It's a pleasure to meet you," Percy said. "Oi!" Ron glared at his brother. "I thought you were coming to say hello to Harry and Hermione." "I've changed my mind," he said while maintaining eye contact with Pansy, whose cheeks had taken on a slight colour. After clearing her throat, she slowly pulled her hand free. Hermione didn't miss the way she flexed her fingers before closing it into a fist and tucking that arm behind her back. Pansy looked around the room as if searching for something important. Probably the portal back to the universe where everything made sense to her. Hermione almost laughed. "If you don't mind the intrusion," Percy said, glancing at Hermione, "I think I'll stay." "Of course not. Pansy here was just leaving." Percy glanced back at her. "Oh, you are?" "To get more wine, of course." Pansy did just that before Hermione could remind her of the half-full bottle on the table. Percy took the empty chair next to hers, smoothing invisible wrinkles from his trousers. She glanced over at Harry, who was observing with a raised eyebrow peeking over the rim of his glasses. Ron started talking about the seats for the game while Hermione tilted her head at Percy, who was now making sure his already perfectly styled hair was just right. Hermione finished her wine. "How was your day, Percy?" "I didn't offend her, did I?" "Why does it matter?" Ron frowned in confusion. "It's just Pansy bleedingÑ" "Shut up, Ron," Harry and Hermione said simultaneously. The answer to his question was no, but it was also precarious. However, it wasn't her place to tell him any of that. Being burned and ostracised from society turned Pansy into a cautious person, a planner who liked to know what was coming so she could adequately prepare herself. She was excellent at reading people and the sort of person who was jadedÑor arrogantÑenough to believe herself immune to being surprised by anyone or anything. Hermione never had the heart to tell her truth: the game of life didn't have a standard set of rules. Humans were more complicated than whatever system she had used to sort them all. One day she would meet someone she couldn't immediately categorise. And judging by the way she'd bolted, that day was today. And that person was Percy Weasley. Percy stood when Pansy returned with a single wine glass in her hand. She offered no excuses for why she hadn't grabbed another bottle either. In truth, she looked far more composed until he pulled out her chair. She stared at him. He held her gaze. The standoff lasted until Ron abruptly stopped talking about the game. "Oh, for fuck's sake, just sit down, will you?" They both glared at him. "Percy thinks of himself as a perfect gentleman. Bit of a tosser, really." Ron only half meant it based on the smirk on his face. Pansy's expression deepened into distaste, eyes narrowing into tiny slits, but she sat down without further argument. Percy adjusted her chair and returned to his before reaching for an empty glass and the bottle on the table, chilled with magic. He poured himself a glass then turned to Pansy. "Would you care for more wine?" She was hesitant, still puzzled by his entire existence, but offered her glass. "Yes, please." Percy smiled. The two most powerful warriors are patience and time. Leo Tolstoy Percy & Pansy Source: Jaxxinthebox Chapter End Notes A/N: Wanted to show Hermione in full-on could-have-been-a-politician mode. And some of you all guessed it, Narcissa was the mystery patient. And you already know that's not over. And you've met my main side couple. Inspired by Pacific Rimbaud's "The Secretary" which got me hooked on the concept of Percy and Pansy. Cheers! Embedded art by Jaxx-in-a-box [ Instagram / Tumblr / Ao3 ] Art for this chapter: Percy and Pansy by hana_bob Chapter 4: Theory of Incompatibility Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Four Theory of Incompatibility March 25, 2011 Hermione had always been fascinated by the stars. Sometimes, on nights that were clear and unseasonably warm, she would take a blanket out into the pasture behind her house to lay out and gaze up at the sky. It made Hermione feel small, overlooked, but she liked that. Every pain and anxiety she carried from war and recovery, the worries that fluttered in and out of her mind, everything that culminated and threatened to consume herÑit was less overwhelming whenever she inhaled, exhaled, and reminded herself that she was just one person in the middle of something much larger. The universe. And Hermione knew her place in it. She was vital to few, important to some, a face in the crowd to others, and a stranger to most. Overall insignificant in the greater cycle of nature. There to serve her purpose. To live life the best way she knew how. During those brief, tranquil moments, while in contact with the earth and dwarfed by the heavens above, she was content. At peace. Free. Insignificance, she'd realised during therapy, wasn't demeaning, simply a reminder that she didn't have to be everything at all moments. Only herself for that moment. Armed with that understanding, the entire universe had unlocked itself, stretching out before her, and filling her up with the notion that anything was possible. Tonight she didn't wait long after Girls' Night concluded before she made her way outside with her blanket. Still warm from wine, Hermione laid out in the grass and observed the sky. The night was clear, with just enough thin clouds to partially obscure the waning gibbous moon, allowing her to properly observe the sky dotted with stars. Some were large, others little more than a speck to her naked eye, but it was all stunning. The chorus of chirping bugs was peaceful. Calming. Hermione spotted the Big Dipper first, tilted right on its handle, then she followed it towards Polaris. Up and over, she landed on the ever-present Draco, which weaved its way between both dippers. From tip to head, Hermione used her finger to draw the dragon's body in the sky. She was moving towards Eltanin when the hum of her wards interrupted. Harry and Ron. A surprise, though not unwelcome. Hermione watched their approach: identifying them less by their magical signatures, and more by the way they walked. Even in the grass, Ron was loud; Harry moved with stealth due to years of being both an Auror and a parent. He was also more identifiable because he'd come bearing another blanketÑone that looked to have come from her sofa. Thoughtful. She never accounted for how cool it would get while she was out there. Harry took to unfolding the blanket while Ron plopped down next on her, greeting her with a boyish smile. The moonlight highlighted the blue of his eyes and flush of his cheeks. "Hey." "Hey," Hermione returned as he settled next to her. "You smell like a pub." Ron laughed like he always did when he was pissed. "Seamus was in town and we had to take advantage of Harry's free night. We met up at The Leaky, but ended up across town at a bar closer to Dean's house. He didn't want to be too far away from Daphne." "She's not due until June." Ron only shrugged; he had no practical experience with babies outside his nieces and nephews. Harry fanned out the blanket and covered them. "She wasn't feeling well." "Ah." She'd have to pay her a visit. Pretty soon, Hermione was wedged between her first friends, huddled under the same blanket. Warm and content, it felt like home. It felt like family. That feeling had never changed. Harry was looking up with his hands behind his head. Hermione knew he had no idea what he was looking at because he'd never bothered to remember what they'd learned in Astronomy. Ron was in a similar position, with one hand tucked behind his head and the other mere centimetres from hers, equally as unaware. She moved her hand away. Ron dozed off in the time that passed, his head right at her shoulder and breath on her arm. It was only when he let out a light snore that Harry said, "I didn't want you to find out about Narcissa Malfoy like that." It was the last thing she'd expected from him. "You don't have to tell me everything." "Yeah, I know, but I didn't tell you because I wasn't sure how I felt about it myself. It wasn't bad. She'sÉ" He lifted up and used one hand to scrub his face. After struggling to find the words, he settled on: "Interesting." It was reflex that made her snort in disagreement, but Harry laughed all the same. The Narcissa Malfoy she'd met was far more than merely interesting. Complicated seemed more like it. Also: shrewd, overbearing, and proud. Still, she understood why Theo had recommended her over the no-nonsense Susan. "I learned a significant amount of fairly useless information, mostly about wizarding etiquette, but she wasn't mean. Malfoy reminds me of her when he's glaring or questioning someone's status as a being capable of higher thought. Which isÉ basically all the time." Harry shook his head in amusement. "Oh, and she asked me why I named all my children after dead people, so that was fun." There was a moment's pause before they both started laughing. "What did you say?" Hermione asked. "I asked her why everyone in the Black family was named after stars. She looked at me as if I were a special sort of idiot before she said: tradition. Then, she gave me a look that said 'your move' and I just shut up after that." Hermione couldn't hold back her smile. "She's sharp, that's for sure." Which made her inevitable decline all the more tragic. And sobering. Humbling, really. Everything she'd created, built, and overcome, she would soon forget. Hermione couldn't fathom losing her memories. They were pieces of who she was: the good and the bad. Connections between the past and present paved the way for the future. Losing that was a fate no one deserved. One worse than death. "She knew I was going to turn her down before I said anything," Hermione confessed. Harry's eyes found hers. "Why did you turn her down? I won't act like I understand your job, because I don't, but I've never seen you decline anyone." "I don't work with people I know." Ron snuffled in his sleep, his hair tickling her jawline. "Do you really know her?" Harry asked. "Outside of the war, you've got little idea about who she is. You don't know who she's become since. I confess I still don't, but as a parent, I understand her reasons. If I had to, I'd ask Malfoy for help if it meant more time with my kids." Hermione highly doubted that, no matter how serious he sounded. "Besides, you worked with Molly after her poisoning." "That's different," Hermione argued gently, unable to raise her voice at him in any way. They'd been through too much for that. "Narcissa is going to need years of care to fight a battle she's never going to win." "Who better to have on her side than you?" She stared at Harry for a long time, repeating his question in her head over and over, unable to formulate a response. Ron moved, mumbling unintelligible words under his breath as he shifted closer to her. "On a different topic: what are you going to do about that?" Harry nodded in Ron's direction. When she gave him a long look, he feigned an innocent expression. "I'm not getting involved, just asking a question." "What's there to do? We broke up. Years ago. Messily, as I'm sure you remember." The look on his face clearly told her to stop kidding herself. He'd never put himself in the middle of their issues, not even while they were dating. He'd always played the role of the mediator. When it got too bad, he just hid from them both until whatever fight had been resolved. Better than choosing a side. "You know what I mean, Hermione." Harry frowned. "He thinksÑ" "I'm aware of what he thinks, but he's wrong." "Then maybe you should start dating." He ran a hand through his dark hair, messing it up beyond repair. Clearly, he wanted to have the conversation less than she did, but that didn't stop him from talking about it. "As long as you're single, he'll always think he has a chance. He'll never let up. He's stubborn like thatÑlike you." She cut him down with another glare that seemed to roll off him like water. "Hear me out, if you're not so available, he'llÑ" "I don't want to date someone just to make a point, Harry. You know that's not who I am." "I'm not saying that." Harry kept his voice low, his words slow and measured. "I'm saying bring someone around and show Ron that you aren't an option." "Saying I'm not an option should be enough." "I know. I do." "Then why bring it up at all? Why not encourage him to move on?" "I have been, but he's insistent this time." Harry sighed. "If you're unavailable, he'll get the message, and I won't have to listen to his plans for getting you back knowing they won't work." Hermione knew how much of a strain their failure to launch had put on him. "I'll think about it." "That's all I ask." Harry knew her well enough to know that she hardly ever made impulsive decisions. Breaking up with Ron certainly hadn't been one. Quitting the Ministry. Healing. Nothing was done rashly, only after deep thought and consideration of all points. Shifting a bit on the blanket, he turned his body towards her, opened his mouth, and paused. "I know what you'll say. You're happy as you are, but I also know you, Hermione. I know you're stagnant because you're looking for something you haven't found yet." She didn't argue. In the quiet, Hermione could admit that he wasn't wrong. "What do you think I'm looking for?" "What most people look for. Something deeperÉ with greater meaning. Connection. Emotion. Something real." Harry struggled with his next words. "I hope you find it. I know I don't say it as much as I should, but I appreciate everything you do. We all do. You give a lot Ñnot just to your patients, but to your friends as well. To us, especially with Albus. I worry it's too much." Hermione shoved him lightly. "Are you getting maudlin on me?" "No." He rolled his eyes. "But it would be nice for you to get something back in return." "It's fulfilling helping everyone. I'm content and know my limits." Hermione saw his expression turn serious in the near darkness, which made her stop. A light breeze danced in the trees. "Everything I do is because I want to. You know that, right? I don't do it to get something back." "And that makes you deserve whatever it is you want even more." March 28, 2011 No one was more surprised than Molly Weasley when Hermione turned up at the Burrow early Monday morning, armed with a wicker basket full of fresh vegetables and eggs from her small chicken coop. It wasn't much, but they didn't need to keep much around anymore. At least not until the first Sunday of each month, when all the local Weasleys, family friends, their significant others, and any children not away at Hogwarts gathered for family dinner. Appreciative as always, Molly asked her to stay for a while and had a cup of tea ready before she could decline. That was her way. Nurturing, albeit a bit pushy, but her heart was always in the right place. "How are you feeling, dear?" Mrs Weasley's motherly smile made her grin. Reaching over, she tucked a hair behind Hermione's ear. "You're not working too hard, are you?" "I'm doing well, not working too hard, I promise." Hermione sipped her tea, noting the slice of lemon in her Earl Grey. Molly had gotten the sugar just right, as well as the questioning. How strange. A question that drove her to irritation with her own mother sounded so different coming from Molly. "I've gotten my last assignment swapped over to a Primary Healer for maintenance. He's doing quite well. It's remarkable." "That's exciting, dear. I saw his wife in Diagon Alley and she couldn't stop singing your praises. I'm so happy for their family to be whole again." Her smile was genuine, if a little sad like it always was when she thought about Fred. It wasn't much, but it was all Molly could muster. Grief never arrived or left on schedule. Hermione readied herself for its visit by scooting her chair closer and resting her head on Molly's shoulder. They stayed like that until she affectionately squeezed Hermione's arm. "Thank you." "Anytime." Molly smiled. "You do look rested." "I slept well last night." She, Harry, and Ron had spent the evening watching movies at Harry's house. It was his last night before Ginny and the kids returned from Shell Cottage, but he had to work so they weren't up too late. Ron had the next day off and had seen her home. "Good, now drink up before it gets cold." Molly watched until Hermione scooted back and obediently took another sip. "I'm glad you're resting between assignments. I'm proud of you, but you do tend to overextend while you're working. Don't forget to make time for yourself." "I won't." Again. The unspoken implication hung in the air between them. More than awkwardness between Harry, Hermione worried most about what Molly would think when she broke up with Ron. Their past was a bit rocky, but by then, Mrs Weasley was more than a mother figure, she was someone she trusted and confided in. They disagreed as often as they agreed, but overall Molly had given her example after example of how blood didn't always make a family. She was supportive when Hermione returned from Australia with only her parents' forgiveness and phone number, holding her long after she'd cried herself to sleep. And she'd continued that support through the breakup with Ron and then when she'd woken up in St Mungo's, fragile and lost. Molly had seen her through recovery and therapy, Healer Academy, and beyond. Never wavering, sometimes lecturing and nagging, but always loving her. Hermione had returned the care, albeit unexpectedly, two years ago. Molly had come into contact with a letter that had been smeared with poison and delivered to the Burrow, and Hermione taken over her treatment, brewing the antidote with time to spare and no lasting effects. After that incident, Hermione figured out the spell that made them all Unplottable. "Have you and Neville started planning the next planting season? If you haven't yet, let me know when you do. I'd love to help." "Oh, you don't. We canÑ" "I insist." Molly smiled wistfully. "The wars, losing my brothers, Fred, and everyone elseÉ It made me realise how precious time is. Being poisoned reminded me of that as well. It's simple things like planting fruits and vegetables with the little ones that make me appreciate life more. It's priceless. They don't stay little forever, you know, but the memories I make with them will outlive me." The statement stuck with Hermione, and made her revisit her argument with Pansy and her discussion with Harry about Narcissa. She thought about what she knew, what she didn't, and what she had learned along the way. Time was something Narcissa Malfoy was poised to pay any price for, but she would never be able to purchase enough. It must have been difficult for her to swallow her pride and ask for Hermione's help when that was something she had given Molly for free. "What's troubling you, love?" Hermione almost diverted to another topic, but she couldn't. She needed someone objective. Someone without a vested interest in the matter. "I had a consultation with a patient." Hermione carefully considered her words. "She has a disease that I can possibly slow the progression of, with long-term aggressive care, but I declined the case." "Oh, dear, why?" "Theo suggested her, knowing that she was against my rules." "Because you know her?" Molly waited for Hermione to nod. "Then I'm sure he had his reasons." "He did." Hermione was still mulling over that bit. There was one thing about the situation that didn't make sense: Theo's role. The reason for his involvement. His game. She simply couldn't accept the fact that she was the mother of someone Theo considered part of his family. "I don't know what his motivations are, but she requested the best Healer and he thinks I'm it." "From what I've heard of your work, and seen of it myself, I'd have to agree." Molly smiled proudly. "I was supposed to take a lot longer to heal from the poison, but you had me back home quicker than anticipated with your quality care." Lowering her head in quiet modesty, Hermione tucked her hair behind her ear. "It's different with her disease than your poisoning. Her disease is not so uncommon in Muggles, but it is rare in wizardsÑand aggressive. So aggressive, in fact, that not much is known about it. I can't even guarantee that I'll be able to provide the care she needs or the time she wantsÉ it's outside my purview." "I'm confident that you'll read and research to figure out if you can give her the proper care." "You make it sound like I'm going to take the case." "Aren't you? If it were me or Arthur or anyone you care about, would you do it?" "I wouldn't hesitate." "Well, that's against your rules." Molly met her with a challenging look. "Why is this person any different?" Hermione thought back on the way she and Narcissa had glared at each other in her office last week. The differences between them were more than a matter of social class and individual temperament. It went down to their core values and blood. "Plenty of reasons. If the circumstances were reversed, she wouldn't spare me a second thoughtÑ" "I've never seen you turn down someone in need. Not even when you don't like them or vice versa. I've seen you accept patients that don't respect you until after they've been in your care. It's never mattered to you. They are your patient and you remain objective no matter what. You treat them no matter what." In response to Molly's argument, she had no words. "I can't tell you what to do, love. In the end, it's your decision and I'll support whatever you decide. But give it some thought." "I will." "Good." Molly smiled. "Now, when is Neville coming?" "Saturday morning. Harry's taking Lily for a daughter date, so Ginny will come over with the boys to help clear the spot in the vegetable patch. All the seedlings are ready, Neville brought over pots for the aubergines, celery, and broccoli. I let them each pick a fruit or vegetable they want to plant in the greenhouse. Any preferences?" "Cucumbers, definitely. Last year's were so crisp and lovely." Hermione nodded; she already had some ready to be planted. "What did the kids pick?" "Al wants watermelon, which is a tall order, so we'll see. James wants grapes, but I'd definitely have to grow them in the greenhouse with the fruit trees. Lily will eat anything off a bush. I've pruned the blueberry bush and it's looking to be a great year for them. She'll like that. Ginny agrees with James about grapes so I can make wine." They both fondly rolled their eyes. "Regardless, it will be fun." "And what do you want?" Hermione shrugged with a soft smile. "A healthy vegetable patch." "You've got to want more than that." She shook her head. "Healthy crops will suffice." "If you say so." Molly was quiet for a moment and Hermione thought she would make another go at trying to make her confess to wanting more, but then she asked, "Did Ron make any suggestions?" Ah. There it was. Hermione's only problemÑand a similar complaint she had with her own motherÑwas the fact that Molly was still determined for her to become her daughter. In one way or another. "He's invited himself to help this weekend." Molly couldn't hide her elation. "Such a good man, my son is. Always willing to help those he cares about." She gave her a meaningful look that Hermione ignored by drinking her tea. "He's even upstairs right now taking care of the ghoul who woke up Arthur and I this morning by banging on the pipes. He should be down soon." Almost on cue, her youngest son strolled into the kitchen, sweeping his hair from his face. He was dressed in dark jeans, trainers, and the Arsenal jersey her dad had given him ages ago for Christmas. "I've finishedÑ" Ron noticed Hermione just as she finished the last of her tea. "Hey, what are you doing here?" "She brought over eggs and vegetables," Molly said before Hermione could speak. "Be sure to bring her a gift for good luck when we plant this weekend." Hermione's eyes narrowed at the pointed look she gave her son. She had never heard of such a tradition. Molly slowly rose to her feet, lovingly patting her on the shoulder. "You two chat, I'll put up the vegetables." "I canÑ" "No, no. I can handle this. I'll be back soon." Ron watched his mother leave, but before he could take her seat, Hermione stood, ready to leave. Unfortunately, the action put her directly in front of Ron, who wore a look she was all too familiar with. "What are you doing the rest of the day?" The question tumbled out in a rush. At the impatient sigh she involuntarily gave off, he cringed and ran a nervous hand through his hair. "It's been a while since we've hung out. Just the two of us." That was intentional. The last time they'd been alone together, Hermione made a few regrettable decisions out of a culmination of weakness, loneliness, and a habit of doing stupid things to avoid bigger issues. Old habits died harder than she cared to admit. She really had known better. Falling into bed with Ron was equally as problematic as it was destructive. Sex wasn't the issue. Ron was good. When he was passionate, he put everything into it. And he was enthusiastic about her. It just wasn'tÉ enough. It wasn't right. And the problem had less to do with her not wanting him than her wanting something else she couldn't identify. More maybe? Harry had been more right than she ever cared to admit. Ron had seized the opening. What was missing hadn't mattered to either of them at that moment. Not when Ron was right there, kissing her tirelessly, caressing her gently. He did every little thing she wanted, all the ways she wanted, not out of instinct, but born of familiarityÑbecause she'd already told him how to make her happy. Showed him how she liked it. And while Hermione tried to focus on the act itself, focus on him, she couldn't blot out the truth. She was trying to fill a hole with air. She was searching for something she couldn't identify in a place she already knew it wasn't. A place it would never be. That had been over two years ago. After, Hermione told him that it could never happen again and maintained her distance when he didn't believe her. She'd created better boundaries. Organised, categorised, and numbered her rules for why she couldn't do that again. She'd even reinforced her walls every time he tried to breach them. And, more importantly, Hermione successfully suppressed that little feeling of something she hadn't been able to identify. It was irrelevant anyway. She had what she needed in her work and friends. Her life was great. She was healthy and content again. All that feeling had done was make her chase something that didn't exist, like smoke slipping through her fingers, and make poor life choices along the way. But while Hermione moved forward, Ron very much wanted to go back. He stood in front of her like personal space meant nothing. "I'm picking colours with Pansy today for my bathroom." Hermione carefully sidestepped him. "You're welcome to come with us." Ron blanched as if he'd eaten something foul. "I still don't understand why you're friends with her. She tried toÑ" "My friendship with her is my own business. We've made peace with the past. I signed a treaty. Literally. It's done. I've let it go and so has Harry. If he can move on, how is it your right to cling to the past? They'll never be best mates, but they're at least civil with each otherÉ" She thought about the time Harry and Pansy had yelled at each other while she and Ginny watched with amusement and shared taffy. "Well, for the most part." "I really don't want to talk about her. I just want to spend time with you." Hermione folded her arms, eyebrow raised at the intimate tone in his voice. "Respect my choice." "I do. I just think you're wrong." "You didn't think that when we broke up. In fact, you were relieved, if I remember correctly." Ron couldn't deny the truth, but that didn't mean he wouldn't argue his point. "Yes, but you were working all hours of the day and night, Hermione. I barely saw you. I wanted you home, but when you were, you hardly ever let me touch you because you would complain when I asked you to do anything. You were stressed out all the time and you pushed me away once I quit the Auror department. You were always going to work functions, and I didn't mind going with you, but we never spent any time together because someone was always there to pull you away. When I complained about it, you were dismissive. Of course, I wanted to end it. You were miserable, and so was I." He wasn't wrong. Hermione could admit her faults, and had on several occasions, both to him and her therapist. She wasn't perfect, but her imperfections were only a part of their issues. It wasn't like Ron to highlight his own flaws. "I have several edits to make to your arguments. First edit: I didn't complain when you asked me to do something. You asked me to do everything. I cooked and cleaned after working all day, and you sat there like it was an expectation. I'm not your bloody mother. Second editÑ" "I'm not trying to argue with you, Hermione." He had the gall to sigh. "You don't want to argue?" She was already mobilising her fleet of responses and her artillery unit for one, ready to pick anything and everything out of the sky with precision and accuracy. "That's interesting when youÑ" "No." Ron huffed, running a hand over his face. "Okay, maybe I said that wrong. I'm trying to point out that we're different now. When you had your seizure and you quit your jobÉ things changed and got better. You're better now and so am I. I've figured out what I want to do, and you'reÑthe timing is right. We're older. More mature. We can do this." "You don't love me, Ron." She peered up at him with her mouth in a tight line. "Not really." "That's not true." "It is. You say that we can do this. That we're older and things are better. But not once did you say that you still love me." Wide blue eyes contrasted his reddened cheeks. Hermione shook her head. "It's okay that you don't, Ron. Stop forcing yourself to settle for me when I'm not what you truly want." "Just because I didn't say it, doesn't mean I don't feel it." "You're circling again because you think things will be easier now, but they won't. You think what happened changed me, but you're wrong. It only changed my career trajectory, not who I am." She didn't need any more data to prove their personalities didn't work together. They had years of evidence to support her theory of incompatibility. Exhaling her frustration, Hermione rubbed her temple then ran her hand over her face and took another deep breath. "I just want to leave us in the past where we belong. I want my friend back." "You like action more than words." He stepped closer. "So, let me prove it to you." Ron stuck to his beliefs. It was an admirable quality, but just now it was bothersome. What he didn't understand was that she didn't have to do anything. She wasn't obligated to give him an opportunity to resurrect their dead relationship because it was something heÑand everyoneÑ wanted. Expected, even. It didn't work like that. Hermione didn't owe any of them a thing. "I'm leaving. I'll see you this weekend for planting, if you decide to come." Hermione went into the kitchen, where Molly stood at the sink pretending to wash vegetables. She'd heard everything. "I'm leaving, I'll see you Saturday." "Okay, love." Molly gave a faint smile, hesitated, then added, "You do know that I love you both. I've always thought you two would work things out in your own time, but give him some thought." At the affronted look blossoming on Hermione's face, Molly held up her scarred hand. "And if you truly don't love him anymore, don't give him a chance to show you his affections. Let him go so he can move on." What Molly didn't understand was that her advice was too late. Hermione already had. March 29, 2011 When Hermione committed to an idea, she was a Seeker with a Snitch in sight. She'd promised Molly she would think it over, and she did. At sunrise she took her tea and disappeared into her office with Narcissa's file to review and reflect. It turned into a deep dive off a cliff that sent her to unexpected places. Like her parents' home. Her mother was out shopping and her dadÑa late riser since retirementÑwas just starting his day. Teacup in hand, he looked surprised to see her standing in the doorway. "Hi?" "Sorry, I know it's early, Dad, but can I use your computer?" "Of course." Her dad accompanied her into the living room. "Your mum never uses it despite insisting we have it." "Thanks." For two hours, Hermione scoured the internet for research and printed articles on the Muggle counterpart to Narcissa's condition: Lewy Body Dementia. The idea was to learn as much as possible about the disease: the history, symptoms, causes, protein build-up responsible, steps in diagnosis, and progression. Hermione absorbed every detail she could, making notes on a scrap of paper that turned into a notebook when her dad took pity on the overcrowded paper. When he pulled up a chair next to her, Hermione asked, "How much do you know about dementia?" "I'm a retired dentist." Her dad shrugged. "I know about as much as the average person who has no experience with it." He eyed the stack of papers next to the printer. "But what I do know is if you print everything out, we'll need more paper. The cartridge is new, though. It's another thing your mum insisted on having but doesn't use." They shared a smirk. Typical. Hermione bit her fingernail. "Can you get more paper, please? It's about a case I promised to have a second look at." "Oh?" He rarely showed interest in her work. "I didn't think wizards could get diseases like dementia. Or if they did, magic would fix it." "It's not common, but in this case, there's something in the magical core that makes it progress quicker." The one time having magic wasn't beneficial. Her father looked confused so Hermione did her best to explain. "Magic can protect us from a lot, but not everything. In the end, we're still human and our bodies are subject to the same decay." "True, but why research Lewy Body Dementia if it's not what she has?" "Because it's the closest one there is to what she has. Dementia is rare in wizards, there's a little research out there. The Americans have more cases, but no cure, only a course of care and treatment plan. There are other books, too, and getting a working knowledge of something similar may be effective in the long run. This form of dementia in wizards has similar symptoms, and the nature of the diseases are almost identical. It's only the speed of the progression that differs." Her dad was quiet long enough for Hermione to return to scrolling. "You remind me so much of your mum right now." Her dad's smile was modest yet indulgent. "What do you need?" Talking to him had been harmonious and fluid in a way she had sorely missed. It feltÉ nice. They had the same eyes and nose and chin, but for the first time in a while, she didn't feel like a familiar stranger. She actually felt like his daughter. "I'll need paper." Her voice was quiet, heart thundering in her chest. "One or two?" Caught up in the rush of emotions, Hermione barely heard him. "Hmm?" "Paper packs. One or two?" She smiled slowly. "Best make it two." With a nod, he left her alone. It took a minute for Hermione to refocus on her task, but she buried herself in research, moving from one article to the next, frowning at what she found. The disease was relentless, even in Muggles, and receiving a conclusive diagnosis took just as long. Pansy had mentioned that Narcissa had been seeing Healers for at least a year before her diagnosis, which begged the question: How long had she been experiencing symptoms? In Muggles, there were seven stages to dementia, but most patients weren't diagnosed until the fourth. Hermione was steadily making notes when a voice interrupted her concentration. "You should eat." It was her dad again, and he wore a black jacket, his hair clearly tousled by the wind. He also looked slightlyÉ wet? Was it raining? Had he already gone out? One look at the two packs of perfectly dry printer paper under his arm answered her question. In the other was takeaway. "That was fast," Hermione said in wide-eyed bewilderment. "I didn't hear you leave." Her dad chuckled with a quick shake of his frizzy head. "I've been gone an hour. Here. It's probably not as organic as you eat from your garden, butÉ" He sat a bag with what looked like a salad in front of her and awkwardly cleared his throat. "I thought maybe you hadn't eaten?" "Thanks. It's perfect." Outside of whisky-induced moments while painting, he wasn't too expressive. Hermione watched him load the printer with more paper, noting the second food container on a nearby table. "If you want, we can eat together." She was barely able to contain the note of hope in her voice. "No, go ahead. You're researching. I'm going to listen to the Arsenal commentary on the radio." Because she wasn't ready to sever the tenuous connection between them, Hermione ran her fingers through her hair. Then began the careful task of untangling her fingers because she hadn't bothered to brush it that morning after her shower. "So, umÉ when's the next game? Or is the season over? I'm not certain." If this was a way in, she would learn if she had to. Her father looked surprised by the question. "Um. Not over yet. The next game is on the second against Blackburn Rovers. Doesn't look good, but we'll see. It's not been all bad this season." He glanced at her screen. "I'll leave you to it. Let me know if you need anything else." Hermione watched until he was out of sight. Maybe it was nothing, but it felt like something. The salad wasn't good, but the sentiment behind it was enough for Hermione to eat every bite while she worked. Another hour passed before she joined her dad on the sofa. Donning a pair of reading glasses, he skimmed the paper with the sports commentary playing in the background. When he looked over the rim, he noticed her patiently waiting. He ruffled the paper. "What is it?" "Do you have a library card?" Not long later, they were at the library. Her mystified father looked around the library while she searched the shelves. He likely hadn't set foot in there since Hermione was a child, but today he followed behind her while she gathered books on Muggle neurological diseases. A natural step in her pursuit of knowledge. "I think there's a limit." Her dad grinned when she struggled to balance four volumes in her arms while picking up a fifth. He already had three in his arms and looked woefully out of his depth. "In fact, I'm quite certain there is." So there was. Hermione used her dad's card to check out the maximum number, leaving three on the counter with a mournful sigh. They made another stop where she bought him a new set of paintbrushes in thanks before returning home with hundreds of printed pages and a stack of books that were due back in two weeks. It was enough to keep Hermione busy for the rest of the day, but as soon as she made it home, her attention returned to the Healers Narcissa had seen prior to her diagnosis. They had to have files on her, too. Files with pertinent information. She would need those, as well. Just to give Narcissa's case a thorough, fair examination. The Floo calls were uneventful at first. The first three Healers didn't answer. Hermione was just about to give up after drafting owls to send later from the owlery in Godric's Hollow when she realised that, while it was just evening there, it was only around lunchtime where the final Healer was based in Boston. Charles Smith was a graduate of Ilvermorny, and a leading Healer in the field of magical neurological conditions. With twenty years of experience with both the Muggle and magical variety of this disease, he was her best hope for answers. He was also probably eating lunch, but persistence was her middle name so she tried her luck and was rewarded when the Floo finally connected. A voice made the flames dance. "This is Charles Smith." Hermione scrambled to sit in front of the fireplace, her notebook ready. "Hi, sorry for the intrusion and abrupt Floo call." "It's no problem. I was just finishing up a consultation. Your timing is impeccable." Good to know. Now down to business. "Perfect. My name is Hermione Granger and I'm a Healer in London. I was calling because you diagnosed a patient with dementia a few months ago. I'm reviewing their file after a consultation and have a few questions." More like several, but she didn't want to scare him. There was a long enough silence for her to wonder if the transatlantic connection had failed, but then, "And the name of the patient?" "Narcissa Malfoy." Another pause. "Ah, her." That didn't bode well, but considering the person, it made sense. "She's quite difficult." That went without saying. "She subjected herself to my unconventional testing methods, which involve a battery of non-magical tests that generally make most wizardfolk uncomfortable. I think she just wanted answers. However, when I offered to take her on as a patient and work with her Palliative Care Healers, I explained what my care would entail and she didn't agree with my methodology. She said she would find her own Healer." "Oh?" Hermione scratched out more notes. "What didn't she agree with?" "My work with a No-Mag Specialist. They would start her on medication and arrange for Specialists in London to begin working with her." Hermione put her notebook down. Intriguing. "Why a Specialist?" "I've noticed that a combination of magical and non-magical treatment is best to lengthen the time between the phases. When I mentioned my partner and what his care involved, she declined and left my office." "Did she say why?" Hermione popped her knuckles. Her hands were tired from their hard work. "No." Words like prejudice and bigotry came to mind, but Hermione didn't want to judge and sentence Narcissa for a crime she didn't know if she'd actually committed. "I was shocked, but she had a distrust of people without magic that's understandable for patients her age and from her culture. My partner's father is a Squib. His sister is a witch, but he was born without magic. He's familiar with wizarding customs, and I tried to explain this to her, but she refused to have anything to do with him or his methodology. And that was that." "Are there any Healers in my area that have experience with her disease?" "I don't know of any, but dementia in wizards is beginning to rise there. Narcissa was my third patient from overseas that month. The other two started in my care and are doing as well as can be expected." That was good to hear. Charles chuckled to himself, making the flames flare slightly. "It's funny you're calling, the owner of St Mungo's reached out to me and asked me the same questions at the start of the year." Oh? "I advised him to pick someone intelligent with tough skin. They need to be willing to learn a lot very quickly, some background in potions and palliative care is good, physical therapy, too. As long as they're able to treat the situation with a dynamic approach and the delicacy it requires, Narcissa will benefit. With little-known but terminal diseases, it's not a matter of speciality but a matter of patience and understanding." Well, that explained a lot. "Is there a way to incorporate Muggle methods into treating her without a Specialist?" "There are. We focus our treatment on alleviating symptoms. The Muggle Specialist handles the medication aspect of treatment. They focus on therapies, such as physical and speech. Lifestyle changes, too. The same healthy lifestyle changes that are used to prevent dementia in Muggles can also be useful in witches and wizards." "Such as?" "Regular exercise, sleep, mitigating stress, mental stimulation, and social engagement. She has a healthy social life, from what I remember, so I would monitor it to make certain she is not wearing herself out. Also a brain healthy diet. The Healer who cares for her would need to tackle her motor control deterioration and the depression that will likely become an issue as she progresses. Lastly, making sure her family is on board and connected is key, but as a Healer, you know this. In her case, her family needs a plan to ensure her safety as her memories and body deteriorate. From what I understand, she is responsible for her grandson in some capacity." "I recall this." "Remember, she still has magic and accidental bursts of magic are more and more common as she advances. Apparition during a moment of confusion is highly dangerous. Splinching is common and can be fatal." Hermione underlined that note twice. "I'm assuming the magical aspect of treatment involves potions." "Yes, but it's tricky. I've read she's on a five-potion regimen, but I'm not certain if it's working." Judging from what Hermione had seen during their brief meeting, it was not. "I personally use a nine-potion system for my patients. The more specifically targeted the composition of the potion is as it pertains to her metabolic response, the better and more consistently it'll work." And then he listed all nine without her having to ask. Hermione scribbled down each. "There is a potion in development to encompass them all into one. In theory, it may slow the progression of the disease, but we aren't certain. Our researchers are still sorting out the composition and then there's the matter of bureaucracy." She knew a little about how politicians dipped their toes in things they had no business in. "Good luck with that and thank you. If needed, would you be available for future consults?" For other patients, of course. Not Narcissa Malfoy. Hermione had always been committed to the continued sharing of knowledge and Charles seemed like a good ally to have. "I have a Magi-Scheduler, just schedule something when I'm free. You can add me. My middle name is Alexander. There are more Charles Smiths than I can account for." "I'm probably the only Hermione Granger, so I'll be easy to add." They both chuckled and she looked at the time, noticing that almost fifteen minutes had passed. "Thank you. I have a lot to review. Apologies again for disrupting you." "None needed. Good luck treating Mrs Malfoy." "Oh, I'm not treating her. I'm just doing a bit of research." Before ending the Floo call, she swore she heard a very confused, "Wait, what?" Hermione got up, stretched, and went to pour herself black tea with a splash of milk. She was beginning the process of sorting through her findings when she thought about one last call she needed to make: a Floo call to Mrs Malfoy's Primary Healer, who had prescribed the current regimen of potions Narcissa was taking. "Kendrick." The wizard who answered sounded extremely put out. "Hello, my name is Hermione GrangerÑ" "Oh!" With some degree of annoyance, she heard him sputter and noted his abrupt change in tone. "Healer Granger, how can I be of assistance?" "Apologies for the late callÑ" "No, not at all. It's not every day one receives a call from the Hermione Granger. It's an honour." Hermione had to remember she was looking for information, which was going to be an easier mission now that he wanted to impress her. "Thank you. I was reaching out to discuss a patient of yours. Narcissa Malfoy. She was referred to me after being diagnosed withÑ" "Ah, yes. Nasty bit of business her dementia is." "Truly unfortunate." Hermione paused. "How much do you know about her condition?" He obviously hadn't expected her call or line of questioning, so she waited patiently while he found her chartÑa chart Hermione requested a copy of. For review, of course. "Admittedly, I don't know much. I'm more of a General Healer. I prescribed her potions based on what I felt would work best. The Healer who diagnosed her prescribed nine potions, which I thought to be excessive." "How did you pick the five?" "Her disease is incurable. When she sought my care, she was taking seven of the nine that had been prescribed. I picked the five with the most benefits and the least amount of side effects so that she could continue on as normal for as long as possible. It was her request." "And you let patients decide their own treatment?" During the silence, Hermione realised just how snippy her question sounded. "Does she have a personal Potions Master creating these potions specially for her?" "NoÉ" Kendrick sounded like he was trying to figure out how he should feel. "She acquires them from the Apothecary herself." "Did you consider her body chemistry when picking the potions?" "No." That was to be expected from someone with only a passing amount of knowledge on the topic. No matter. The more questions Hermione asked, the more she appeared to irritate him. She didn't know why. Knowing the quantity and quality of ingredients in each prescribed potion was a vital aspect of the art of Healing. When she reminded him of thisÑwell, apparently the firecall disconnected. What a pity. But she could find out for herself. Even without magic, dementia is a strange mind game, Hermione thought as she scoured all the research she had gathered that day. It seemed like magic itself fuelled the disease, making it more unpredictable and aggressive. Full of uncertaintyÑwhich Hermione didn't like. She could keep every variable the same, do everything right, and Narcissa could still die in three years. Or she could live for six. Nine? WellÉ that was unheard of, according to the book. The point was that there were no guarantees, no way to control the outcome or timing, which didn't make her comfortable. But Narcissa wasn't asking for a cureÑshe was asking for time. It wasn't something she couldn't guarantee or even promise. All she could do was try. But would that be good enough? Well, Hermione frownedÑif she were accepting her as a patient. Which she was not. Too full of nervous energy to sit, Hermione drifted around her office, reading and organising her printed research into three piles: relevant, irrelevant, and unknown. She was more than a quarter of the way through when she realised that the sun was no longer in the sky. "Hmm." Then she noticed the haphazard state of the rest of her office. "Well." Someone knocked on her door. Only Pansy. She hadn't even felt the wards tingle in response to her arrival. Interesting. Hermione had half a mind to clear away the papers, but it was too late. Pansy threw open the door. "Granger, you've been in here forÑ" She took one look at the state of her office and gaped. "Are you cracked?" "I'm researching. This is your fault, mind you." Her voice went up an entire octave. "What theÑ" "You're the one who wanted me to reconsider Narcissa!" "Yes, I did, butÑ" Pansy narrowed her eyes. "How long have you been in here?" "It was daylight when I last looked up." "Have you eaten?" "My dad bought me a salad?" Pansy looked her normal level of murderous. "You went to your parents'?" "And the library." She gestured to the books under her 'keep' pile. "I made a few Floo calls, too. One to the American Healer who diagnosed Narcissa." "Okay, let me rephrase this." Pansy's patience was clearly wearing thin. "When was the last time you ate?" "I had a salad." "Fucking hell." Pansy turned on her heel and left. "I'm calling Weasley!" Hermione swore violently, nearly tripping over a pile of papers while she tried to catch up. She called after Pansy but she only covered her ears and made some squawking noise that Luna would have been proud of as she marched to the living room Floo. The last person on Earth Hermione wanted to talk to about her eating habits was Ginny Potter. She craved every moment of peace; there would be hell to pay if she had to show up, not to mention the disappointed looks she'd mastered since becoming a parent. The last time Ginny had worried about her, there had been many memorable threats about calling her therapist Or worse, her mother. No thanks. There was only one option, one move that she could use. She knew better than to bargain with a Slytherin, but desperate times called for desperate measures. "Dinner! Anywhere you want!" Both Pansy and her obnoxious noises stopped. She whirled around, smirking. "I believe we have a deal." March 30, 2011 Roger Davies was a brilliant Healer. His older brother was in the Janus Thickey ward for trying to Obliviate himself after his Muggle-born wife was killed by Snatchers. Because of his brother, and everything his family had endured in the aftermath, maintaining quality mental health was Roger's personal mission. There were countless cases of traumatised Aurors having flashbacks during mission and training exercises. Potions and substance abuse statistics were astronomical, and the sharp increase of instances concerning incorrectly applied memory charms resulted in a surplus of patients in the Janus Thickey ward. After endless petitions, the Wizengamot had allowed Roger to present his proposal for funding to create an Alternative Health department at St Mungo's. The department would research and treat patients whose ailments were primarily mental and didn't fit anywhere else. Working in the Department for the Regulation and Control of Magical Creatures hadn't allowed Hermione time to attend the hearing, but she'd heard that his proposal had been rejected almost unanimously. Kingsley had been the only approval. Roger's failure was worth mentioning because of what happened next. Roughly six months later, Parvati Patil had published an article in The Daily Prophet about her difficulties with survivor's guilt following the death of Lavender Brown. She'd talked about how she wished the Ministry would help those who were still strugglingÑnot with financial compensation, but actual help. Someone who could help navigate the new normal in their community. A companion piece by Dennis Creeveyhad followed the next day. He'd paid tribute to his fallen brother by using Collin's camera to tell the story of his family adjusting to life without him. The two articles had resonated so strongly with everyone who had lost someone during the war that the public actually started talking about their problems, rather than burying them. In fact, the response had been so powerful that the Wizengamot was forced to approve his proposal or risk the wrath of already waning public opinion. In Roger's eyes, the journey didn't matter, only that he'd won the race. His research on PTSD's effect on magic was ground-breaking, with articles published in papers all over the wizarding world. Precedents had been set. Mental health was no longer a taboo. Even before Hermione's abrupt change in careers, she'd read his papers on the effects of trauma and stress on magic while navigating her own life after the war. It should have prepared her for what happened, but hindsight was always perfect, as the saying went. Hermione held Roger in high regard, but there was just one thing. Well, actually, there was more than one, but first things first: She could never trust anyone with a spotless office. Hermione was a tidy person, but she preferred a certain method to all the madness in her world. She preferred just enough clutter in life to make things interesting without coming across asÑwell, cluttered. With the research that went along with their work, there was always bound to be something lying about. Both Roger and his office looked like they were being featured in Witch Weekly. And with his popularity, that very well could have been true. HermioneÑwho wore coveralls that had seen better days, a white shirt, and trainers with anti-slip charmsÑfelt underdressed. But then she remembered the purpose of her visit. She wasn't there to impress anyone. Hermione knocked on his open door twice, trying not to look too judgmental when he lifted his head from his work and waved her into his pristine office with a genial smile on his face. Roger was handsome in a clinical sense. Brown eyes. Medium build. Short brown hair neatly combed. He gave all appearances of the successful person he was. "Punctual as always. Please, come in." Hermione shut the door behind her. "Afternoon, Roger." When she sat down, Hermione peeked at the file on his desk. Not a patient's file, but research. His latest project involved experimental treatment for the extremely traumatised patients that resided full-time in the ward. From what Padma had told her, the trials weren't going well. "How are you?" Roger greeted Hermione with a friendly smile. Even though their individual war stories had made them famous, they were little more than colleagues with a healthy mutual respect for each other. "I was quite surprised when your name appeared on my scheduler. I thought you were between patients." Everyone was well aware that when that occurred, she barely made an appearance at the hospital for anything except meetings with Theo. In lieu of making forced small talk, Hermione got to the heart of the matter. "I was wondering if you've started working on Narcissa Malfoy's case." "I have not." Roger rested his elbows on his oak desk. "I did, however, review her contract. We're scheduled to meet to discuss my terms tomorrow." "Without looking at her case?" Roger bordered on conceited, never once downplaying his talent and successes. Healers were an arrogant bunchÑHermione, too, by extension. How could patients trust them if they weren't confident in their abilities? "I have reviewed her file, but I also have four other priority cases ahead of hers. If I'm being honest, I only accepted Mrs Malfoy because of your reference and, well, curiosity. I've never seen a case like hers. I'm interested in the possibility of running tests to determine if her condition is genetic or environmental." Tests were all well and good, but Narcissa's disease was in the early stages. The quicker they attacked it, the better chance they had. Which led to something else that bothered her about him: Roger was a researcher. He was meticulous, had an eye for detail, and knew how to back up an argument. Hermione was like-minded, found it easy to work with him, but where they differed vastly was in their bedside manner. Roger looked at his patients, not as individuals, but rather in terms of how they might usefully contribute to his future research. He worked tirelessly, and had a record of successes to warrant his method, but at the end of it all, they were walking, talking, and breathing case studies. Roger worked only high-profile cases, people who'd experienced extreme trauma, or those who had severe psychoses and were too dangerous to be in public. His work, as of late, had been developing an elixir used to combat PTSD with several potion makers, but had a growing interest in cases like Narcissa'sÑwhere Muggle disorders were exacerbated by magic. It had been the reason Hermione had referred the case to him to start with. "Have you created a treatment plan?" Hermione asked. "An outline. I want to run more tests to try and determine the cause." That was perfectly fine, butÉ "When do you plan to start treating her?" "A few months of research will do." Roger stroked his chin, deep in thought. "Then we can begin experimental treatment options." "Aggressive options, right?" "I'll experiment with some known potions right nowÑ" She gave a hard exhale, trying to disguise her frustration. "What combination will you use?" "I haven't thought about it." That made what little patience Hermione possessed slip away. "If you don't mind me asking, what exactly have you thought about?" She was done skirting the issue. She wanted an answer. Sooner rather than later. The abrupt question made him falter, then he cocked his head to the side, fixing her with a hard look. Right then, Hermione realised she'd gone too far. "This feels like an interrogation." "Sorry, that wasn't my intention."
