It most certainly was, but she knew when to stand her ground and when a tactful backtrack made the most sense. Right then, the latter was quickly determined to be her best optionÑone that didn't ruin a perfectly good professional relationship. "I've spent a bit of time researching her condition and wanted to have a meeting of minds, but I didn't know you hadn't started. My apologies." She gave a soft, self-deprecating laugh and rubbed the back of her neck with one hand, trying to appear awkward. "You know how I am. Insatiable thirst for knowledge." She was reaching, but falling back on history always worked with schoolmates like Roger, and she watched the stern look on his face soften. Then he smiled, shaking his head as he chuckled. "I do know how you are. I remember seeing you in the library all the time. I still believe you would have done well in Ravenclaw." Everyone believed that, but she'd been sorted into Gryffindor and had no regrets. "As a Ravenclaw, I would have still lost a lot of sleep trying to keep Harry alive." "But gained us a lot of house points." He flashed her a charming grin. Hermione gave a nostalgic sigh. "The things I did for the Gryffindor tapestry." Roger laughed harder while she chuckled along for nothing more than the noise it provided in the silence of his office. Comradery. Her misstep was forgiven. She wouldn't let her impatience cause another outburst. Now, for a different angle. Roger loved to pick her brain when he got stuck or something didn't work. Hermione only gave little clues to satisfy him. And she never gave them for free. "When I came in, I saw that you were reviewing your research on the new experimental treatment regimen for extreme cases of emotional trauma." He didn't bother asking how she'd seen all that so quickly. "Yes, the trials aren't going well." "I heard." Hermione frowned, briefly lamenting with him. "And I might have a few thoughts about it." "Go on." "I believe your failure was due, in part, to the addition of the Draught of Peace. Long-term use of it causes blunted reactions. Have you considered the Elixir of Tranquillity? It's stronger, but not habit-forming or dangerous if brewed incorrectly. Might be worth looking into." With his face lit up, Hermione knew she had piqued his interest. "I'll do that. You truly are brilliant, Hermione. I know you have your patients and interests, but if you ever want to collaborate or even join the research sideÉ" "I'll think about it," Hermione lied smoothly, then circled back. "In all honesty, I'll take my appreciation in a favour." He made a gesture. Go on. "Move Narcissa's case up on your list. I only suggest it as her condition hasn't progressed too much. The faster you start, the more you can work with her from diagnosis and beyond, accounting for every factor conceivable along the way." He looked at her for a moment. Then conceded with a nod. "I can do that." "If you're interested, I've done a bit of research on Narcissa's case. Just to give you a head start, of course. You're a busy Healer, after all." Flattery never hurt, judging by how quickly he agreed to listen to her. Hermione reached into her bag, pulled out a thick roll of parchment, and sat it on his overly neat desk. "A bit?" Roger was amused. "Just a brief synopsis of all the research I've compiled on both the Muggle and magical versions of her disease." Because of the summary he was currently skimming, her desk was a mess of books, parchment, paper, and every bit of research that she'd found. After sushi and sake in London, followed by a film Pansy wanted to see, Hermione had returned home the night before and decided to schedule a short appointment with RogerÑjust for a few follow-up questions. After all, he had to have done just as much research as she had. Right? The answerÑas she now knewÑwas no. Hermione had doubts, and they all started with the state of his officeÑand the man himself who sat behind the desk with only his nameplate, a glass of water, his research file, and a picture of his family facing toward him. She'd done enough digging to know that the time Narcissa wanted wouldn't be possible if he treated her like an experiment. No matter what he promised, she still didn't feel right leaving Narcissa in his care. Her decision, she realised while she watched his eyes scan the parchment, was made. "Actually, I'm going to need that back." Before she could talk herself out of what could potentially be a poor decision, Hermione took her parchment and left with one destination in mind. She didn't stop until she stood outside Theo's door. In an uncharacteristic move, she knocked and didn't wait for someone to open the door before she walked in. And froze at the sight. The man himself was sitting on the sofa in front of the fireplace, his arm thrown over the back. Nothing unusualÉ except for the child sitting next to him, flipping through the children's dictionary while he looked on. There was a softness in Theo's eyes, a fond expression she had never seen. It was bizarrely warm and intensely attractive, but she didn't have time to sort through that because standing in front of the mantle was her target. Narcissa. Wearing stiff grey robes, she appeared deep in thought, touching the gold band on her necklace. Hermione cleared her throat. Three heads snapped to her. "My apologies for the intrusion. I was coming to talk to Theo about your case, but since you're here, I'd like to discuss the terms of your contract, Mrs Malfoy." The smallest head in the room tilted in innocent curiosity, eyebrows knitted together as if he were patiently waiting for her to finish her statement. It wasÉ strangely adult. Even more so because Theo wore a similar expression. "Or I could make an appointment if youÉ need to not speak of these matters around him." She awkwardly gestured to the boy wearing black trousers and a black shirt, his platinum blond hair parted severely to the side. "My grandson is five." Narcissa's face remained impassive. "Scorpius?" The boy immediately abandoned the dictionary and stood. Obedient, even without instruction. Trained. Hermione's frown deepened when she noted a matching expression on Theo's face. Before she could give instructions to her grandson, Theo rose to his feet. "We'll take a walk around the floor while you two speak." He offered the child his hand, but with a look that reminded Hermione very much of his father, Scorpius lowered his eyes to Theo's hand then up at the man who seemed to be imploring him to accept it. "Your manners." Narcissa's voice was nurturing and lecturing all at once. An odd blend. Despite his reluctance, Scorpius complied, placing his hand into Theo's much larger one and allowing himself to be led away. Hermione stepped to the side, her attention so focused on Narcissa that she barely noticed when they left. Narcissa didn't wait for the door to shut. Hermione hadn't expected her to. "I believe you have already refused me. I have a meeting with HealerÑ" "Cancel it. I've decided to accept your case." "Why?" Hermione hadn't expected the question, but had an answer already prepared. "I've got a soft spot for helping those in need." "I am no charity case, Miss Granger." "No, you're not." Hermione decided to approach her from a different angle, one that put them both on the common ground they would need going forward. "You once saved us, and while I can't return the favour, nor do I offer any guarantees, if you let me, I can try to give you what you've requested." Narcissa stiffened. "And what is that, Miss Granger?" "A fighting chance." We rise by lifting others. Robert Ingersoll Chapter End Notes A/N: I have one more main character to introduce and he'll be here in chapter 5 as he and Hermione have had zero reasons to interact thus far. Then the fun begins. Art for this chapter: Scorpius Malfoy by bookloverdream-blessedindeed Chapter 5: Granger Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Five Granger April 2, 2011 The greenhouse was not actually green. It was a homemade hut built of glass, wood, and metal framing. Crafted with extendable charms, the outside wasn't imposing; quaint enough to blend with the scenery around her vegetable patch. The inside was spacious, with room to grow, featuring a pathway through the rich foliage. Some plants were potted, others were mounted on trellises, and more hung from the ceiling. Everything was bursting with colour. Organised and spaced out into sections, Hermione had methodically arranged her plants. Fruit trees in the back, certain flowers in the middle, vegetables that wouldn't survive outdoors dotted between, and an ever-expanding selection of herbs needed to brew her potions on the tables. The empty surfaces served as a reminder of its status as a work in progress. Maybe one day it would house every herb imaginable, but until then, she used what she had to make a difference in her own way. Today, the sun was out but there was a chill in the air from an easterly wind. High clouds softened the light and prevented harsh shadows from forming. Inside the greenhouse was warm and lush, thanks to natural light and temperature control charms. Hermione shed her jumper the moment she entered, placing it on a bench next to the door. With a flick of her wrist, water sprouted over her fruit trees while Hermione contemplated adding a dwarf mango to the mix of pears, lemons, figs, and her latest addition: tangerines. The kids would like that. She moved to check the flowers, fruits, and vegetables before turning her attention to the main reason she'd started the greenhouse: the herbs. Particularly the ones for potions. Her first stop was the dittany plants, which had been on the brink of death for the last year. Her current attempt to grow another stubbornly remained in the windowsill of her office, refusing to sprout. It still looked grim, but resilient. Neville would probably have to take it to his greenhouse for rehabilitation. Nettle was next, but she was careful around it, tending to it with dragon-hide gloves. Asphodel, leaping toadstools, neem, ipecacuanha, and wormwood all looked good. The aconite plants had their own table, and had grown to twenty since Daphne had started collecting them for Hermione over the last three years so she could brew Wolfsbane for Padma's patients. They were thriving, mainly due to Neville's efforts, teachings, and special fertiliser. Hermione had just finished watering them when the doors parted and Daphne walked in. Right behind her, Neville was carrying her latest procurement. Always a gentleman. "Arka." Hermione reverently touched the leaves of the plant with her gloved hand when Neville placed it next to her. It was a wonderful addition that would help create potions to ease the pain from the Cruciatus Curse. It was also an ingredient in several of Narcissa's potions. She hadn't asked for it, but the timing couldn't be more perfect. "Where did you find it?" Daphne gave a sly smile. "A birdie at George's birthday extravaganza last night told me that you have a new patient and arka is an ingredient in a few of their potions." Hermione cut her eyes to NevilleÑthe only person who would have known about her need for certain ingredients because she'd raided his greenhouse before the party. He whistled innocently before giving her a crooked smile. "The birdie came with me to pick it up." Daphne rolled her eyes. "Where are you going to put it?" "We should give it some space away from the others." Neville took it to the last empty table on the row. Hermione turned to Daphne. "How much do I owe you?" "Nothing. That was all good timing. I always let Neville know when I acquire a plant in a deal. Just happened to be your lucky day that the owner died and I was able to negotiateÉ Oh!" Daphne went into her pocket, pulling out a small bag of dried herbs. "Kava, as requested." Something Hermione had needed, as she used a small amount of it in her teas for new patients. It always helped settle their nerves. "I tried to find you the plant, but my seller wasn't willing to part with it or provide a clipping. It'll harm the plant, apparently." "Thanks." Hermione put the herb and her wand into the pocket of her apron. "Blaise will be by later with the rest. He's tied up negotiating a rare painting for a client." The less she knew the better, judging from the expression on Daphne's face. Daphne and Blaise had been partners in the procurement business longer than Hermione had been gardening. It started as a pastime that quickly became a career. An odd choice, but Daphne apparently had a knack for finding things. She'd acquired the almost extinct ingredients to make the antidote for Molly, and done it with time to spare. Their business wasn't just tracking down herbal rarities; they worked with an array of private collectors. From Magizoologists seeking to rescue unusual magical creatures from illegal trade to Ministries all over the world procuring rare, requested items. Daphne left the practical work to Blaise and their underlings while she focused on less complex finds and researching what they were looking forÑespecially since she'd found out she was pregnant. Neville examined the arka plant and took a sample of the soil. "It's extremely healthy. Better than I noticed at first glance. We can let it settle for a week or two then re-pot it. It'll need to be in the exact same quality soil so it doesn't ruin the plant." Hermione cast another look over his shoulder at the plant. "How long before I can prepare it myself?" "A month?" "I've found what you need in the meantime," Daphne said before the initial tickle of worry could form. After all, she had spent the last two days working on her potions alone and the thought of missing pieces tingled at her anxiety. Excellent. First problem handled. It was decent enough outsideÑit wasn't raining or too windyÑso they grabbed blankets and snacks and ventured out to the pasture behind her house. Hermione transfigured a weed into the ugliest mustard yellow sofa she'd ever seen, but after exchanging shrugs with Daphne, they both lounged on opposite ends under a shared blanket. Neville, meanwhile, spread a blanket out on the grass in front of them and laid on his back, propping his hands behind his head. Hermione cast a quick warming charm on him. They stayed like that, enjoying the strange peace while passing around the bag of crisps. There was a silent agreement to let Daphne eat the majority of them. She was eating for two, after all. "Where's Luna?" Hermione asked. She usually came with Neville. "Clearing Nargles from Harry's house. Apparently, there's another infestation." "Ah." "Tell her I said thank you for the gifts for the baby, but I don't think she got the colour right." Daphne chuckled. "Pretty certain I'm having a boy." They wanted it to be a surprise, which didn't seem practical to Hermione, who thought things like preparation and names were important. Not that it mattered. Everyone thought they were having a boyÑsomething about the way she was carrying, but Hermione was no expert. Luna firmly believed she was carrying a girl. In fact, her belief ran so deep that while they were all coming up with masculine names at Girls' Night last night, she only came up with one. Halia. In remembrance of a loved one. Far more fitting than Cho's dull suggestion of Paul that made Pansy stare at her as if she were an escaped mental patient. Neville shrugged. "Maybe you are having a girl." "Of course you'd agree with your girlfriend." Daphne shook the bag of crisps and sighed after realising they were all gone. "You both ate them all." Lifting his head, ready to argue the contrary, Hermione caught Neville's eye. Best if he didn't. There were actual tears when Parvati loudly marvelled at the fact that Daphne had eaten two bags of crisps in one sitting. "Ah, sorry about that. I'll make lunch?" "I suppose I could eat." Daphne sniffed. "But will there be pie?" "I can make that happen." After not being able to eat much during her first trimester, Daphne genuinely looked excited. "Oh, I'm going to start Kingsley's bee garden soon." Neville's news made Hermione perk up. "I'm looking into getting the plants right now and I need to pick from my students who volunteered as soon as they'd heard it was for him." "Understandable." Daphne stretched. "If it's for Kingsley, let me know if you need Blaise and I to find anything." When they both looked at her, she just shrugged. "What? I like him. If he were Minister again, I wouldn't oppose." There was an entire underground movement dedicated to making that happen. "Well, if I need anything, I'll let you know." Neville sat up, crossing his legs as he leaned back on his hands, a breeze blowing through his hair.. "SoÉ your new patient?" "Yeah." Daphne perked up. "What made you change your mind? Pansy said you'd refused her." "How badly did she talk about me?" The blonde said nothing, but her look spoke tomes. Hermione winced. "That bad?" "The phrase 'stubborn bitch' was thrown around, but I think she meant it in a loving way?" Doubtful, but Daphne offered an awkward shrug before it transformed into something more resigned. "She respects Narcissa for reasons I don't understand, but that may be my bias speaking." "Your sister and nephew?" Hermione asked automatically. "Yes. And Draco." Neville's eyebrows climbed even higher than hers. "Malfoy?" Daphne's sigh sounded exactly like Harry's when preparing to launch into a Malfoy-inspired rant. She had one chance to pass a glance at Neville to change the topic, but found the man intrigued instead. "She treated my sister like a placeholder, just waiting for her to die so she could find someone better to take her place as the next Malfoy Matriarch." The disgust in her voice drew Hermione in rather than repelled her. This was pertinent to learning more about the dynamic she was walking into. So far, it wasn't pretty. "I've already given my word about treating her," Hermione said to carefully draw the line between personal and professional. "And I'd never come between that with my opinions. I may not like her, I may find her slowly dying horribly ironic, due to her treatment of my sister." She shook her head. "But for Scorpius not to lose someone else so quickly, I'm willing to put my feelings aside." Spoken like someone who loved him absolutely. Family. "I'm only stabilising her care to give her time. But there was also figuring out the family dynamic to determine just how usefulÑor difficultÑDraco Malfoy would be when it came to his mother's care. Daphne's blue eyes were fixed on her; full of emotions so vivid and complex that no words existed to describe them. "Whatever happens, for what it's worth, I'm glad it's you." Hermione lowered her eyes to the hideous, colourful blanket that kept her warm. "As far as Astoria goes, my grievances run deep," Daphne confessed. "Having Scorpius didn't change her relationship with Narcissa, but I think it appeased her. It gave her someone else to focus on." That didn't sound like a good thing. "Draco was drowning in work and trying to protect them. He did what he could. Astoria handled as much of Scorpius' care as she was able. And when she died, Narcissa took over with her silly rules, rigorous schedule, and expectations. He's five!" "He'sÉ" Hermione frowned. "You've met Scorpius?" "Briefly." "And what did you think?" "He's very well-mannered and obedient. Seems oddly perceptive. Oh, and very quiet." "Everything Narcissa thinks he should beÑexcept one: a child." Now that she mentioned itÉ there was one more thing. "I don't think I heard him utter a word." "And you won't." Daphne sounded brittle. "He doesn't speak." "Can't?" Neville asked. "No, won't. He justÉ stopped." Grief would do that, especially in a child. Daphne ran a rough hand through her hair. Having nothing to offer, Hermione pressed the sole of her shoe against Daphne's in silent support, which was rewarded with an appreciative look. Laying a hand against her bump, Daphne looked to the forest. The trees swayed in the breeze. "Watching him shut down was distressing to Astoria. I promised to keep a close eye on him, butÉ" Daphne shook her head. Her unkept promise lingered in the air around them, resting heavy on her shoulders. Hermione watched as she continued weathering the storm of her emotions, each wave stronger than the last. Daphne tried to tread water. But she sank into the depths of honesty. "It's been hard sinceÑhe hardly looks at me. It's almost like he can't." "Give him time," Neville suggested. "My parents are still alive and sometimes it's hard for me toÉ" He stared up at the sky. The residue of his lost words left the air heavy with the weight of collective pain and loss. The latter, Hermione knew, came in many shapes; it was difficult to distinguish one from the other or rank them in order of magnitude. But hope still lingered. People carried their struggles in different ways, mostly in silence, but it was amazing how some were able to reach out with ease, not only in help, but in solidarity. Neville did just thatÑhad been doing just thatÑfor Daphne since Astoria's death. They were never close before, but now their bond was an offered hand, and in a show of ever-increasing trust, she accepted his support. "Give him time," Neville repeated. "How much?" "As long as he needs." Time passed in a blur of thoughts and internal musings. Somewhere in between, Daphne let go of Neville's hand and he went back to watching the clouds in the sky. It probably wasn't the time, but there was a question begging to be asked. Hermione tried to coax the words back, but they came anyway. "I understand your bias about your sister and nephew butÉ why Malfoy?" Hermione drew the blanket up to her neck, catching the chill in the air despite the warming charms imbued in the fabric. Daphne wasn't as cold, likely heated from within based on the fire in her eyes. "Draco's actionsÑor inactionsÑdirectly affect Scorpius. I'm not sure he even knows how damaging the emotional distance he keeps is. Scorpius is painfully hesitant around him and he needs to do more. Draco doesn't talk to me about his reasons, butÑ" Daphne's expression hardened. "He doesn't have to. Draco is a product of his upbringing and the journey he's been forced to take. It's hard for me to look at Narcissa and not blame her for both of them." April 4, 2011 Hermione once read somewhere that the secret to successful negotiations was finding common ground and acting as an observer rather than the opposition. Reason with them but never issue a threat. The best way to win was to find the information needed, lay out the options, and present the situation with a healthy dose of poise and logic. It reminded her of the wizard's chess lessons Ron had given her, lessons she remembered well while weaving in and out of the Ministry's political maze. He never took mercy on her. Ron would always repeat that the key to winning was to think several moves ahead and only sacrifice pieces she could survive without. There were two problems: One - Hermione was absolute pants at chess. Two - she was far too stubborn to sacrifice any of her pieces. And that was why she and Narcissa were sitting in a silence that threatened to swallow them whole. But it didn't scare her one bit. Hermione was on her third cup of tea while Narcissa was finishing her second of a minty blend that she seemed to enjoy. Under the orders of her son and their boss, her guards were not allowed to take their eyes off their charge at any given time during the appointment. So, they had each taken a corner in her office. One swayed on his feet while the other admired the young Abyssinian Shrivelfig in the corner that wasn't quite ready to go into the greenhouse. Narcissa took another sip of tea. It hadn't been such a terrible appointment thus far. They'd both started by signing the original agreement. While blood status was still important to maintaining prestige in proper society, after the war, the upper echelon of the wizarding world's social order transformed, expanding to include affluent half-bloods and very influential Muggleborns. Proof of their tolerance. Their forward-thinking. A sign of change. Hermione had been to several events before her career change, but not many since. She had a vague, working knowledge of how high society operated. How rigorous each season was with galas and glittering balls, charity and sporting events, magical garden shows and festivities, dinners and tea parties. It wasn't unheard of for there to be an activity every day for a solid month. She was mentally calculating the time difference when she heard a throat clear. Prim and high-pitched. She lifted her head from where her notes were spread across her desk, a single eyebrow arched as she met Narcissa's cool expression. "Yes?" It was intended to sound patient to the point of bordering on condescension. Narcissa's eyes sharpened into shards of ice. Hermione was right on target. "I am willing to negotiate scaling back, Miss Granger. You were gone for years from every society circle." "Which makes it more imperative that I am seen." "I'm sure your son can take your place," Hermione suggested with a flourish of her hand. In his own way. With the person of his choosing.
