Most of the time, she went to the theatres for productions, cinema for films, ballets, or concertsÑand any other events she was interested in. Sometimes, she would walk in the park and marvel at the world around her, or take a book to read until it was too dark to see. She would buy herself gifts, little indulgences she rarely allowed herself to have. Flowers. Sweets. Trinkets. Other times, she would go to the market and buy everything she needed for a quiet night in with an excellent meal and even better companyÉ Herself. Most thought it was bizarre that she actually kept to her dates, but Pansy and Parvati found it liberating; they'd started doing it as well. Ron would infrequently invite himself alongÑan invitation she always declined, as that defeated the entire purpose of self-partnering. Dating herself allowed Hermione to get in touch with what she expected from herself and potentially what she would need from an eventual partner. It allowed her to connect with herself, gave her the opportunity to improve her self-care habits, and afforded her a chance to fulfil her own desires without relying on someone else. In truth, Hermione wanted to take herself out more, but one day a month was all she could manage. Maybe it would change in the future, but for now, it worked. She'd skipped March and was determined not to do the same for April, but date night fell on possibly the worst day imaginable. It started with a storm the previous night shattering a glass pane in her greenhouse. The magical repair had gone quickly, but it had thrown her morning gardening schedule awry. Then, Theo's name appeared on her calendar for an emergency meeting, but he'd neglected to mention the fact that she would be walking into a trap named Roger Davies. "You poached my patient!" There was no point in lying. "I did." Roger faltered, flummoxed by her blunt admission. His mood shifted from astonishment to self-righteous anger. "I never thought you were capable of something like this. Of all people, Hermione, I thought you operated on a higher moral planeÑone where you don't go behind a colleague's back and snatch their patient. A patient, might I add, that you declined! It's uncouth, unprofessional, andÉ" Hermione stopped listening in favour of letting her eyes drift to Theo. To the casual observer, he appeared detached. Exactly the attitude he wanted to convey. But she knew better. There was a glint in his eye that spoke of just how vastly entertained Theo was by Roger's rant. Hermione also caught him chuckling into his tea, not intending on stopping him. Theo's lack of response meant only one thing: he was testing her. Sometimes he did it out of boredom, but more often than not, it was for his own amusement. He had a bit of a god complex that stemmed from being the most observant person in any room. Theo loved to see people squirm, but mainly Hermione because she was widely considered unflappable. He also liked to test her because, no matter how many offers she had refused, he hadn't let go of the belief that Hermione would one day run the Ministry. Even though he was wrong as hell, Hermione had never failed an exam in her life and she certainly wasn't going to start now. So, she took his tests and marvelled at how effortless it was to achieve top marks. Roger had been a blip on her radar when she'd taken Narcissa's case, small in the grand scheme of things. Knowing he wouldn't take too kindly to the theft, Hermione had already prepared a little speech that played to the things Roger valued most: his work and his ego. "Narcissa Malfoy needs to begin treatment immediately, rather than in a few months while you run tests to determine where her disease originates. Its progression is too rapid for you to waste time." "It's insulting that you don't thinÑ" "Do you know what would happen if Narcissa MalfoyÑor even DracoÑgot any sort of inkling that your treatment delays had cost her the time she wanted with her family?" The threat was partially empty, but not one he was willing to test. Silence fell, and Hermione used it to hammer that thought home. "The liability exposure alone would dismantle your career and put all your research into question, Roger. SoÉ yes, I accepted her case." She folded her arms. "And I broke my own rules to do it. I hope that your ego won't blind you into seeing that I've done you a massive favour to protect our department and our work as a whole. Oh, and you." Roger rubbed the back of his neck, as most of the men in her life did when they felt guilty. "IÉ I didn't see it that way." His cheeks turned a touch pink. "I'll still review her file, if you'll provide a copy, and should you need assistance, I'll make myself available. I'd still like to research her family history as well. I want to explore a possible causeÑ" "I can handle that when I'm settled into treating her." "Very well then." He glanced at his watch. "I have a meeting." When Roger left, Theo placed his teacup on the saucer and stood tall. He fixed his cufflinks first before running a hand down his white shirt, smoothing his navy tie and straightening any creases. "Nicely done." The fact that he looked fit didn't stop Hermione from glaring. "A warning would have been appreciated." It was never going to happen. There was a knock on the door. Padma stuck her head in, surprised, but happy to see Hermione. "I need help." The task ended up taking three hours, an exhausting number of tests, and finally the diagnosis: powdered silver poisoning. Then she'd found the bite mark that caused Padma to make the call and report a newly made werewolf. By the time Padma gave them a list of therapy times and the Ministry launched their investigation into how they'd been bitten, it was after four and Hermione only had an hour until her meeting with Narcissa and her current Healers. Hermione was planning to take herself to a show in the city that started at eight, but realised she wouldn't make it on time unless she left from the Malfoys' home. Which was how Hermione ended up in their spacious kitchen on a Thursday evening, dressed for a night out in a dark green lace cocktail dress and nude heels. Her long hair was tamed into soft curls with the help of more than a little Sleekeazy's. Narcissa had given her the first approving look of their brief association. They convened at a long, walnut kitchen table that was larger than her dining table. Hermione laid out Narcissa's treatment plan, research parchment, and the potions she had brewed the night before. They were separated into morning, afternoon, and evening. Nine in total. Keating and Sachs, Narcissa's Palliative Care Healers, sat across from her. As far as she knew, they came once every few days and accompanied her to events as members of her staff. Those roles would have to change over the coming months and years. They read over her instructions regarding Narcissa's potion regime and meal plan while Narcissa flipped through her copy of everything. Oddly quiet with no complaints. Hermione put observing her aside in favour of assessing the private Healers. They were perfectly adequate. Nothing particularly special. Older than Hermione, but younger than Narcissa. Nondescript like most private Healers, with forgettable features; they were everything they needed to be: capable of following instruction and talented enough to do their jobs. They were loyal to Narcissa and Hermione had no way of determining if that would be an advantage or a detriment, but what she did know was they were sceptical of Hermione's presence. And her treatment plan. "Healer Granger," Sachs said after Hermione asked if there were any questions. "This is an extensive list of potions, how do you know they won't counteract one another?" She'd done her damn research, that was how, but Hermione didn't say that. "The plan isn't one I developed myself. I'm following a working regime from a reference." And left it at that. Narcissa coughed delicately. Keating picked up the second in the line of nine vials that she had laid out to show what they all looked like. "And you want her to takeÑ" "The schedule is clear." Hermione maintained a professional tone. "It says here that you'll be providing meals." Sachs pointed at the parchment. "I will. One meal a day for now, starting tomorrow." Truthfully, she hadn't had the energy to prepare meals and brew potions. The latter had taken longer than she'd expected. It probably wasn't her best work, but she hadn't made a mistake. "I don't understand why the house-elves can't prepare all of her meals anymore." Patiently, Hermione gestured to the carefully arranged parchments. "Please review my research as it answers all the questions you may have. You may call me with any additional inquiries you have or anything you wish to add. I would like the next thirty days to monitor NarcissaÑexclusively. But once that passes, I expect you both to return to your normal schedules. Enjoy your holidays." "Paid," Narcissa added. The two were pleased by the sudden vacation and their boss' generosity. "That's all I have for today." Hermione gave them both a smile. "I look forward to working with you both." Handshakes were exchanged before they both gathered their rolls of parchment and left. "You do not seem fond of my Healers, Miss Granger." Hermione didn't have an opinion of them just yet. Only an evaluation that couldÑand likely wouldÑbe adjusted with further interactions as she got to know them better as Healers and people. "I work alone, so it will beÉ different." But not in a bad way. Their presence would allow Hermione to have a life outside of working and brewing once things worsened. The bit about their loyalties still made Hermione worry. "How long have they been employed by your family?" "They cared for Astoria for the duration of her illness. They are trained for Terminal Care." Ah. Years, then. Loyalty might be an issue, should Narcissa stray from her treatment plan. It didn't look like either had the fortitude to stand up to her. That would be a problem if she decided to stop complying, which was likely given the disease progression. The fact that the Malfoys likely had a house-elf was a positive. Should anything happen, Hermione would be alerted. Speaking of elves, Hermione scanned the room with slightly furrowed brows. The last thing she did before quitting the Ministry was persuade the Wizengamot to vote in favour of their emancipation. It had caused quite the stir amongst the families wealthy enough to have the beings passed along generationally. With the new law, families were required to free them and provide an option for rehiring: paid and provided with liveable conditions. Furthermore, extensive documentation had to be provided to the Ministry before any family was allowed to retain an elf. The Ministry performed checks for undocumented elvesÑthe Aurors would be called in if the Beast or Being Division had any trouble. The fines and public shame alone were not worth breaking the rules. Hermine had even started the plans to set up a small sanctuary in Scotland for them to live free of servitude, allowed to come and go as they pleased for workÑshould they decide not to live with the family. She wondered if the plan had lived on. "Miss Granger, if you are looking for our house-elf, we only employ one part-time. Draco feels this house is too small for a full staff. He mainly does the cleaning and helps with Scorpius' evening plans." Hermione hadn't had an opportunity to explore their home, but based on the kitchen and the adjacent living room alone, she doubted the Malfoys would live anywhere that could be described as small. It was modern in design for such a traditional family; another disruption in her perception of them. Narcissa, in her plum dress robes, staple necklace with the gold band, and hair styled under her matching fancy hat, seemed out of place and too old-fashioned while she sat next to Hermione at the head of the table. The area itself was clean, open, and bright, with windows that let in plenty of light and white walls. Light wood flooring offset the dark coloured cabinets in the kitchen, and the granite counters matched the waterfall island. The living room had dark grey sofas with a coffee table between them, all adequately spaced away from the fireplace she'd stepped out of over thirty minutes before. Artwork was tastefully spaced throughout, and Hermione saw more leading up the stairs on the other side of the room. She knew Narcissa hadn't designed it. There were also a lot of Pansy's dŽcor staples present in the room, but it didn't explain how modern it was. And she found herself wonderingÉ "Zippy!" Narcissa called politely and the house-elf materialised, wearing a black bowtie and looking quite healthy. "Yes, Mistress." "This is Miss Hermione Granger." Hermione gave a courteous nod at the elf, who stared at her with large eyes, filled with recognition. AndÉ admiration. "She will be tending to me along with Keating and Sachs," Narcissa said. "You're to do as she instructs as if she were a member of this family." "It will be an honour." Narcissa eyed the smaller creature before her eyes slid to Hermione. "Thank you, Zippy. Now, please update me on Scorpius' progression through his schedule." "Young Master is finishing etiquette. Miss Prichard will dress him in proper dinner attire and he will be ready shortly. Master Draco will have no need for me. I will go home." "Thank you. Enjoy your holiday." Zippy gave another reverent nod and vanished with a snap of his fingers. Hermione was baffled on so many levels that she wasn't even going to try and point them all out lest she confuse herself further. Not only was it a horrible feeling, it was a completely foreign concept. But what made matters worse was even though Hermione tried not to think about it, she found it was like trying not to breathe: very soon the strain would become too great and she had to take a desperate breath. Which was exactly what happened. Her thoughts ran wild. Zippy was well-spoken for a house-elf. More so than Kreacher. Reverent, as most were of the families they worked for, but he worked part-time, had a home, and days off. That was enough of a shock to last her a long time, yet the unsettling surprises continued to pile up. Etiquette lessons for a five-year-old? Ginny had a tough time finishing a meal with Albus without him getting food on his clothes. Teaching him to use the correct cutlery while doing it sounded like an impossible feat. Blessedly, after that, the wheels in her brain slowed long enough to catch and spit out her last two points of confusionÑtheir modern home and, hell, themÑbut she couldn't muster up an analysis on either. Those would take time. Loads of it. And a whiteboard. Hermione realised she was broadcasting very loudly in the silence that followed Zippy's departure when she noticed Narcissa regarding her with a raised brow. Her head was cocked to the side, already prepared for another skirmish. To be fair, discussions that occurred due to their conflicting philosophies already seemed normal, even after only a couple of interactions. It was not as professional as she'd like, but better than one could hope for, given the amount of time they would spend in each other's company. Hermione had a rule: she only played defence. Offensive action was left to Narcissa, who played so well it begged to wonder if she was only asking questions to test Hermione's boundaries, poking and prodding to see how much she could take before reaching her limit. Every patient tried her professional patience; so far, none had succeeded. "Tell me, Miss Granger, were you expecting something different from the house-elf?" Based on her tone, her tense body language, and the thin line of her pursed lips, Hermione knew better than to answer that question as honestly as she otherwise would have. Narcissa looked more inclined to start a war than make peace. "I look forward to working with him." It was the wrong response. "I know that my family does not have the best reputation." Another one of those understatements, to say the least. Narcissa folded the parchment with her treatment plans. "However, I assure you that Zippy is treated fairly. He is not bound to my family, as he also works for the Greengrass family. Times have changed, Miss Granger." "They have," Hermione agreed with a slight nod, but because she couldn't help herself, she added, "By law." Narcissa dug her sanctimonious heels in. "Be that as it may, it still should speak for itself that we are abiding by said law. There are always ways to circumvent any rule." They both knew that better than most. "True, but the alternative would bring unwanted attention to your family; attention that you don't need if you want to maintain the place you've worked so hard to regain in society, which seems to be important to you. So, your compliance is a tactical avenue and, therefore, means little to me." Hermione paused. "It is, however, nice to see." Then she stood, gathering the nine potion vials she'd placed on the table earlier and sliding them in with the others in the wicker basket she'd brought with her. Hermione went to search for a place to store the vials. Narcissa's eyes were on her the entire time she surveyed the long row of walnut cabinets. The open cabinets were lined with decorative kitchen items. Hermione opened the door in front of her. Glasses, cups, teacups. She closed it. "Miss GrangerÉ" Mrs Malfoy's tone was thoughtful; it sounded like she had been contemplating whatever she was about to say for a while. "I have noticed in our interactions that you are very opinionated, and you do so little to censor yourself." "You're not wrong, but in this regard I think we might be more alike than you'd care to admit." Narcissa gave a flippant wave of her dainty hand. "At my age, I believe I have earned the right to speak my mind, wouldn't you say?" "You do have that right, just as I have a right to speak mine. Age doesn't mean that you have a free pass, nor does it mean that you're exempt from being held accountable for your words and actions." "I mean no offence, of course. I am just getting to know you better, since we are to be around each other for the foreseeable future." Narcissa cleared her throat like she wanted to say more, but stopped. Hermione glanced over her shoulder at her, eyebrow raised expectantly. "Go on." "It's just that during our interactions, I've noticed a few things. You are Draco's age, yes?" "From what I remember, I'm nearly a year older, but close. I'll be thirty-two in September." "Are you married?" "No." "Divorced?" There was an antiquated disgust in her tone at the word. "No." "Engaged?" "No, I'm single." "For years, I imagine, hmm?" The question sounded innocent, but there was something patronising beneath it. "Your lack of response means the answer is yes. Interesting." Narcissa ran her fingers over the necklace that often clashed with her attire. "That you are unmarried at your age makes sense. Your uncompromising attitude is unbecoming of a woman who seeks a husband." "Not everyone wants to marry." She levelled Hermione with a knowing look. "You are quite liberal, Miss Granger." And it was the same tone someone very rich would use to call another person very poor. "However, you are a woman. It is in our nature to want to marry, raise a family, settle down, and be a wife." There were two vastly different opinions competing for dominance in Hermione's head. The first was the bothersome truth that Narcissa wasn't wrong. Hermione had toyed around with the idea of committing to building a life with someone and having children of her own, but it hadn't worked. If she were being perfectly honest, after Ron, she'd never given it an honest try. Perhaps it was out of fear of making mistakes or her hesitancy of commitment itself. However, the second idea was louder, more rankled by Narcissa's accusation. Hermione was indeed a woman. While she wouldn't judge anyone who wanted to go that routeÑbecause it was their choiceÑshe had more lofty aspirations than only being a wife, married for the sole purpose of being a mother. But as a professional, she refused to let Narcissa provoke her. In lieu of a response, she continued searching for a cabinet to store her weekly potions. That didn't stop her from formulating at least seventeen responses in her head. Because one wasn't enough. "The upper corner cabinet is empty and will do just fine." As Hermione started carefully placing the labelled vials in rows and organising them, Narcissa delicately cleared her throat as if she were about to address a captive audience. Already flustered from having to censor herself, Hermione tensed and gritted her teeth. "Yes?" "I must confess I am intrigued by you, Miss Granger." She wasn't certain if that was good or bad, but decided it best if she allowed Narcissa to continue uninterrupted. If her voice had been a little higher in pitch, she would have reminded her of Umbridge. "I see a lot of myself in you, and because of that, I have decided to give you a bit of friendly advice. Just a little something my mother once said to me." Hermione ignored the way the woman's words made her feel like a rat who had learned how to push a button without being trained. "I am not accustomed to your culture, Miss Granger, but wizards do not want a wife who emasculates them, who challenges them in every aspect, and is too capable of taking care of themselves. Too used to being alone. Wizards want to be needed. Adored. Catered to. They want to lead, not be led. Even if you have to fake it, Miss Granger, I suggest you learn to do that if you ever hope to marry." Hermione paused halfway between placing the last of her morning potions in a neat row on the shelf as she decided whether to respond or pretend she hadn't heard a thing. It wasn't the first time someone had said that to her, so it didn't grate at her like it would have in any other situation. In fact, Hermione silently thanked Mrs Weasley whoÑduring all their little talks when she was dating RonÑhad unwittingly prepared her for the current situation. Right down to her response. Yes, response, because she could no longer hold her peace when it had been so thoroughly disrupted. It simply wasn't in her nature. Hermione placed a vial on the shelf and continued working, finishing the last of the morning vials before she inhaled, exhaled, and summoned a chill in her voice that even she hadn't expected. "That's the difference between us, I suppose." "Oh?" Hermione started on the afternoon vials, still facing away. "Unlike you, I won't fake it or play a role just to appeal to or please a man. Yes, man, because wizards are men, first and foremost. There is no distinction between the two. If a man wants me, he'll want me as I am now and he'll want who I will become. I'm constantly growing and changing, just as I expect he will be. He won't be intimidated or emasculated by my powerÑor meÑbecause the man I choose will be just that: a man. Not an insecure child." She tried to force the energy that accompanied her words to pass, but it didn't. "The man I choose will be secure in himself and the roles that we will play in each other's lives. And when I cater to him, it won't be because I'm appealing to his ego, but because I want to. Because I love and respect him and what we share. When he chooses to lead, I'll follow, not blindly or as an act of appeasement, but by choice. Before anything else, we are partnersÑwhich means I'll trust him not to make decisions that will affect us both jointly and individually without consulting me first. It'll be our duty to each other and theÑ" "Draco, how long have you been standing there?" It happened in a series of blunders that might have been avoided had Hermione been more cognisant about what was going on around her and not fully immersed in her rant. It started with the wind vanishing from the sails of her argument; the energy behind her fervent words died in her throat at the interruption. Then she lost her footingÑboth figuratively and literallyÑas her heel lost its grip, skidding out from under her. Narcissa's gasp was all she heard when she only just managed to keep a vial safeÑand not ruin a night's worth of workÑby catching herself with her elbows on the counter. "Miss Granger, are you all right?" Narcissa had the decency to sound worried. "I'm quite all right." Hermione pulled herself back up and resumed her task. Her ankle hurt as much as her pride, but she would rather break the former than admit she was anything except fine. "Just going to finish up here and head out. I'll be back in the morning with your meals." "Are you certainÑ" "I'm fine!" And because that sounded too sharp, Hermione tried again, still with her back turned. "Thank you for your concern but I am perfectly well." The silence that followed was as long as it was deafening. She could feel their eyes on her. Her cheeks warmed, but Hermione took her time and composed herself as she continued placing the vials of potions on the shelf. Noticing she was running low on excuses not to turn around, she was relieved when Narcissa started talking to her son. "How long have you been home, Draco?" "Not long. How was Scorpius today?" The first thing Hermione noticed was that he sounded olderÑof course he did. They were no longer scared children on opposing sides of a war. It made sense that his voice was different, that his pitch had deepened to a more confident, richer tenor. He still sounded posh, something that would never change, but Hermione found it strangely reassuring to know that while some things could change, there were still a fair few constants. "He was well-behaved and there were no incidents. Not that you need to worry." The phrasing made Hermione tilt her head before she pushed the twinge of curiosity aside. She had another ten minutes until she needed to leave in order to make it to the theatre in time for the show. The tension in the room had spiked so high she knew better than to draw any attention to herself. Or her hurt ankle. "Good." He took an audible breath and Hermione carefully clanked two vials together to hide the fact that she was listening. "Where is he?" There was a tinge of something there that she couldn't define. "I am taking him to dinner at the Greengrass Estate," Narcissa said. "I assume you will be working late againÉ" "That is correct." Another silence fell, but it didn't last nearly as long. "Please shave that ghastly facial hair off your face. I have scheduled a marriage meeting with the elder Sayre sister. She is twenty-five now, and while I feel that is old, youÑ" "Cancel it." Malfoy's tone held a sort of finality Hermione understood. The conversation was over. "I'll be working in my office tonight." "You could at least show your face at dinnerÑ" "No. Enjoy your evening." "I know Scorpius comes to sit in your office when he is put to bed. Do not keep him up late, Draco. He has a schedule to keep. The nanny says he has been grouchy in the mornings lately." "What would you have me do, Mother?" The question was hollow. Empty. A brittle pause followed. "Would you have me turn him away?" "DracoÑ" "We're finished here." Hermione was still facing away from them, but the tension that lingered was loud and angry. Visceral. But that strain was coming from Malfoy, tangible enough that she thought she could reach out and touch it. She wouldn't though. She knew better. It might have seemed as controlled as Malfoy's tone, but something that inherently volatile would surely explode with the slightest bit of carelessness. The sound of Narcissa moving reached her just before she heard her heels connect with the hardwood floor. "Is that all for today, Miss Granger?" There was a layer of exhaustion in Narcissa's voice that hadn't been there earlier. "I have another engagement to attend." "Yes," Hermione answered, but only after a small delay as she triedÑfor the sake of proprietyÑnot to appear as though she was eavesdropping. Which she was. Honestly, how could they expect anyone not to listen? Hermione was nosy. Too much so for her own good. With an insatiable thirst for knowledge in any formÑeven when it didn't pertain to herÑhaving a cryptic conversation in her presence was like placing a glass of Firewhisky in front of an alcoholic and telling them not to drink. An impossible feat. When she considered it, Hermione arrived at an excellent question: Could it be considered eavesdropping when they were speaking so freely in front of her? The answer was simple: No. She cleared her throat. "I'll finish here and see you in the morning." "Very well." Narcissa's heels clicked across the wood floor, echoing as she exited. She paused, but then continued, her footsteps fading into nothing. Hermione relaxed and placed the last vial on the shelf, then remembered something important. She wasn't alone. Closing the cabinet, she carefully turned, keeping her eyes on the floor in an attempt to mind her aching ankle. She braced her hands on the edge of the granite countertop as she lifted her gaze to the remaining Malfoy, who was partially obscured by the island between them. Despite Parvati's recent rants about Malfoy being climbable, for the last year and a half, Hermione had been listening to Harry vocalise his grievances about his behaviour and character. Harry's complaints coupled with how she remembered him had created a detailed picture in Hermione's head of what Draco Malfoy was supposed to look like: a thin, pale, sneering prat with cold grey eyes who wore his hair long like the father he sought to emulate. The real version wasn't anywhere close to that. Except for his attitude, Malfoy had never been described as ugly. If anything, the passage of time only served to make him more aesthetically appealing. The thought was so ridiculous Hermione instantly rejected it. For good measure, she then had the banned notion burned, the ashes swept up into a neat pile, and binned them. Sweeping always left a bit of residue, but not enough to matter. Malfoy was taller than she remembered, perhaps a head taller than Harry, still lean and pale, but not in the hollow and almost translucent way he'd looked after living the hell of having Voldemort as a murderous house guest. Today, he looked stronger. Serious. Poised. He stood straight, far more confident. Hermione figured that had come with age because Malfoy had maturedÑat least, physically. TimeÑand a bit of facial hairÑhad sharpened his features, made him more defined. But not refined enough to make him look like his father. On that note, Malfoy wore his blond hair different than she'd expected, not long like other pureblooded men, but short and parted. ItÉ suited him. He wasÉ He was walking towards her. His shoes echoed on the wood floors. Hermione never averted her eyes, aware that she was staring boldly at him. But he returned her gaze with one of his own, grey eyes unreadableÑexcept for a twitch in his jaw and a glimmer of what looked like an odd mix of curiosity and suspicion as he stopped at the opposite end of the kitchen. The way they stood now, facing each other with no island between them, felt as if they were readying for a duel. It made her pulse quicken, readying for a fight. Her wand hand itched to feel her trusty vine wood in its grasp, but it was tucked in her beaded bag halfway between them. Hermione pushed herself off the counter, walking normally, confidently, despite the stabbing pain in her ankle. She retrieved her bag and cut her eyes back to the man who hadn't moved a muscle, noting little things: the way he stood, his signet ring, the way he watched her like a chess master watched his opponent's moves. But mostly, Hermione noticed his body language: relaxed and completely at odds with the intensity that rolled off him. She had to say something. The air between them was too taut to remain silent, so Hermione opted for something simple. "Malfoy." She searched his face, but saw nothing discernibleÉ except for the flip of the switch when he stopped looking and started seeing. The weight of his gaze on her was unsettling. Hermione fought off the uncomfortable feeling, hardly recognising herself in this scenario where she should have had plenty to say, but was unable to speak. Malfoy's eyes cut from her feet to her face so fast, but Hermione did not miss the fact that in just that look, he had assessed her almost as completely as she had him. A quick glance at the wall clock behind him, coupled with a strange little jolt, told her that it was time to go. Turning on her heels, Hermione made sure to keep the pain off her face and out of her gait, and she walked away. Careful steps, but not too careful. She felt his eyes on her like pin pricks. It only made her want to leave quicker. Her exit strategy was nearly a success until he said one word. "Granger." It took until intermission for Hermione to realise that something else had changed about him. Malfoy hadn't said her name like a curse or a filthy word that he had to spit out of his mouth. Instead, it sounded like a riddle. One he intended to solve. The beginning of wisdom is to call things by their right names. Proverb Chapter End Notes A/N: And the ghost of Draco Malfoy appears in the flesh. Art for this chapter: Hermione's Greenhouse by hohoberiberi Chapter 6: ThereIs NoCrown Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Six There Is No Crown April 10, 2011 Percy Weasley didn't do social calls. At least, social calls without purpose. That would be a waste of his time, and he had never been the sort to care for such a thing. He and Hermione were close, having been friends for yearsÑthrough Ron, obviously, but outside of him, too. Percy managed her legal documentation, concerns, and questions. He also kept her abreast of the Ministry's woes, but nothing else. Percy was careful, just in case anyone had questions for her. So, when Percy stepped out of her Floo after nine, while she was kneading dough, Hermione regarded him with a puzzled expression that cooled to something wary when she noticed his casual attire: grey trousers and a plaid shirt. He might as well have been in pyjamas. "Are you all right?" she asked with a tilt of her head. "Everything okay with Arthur and Molly?" "Yes, of course." Very strange. It got weirder when he washed and dried his hands before approaching. Ready to help. Hermione arched an eyebrow. "Do you even know how to bake bread?" "No, but I make an excellent banoffee pie." Good enough. He took over kneading while Hermione prepared the bread makerÑa gift to herself after several disastrous attempts. Percy worked quietly, his mind elsewhere. The pan was ready before he finished and she had to bump his shoulder to get his attention. Embarrassment tinted his features in a way that reminded her so much of Ron that she snorted. Stepping to the side, he allowed Hermione to finish, and by the time she washed her hands, his were cleaned and he was seated at the island. "I know this isn't a social call." She looked over her shoulder at the redhead whose pensiveness had returned amid the silence. "It's not." Percy had never been anything except direct. "More than one thing?" "Of course." Hermione joined him, but only after grabbing Schiava from the chiller and summoning two glasses. He poured while she sat down and they both swirled their glasses before drinking quietly. It was good. Sweet like berries and cotton candy. She'd picked it because, while Percy would claim to prefer merlot or chianti, Hermione knew he had a penchant for sweeter dry wine that was light and elegant. "Where do you want to start? Business might be easier. How's your pet project?" She shot Percy a meaningful look, knowing little about it beyond its existence. Tactical ignorance. "Slowly coming together. I'm trying to arrange a visit to a Cambridge library with a secret, private wizarding section. He happens to have access that I've been trying to obtain for the last four years." Hermione's eyebrow lifted in silent curiosity at the very mention of a secret library. "It has records from around the time of the Wizengamot and the Ministry's formation. The less you know the better." "True, but I wasn't aware that any of you worked jointly on the pet project. Or really knew each other outside of wherever it is you all meet to plot." "We don't. We're all linked by a common goal. I just happen to be working with this person." Hermione continued sipping her wine, the smell of baking bread beginning to suffuse the kitchen. "Anything else?" In another uncharacteristic move, instead of savouring, Percy finished his wine in two mouthfuls and refilled. Whatever was bothering him wouldn't leave him alone. Frustrating for someone like him. Hermione could relate. It had to be a more intricate problem with an elusive answer. Human in both element and scale. Personal. Hermione couldn't think of the last time either of them skirted those lines. Maybe around two years ago when Penelope Clearwater eloped with a famous American Quidditch player. Percy had taken the blow with ease, but still used her home as an escape from everyone's prying eyes and Molly's nagging about when he was going to settle down. Tonight was different. "Just one thing." Percy exhaled the last little bit of what held him back and addressed his issue head-on like the Gryffindor he was. "Pansy." Hermione looked at him once before finishing her glass as well. She needed a fresh one to tackle that issue. They both did. "What about her?" Hermione carefully asked as she poured, glancing at him. Percy ran a frustrated hand through his styled hair, messing it up. Then scowled at himself before fixing it, but it was no longer the same. He wasÉ off balance. Both shaken and still composed in a way only he could be. She knew the reasonÑthe who that had slipped her way under his skin with a single interaction. Admittedly, it had lasted long past dinner. They'd gone from the table to the sofa in the conservatory, then to the swing outside. The exchange hadn't been constant chatter from what she'd remembered, just bits of conversation. Pansy's curiosity was louder than her apathy, and Percy shed his perfectly crafted skin to laugh at something she'd said. Small smiles had been thrown back and forth between the two, which served as confirmation of the formation of tenuous strings of connection. Hermione had witnessed most of their exchange from the vantage point where she, Harry, and Ron had drank and talked until they were laughing at nothing and grinning at everything. Even through the haze of wine, even while brushing Ron's hand off her knee every so often, she could still see that Percy had seemedÉ at ease in a way Hermione wouldn't have believed possible had she not seen it for herself. Far less removed and more engaged, there had been an air of genuine contentment around Percy, one she'd never experienced for herself with another person who wasn't a close friend or pseudo-family member. Hermione was self-aware and realistic enough to know that something like that wouldn't happen with her. Just as she knew it was unlikely that she even could expose herself the same way Percy naturally had around Pansy. He was in deep, far before Harry had taken one good look, laughed, and said just that. "How long have you been friends with her?" Percy was trying not to sound as hungry for knowledge as he was. "A couple of years. We have a peace treaty." "Literally or figuratively?" "Both." "What?" Percy sounded equally confused and intrigued. "We've come a long way from arguing about kitchen cabinet colours. We're not as different as I'd once thought." She smirked fondly. "Well, we are, but I've learned to appreciate that." "She has a story." Then he frowned. "It's probably not your place to say." "Everyone has a story, but it's easy to get swept up in what we think a person's story might be, when in fact, we really have no idea unless they share it with us. But you're right. It's not my place to tell you hers. I don't know everything like Daphne does." But since Hermione was a good friend and had a great deal of respect for him, she levelled with him. "However, I can offer you a bit of advice, if that's something you might want." "I doubt I'd be here if I didn't need a bit of guidance." This was outside her comfort zone, but he had askedÑjust as she had offered. "PansiesÉ are colourful and delicate." Hermione ran her finger along the rim of her wine glass. "Common but beautiful. They live under conditions where most things would die." She looked over to find Percy watching as if he were committing her words to memory. He truly wanted to understand; it was endearing. "Like her namesake, Pansy may not look like it, but she's tough and stubborn. She won't quit, has lived through harsh conditions, and doesn't need you for her continued survival." "That doesn't sound daunting at all." "It's as simple as it's not." Hermione shifted in her chair. "Pansy has to want you. She has to choose you. She has to look at each of her options, each of her reasons for saying no, and decide to say yes, despite it all. And you have to be patient until she does." Percy drank more of his wine. "Explains why she's ignored all my letters. I even sent her a rose today. PeachÉ for sincerity." He blushed from the neck up, resting his elbow on the table, then set his chin on his fist. She found him relatable like this. Percy wasn't all waistcoats, confidence, and a member of a quiet revolution right now. He was just a man who didn't understand a woman's heart. Or anything else about her, for that matter. "I'm not certain if she even liked it." "Did you get it back?" Hermione asked. "No." "Then she loved it." That much she knew for certain. Pansy had no problem rejecting anyone. "She's prickly as hell, so be consistent, but not clingy. Don't back her into a corner. Let her breathe. She's got commitment issues, but let her tell you about her history because it makes her who she isÉ which you seem to be drawn to." The flash of smile she saw was boyish for someone so serious and it disappeared before Hermione could call him out on it. Percy sat up straighter. "I have a lot of work to do." "You do. Arithmancy might be easier." "Well, it's a good thing I got an O." Hermione laughed, and glanced at the clock. The bread would be done soon. "You know, HermioneÉ" Percy spoke deliberately, signalling a purposeful shift in their conversation. "You're good at giving advice." With a shrug, she took a sip. "I happen to have knowledge on this particular subject." "Relationships?" "No, Pansy." Percy chuckled, appearing far more relaxed and grounded than he had been when he'd first arrived. Unfortunately, he was secure enough in himself to turn his focus to her. "What about relationships? My little brother is circling againÉ" "It seems everyone knows this." Hermione drained her second glass, beginning to feel the flush of inebriation. "Isn't there something better to talk about? George and Angelina? They're making a real go at it." "Which is why everyone is turning their attention to him." "And not you?" Percy rolled his eyes. "I've been a bit preoccupied trying to restore a government. Apparently, it's still not a good enough excuse for my mother." Hermione barked out a laugh, then covered her mouth when he scowled. "Trust me, my mum is the same. At least yours understands the good you're trying to do. Mine?" She winced. "If she had it her way, I'd be married to Ron with two children by now." "I think that's my mother's wish for you as well. Ron wouldn't oppose either." His tone turned careful, blue eyes exploratory. "What about you? What do you want?" "Not that." "Not ever?" Hermione considered it. "I won't say not ever, but not with Ron." Percy allowed the silence between them to linger, but not for long. "You do know that you didn't answer the questionÑthat you never answer the question about what it is you want. Only what you don't. I won't argue about it. It's just something for you to consider." Coupled with Hermione's trust in him, the wine began to dull her senses and her walls dipped lower. She exhaled her confession. "I haven't put much thought into my wants. I have everything I need. I'm comfortable. I'm content. I have a career and purpose. Yes, I have Death Eaters to worry about and there is the threat of Greyback lingering, but I'mÑ" "Surviving." Percy gave her a look that made Hermione's skin itch. "Yes, I know. But you're also lying to yourself if you believe you don't want more." "Have you been talking to Harry?" "No. Should I?" He smiled when she pulled a face. "Without a doubt, you are one of the smartest people I know. You're unstoppable. People like you are always in pursuit of something. It's not in your nature to be stagnant, but you are." "I'm allowed to not push myself harder than I have to." He raised the wine glass to his lips. "Of course, when it comes to working long hours or over.extending yourself to meet everyone else's needs, absolutely don't push. However, when it comes to self-fulfilment, determining what you want, what you're looking for, and what will make you happyÑor even who, if that's the path you wind up onÑyou need to invest the time." She knew deep down Percy was rightÑno reflection was needed. And that was just as difficult to accept as it was to correct. The truth was that there was nothing except her own self-awareness to push her forward. And it wasn't enough. "My brother clings to this idea that you will come to your senses and realise that you two belong together." "And do you think he's right?" "No. Ron'sÉ Well, Ron's still trying to figure out what he wants. But it's different than with you. He actually does know. He just hasn't looked anywhere else to find it, and until he does, he'll always come back to what's familiar. He'll try to make you fit even when you don't." That simply would not work. She and Ron were still the same people, just newer moulds of who they had once been. Changed by experience and circumstances, their contrasts were more striking. They did work in the past, but with all the shifting and moulding, their pieces stopped fitting at some point. Hermione understood this. But Ron? Not so much. "That doesn't make me a solution. I wasn't the answer the first time, and I won't be this time either. I'm not sure what else I can do to make him understand." "I understand." Percy swirled the rest of the wine in his glass before taking another sip. "But you don't know what you're looking for or what you want." "I'll know it when I see it." She went to check on the bread, but it wasn't ready. "We're a lot alike, Hermione. And speaking as someone who's trying to both sort logic from conjecture and make wise decisions about a person I barely know, I can safely say you won't." Hermione stopped and listened. "First and foremost, you'll need time for your emotions to catch up with your rationale. You never make any moves without laying out the entire plan, along with a contingency plan should the original fail. A classic overthinker." True. Quite true, yet Hermione couldn't help but argue. "Okay, but so are you. Surely you haven't completed your Pansy-related calculations, risk versus reward, and arranged your backup plans after one meeting." "Actually, I have." Hermione MoaM Source: Jaxxinthebox April 11, 2011 Hermione was an early riser. She relished the quiet hours when the world was dark, hushed and peaceful. Mornings gave her time to think, plan, and prepare herself for each day. But that morning was unique. It was her first official day as Narcissa Malfoy's Healer, and she gave herself extra time to give the beginning of her new journey the respect it deserved. It wasn't going to be easyÑterminal cases like Narcissa's never wereÑeven without their clash of worlds. But as Hermione scanned through printed articles, she readied herself for a long trip, mentally packing every ounce of patience and stamina she would need along the way as she took her first steps down the road. One that, no matter how long she delayed the inevitable, would ultimately lead to failure, but Hermione tucked the thought away. It wouldn't do if she started the journey already thinking about the outcome. And what did she know? Maybe her detailed records on Narcissa would one day help the researcher who would eventually find the cure for her dementia. From that perspective, Hermione couldn't see any of her upcoming work as a failure. Besides, not all failures were bad. They sometimes empowered a person in a way that successes couldn't. Her own had taught Hermione more about humility, strength, and perseverance than any of her triumphs. In the quiet hours before dawn, Hermione looked out the window of her office at the sky and privately wondered what lessons she would learn during this oneÉ She was a lifelong student and prided herself on always learning new things and challenging her own ideologies. She was bound to learn something, even if it was that she would never understand pureblood culture or Narcissa Malfoy. Well, Hermione thought with a chuckle as she made her way to the kitchen, at least she could work on her list of retorts for Narcissa's brazenly stated opinions. Always best to be prepared. Normally, she spent her mornings tending to her conservatory plants before moving outdoors after sunrise, or she would read for pleasure. But that morning, inspired by Narcissa's preferred foods of choice, and the fact that she would be observing her for the entire day for any adverse reactions to the potions, Hermione carefully packed utensils, cookware, and enough food to make three meals. Confident she had everything organised in her bag, she stepped into her Floo. Her destination? The Malfoy residence. It was nearly six by the time she stepped out of the fireplace. Ceiling lights automatically turned on as the wards admitted her with a tingling, magical warmth. In truth, she hadn't paid much attention to the wardsÑor even her surroundings. But now she had time to look past furniture placement. Hermione didn't venture beyond the invisible boundaries of the open living room and kitchen, but she did take curious steps in the opposite direction. Towards the floor to ceiling glass wall Ñwith grey curtains pulled back on each sideÑthat led to their garden. The sky was beginning to stir. Pretty soon, warm pinks and oranges would trickle across the horizon as the sun began its ascent. It would be beautiful to witness outside the Malfoys' home where, beyond the white outdoor furniture just past the window, sat a blank grass canvas. She noted the telescope towards the back with a small nod of interest. A decoration, perhaps? Beaded bag in hand, Hermione slowly circumvented the pristine living room. She took note of the marble staircase by the wall and the long hall that extended past the kitchen, magic turning on each light one by one down the hall, inviting her to explore. She didn't accept. Instead, Hermione frowned at her surroundings. Truth be told, she wasn't surveying their home out of curiosity or to be nosyÑwell, not completely. A patient's home told her a lot about them: things she couldn't discern through any other means. There were always little odds and ends lying around that she could use to determine their motivation, nature, and character. It made her job easier in the long run. And while the Malfoys' unexpectedly modern home drew forth a lot of questions, Hermione couldn't get past one little thing that tickled at the back of her skullÉ The house was clinical, impersonalÑnot at all like a home. Like a museum exhibit, there were no accents or decorations. No bookshelves spoke to the subjects they were interested in. No tapestries with faces. No portraits, not even ones that would curse her blood. In fact, there were no pictures of any members of their family, only abstract paintings that could be found anywhereÑartistic and expensive, but ultimately lacking personality and warmth. It was frustrating. And the feeling followed her into the kitchen where she observed blank countertops. Their home told Hermione nothing about who the Malfoys were as a family. Or individual people. Well, that wasn't entirely true. She knew one thing: they were intensely private. That much was obvious. Hermione placed her bag on the kitchen island, staring thoughtfully into the immaculate, nondescript living room. Their house had also told her another thing: The one who approved the design wanted nothing to do with who they had been before the war. While her current surroundings screamed superiority and wealth, there was nothing that told of the rich family history they had once been so proud of. One that was still burning thirteen years later under a warded dome of magic. It was only then that Hermione realised there was also nothing in the house that so much as hinted at the existence of a child. Scorpius. Harry had three kids, but even when James was the only one, signs of him had been everywhere. There were no drawings on the fridge. No toys left strewn about. No smudges or stains that magic couldn't clean. Having met Scorpius, albeit briefly, it made sense. Hermione put the thought to the side. Solving all the Malfoy family's mysteries in one morning was beyond unrealistic. Family was always a tricky aspect of caring for a terminally ill patient; she still had time before she needed them on board. After looking around for appropriate cookwareÑand finding none for her intended purpose ÑHermione was grateful she had brought her own. She put the perishables in the refrigerator and set to work. Breakfast was simple: porridge and a fresh fruit salad with pecans, chia, an assortment of berries, cut apples, with freshly squeezed orange juice. Something hardy for her first dose of potions. She put a kettle on for tea, pulled out Narcissa's morning potions, and had just finished when she heard a throat clear from the open entrance of the kitchen. Hermione lifted her head to find Malfoy, who seemed confused by her presence. Well, that made two of them. Hermione was just as baffled by the sight of him. Freshly-shaven, she guessed Malfoy had done it as an act of appeasement to his mother. But never mind that, something else distracted her. He was wearing glasses. He hadn't been wearing them before. She would have remembered. "Malfoy," Hermione greeted. He carried the Prophet and a crossword puzzle book in one hand and a pen in the other; his outward appearance sent a clear message: Back the fuck off. Typical. Malfoy wore black like it was invented for him, as if it defined him. Though a contrast to his mother, who wore colour all the time, his choice wasn't shocking. Black was a symbol of power, elegance, and perceived superiority. Harry had mentioned he rarely wore the navy robes that denoted his position and rankÑrefused to, even. No one argued with him either. Hermione amused herself with the mental image of his closet having rows upon rows of black suits, robes, dress shirts, and ties. All black everything. Or maybe Malfoy had seven outfits, one marked for each day of the weekÑand on the floor there were seven pairs of black dress shoes organised in a row. A rigid attire for an equally rigid man. "Granger." Malfoy greeted her with a nod that was every bit as cool as his eyes were piercing. Perhaps she had hallucinated it, but Hermione swore she saw his eyes cut down, then back to hers. "You lookÉ normal today." Malfoy's comment set her on edge. She considered her jeans and black fitted shirt. There it was again. That surge. That sharp swell of energy that made her shaky, much like she always felt before a fight. She found herself unable to hold back her reaction to a statement she would have otherwise ignored. "What's that supposed to mean?" "Your ankle is better, I presume." Malfoy placed the paper, book, and pen on the countertop. "My ankle wasn't hurt." Hermione had all but forgotten about her twisted ankle, having healed it after leaving. Malfoy said nothing, only approached with a walk that managed to be business-like and efficient, but casual. His energy was distinctive and piquant, but difficult to explain, and impossible to ignore. In a low voice, just as he passed, Malfoy said, "You're a terrible liar, Granger." Hermione's hand flexed, hesitating overÉ whatever she had been reaching for. Flustered by her own jitters, she tracked Malfoy's movements with her eyes, observing as he opened an upper cabinet. He reached, but stopped mid-way, turning to her, eyes sharp and probing. "Can I help you?" Hermione averted her gaze, but kept her ears open as he went through a sequence of actions that seemed routine. And she resumed hers, pulling her wand out and spelling the island clean of the evidence of her meal preparations, then setting a stasis charm around the food. More footsteps on the wood. Not walking back in her direction, but taking a different path around the island. Malfoy placed a note next to a small bowl and spoon. The refrigerator opening jarred her, too focused on Malfoy's movements, then a container of milk floated to the table and landed in front of the bowl, next to the box ofÉ Frosties? "I can hear you thinking over there, Granger. I was merely making an observation." "Your observation sounds a lot like judgement, which you have absolutely noÑ" "Observation, Malfoy, is a neutral act of taking in information. Judgement involves formulating an opinion regarding both the value and merit of what's being observed. If you're going to use the two words, know what they mean and know that they aren't interchangeable." The hard look he gave made Hermione straighten her spine and meet his stare just as boldly. "Bullshit, Granger." "No, it isn't. ItÑ" Malfoy scoffed. "You don't know how to separate observation from assumption and judgement. You observe something and immediately formulate an interpretation, and from that interpretation you make a decision. An assumption, really." "That's not true." "Case in point: my son's cereal choices. You observe and assume that because we're purebloods we don't know anything about the Muggle world, much less allow him to have anything from it. Which validates my previous statement." Hermione attempted to make a counterpoint, but hesitated becauseÑ Well, he had a point. The look on his face transformed; he recognised his victory, but she wasn't finished. "Fine, then help me understand. I'll be treating your mother, and whether or not you know it, treating her is more than giving her potions to ease the symptoms of her disease. It's about understanding her drive to seek care in the first place and making sure I can keep her motivated to stay the course, even when things become more difficult down the line. Family has always been a motivator to her. It's why she sought my help in the first place and it will make my job easier when I know what I'm working with." Malfoy's head tilted slightly to the side as he observed her with what looked like bewilderment. Hermione had no idea. She had no real baseline that she could use to decipher him. Except Hogwarts, and that was as skewed a reference point as any because he wasn't that child anymore. "My mother is your only patient. You would do well to remember that." Before he left, he pointed at the bowl of porridge under the stasis charm. "However, in the interest of helping," the last word came out in a sneer that took her back to Second Year, "I'd like to wish you luck, Granger. You'll need it to get my mother to willingly eat that bowl of slop." As she watched him go, Hermione considered testing her luck to see if she could make a direct hit when she threw it at his head. Draco - Newspaper Source: Jaxxinthebox Unfortunately, Malfoy was right. Narcissa stared at the bowl of porridge like it had personally offended her. Or like it would spontaneously come to life. Or like she was trying to identify it without having to outright ask. Hermione wasn't certain which, as her face seemed to go through a wide range of emotions before settling into a look of extreme suspicion. She heaved a patient sigh, then glanced at her watch. Almost half an hour had passed since Narcissa had floated into the kitchen dressed in long, flowing periwinkle robes and the simple necklace with the plain gold band. She must have already dressed for hosting tea with acquaintances at noon (before her afternoon potions)Ñan hour Hermione would make herself scarce. Narcissa's symptoms weren't obvious that morning, but they were present. There was a light sheen of sweat on her forehead that she'd tried to dab away, despite the hand tremors. She voiced her deep frustration at having forgotten where she'd placed her favourite broach, forgot an answer to one of Hermione's questions, and was agitated from a poor night's rest. Hermione hoped the complete regime of potions would help, but it would be days or even a week before she would be able to definitively determine that. Still, Hermione placed several charms on one of her bracelets to turn it into a tracker that would continually monitor her vitals. The results would appear in real time on charmed paper that Hermione would check regularly. She also quietly set a deterrent charm, just in case Narcissa decided one day that she wanted to remove it altogether. That could not happen. Hermione's second cup of tea was nearly emptyÑher food long since finished, and dishes washed by hand and put away. She found herself pretending not to watch Narcissa Malfoy. Hermione skimmed the calorie content from the box of cereal that was still where Malfoy had left it. The sugar content was obscene. Narcissa delicately poked her porridge with her spoon, and Hermione tried to ease her feelings, much like one would a petulant child. "It's quite good, if you give yourself a chance." The suspicious look only deepened. "I do not eat breakfast in general." "Which is why I made porridge. It's a start. You'll have to eat, as the morning potions will make you nauseous if you don't." Besides, Hermione had watched as Narcissa tried to cover the tremor that had passed not long ago by attempting to start a conversation about changing the drapes by the window. The last of her old potions was out of her system and, while it was informative to see her baseline, Hermione wasn't interested in watching her struggle to hold a spoon any longer than necessary. "Perhaps if I drink teaÑ" "It's not enough." Hermione folded her hands in a show of her thinning patience. "Perhaps if we determine what you would eat for breakfast, maybeÑ" She poked the porridge again with her spoon. "There is a very long list of things I would rather eat than this." "You haven't actually tried it." Hermione wondered if she should have tried a more traditional breakfast for their first day. "It looks most unappetising. The fruit salad at least looks palatable, but I have no taste for it at the moment. Not when it is room temperature." "I have it on good account that you like pie, which is essentially warm fruit." Hermione frowned. "Perhaps, I could make you a smoothie." If possible, the look on her face soured even more. "That sounds even more unappealing." "How could you possibly know?" "I know because I have extremely delicate sensibilities, Miss Granger." She was about to retort when she heard footsteps approaching on the wood floors. Much too quick to be Malfoy's. Or any adult's. Which meantÉ Hermione turned just in time to catch sight of the youngest Malfoy's approach. "Remember your manners, Scorpius." Narcissa didn't even look up from the porridge she had finally scooped onto her spoon. He halted in the entryway, but scanned the room, obviously not finding what he had all but ran into the room in search of. His shoulders fell in disappointmentÉ until he spotted Hermione, who was unabashedly staring at the little boy, struck by just how much he looked like his father. Except for the small differences. Malfoy had always been just a bit taller than Harry, but Scorpius was slightly shorter than Albus. He was a slight boy with pale skin. More blond than white, his hair was parted and neatly combed. He was dressed like a schoolboy in black shorts, white shirt, a dark blazer, socks rolled up, and leather shoes. It was likely his everyday attire, judging from the way he didn't pick and pull at it. Or perhaps it wasn't in his nature to do such a childish thing. "You may approach." Narcissa's formality seemed bizarre; she was speaking to her grandchildÑnot a stranger. She'd heard so many stories about how much she doted on Malfoy, and she had seen the evidence of it with all the sweets he'd received during school. So, it struck Hermione as odd to see Narcissa being so stern with Scorpius. She considered laughing at the sheer ridiculousness of the entire situation, but was unable to do so because it wasn't a joke. It was real life. And it called into focus Daphne's words from a few days ago. Especially when Scorpius did as instructed, obediently advancing with careful steps. His chin was slightly raised, back straight, and shoulders held in a posture so perfect it looked robotic. Trained, was the first word that came to mind. Hermione turned back to tea, taking the last sip to rid herself of the peculiar feeling associated with the word. Scorpius stopped next to Narcissa's chair, waiting to be addressed. Hermione placed her teacup on the saucer with a small clink. "How did you sleep?" She asked her grandson. Only then did Hermione remember that he didn't speak. At that, she turned and locked eyes with the bluest pair she'd ever seen on a person. Piercing and bright, they weren't his father's eyes, but she caught the stubbornness and shrewd intelligence in them that he'd inherited from the man himself. As well as that childlike curiosity she remembered from Theo's office. When she smiled politely in greeting, his ears turned red and he briefly looked down. Hermione's grin transformed instantly for one reason only: Scorpius was adorable. Not nearly as pointy as his father, he was softer. Not as cold. In fact, the more she looked at him, the more she could tell that there was something warm about him, despite his grandmother's treatment. "You mustn't stare, Scorpius," Narcissa admonished. "It is impolite." The little boy looked down once again in a bashful apology. Then he must have remembered himself because he stood straightÑlike he had been taught to doÑaverting his eyes. But it only lasted a moment because Hermione turned in her chair towards him, leaning in a bit as she offered her hand to shake. "I'm Hermione." Scorpius looked at her hand, but like with Theo, he made no move to accept it, simply lifting his eyes back to hers. And though it was a rejection, it didn't feel like one because Scorpius kept staring at her in the way small kids often did. Curious. He wanted to learn. She was something new and different. A quick glance at his grandmother made him remember his manners, which was why he bowed politely. Hermione's eyes followed him all the way to the chair across the table from hers. Her smile waned, transforming into something more thoughtful as she watched him take his seat after removing his blazer and patting his pockets. The chair was charmed to automatically bring him up to the appropriate height to sit comfortably. A bit of magic Hermione hadn't seen before, but Scorpius never reacted. Apparently, this was normal. His routine. It was odd to watch a child methodically settle in for breakfast. Scorpius knew where everything should be; he didn't even look when he first reached for the scrap of paper she had seen Malfoy leave. The note, Hermione corrected herself. Draco Malfoy left a note for his son. Not out of the ordinary, but the way Daphne spoke of his distance didn't make sense when coupled with the note. Hermione pondered it further as she watched him unfold the note, and scan the words with deep concentration. As if heÑ "Scorpius can read a little, but I doubt he can read Draco's handwriting. It is incredibly poor." Almost on cue, his head tilted to the side. Hermione disguised her laughter as a cough that fooled no one. Narcissa frowned and Scorpius' unamused expression was eerily reminiscent of his father. "He is quite shy, Miss Granger." Narcissa poured milk into his cereal bowl. Briefly, she left and returned with a glass of juice, placing it on his right. Scorpius looked confused by the drink. "Do not be offended if he does not speak to you. I make sure to speak to him normally, even though I know I will not receive a response." "Oh." "Make no mistake, he can speak, Miss Granger. He just stubbornly chooses not to." Scorpius looked directly at Hermione, as if confirming his grandmother's statement with a blank expression. Then, he picked up his spoon with his left handÑyet another thing he had inherited from his fatherÑand tucked into his sugary cereal with manners highly unusual for a boy his age, or any age for that matter. Narcissa delicately tasted her porridge. She didn't use her napkin to spit it out, but it was a close call. Upon her first couple of chews, her face morphed from sceptical to cautiously impressed. "It is not as horrific as it looks." "Good." Meanwhile, Hermione's mind was short-circuiting from the large quantities of rapid-fire processing it performed in the seconds after her statement. Not about her finding the porridge palatable, but beforeÉ about the silent boy politely eating his cereal while staring at the note next to his bowl. He looked like a miniature Draco Malfoy reading the paper. Just without glasses, the cold disposition, or the attitude. "Scorpius," Narcissa gently chastised. "Do not slouch." She ate her porridge slower when he started watching her, emulating her. Hermione cleared her throat. "You should finish soon. I'm trying to keep your potions on a schedule and you're running a little close to the end of it." Which was enough to distract Narcissa from further conversation. Spoon halfway to his mouth, Scorpius regarded her for a moment with an expression she couldn't read before he went back to his breakfast. The meal progressed quickly from there. Pockets of conversation with Narcissa interrupted her continued observation of the silent Scorpius. Once Narcissa finished, Hermione watched as she proceeded to take her first set of potions, chasing each with tea. They were quite disgusting. After running a series of diagnostic charms that left the youngest Malfoy in wide-eyed wonder, Hermione checked the results on the charmed parchment then went about clearing the table. "Do not trouble yourself, Miss Granger, the nanny will be in shortly to sort it. She usually fills in on weekends and when Zippy is on holiday." "It's no trouble at all." Hermione picked up her empty teacup and Narcissa's bowl and walked them to the sink. Scorpius appeared, politely handing Hermione his empty bowl, cup, and spoon. He was helping. "Thank you, Scorpius." Hermione smiled graciously. Narcissa called him and he returned to her side. Making quick work of cleaning his bowl and drying everything with a wave of her wand, she levitated each piece back in its place while pretending not to listen. "You did very well yesterday. Let us make it another day with no incidents." There was a chill in her tone that settled on Hermione's spine. Scorpius responded with that strange bow. "You have two minutes and your tutor is waiting for you upstairs in the library." Hermione made certain Scorpius was long gone before she moved from her spot at the sink. "How old is he?" She already knew, but the question was asked to make conversation. "Five." "HeÉ is extremely well-behaved." That was a massive understatement; he seemed more like a miniature adult than a child. Except for moments when she saw hints of the childÉ "We pay excellent money for tutors to make sure of it." The detachment in her voice made Hermione anxious to change the subject. "The schedule you provided states that you are hosting tea. Is there a place where I can make myself scarce during that time? I need to do some reading anyway." "As you plan to monitor me throughout the day for the next thirty days, you may use Draco's office to work. It is just down the hall." She pointed in the direction her grandson had just disappeared, which led Hermione to believe there was more than one staircase in their home. The only one she knew of was just off the living room. "It should be to your liking." Hermione nodded absently, noticing the small changes in Narcissa's physical symptoms since she'd taken her morning potions. Even her eyes looked brighter. "How do you feel? The combination is designed to give you a little energy boost." "Then they are working as planned. I actually feelÉ" Narcissa sounded as though she couldn't believe her next words. "Quite good." It was either evidence of success or a Placebo Effect. Only time would tell. Scorpius Malfoy Source: Jaxxinthebox If Hermione had to predict how Malfoy's office would look, she would have only earned half marks. She would have expected it to resemble the rest of the home: modern with neutral tones, elaborately furnished, and zero personal touches. Instead, Malfoy's office reminded her of her own. It was about the same size, too: cosy, and not too large. There were no plants, but there was a large, faded Turkish rug that looked more like a tapestry that belonged on the wall than the floor. It was terribly unsightly. His desk was made of dark wood and held a healthy amount of clutter. In front of the fireplace a black leather sofa sat next to a small glass table. Above the fireplace something caught her eye. A recent family portrait hung in an ornate frame. Narcissa, Draco, and ScorpiusÑall very stern and all dressed in black. There were two smaller portraits next to it. One of Draco standing alone, frowning at her presence in his office. The next showed Narcissa and Scorpius, likely taken the same day as the larger one. Portrait Narcissa had her hands on his shoulders and the boy looked like he wanted to be anywhere else. Odder still, none of them moved. Every painted subject stood perfectly still, suspended forever in a slice of time. It was sad. Hermione took in the wall lined with floor to ceiling bookcases, all organised and packed to the brim. But that wasn't all that piqued her curiosity. From the doorway, at a glance, his office looked like it belonged to the conservative man society thought he should be. Books that spoke to his intelligence, family portraits that spoke to his dedication to his duty to uphold his family's name, and a space that was just lavish enough to remind a visitor of his wealth. But when she took a deeper look, she spotted the inconsistencies only noticeable to the most observant. The books themselves were a study in contrasts: a dichotomy between what society knew of Draco Malfoy, how he presented himself to the world, and the identity of the man whoÑ judging from the clutterÑspent a lot of time in this room. They weren't all on magical subjects by magical authors. No Dark Arts texts or any manuscripts by any extremists. Instead, there were books on Chemistry and Geology, Botany and Physics, Philosophy and Art History. Buddhism. She spotted Blanchot, Derrida, Tolstoy, Nietzsche. Classical fiction. Autobiographies. Poetry. And that was just what she could see. There was so much more to explore if she climbed the black ladder and scanned the higher shelves, but Hermione didn't. She left it alone with one question to the empty office: "Who is Draco Malfoy?" Nothing made sense. Not the home and certainly not the people living within its walls. None of it fit in the neat box she'd created for them. For someone like Hermione, who liked things that made sense because they brought order to chaos, it was wreaking havoc on her worldview. It would have been easy to brand them as a pureblood clichŽ, reduce them all to the vision of what they ought to be, but how could she possibly do that now? Malfoy's earlier words were still ringing in her ears. She had to take a hard look at her own flawed logic and question it. And, by extension, question herself. There wasn't a metric to measure people, and yet, for some reason, that rule hadn't been applied to the Malfoys. MaybeÑokay, most definitelyÑit was her bias. That stubborn, know-it-all part of her that just knew she had them all figured out and was right about the sort of people they were. The part of her thatÑ Hermione sighed. The part of her that judged. There. She said it. Hermione could admit she'd made assessments that hadn't been correct. Her character flaw. Would it change now that she was standing in the most confusing room of the house thus far? No. But what she could do was something she should have done the moment she'd accepted Narcissa as a patient. Start over. No, truly start at the beginning with them, as she had with every other patient. She needed to scrub her mind of everything she knew about the Malfoy family and start with a clean slate. Hermione could admit to herself that she was well outside of her depths when it came to them. She had no idea who they were and she knew she needed to go about learning them in a better, more productive way. Only then would she learn without biasÑwithout harming or drawing any inference from one belief about them to another. No matter what Malfoy thought, Hermione really was treating the entire family. Malfoy especially. Narcissa's only child. The one who would take her loss the hardest. So, Hermione packed away the assumptions, excavated her unconscious attitude, and stepped back from all she'd known, leaving her withÉ nothing. She took a deep breath. Crossing the room to Malfoy's desk, with her bag in hand, she was ready to dig into research. But when she sat at his cluttered desk, she sawÉ well, Draco Malfoy. Not just as an aggravating man, her old classmate and adversary, and Narcissa's only sonÉ No, Hermione saw him as a father. There, in a neat and simple frameÑturned away from the room, for his eyes onlyÑwas a moving picture of him holding a newborn Scorpius. His look of confused wonder slowly transformed to a soft smile. The photo was so intimate, so warm, so unlike who she knew him to be. Hermione turned her head only to catch another photo. It had been taken recently. Both father and son were dressed in black bespoke suits, not smiling or touching or anything. Cold. Hermione stood and claimed a spot on his sofa. After rifling around in her bag for her research, she immersed herself in her task. She needed to clear all the excess from her mind and focus on something that required her full attention. Eventually, though, Hermione needed more space, so she spread her work out on the glass coffee table, making notes on the printed paper in pen and highlighting important bits that warranted further research. In fact, she was so wrapped up in examining everythingÑflipping through article after article, comparing them to the books she'd brought alongÑthat all of it nearly went airborne when the Floo flared to life and Malfoy walked out as if he were stepping out of hell and back onto Earth. MalfoyÑno longer in glassesÑwas so surprised by her presence that it stopped him in his tracks.
