His mouth opened then closed once. Then his eyes darkened. "What are you doing in my office, Granger?" Hermione almost apologised, but she stopped herself. Apologise for what? "Your mother said I could work here while she hosted tea. She said you would be late, as always." "Did she now?" A statement; not a question. "I left a roll of parchment hereÉ for work." That unique discomfort returned, creeping along her spine as she started gathering her papers together in a rushed pile, mixing things she'd wanted to keep separate. It didn't matter. "I can leave." A fleeting thought was given to the photos on his desk and really, Hermione felt guilty for invading what was obviously his private space. "I didn't mean to intrude." "That won't be necessary." His words halted her move to depart. "I'll have a conversation with my mother later." "She didn't seem to think you'd mind." "That's the problem right there, Granger. She didn't think." Finally, he moved from his spot in front of the fireplace, crossing the room to his desk. She watched, waiting in silent tension as he picked up a roll of parchment off his desk. Without turning around, he asked, in a tone so deadly serious that she continued gathering her papers because fuck this, "Did you sit at my desk?" Exactly how he knew, Hermione had no idea. She'd been careful to not touch anything. For a breath, she considered lying, but decided it was pointless to lie to someone who already knew the answer before asking the question. "I did, but only for a second. Your desk didn't provide the space I needed, so I moved to the sofa." Malfoy turned his head to her, eyes narrowed with suspicion. Contempt? "Did my office provide you with everything you needed to make your judgement of me?" "My observation, Malfoy, is simple. I don't know the person you've become in the last thirteen years, that much is true, but the same goes for you." She took a step towards him. "Perhaps I'm not the only one with preconceived notions." "Ah, so you admit it." "Something about me that you don't know, Malfoy? I'm not scared to admit when I'm wrong." Hermione shrugged and when his expression slowly transformed to something akin to tightly focused bewilderment, she took one more step forward. Then another. "I've never claimed to be perfect, nor do I expect myself or anyone else to hold themselves to such an impossible standard." "That doesn't quite fit with your reputation as the Brightest Witch of our Age." "My reputation may be that, but it's not exactly who I am. That's who others think I amÑ" Just like you aren't exactly your reputation, Hermione almost said. But she backtracked. "I get angry and can be vindictive. I can be self-righteous and hypercritical. I have little shame in who I am, because that person is ever-evolving. Right now, I am challenging myself, not just as it pertains to your mother, but also as it pertains to you." She paused when Malfoy's brows drew together. "However, just as I have made assumptions about your family, you have made them about meÑ" "And yet, I feel there's more that you want to add." Malfoy folded his arm, leaning back on his cluttered desk. His mask feigned ease, but his clenched fist spoke of agitation. "No addition. Only perhaps." Hermione held up a finger and offered a metaphorical olive branch. "As in, perhaps, in time, I hope we might have a better, judgement-free understanding of one another." Malfoy's cold glare and snide question was quintessentially him: "And why would we do that, Granger?" "Because we're adults, not quarrelling children. Because of your mother, I'll be a part of your lifeÑin some respectsÑfor the rest of hers. You might not like it, but it'sÑ" "Fine." He stared at her long and hard for several moments before he pushed off the desk. Two steps later and he was directly in front of her, speaking in a voice that kept her single-minded focus on him. "Speaking of the future, for future reference, my office is off limits. To everyone." Malfoy left the same way he came in. Hermione stood in her living room after a long first day for a solid thirty minutes just blinking at nothing before she waved the proverbial white flag and called an emergency gathering. On a Monday. By the time Hermione arrived at the not so crowded pub in Hackney, no less than an hour after she'd sent off the request via Patronus, Parvati and Pansy were already there, sitting at a quieter table near the back. The former had the most obnoxious fruity pink drink waiting while the latter had four shots sitting in front of her. Hermione eyed them sceptically before taking the seat between them. "I Apparated here, are you both trying to make me Splinch myself going home?" She examined one of the shot glasses full of clear liquid. "Is thisÑ" "Russian vodka? Yes. Two for you and two for me." Pansy grinned. "Have you eaten? I ordered Beef Wellington for you. Should be around soon." "I haven't eaten since lunch." Which had included meeting Scorpius' young nanny, who'd delivered him for lunch with his grandmother, but hadn't stayed. Narcissa hadn't returned from tea at that time so Hermione had spent ten long minutes locked in a staring match with a five-year-old. He'd won. She hadn't expected his presence for the meal, but he ate the chicken salad she'd given him without fussÑwell, the lettuce, tomatoes, and cheese. Scorpius carefully picked around the chicken. The fact that the opposite hadn't happened was strange, but it wasn't her place to give it much thought. Hermione left that bit to Narcissa who kept prodding him to eat the chicken when she arrived for lunch. But he never did. "In fairness to us," Parvati said. "We didn't know what state you'd be in when you arrived. The last time you called an emergency outing on a Monday was four years ago when Krum visited and you had that one-off." Hermione whipped her head around so fast she vaguely heard Pansy's "What the fuck?" when the ends of her ponytail hit her in the face. "First of all, I didn't shag him." She'd gotten cold feet. Viktor had been nice about it. After all, they had been essentially pen pals for years. It didn't seem like a good idea. "Second, how did you even know anything happened?" Parvati gave her a sly look. "I put two and twelve together." Hermione kept glaring and folded her arms. "I'm a journalist. It's what I do. You went to dinner with him and after that, every time someone mentioned him, you deflected." Parvati leaned close and whispered, "Was it terrible?" "Pansy, tell herÑ" Hermione found the other witch waiting eagerly for an answer as well. "Nevermind. Who else is on their way?" "Weasley will be late. She's delivering the kids to the Burrow because Potter's working late and Luna's coming. Daphne is working, Padma and Susan are still at the hospital." Suddenly, Parvati remembered something. "Pansy, weren't you supposed to invite Cho?" "Oops, I forgot." Pansy covered her fake gasp. "What a shame. Such a pity." Hermione snorted while Parvati rolled her eyes before sweeping her long wavy curls over her shoulders. "So why did you call the emergency dinner?" "Today was my first day with Narcissa Malfoy." Pansy perked up. Parvati cringed. "You should start off with the Russian vodka." "How did it go?" Pansy asked. "As well as it could. There's the part where Malfoy isÑ" "Oh fuck, you've seen him?" Parvati squealed and clapped her hands as she bounced in her seat, garnering attention from the man at the bar who eyed her appreciatively. "He's fit as fuck, yeah?" Parvati nudged her in the shoulder. "I need all the details." It took every shred of effort for Pansy to keep her opinion to herself, but she remained quiet. "All the details fall under the Patient-Healer Confidentiality Agreement andÑ" "Not about your job! About him! Was he not as climbable as I said?" "That's quite literally not the point, Parvati." Pansy nudged one of the shot glasses in her direction and Hermione drank it in one go, bearing the burn with a tight grimace. She'd never been one for hard liquor, but today it was warranted. "Malfoy was a bit intense." An understatement. There were less intense natural disasters. Parvati sipped on the fruity monstrosity, paying close attention, while Hermione rubbed the side of her neck. "I don't remember him being like that. A tosser, yes, but he'sÑ" "You weren't the only one changed by life and circumstances, Granger. I think it's safe to say Draco has a lot to contend with, in addition to the heavy weight of responsibilities and expectations being piled on him." "I understand, butÑ" "We all cope in different ways. Draco's is to bear it in silence, completely alone." "That's not coping, that's avoiding." "That's Draco." There was a sigh to her right that sounded like an infatuated schoolgirl. "Uneasy lies the head that wears the crownÉ or whatever Padma says whenever she's feeling maudlin." Parvati snapped her fingers. "Damn, I should have used that line for his feature!" Hermione rolled her eyes as Pansy tossed back the second shot. If she felt the burn, she didn't show it. "Nice quote, but Draco is no king," Pansy said. No, he was just one man. The greatest wisdom is seeing through appearances. Atisa Chapter End Notes A/N: Draco and Hermione in this story will clash and reassess, they will make progress and have setbacks, they will make unpopular decisions that a reader might not make themselves because we don't all think the same way, and it's a matter of perspective. Hermione has a different perspective. I drew her characterization from who she might become based on book canon (not so much movie canon as she has no Mudblood scar) and the circumstances that occur prior to this story's start. Trauma changes people and it takes different forms. It might make someone reassess their life choices but also may make someone brazen more of an overthinker to avoid repeat errors. Anyway, I loved writing Draco here and being the first person to truly make Hermione go, "well shit. I'm wrong." Now is it going to be a 180 turnaround immediately? No, because people don't do that. Change is a slow process these two flawed characters will both undergo as this story unfolds. Embedded art by art by Jaxx-in-a-box [ Instagram / Tumblr / Ao3 ] Art for this chapter: Draco reading the paper by dragonlyart & & Draco Portrait by kikorenart & Hermione in the Greenhouse by animusdiscidium Chapter 7: The Art of Compromise Seven The Art of Compromise May 11, 2011 Time passed like it always did: slow and surreal, yet in a rush. A complete contradiction. A constant presence that was never staticÉ Before Hermione knew it, four weeks had passed since she'd begun caring for Narcissa. Yet, she found herself right back where everything had started. Sitting in Theo's office. It was her first status meeting. This was not something Hermione did with every patient, but this assignment warranted the appointment that had appeared in her Magi-Scheduler that morning. They sat on the sofa instead of at his desk, and sipped lemon ginger green teaÑhis first cup, her third. His eyebrow quirked, but he wisely remained quiet. The silence was foreboding; the proverbial calm before the storm. Hermione was ready to drown them both in the sheer volume of words she needed to say. The remaining twenty-six minutes wouldn't be enough. Theo must have felt her glare, must have heard her mental diatribe, because he was about to take another sip when he sighed like Hermione's energy alone had disrupted his peace. He placed his teacup on the glass table, which made Hermione gulp hers down, ignoring the burning combination of hot water and ginger. Her agitation seared hotter. But she tried to hide it. Her mood was a disagreeable companion that had followed her for weeks, a shadow that grew longer and more distorted as the days went on. "How have the first thirty days of your latest assignment been?" The question was followed by an almost wincing pause. "Feel free to be honest." "I quit." Theo had no reaction. "You don't mean that." "I don't, but I feel better now that I've said it out loud instead of in my head." Three hundred and nineteen timesÉ for a myriad of reasons. Theo's expression was calm and neutral, reminding Hermione of how she interacted with a patient to earn their trust. "Talk to me, Hermione." He had the gall to look handsome in his sincerity. It made her scoff. Loudly. "You sound like my therapist." Theo casually leaned back on the sofa, legs crossed, making himself comfortable. Hermione hated both the attractive sight he made in burgundy trousers and the continued sentiment of his next words. "I was hoping to sound like a friend." "A friend wouldn't have sent me into battle with the wrong weapon. You gave me a butter knife when I needed a blowtorch. And an army." For all his posturing, he didn't look the least bit apologetic. He looked interested. Far more than usual. "I've not seen you this rattled beforeÉ Intriguing." "Are you serious? This isn't the time for one of your little tests." "I don't test you for my own amusement, it's mainly for your self-improvement." That made her argument fall limp before the finish line. "You're excellent at your job, but I've wanted to pull you from your comfort zone for quite some time now. I've also always wondered what an immovable force and an unstoppable object meeting would actually look like. I didn't think it would happen quite like it has. Apparently I'm overdue for a surprise." "Not the time, Theo." "I don't feel you entered into this with reasonable expectations for yourself." Lacing his fingers together, he spoke slowly, despite the metaphorical steam coming from her ears. "Did you think it would be so simple?" Hermione opened her mouth once, twice, but nothing came out, then a third for good measure before shutting it for lack of a complete answer. No? But also yes? Slightly? Okay, perhaps she'd taken an arrogant approach to the assignment. Recollection brought her past thoughts of keeping out the storm into sharper focus. Her high hopes of figuring out a balance with Narcissa, an alliance with Malfoy regarding her future safety plans and preparation, and the possible levelling of her symptoms. She was no closer to any of those goals now than she had been a month prior. Hermione needed a different plan. One that got her closer, but doing so risked her ability to remain disconnected and neutral. It would test her ability to not speak her peace about every aspect of Narcissa's life that didn't involve her direct care. The problems she'd seen. The growing flamesÉ The fire-warped pieces of Narcissa's life made Hermione uncomfortableÉ But it wasn't her job to fix them. It was Narcissa's job to use the time she was given wisely. Dramatic changes weren't completely unthinkable, yet a thirty-day objective with a family like the Malfoys meant her goals had been impossible at best from the start. And now that Hermione had time to think, she realised her frustration had less to do with the source of her discontent, and more to do with herself. And the fact that she hadn't made much progressÑon any front. The Malfoys were still a secretly broken family at the top echelon of a society that praised them for their unity during the most difficult times. Ironic. "I'll take your silence to mean that by now you've discovered the situation isÉ complicated. As is the family." "Complicated?" Hermione baulked. "They're painful. Malfoy isÉ I have no idea. I'm pretty certain I see him more than anyone." Theo's brow lifted in silent query, but Hermione only shrugged in response. Malfoy left before either his mother or Scorpius came down and was still gone when Hermione departed each evening. "Narcissa's results have also been strange from the start." "So I've seen from what you sent over." "You had time to look?" Theo didn't respond butÑoh right, his strange attachment to this case. Hermione itched to dissect that more. "She declines sharply in the evenings and overnights are awful. Her sleeping patterns have deteriorated. She's agitated and is beginning to have spells where she wanders to different parts of the house and doesn't remember how she got there. From my understanding, she hardly sleeps, just tosses and turns, which makes her irritable during the day. More than she already is. If she yells at Scorpius' nanny one more time, the poor girl will either cry or quitÉ maybe both." "She was temperamental at dinner with Pansy, Draco, and I last week." Theo winced. "Which was why I asked to see her results and your notes thus far." "Find anything worth mentioning? Anything I haven't already analysed to death?" "No, but I sent a copy over to Charles Smith in Boston. He says you two have been discussing her case for the last month or so. Is that correct?" Yes, they had. In addition to Narcissa's attitudeÑas well as her symptomsÑher episodes of blankness and tremors had not decreased like they should have under her new potions regime. Nothing had increased either, which was the only silver lining. And while there had been several incidents where Narcissa had forgotten her and everyone else, there had only been one incident of accidental magic, when she had Apparated across the room. To everyone's relief, no Splinching had occurred, but it made Hermione desperate to figure out the root cause of her issues. "Yes, we've been in contact," Hermione said. "Have you heard back from him?" "I've scheduled time to discuss it with him tomorrow, should you want to attendÉ" He gave her a look that bordered on amusement. "That is, if you haven't yet quit." Hermione cut her eyes at him. "I absolutely would like to be on the call. Regardless of how I feel, I intend to see this through. There have just beenÉ growing pains." To say the least. The potions should have worked. Narcissa's symptoms should have curbed, and she should have levelled off by now. The fact that they hadn't was a sign that Hermione was missing something important. And that simply would not do. "And Scorpius?" It was an odd question, but she supposed he would want to know about his godson. Narcissa brought him by to see Theo weekly for visits, but that was all she knew. "What about him?" "Merely a question. I don't get much time to see him." "He'sÉ" At the curious tilt of Theo's head, she had a moment of honesty. "I've never met a child like him." She doubted she ever would. Scorpius lived by a routine so unwaveringly strict Hermione could tell time by his entrances and exits. He was keenly observant, more so than even she'd realised. He watched and waited and listened to everything and everyone around him, hyper-focused and anxious in a way that made Hermione uncomfortable to even speak around him. There were many things she had observed, things she had yet to piece together, but the main difference between Scorpius and every child Hermione had known was simple: He was so detached that Narcissa's treatment of him didn't seem to register as anything except normal. So affection-starved that he preened at every moment of kindness, no matter how small or mundane. So lonely it was almost painful to watch him day in and day out. And the worst part was the sadness just under the surface of each of his actions. It was indescribable, chilling, and unmistakable, yet not acknowledgedÑby anyone. There must have been something open about her expression, honest even. It hadn't been intentional, but it made Theo heave a sigh. "You've seen it then." "What?" "His misery." The terribly penetrating power of his statement made Hermione suck in a breath, trying to fight the growing feelings of unease with what was undoubtedly her best weapon: pure logic. "He's not my patient." Theo's look deepened, probing until Hermione allowed her eyes to slide towards the door with the quiet hope that someone would interrupt. But no one did. "Per my own rules and conditions, the only way I can do this job effectively is if I remain detached, unaffected, and objective. I am trying hard to do just that." She turned accusatory eyes on him. "If you've seen it, then you do something about it." "I've tried. I'm trying." It was the most emotional she had heard him, which stunned her into perfect silence. "Narcissa thinks she is doing the right thing and Draco isÉ" Theo never finished. He didn't need to. Malfoy wasn't there. Knowing what she knew now, she wished she would have paid more attention during her chat with Daphne over pie. ButÑ She tilted her head, focusing on Theo as fragments of thoughts and ideas gathered. The more Hermione pondered over it, the more pieces fit. The assignment request. The personal nature of Theo's involvement. Yes, it had to do with Narcissa and Malfoy, but the key to unlocking the man before her was small and at the mercy of the adults in his life. "It's Scorpius, isn't it? The reason you're so invested in her care, I mean." His face cooled into his default visage of powerful omniscience, but Hermione knew she had read him correctly. Theo uncrossed his legs and picked up his still-steaming teacup, taking a long drink to finish it out. "He is my godson. Draco and I have known each other since childhood." "Known, you say, but you weren't friends. Or, at least, I don't remember you being close. Not like Crabbe and Goyle, or even Blaise." She could tell he wasn't being completely truthful. "Malfoy isÑ" "Not the same as he was." "I know." That much had been obvious since before he'd first said her name. While his mother had been the cause of Hermione's mounting frustration, and the reason for her late nights of books and research and transatlantic Floo calls, Draco Malfoy had become the source of her endless questions and curiosity. He was a confusing presence and Hermione couldn't determine which version of him was real and which one was for displayÑa facade he wore to remind her (and everyone else) who he was supposed to be. A massive prat. "You can't understand the son without understanding the father," Theo said. Hermione wasn't trying to do either beyond the scope of her job. The son wasÑwell, that was a slippery slope indeed. And the father was someone that didn't fit the realm of her current comprehension. "I'm trying to remain impartial, Theo. I only wish to understand him enough to secure his cooperation. That's all I need." "Tell me then, how are your attempts working for you?" Hermione wasn't certain how to answer that question. Malfoy hadn't spoken to her much after their first face-offs, which had been expected. But that didn't stop Hermione from greeting him each morning and trying to start a conversation. Initially, there had been sincere attempts to earn his cooperation, and figure out the reason behind the riftÑafter all, he was always there when she arrived. After days of little success, it shifted into speaking to him out of sheer stubbornness and a mounting curiosity about a man who completed puzzles with a pen, didn't seem to sleep much (given his long hours), yet still got up early enough to ensure he left a note for Scorpius each morning. Draco Malfoy was a man who went about his day so deliberately it seemed like he was purposefully avoiding his family. His problems. Like a cliff on the edge of the sea, he met each crashing wave of her attempts at gathering information with silence stronger than scrutinising looks and strange facial expressions she didn't know him well enough to identify. But that randomly changed eight days into her assignment. Malfoy hadn't been there when she'd arrived. Uncommon, but not too strange. The minutes had ticked on. Five. Ten. Fifteen. Twenty minutes had passed before he rushed in with no paper or crossword. His tie had been undone, hair barely dry. Hermione had been in the middle of making breakfast, but was able to gather that he'd lost track of time while swimming in the pool Hermione still hadn't seen. Uncharacteristically flustered and disorganised, he swore about how nothing was where it belonged, his scheduleÑand dayÑin ruins. Hermione's reaction had been instinct. She'd stopped and helped. Fixing his tie with a flick of her wand and packing breakfast and tea for him before sending him on his way. Malfoy had been halfway out the door when they'd both suddenly realised what the hell had just transpired. But then Malfoy surprised her with just two words before he left: Thank you. After that, Hermione couldn't say things were good, but he stopped ignoring her presence and started engaging, in his own frustrating way, by answering her questions with terse responses of his own. One sentence. Then two. Theo interrupted her reverie. "You didn't answer the question." "Hm?" Hermione had been so caught up analysing Malfoy's every move for the umpteenth time that she'd forgotten what Theo had said. "How are your attempts working for you?" "Oh well, I'm not certain how to answer it. I can't say that they're working at all, truth be told, but Malfoy's spoken to me a few times in the last couple of weeks." Somewhat. Sort of. Conversation had been stilted, firmly rooted in extremely mundane subjects that carried no risk. Malfoy never initiated these engagements, only responded, and Hermione found herself initially attempting to tailor her attempts at conversation to things he might enjoy. Quidditch had been her first attempt, but Malfoy had struck that topic down. "You don't care for Quidditch. Don't waste my time. Or yours." So, Hermione hadn't. Instead, she'd taken one look at his paper and mentioned the ineptitude of reporting in the front-page article about yet another Ministry achievement, calling it the "the embodiment of propaganda." When Malfoy hummed his agreement, she'd found herself intrigued. In the days that had followed, Hermione picked topics that were easy to gather from the parts of the paper she could see: The debate regarding the removal of the Statute of Secrecy. His response: "Never going to happen. Also idiotic." Lowering the age of the removal of The Trace to sixteen. His response: "I would argue for raising it." An article noting the rise of sales on defensive items following the Death Eater attack in March. His response: "Potter hasn't given up his belief that Mathers is alive. He's probably dead." But when Hermione voiced her opinion about the possibility of a third wizarding war, Malfoy's response had been the first complete one yet. "Those with the most power don't want peace. There's no profit in it. Peace would level out the balance of power and turn the public's attention to things that matter, such as why the Wizengamot hasn't restored power to the Minister after the agreed upon ten years." It was a true and perceptive statement, and had momentarily left her speechless. It made Hermione want to sift through his mind for whatever gems she could find. Thoughts. Opinions. Ideas. Observations. After that morning, their dialogues had morphed into chats that became less about what she could find and more about his thoughts on various topics. They began to do nothing more than talk for the sake of it. Which wasÉ unexpected. Each conversation was like opening a different box, and Hermione never knew what was going to be inside. She knew she had the option not to open it, but she did so anyway. Magical theory. History. Arithmancy. Charms. Malfoy picked her brain about the fact that she brewed potions, and she discovered his quiet passion for the subject after a lengthy argument about copper cauldrons versus brass for brewing Dreamless Sleep. And when he started bringing up Muggle topicsÑLiterature, Science Fiction, PhysicsÑ Hermione pretended not to look surprised. But she was. Some days were like pulling teeth. Others were easier. He would mostly engage, showing hints of something more than apathy, until he'd inevitably realise what he was doing and shut down. But Malfoy consistently argued against each of her points and rose up to challenge every statement. He didn't always win. But neither did she. And that wasÉ different. Oddly refreshing, but baffling nonetheless. Conversation was more than a sum of words, more than communication and the exchange of information. Hermione had always found it easier to understand and relate to people when she just talked to them, but with Malfoy the opposite was true. Each conversation left her more puzzled than before, less about his interests and opinions, and more about the ins and outs of who he was. His identity. May 13, 2011 It couldn't be considered paranoia if it was real. Hermione stepped out of the Floo Bank in the Ministry on a busy Wednesday afternoon, walking alongside other people into the bustling Atrium. True visitors drifted to the sides for their wands to be checked, but Hermione kept walking, all while feeling eyes on her. In most cases, they only looked because she was famous and rarely seen in public, but Harry's private Floo hadn't worked since he'd become Head of the Auror's Office, and no other Floo was open for her access. So, there she was. A known face in the crowd. In most cases, like the two workers sitting at the fountain who suddenly stopped talking in favour of staring at her before whispering again. The watching was the result of all the rumours surrounding her departure from the Ministry. Or the sprinkle of rumours that had followed ever since, each more absurd than the last. But in one case, she knew it was more than that. The man who watched her had been waiting in the Atrium, and suddenly realised he needed to walk directly behind her. He had been tracking her comings and goings since the Ministry had started sending her job offers three years ago, following her as she went, surely reporting her every move inside the Ministry's walls. Hermione had been aware of his presence, and he knew it, too. It was complicated at best. It felt like a game. Her watcher stepped into the empty space next to her in the queue for the lift. "McLaggen, I'm just having lunch with Harry. What threat do I specifically pose to need you as an escort?" "Technically, since you don't work for the Ministry, you shouldn't be roaming on your own. Especially since you didn't check your wand. My uncle grows more and more restless about the moves being made to unseat him. Your presence would only add to his distress. I am merelyÑ" "Wasting your words on a topic I don't care about." "Funny, my uncle seems to think you know about the movement somehow. Maybe you're involved, maybe you're not. All I know is that he's beginning to question people." Hermione stored that knowledge away for later. "This feels familiar." "Familiar how?" "Like the history we're about to repeat unless things change." "Maybe not. If you decided to return and pledge your allegiance to the MinistryÑ" "You mean to him? No thanks," Hermione said with a flippant twist of her wrist. "Tyrants come in many forms and wear many different masksÉ or ornate robes, I should say." Her words likely went right over Cormac's head. "Not only am I not willing to pledge my allegiance to any man, I'm also not searching for a change in career. I'm happy where I am." Cormac made a small, dismissive noise. "I'd believe you more if I knew you less." "You don't know me at all." She kept her public mask on effortlessly and the irritation that accompanied his presence out of her voice. It would only egg him on and draw more looks than necessary. The next lift arrived and the queue moved, but there still wasn't enough space for her to squeeze on and get away from him. Hermione looked around, searching for a familiar face in the crowd, but saw none. With an internal sigh, she turned to Cormac. Still broad and muscular in a way that fit his frame nicely, he hadn't changed much since Hogwarts. He still had strong features and a smile that could be charming; his dark blonde curls were tamed in a way that most might even consider flattering. Today, instead of his normal neutral colours, he wore plum trousers and a white dress shirt with expensive-looking cufflinks. Robes displaying his high position in the Wizengamot Administration Services were draped over his arm. Unfortunately, all that glittered wasn't gold. It really was a shame that Cormac hadn't grown past his aggressive, arrogant nature. Cormac's shoulder brushed hers, and his voice dropped low enough for her ears only. "I happen to know women like you very well. I can show you how well overÑ" "Do you lurk every day in hopes that I'll turn up at the Ministry, or do you have an actual career?" "Oh, Hermione." She didn't appreciate the pretentious way he said her name; she also didn't like the fact that, for the second time, he brushed her shoulder. Cormac was standing far too close for comfort. "Surely you're aware that I'm set to inherit my uncle's seat on the Wizengamot when he's ousted." When. Cormac knew something was changing, too. The timing of Tiberius McLaggen's appointment to the Wizengamot wasn't important, all that mattered was what had happened in the years since he'd become Chief Warlock. After buying nearly every business in Diagon Alley from desperate owners just looking to survive, heÑ out of the kindness of his own heart, of courseÑturned around and allowed those business owners to rent the stores they'd previously owned for a percentage of their annual sales. It was undoubtedly helpful in the years right after the war, when some shops went days without a single patron and people were still too afraid to return to the normalcy of things like shopping trips and expenditures that weren't strictly necessary. The questionable actions came when, after his appointment as Chief Warlock, he pushed through the major rehabilitation project that poured millions of Ministry Galleons into rebuilding wizarding businessesÉ in Diagon Alley. As businesses recovered and sales climbed, so did their rent. Tiberius McLaggen made millions. Anyone that challenged their rental agreement was quickly shut down. Percy had been quietly checking into the legality of the agreements with his tenants, but he'd run into obstacles. No one who had been privy to Tiberius' unforgiving nature had been eager to cooperate for fear of losing what they'd worked so hard for without a fair fight. Even now, gathering information was a slow processÑmuch like Percy's pet project. While not every member of the Wizengamot was as corrupt as the Chief Warlock, there were just enough members who liked the perks of the current status quo to keep any true change at bay. They were no better than the ones whose vaults were amassing the unethical Galleons. The lift arrived and they filed on with the others. Hermione took a spot in front of Cormac, fully prepared to ignore him like the pest he was. In enclosed spaces, it was a habit for her to observe her surroundings. Her eyes fell on a familiar white-blond head in the front corner by the button panel. Malfoy. One more wizard decided to squeeze on rather than wait for the next lift. Everyone shifted to accommodate. The man in front of her shuffled backwards. Automatically, Hermione tried to move out of his way, but found her back pressed against Cormac's broad chest. It was instinct to apologise, but she stopped herself. Best if she didn't acknowledge him or their current state. Not that it mattered. It was Cormac McLaggen, after all. It wasn't like him to ignore a perfect opportunity. Despite the audience on the now moving lift, he lowered his head, and in a predatory voice whispered, "If I were you, HermioneÉ" His hand ghosted up her arm to push her hair off her shoulder. "I would try a little harder to endear myself. Don't you want me to remember you when I'm in my new position?" "If you touch me one more time, McLaggen, I'll be the scariest thing you've ever seen." Her voice was low, serious enough to make him back off ever so slightly. "Still so feisty. I've always admired that about you." "And you're still an arrogant bastard who will be doing the Wizengamot's legwork for the rest of your miserable life." "I don't see following a beautiful woman around on scheduled visits for lunch with her best friend as a particular hardship." Cormac's voice dropped even lower as he whispered, "More like my pleasure." "This is why you can't keep a wife." His second divorce was playing out nastily in the papers, at least according to the ones she'd lined her chicken coop with last week. "Third time's a charm." Before Hermione could verbalise her disgust or turn around and club him over the head with her beaded bag, the doors opened and a few employees filed out, still absorbed in getting to their destination as quickly as possible. It wasn't the floor where Harry's office was located, but at least with only one man entering the lift, there was now enough space for her to step away from Cormac without bumping into anyone else. She reached for the strap above her head in preparation for the lift to begin moving, then glared daggers at Cormac, who remained in his spot against the back wall. Watching her. Waiting. Like a lion on the prowl. Hermione ignored him in favour of glancing around the crowded lift. She now had a clear visual of Malfoy, who regarded her with an indecipherable expression. It wasn't the first time she'd seen him that day. That honour went to that morning when Malfoy had asked for a cup of whatever tea she had been drinking: a fruity mint mixture she'd concocted for Narcissa. He drank it with zero complaints. The front page this morning had featured the Death Eater sightings in Wales. Hermione had asked a standard question to start: "Do the Task Force and Auror's Office have enough to properly investigate?" "Not particularly, Potter has a spare team that has just arrived back from an assignment that he'll be forced to send. I just returned this morning actually and will be taking a Portkey back this evening to return, once again, the following morning." She hadn't been able to stop her next question: "Do you sleep?" And that had promptly ended their conversation. Now his probing look between her and Cormac was even stranger. As far as Hermione was concerned, the extent of his curiosity regarding her began and ended with why she'd taken on his mother as a patient. He'd reserved his high irritation when she looked on while he worked on his crossword. Finally, the doors to the lift opened on Harry's floor and more people filed out. And if Hermione whispered a Trip Jinx that left Cormac a sprawled mess on the floor of the liftÉ Well, that was between her and anyone who noticed. The gates shut and the lift left with Cormac yelling something on his way to whichever floor the lift would stop at next. Feeling proud of herself, and with a smile on her face, Hermione took two steps in the direction of Harry's office, then remembered that someone had noticed. And that someone happened to have longer legs, which allowed him to fall in step alongside her with relative ease. "Lover's quarrel?" Malfoy's voice was as dry as it was posh. It made Hermione's hair stand on end. Along with her nerves. "Excuse me?" "McLaggen," he said. Face drawn, his expression fell somewhere between grudgingly curious and outright annoyedÑtwo emotions that didn't belong on the same scale. "I saw you two on theÑ" "That pompous wanker is notÑI repeat, notÑmy lover in any definition of the word," she seethed with such strong vehemence she nearly missed the tiny stutter in his step. "Cormac wouldn't know how to love anyone other than himself if someone gave him a map and a guide." There was a short pause. "Ah, well, excuse me." Malfoy calmly turned and went in the opposite direction. Unspeakably baffled, Hermione watched him go. "What the hellÉ" She shook it off, chalked it up to him being Malfoy, and followed the path into the controlled chaos of the ever-busy Department of Magical Law Enforcement. Pointedly ignoring the eyes and whispers that followed her presence, Hermione forced herself not to look down until she found herself at the door to the Auror's Office. Greeted by a nearly empty office, she guessed most Aurors were on assignment or lunch at that part of the day. Only a handful were at their desks doing paperwork, not paying attention to the new person in the room. The only person who paid her any mind was the secretary, Deloris, an older witch with greying black hair who always wore purple robes. "Miss Granger, it's lovely to see you." Deloris was like the office mother. She brought a meal for everyone at least once a week and even baked cookies every Friday. Like most secretaries, she knew everything that happened in the Ministry, all the rumours, both significant and dull. Hermione had told Harry when he'd been appointed Head Auror that he should always stop and listen to anything she had to say, including the latest gossip. She was more informative than the Prophet. "Lovely to see you as well, Deloris." "How's your gardening, love?" "By next visit, I should have strawberries, gooseberries, peas, broad beans, and more for you." "That sounds lovely. Make sure to bring extras so I can make you some jam." Deloris made the best jam. Last summer, she'd brought Hermione several jars from the extra fruit she'd given herÑshe and Al had only eaten toast and their jam of the day for breakfast during his visits for months. Hermione was already looking forward to this year. "I can't wait. Oh!" She opened her beaded bag, and reached deep until she found what she was looking for. "I brought you more salve for your husband's knee and your pain potion, as well." Deloris had been hit with a dark spell while protecting her Muggle-born husband from Snatchers during the war. St Mungo's hadn't been much help, outside of healing the immediate damage, which had led to years of her suffering in silence. Harry had asked for Hermione's help. After research, she'd found success in an obscure plant that boosted the potency of the regular pain potion. Deloris only needed one drop. Hermione handed the witch the vial and a tin canister of salve. "Thank you so much." Deloris graciously accepted her offerings. "You really should let me pay you." Hermione shook her head. That wasn't why she did it. "It's no trouble at all. Keep looking out for Harry, that's all I ask." Speaking of her best friendÉ "Is he busy?" "He's just returned from his meeting with Hestia and Mr Malfoy regarding the canvassing team they need for that unfortunate bit happening in Wales. He's also tied up with staffing for the raid that no one is talking about." The two women exchanged knowing looks and matching eye rolls. Hermione was more worried about the almost open secret getting too far out and failing, but didn't share those concerns with Deloris. "Has Harry eaten?" "No, even though I told him he should. He's had an incredibly busy day, and his afternoon is completely booked. He has another private meeting with Mr Malfoy in fifteen minutes." Ah, one of their strategy meetings. "I brought him lunch." She held up her beaded bag. "Is he doing anything right now?" "Paperwork." "Ah, the bane of his existence." Deloris smirked. "Too right you are." "Better go save him then." The older witch grinned, shooing her along. Hermione gave her a fond look before walking past her desk and knocking on the closed office door with Harry's name and title etched in the gold plaque. The heavy door opened with a creak that Magical Maintenance had yet to fix. Harry's office, as always, was a minimalistic mess. She never could figure out how that was possible, but it was. He didn't have much: a few books, important keepsakes, and framed pictures of Ginny and the children. Nothing on the walls. Simple. In the corner was a rack of hangers with jackets and robes on them. The newest addition was a table in the centre of the room with what looked like a map covered in different coloured pins strategically spread across the full length of it. Even with few things, though, it was in a perpetual state of disarray. Nothing was ever organised, and he had a ton of paperwork stacked on his desk at any given time. She spotted her best friend sitting behind a mountain of parchment, scratching away at something she couldn't see. "How can I help you?" Harry asked without looking up. "Well, I'm looking for my best friend, who happens to be The Chosen OneÑ" He looked up and laughed, glancing at his gold watch as Hermione approached. "Blimey, is it one already?" She placed her beaded bag on the desk and opened it up, pulling out a simple lunch of roasted chicken sandwiches stuffed with tomatoes, cheese, cucumbers, and romaine lettuce. Hermione made sure to pack some oven-roasted potato wedges along with cold cans of VimtoÑ blackcurrant because that was their favourite. "Yes, I just finished with Narcissa's lunch potions and she's overseeing her grandson's lessons." More like making suggestions and lesson plan changes. She'd be tied up at least until dinner, when she was sure to sharply decline and get irritable. Narcissa could be downright mean towards Scorpius' nanny, who was understandably stressed at all times. His tutor wasn't much better, but he remained in Narcissa's good graces as he was the traditional sort she respected. Harry moved the parchment aside, giving her and their lunch his full attention. "How is that going?" "Interesting. They'reÉ a lot. It's different than I expected." "How so?" Hermione let out a breath that turned into a chuckle as she made a series of exaggerated hand gestures to emphasise each word. "I can't possibly list it all during the fifteen minutes I have between now and your strategy meeting with Malfoy. We'll have to talk about it another time." "One sentence then." "She's the most infuriating patient I've ever had." He laughed. "She's your Draco Malfoy, then." "I suppose so. I often forget she's even sick." Until the evenings and nights remind her. Hermione pursed her lips, deep in thought. "Ever felt like you were missing something that was staring you in the face?" "All the time." Hermione chuckled. "That's how I feel about Narcissa's treatment. The nights are rough to the point where I've considered staying." "At Malfoy's house?" "Yes, just until I figure out what's wrong with her evening potions." "Well, you won't have to worry about Malfoy much. He'll be in Wales coordinating the sweep where the Death Eaters were spotted. He thinks there may be a hideout nearby." She stabbed one of the potatoes with her fork and brought it to her mouth while Harry bit into his sandwich. "Does he actually sleep?" "How should I know? I imagine he gets some sort of sleep. Has to or he'd be more insufferable than he already is." That seemed doubtful; hard evidence pointed to the opposite. Perhaps he wasÑ "Enough about him. I'll be seeing him soon enough." A quick frown expressed Harry's distaste, but it was followed by a wiggling of his eyebrows. "Have you had any interesting conversations with his mother lately?" "She hasn't said anything particularly rude since she tried to give me advice." She emphasised the last word with air quotes, her fork still in hand. Harry laughed almost as hard as he had the first time she'd told him the story. "She's been busy with society activities and her grandson's lessons. We haven't had a chance to speak much. Well, outside of her complaints about each meal before she takes a bite and finds it remarkably palatable," Hermione mocked in a poor imitation of Narcissa's voice. "Sounds like a compliment to me." "Honestly it's the closest I might ever get. She'd rather walk on the surface of the sun than admit she likes my primitive cooking." Snorting in response, Harry took a sip of his drink. "And before he gets here, what about Malfoy? Is he the model son? Does he call you incompetent and argue about the best way to care for his mother because surely only he knows best?" "ActuallyÉ no." Malfoy was incredibly hard to pin down. Distrustful and private, aloof and sarcastic, astute and defensive. He was more perceptive of the world around him than she'd expected from someone who grew up believing he was the centre of it. Malfoy didn't fit the image that Harry, himself, had put into her mind. Maybe he acted differently around Harry. Maybe Harry had the same effect on Malfoy as the reverse. Hermione had no idea. "He doesn't want to be involved in any aspect of her care. Not now or as she worsens. I've no idea why." Harry's smirk faded. "That's surprisingÉ I've always thought they were close. Narcissa's letters practically sung his praises." "Either she's extremely deluded or blatantly lying to cover up the rift, but it's there. Loud. Granted, I haven't seen them in the same room together since the first day, but it's awfully tense." "I'm shocked, really. I mean, he seemed disillusioned with his father during his trial, but he never let go of his mother's hand." Hermione had her own vague memories of the day, now scattered by time and her own life events that occurred in the years between. The Malfoys had always seemed like a complicated, yet tight-knit family. Appearances were often deceiving. "They aren't close." Hermione finally picked up her sandwich. "Their dynamic is strange. I don't know how anyone can stand itÑhow I stand it." "I mean, it's not like we sit and chat like old friends." Harry snorted as if the thought were utterly inconceivable. "But they've had a death in the family in the last six months. Deloris told me of the rumours going around. Apparently, he'll be married again within the year, should his mother have her way." Hermione winced, but took another bite, her mouth suddenly dry. "Might be a source of contention, but I doubt it." Harry gave a lazy shrug. "Ginny says the Malfoys are all about fulfilling their duty to family over self; it's practically etched in stone on some ancient rock somewhere." She sipped her drink, nodding along even though she should have been laughing at Harry's joke. He sobered after finishing his potatoes. "I wasn't working directly with him at the time. They had me doing a lot of public appearances before my promotion, but he was gone for weeks after his wife died. When he returned he was just as"ÑHarry waved his handÑ"Malfoy-ish as usual. Nothing out of the ordinary." As she continued eating, Hermione sorted through the mental data. She recognised the division between the adults as something she would have to address as Narcissa worsened. Who would make important decisions for her? Draco? Yes, but would he give a damn to make the right decision for her? No was looking like the most accurate answer. What safeguards could she employ to ensure the older witch's safety? There would be legal documents and aspects that absolutely required Malfoy's cooperation. He would have toÑ Hermione took a deep breath. She couldn't let herself follow that train of thought, not when another was far more complicatedÉ Scorpius. He was none of her business, but Hermione noticed the dynamic between Malfoy and Narcissa as it pertained to the boy's care. Malfoy was diligent whenever Scorpius wasn't around. He listened intently to status reports from Zippy and Catherine, set his place at the table, and left notes that Scorpius clung to. But it was Narcissa overseeing her grandson's complicated daily schedule, which was packed enough to give Hermione Third Year flashbacks. It was Narcissa who made sure he sat up straight, was polite, and trainedÑa word that still made her shudder. She treated him so unlike she'd treated Malfoy during their years at school that it was almost beyond belief. With her rules and regulations, it was a wonder she hadn't burned the curiosity out of Scorpius. Remarkably, she hadn't, but The Malfoys were a topic better left for another day. Preferably one when they could chat over a fruity wine Harry would never admit to enjoying. "Something else that's strange?" Harry pointed out. "No McLaggen. He usually follows you here." A smug grin curled the edges of Hermione's lips. "Wandless Trip Jinx in the lift." "Nice. I suppose that's why you won't accept the security detail, then. Theo told me." Of course he had. "Honestly, Harry, you know me better than that. I can take care of myself." "Trust me, I'm aware, but they're getting closer to us than I'd like. A threat came to James' school." Hermione's heart stuttered. That really was too close. At her wince, he sighed, looking far wearier than anyone should at their age. "No one was hurt. The teams came in and did a sweep, but found nothing. Ginny and I are beginning to wonder if we need to move schoolsÉ or possibly send James to a wizarding primary school, which would be more equipped to handle the threat of Death Eaters, should they attack. Molly thinks we should pull them all out and let her home-school them." The look on his face said that would be a last resort. "Are you looking into private security for the children?" "A pair at each school." Harry nodded. "Malfoy gave me the name of the company he hired to watch his family." She raised an inquiring brow. "You two managed to have a conversation long enough to get to that point?" "I just asked. When I mentioned it was for my kids, he didn't hesitate. I'm serious though, Hermione, I think you should consider it." "As I told Theo, I am my own security." Harry's expression turned serious. "You don't have to be." They finished eating, packed the glass containers back in her bag, and were busy arranging Al's weekend visit when there was a distinctive knock on the door. Two quick taps. A pause. Then a single knock. Harry took a calm, cleansing breath. He knew exactly who it was. "Malfoy." With a wave of his hand, Harry's office door creaked open, and the confirmation of his statement stood there like a brooding statue. His arms were even folded as his eyes cut back and forth between them. "Come in." Malfoy entered the office as Hermione stood to leave, grabbing her bag off his desk. Tucking her hair behind her ear, she felt the mood in the office shift with the new addition, turning from friendly to something far chillier. Both had serious expressions on their faces, prepared to work. Or battle. Likely both. Malfoy approached Harry's desk, stopping just at the edge. He didn't even try to hide his contempt with the subtle glance at the mess. "Potter." He didn't spit his name like he used to, but it still made Hermione shift uncomfortably. His tone was dry yet polite, the product of someone who'd spent years entrenched in 'proper' society. Then grey eyes cut to her. "We meet again, Granger." Harry's mouthed 'again?' "We saw each other in the lift with McLaggen." "Ah." They stood in awkward silence, each of them looking everywhere to avoid looking at each other. Hermione clasped her hands together. "Well, I'll leave you both to it." She silently wished Harry all the luck in the world when he scratched his scar, not because it itched, simply a force of habit. Something he only did when supremely uncomfortable. Given the awkward energy, his feelings made sense. Hermione looked from Harry to Malfoy, who was regarding her with a potent yet slightly bemused expression. Then he huffed and glared at Harry. "You didn't ask her, did you?" "Ask me what?" What Hermione hated mostÑwell, after tardiness, laziness, and mouth-breathingÑwas being the last to know anything. Especially if it pertained to her. Harry patted down his messy, dark hair and rubbed the back of his neck. Guilty. "We're developing a strategy for the raid and need a third party's opinion." "What about Ron?" The moment it escaped, she immediately reconsidered. "Are you serious?" Malfoy drawled. "Weasley's idea of foresight is putting his socks on before his shoes." Hermione found herself suspended in a state of disbelief. Not that he was the most amiable person, but at least Malfoy tried to hold his tongue in his own home. There was also the small part that whispered that he was the same person who left notes for Scorpius and had politely asked for tea that very morning. It felt like whiplash. Harry's infamous temper flared in defence of Ron, Malfoy responded, and soon snide comments were flying back and forth. Before it escalated into unprofessional insults, Hermione stepped in. "I actually have better things to do than listen to you squabble like children." She glared at them both. "If we could please get on with it, I'd appreciate it. Harry, stop letting Malfoy regress you fifteen years, it's ridiculous." The anger in her friend's eyes died when he finally realised how immature he was acting. His cheeks flushed. One down. Hermione shifted her weight from one foot to another before facing her last obstacle between them all and peace. "And Malfoy." His expression was stoic defiance. "We all know and remember quite clearly that you're a massive prat. There's no need to remind us by posturing. No one needs that kind of energy on a team, especially not when there's so much on the line. You both have common goals. Remember that." As if she'd slapped him, he visibly recoiled, taking a full step back before remembering himself. "Oh, and just so you know, Ron's probably a better strategist than I am. In some respects, at least. But Harry was right not to ask him because he'll side with Harry out of spite. I won't." She unfolded her arms, resting them on her hips. "Don't judge what you don't know." While Harry was chastened, Malfoy's glare only intensified at the call back from their first full conversation. But she honestly didn't care as she stepped forward, closer to him, meeting his opposition with a scowl. "Remember, Malfoy, you two have a bigger fight ahead than the one against each other. It doesn't just involve your jobs. It doesn't involve just you as individuals. It involves your families, too." She didn't miss that small tick in his jaw or the way he seemed to reset. Exhale. Refocus. "Now, are you both finished?" Malfoy acquiesced with a subtle nod. Harry's agreement, on the other hand, was crystal clear. "If you have any other suggestions for candidates, I'll gladly step aside as I want as little to do with the Ministry as possible. At least in its current state." There was a long pause while they waited for the last person in the room to get on board. They didn't have to wait long. "Since you're already here, I suppose you'll do." Malfoy's tone regained its professional edge. "Shall we?" "Is there a blueprint of the Lestrange Manor where the raid will take place?" Hermione asked. "There." Malfoy gestured to the table in the centre of the room. "The only uncluttered surface in Potter's office." His comment wasn't critical or hostile, merely matter-of-fact. So much so that Harry only shrugged in response. They gathered around the table, looking down at the blueprint that had pins colour-coded for a particular purposeÑHarry and Hermione on one side, Malfoy on the other. Malfoy began with a sweeping gesture. "This particular Lestrange Manor hasn't been occupied for at least fifty years, but it's complicated and likely has traps. Its wards can only be taken down by someone of Lestrange blood, but after speaking to several experts, I've found that there's a way to trick the wards." "Really?" That was fascinating. Impressive, really. Apparently he hadn't shared any of that with Harry. "Is it possible to trick a ward?" The look on Malfoy's face spoke volumes, namely that Harry was too stupid to live. "Yes, it is when the wards are old and very specific, weaved into blood magic. The Manor's wards were specific like that untilÑ" Cursed fire that never burned out. "How does it work?" Hermione asked. "It's astoundingly simple." Malfoy reached across the table to fix one of the pins. The action caused his cuff to rise higher than usual. The movement wasn't noteworthy, except that it teased something she never expected to see on a man who wore black like a second skin. It wasn't just the tattooÑa very large, scaly tattoo, from the looks of itÑthat wrapped around his wrist and disappeared under his suit. Yes. Okay. That was highly unexpected. But what caught her attention was the colour. Burgundy red and burnt orange and a hint of deep green flashed in her field of vision before Malfoy righted himself. Hermione looked away when his scrutinous gaze settled on her, daring her to say something, but she didn't accept the bait. "From my inquiry and research, it seems that the Ward Specialist has to be, at the very least, a pureblood." Malfoy's briefly looked over to Harry. "I happen to have found one you both know and trust. Ernie Macmillan. He primarily works on wards for businesses, but he knows how to do it because his family's estate has similar wards. He's already agreed to the job." Hermione recalled Harry's rant about him changing the Ward Specialist, but now it made perfect sense. "Ernie doesn't work for the Ministry, Malfoy, we don't have the budgetÑ" "Technically, he'll be working for me, as I'll be handling his fee." At the surprised look on Harry's face, Malfoy set his jaw. "I thought I made myself clear when I said I was willing to do whatever it takes to end this. Money is no object. Will that be an issue, Potter?" She and Harry exchanged looks but Hermione said nothing. It wasn't her place. Harry compromised with an exhale. "It won't." "Very well then." Malfoy redirected their attention to the blueprint. "According to the mole, the black pin is the meeting location. Red pins are the entrance points, not including windows, of course. Anything else you need to know before making an assessment, Granger?" Hermione looked closer. The room they were meeting in was circular, situated near the centre of the Manor, and it looked like it could pass for a small ballroom. There were four clear entrances into the mansion but five red pins. It looked like there were two doors that served as both entrances and exits. Not much room for escapeÑfor anyone. Which would go either be good or very bad. There was also no telling if there were any sort of traps waiting for them in the house or on the grounds. Hermione bent forward a little, touching the only pin that didn't make sense. A red pin, which meant entrance, but there were no doors leading to the outside. "What is this pin?" "A possible secret entryway," Malfoy replied. "I've confirmed there's a tunnel that runs beneath the house, which stops under this room. I believe there's a way to get into the room from the tunnel." "Ah." She tucked her hair behind her ear, wishing she'd brought something to tie it back. "Harry? Do you have a rubber band?" They looked at his messy desk. "Never mind." Her best friend's smile was sheepish. "I should clear that up." "Undoubtedly." "It's fine." Hermione waved him off. "First, I'd like to hear your strategy, Harry. Then yours, Malfoy." She moved to the head of the table, leaving the other two facing each other. Hermione listened to each plan, following their steps and asking questions while noting that they both had strategies to leverage various advantages to compensate for the lack of manpower. Harry's strategy was reflective of his personality: simple and to the point. Storm in, block all exits, make sure they can't Apparate out. Battle until they surrender. How very Veni, vidi, vici of him. She guessed Harry hadn't considered the secret passageway in his plan because it hadn't been confirmed. Malfoy's plan was clever and careful, far more flexible than Harry's, leaning heavily on strategy to compensate for the lack of experience from most of their fighters. It would be easy enough to adjust for any surprises based on their opponents and circumstances. "So, what do you think?" Harry asked once Malfoy concluded. Hermione asked a few final questions before closing her eyes and mapping it all out in her head. A few minutes later, she had an answer. "Neither." Malfoy gave her a thin, testy look, while her best friend responded with bewilderment. "What?" "Hear me out." She drew their attention to the blueprint. "I think the more optimal strategy would be a combination of the two. A compromise, if you will. Harry's right in needing to pack a decisive and strong blow, but Malfoy's flexible plan would be helpful, should anything go awry at the last minute. Send Teams C and D through the main two entrances along with E, but have Teams A and B come through the secret passage. Your opponents will think they have the upper hand until your very strong Teams A and B attack from behind. Malfoy, have you confirmed the passageway?" "I'm working on that as we speak." She rearranged the placement of the teams to different entrances. "On the off chance that there is no secret passageway, just send Teams A and B as a second, stronger wave. Although less experienced, the three teams should be able to wear your targets down, leading them into a false sense of security, before the elite teams show up. It'sÑ" "Impressive." Malfoy sounded like he meant it, albeit reluctantly. "I didn't do anything except combine both of your ideas. It's something both could have done without my influence." She shrugged, noting the growing interest on Malfoy's face. Had they put forth the effort to work as a team, they would have arrived at the same conclusion. "I can't guarantee it'll work. The best laid plans of mice and menÑ" "Often go awry," Malfoy finished. Their eyes met and she was unable to look away untilÑ A throat cleared. Harry's expression was pensive, but his eyes were cutting back and forth between them, narrowing more with each pass. "What do you think, Harry?" she asked. The question snapped him from his thoughts. "Oh! I think it's brilliant." "It'll likely need adjusting, of course, as we receive more intel," Malfoy added. "I'm sure Potter and I will be able to make the appropriate modifications." It was a solid place to start. "How much time do you have for training?" Hermione replaced the pins representing the two elite teams. "A month, perhaps a little more. Malfoy believes the meeting will take place at the end of Spring." Harry's statement was confirmed by Malfoy's nod. "I know that's not a lot of time, but If we could get everyone together more than once a week, we could run drills and help the ones with the least amount of experience improve. However, we don't have the extra time, space, orÑ" Hermione scoffed. "Since when have you really ever cared about any of that, or even rules?" Malfoy made a small, snarky noise, which caused them both to shoot him matching dirty looks. "You'll have to go about it like in Fifth Year." "Sneaking around to train people? That'sÑ" "Do you have any better suggestions, Malfoy?" Hermione only tilted her head in challenge. When he folded his arms and looked away, she turned back to Harry. "I think this could work. How many people on each team?" "Eight." Harry shrugged when he noticed her cringe. "It was all we could pull together. Hestia tried to appeal for more, to pull more people in off assignment, but the Wizengamot said no. As it stands, the department is already spread thin. The Hit Wizards are at capacity as they've pulled half their ranks for Malfoy's Task Force, Magical Law Enforcement Agents are splitting their time between security for members of the WizengamotÑ" "Wait." Hermione held up a hand. "That's not their job." Malfoy levelled her with a look. "Do you honestly think they give a damn, Granger?" She knew what he was trying to say, but it was inconceivable. "So, they acknowledge the threat of Death Eaters and provide just enough assistance to blame you both should anything bad happen, but they turn around and seek to protect themselves? Wow. That is completely Ñ" "Unsurprising," he drawled. Harry agreed with a nod, then his face twisted like he couldn't believe he'd had a moment of accord with Draco Malfoy. Stranger things had already happened. "How can you do nothing about it?" Hermione knew she sounded every bit as self-righteous as she could muster. "It's a predicament, sure," Harry said. "But there's nothing to be done about it." "There's plenty to be done!" "As much as I despise the very idea of agreeing with Potter, and will never admit to doing this should anyone bring this upÉ he's right. What would you have him do? Take on the entire Wizengamot?" Hermione shrugged. "Not the worst idea you've ever had." "It's short-sighted, Granger, and you know it. They'll sack anyone who steps a toe out of line." Malfoy cast a glance over at Harry. "I don't know about Potter here, but I'm not interested in living the rest of my life looking over my shoulder. I'd rather be on this side, where I have control. I refuse to depend on someone who doesn't have a stake in this to ensure the safety of my family. So, if that means smiling in their faces, spending my own money, and working around the corruption to suit my needs in order to finish this job, then that's what I'll do." Hermione didn't like it, but resigned herself to the fact that Malfoy had a point. His statement tickled at the part of her brain that wanted to say a lot moreÑespecially along the lines of the distance he kept from everyone he sought to protectÑbut Hermione knew that would not go over well. More than any of them, Malfoy would be a sitting duck if not for his current position. It gave him more than control, it gave him a say in what happened and how it would affect him. And she wondered if that had been his purpose in taking the position. Or even starting a career to begin with. He worked for free. "I don't completely disagree with Malfoy," Harry said carefully. "I've sworn to use my position for as much good as possible. We've got forty wizards to take part in a raid that may finally end it all. That's all I can focus on right now. That and making sure I do what I have to in order to keep everyone I care about safe. After? Maybe I'll be able to look towards the fight against corruption, but right now, this is where I am." And no matter how much it grated her just how unfair things were, how insane it was that they had to make that choice, Hermione wasn't self-righteous to the point where she could ignore the logic behind their position or the order of their priorities. They were both more than their jobs. More than childhood enemies. They were men. Fathers. "Okay, since we're concentrating on this, perhaps you could schedule weekly meetings with each team and use those meetings for training. With eight people per team, you both canÑ" Harry interrupted first, "You want me to train people. With Malfoy?" "Why not?" Hermione blinked slowly, trying to figure out if she'd missed something important along the way. "He is an Auror, just not in England." Grey eyes widened before he could guard his expression. "Who the hell told you that?" "A bee." Harry chuckled while Malfoy glared at her even harder. "Is that supposed to be a cipher? A name, Granger. I need a name." "Do you think I'm the sort that would reveal my source?" Stepping around the head of the table, she rested one hand flat against the surface. Malfoy angled his body towards her, arms still folded as he waited for an answer she would never give. "You say you don't want to look over your shoulder forever. What does it matter how I know you went through Auror training? I just do. I just know. Are you going to help or not?" Emboldened when his glare started to recede, she felt her upper hand slip when his head tilted slightly to the left, much like it had in the lift with McLaggen. What was he trying to figure out? "You're especially aggravating when you think you're right," Malfoy said. Harry snorted, which made Hermione glare. "What? You are." Just when Hermione was going to retort, she heard Malfoy sigh. "I'll help. Only to help increase our chances at finishing this job and only if Potter stops being aÑ" "How about you both put your differences aside until this is done, yeah?" she interjected. "I can if he can," Harry said. "Well, I'll leave it to you both to coordinate schedules." With that, Hermione took a step back. "Most of your time should be dedicated to Teams D and E. If you need, I can create training manuals for them." Malfoy's face twisted. "You aren't an Auror." "True, but I am a fighter." "Is that so?" Bemusement quickly crossed his features. "I'm increasingly baffled as to why you'd waste your time as a Healer when you're obviously good at this particular line of work. If things were different with leadership, you would get a lot more recognition doing this than what you're doing now." Harry looked downright shocked at the backhanded compliment, but Hermione wasn't focused on why Malfoy had felt the need to acknowledge her competency; she was hung up on his blind eye to the larger moral dilemma in his statement. "If I wanted recognition, I would have accepted the offers, but I haven't and I won't because I don't. I want to make a difference. And I've learned there's more than one way to do that." She didn't try to decipher his expression; she couldn't read it. "I worked for the Ministry almost as long as I've been a Healer, and I've found it more fulfilling to make a large difference on a small scale rather than to change thousands of lives in a very minimal way." "How noble of you." Malfoy's response was dry. Aloof. Dismissive. "That's the thing, I'm not being noble. I'm not being honourable. I'm just being myself." A knock halted all conversation. Harry opened the door to reveal Percy wearing a grim expression. "Apologies for the interruption, but The Chief Warlock would like to speak to you in his office." "What does he want now?" Harry sighed. "We've answered all his questions. He's obsessed with sussing out any conspiracy against him." Hermione furrowed her brows, recalling her earlier conversation with Cormac. "With power comes paranoia of losing that power." Had she been a jumpy person, Hermione would have had a physical reaction to the fact that Malfoy was now right beside her, but there was only an uptick in her pulse, a cord of tension pulled taut. "What time are we being summoned, Weasley?" There was something about Malfoy's tone thatÑ "He doesn't want to speak to either of you this time." Blue eyes fell on her. She already knew what Percy was going to say next. "He'd like to speak with Hermione." Everything about Tiberius McLaggen's office was ornate and extravagant, ornamented with gold tones and the finest dŽcor she had ever seen in a Ministry office. It was fitting for him: a bit tacky. She stood in front of the exquisitely crafted maple desk decorated with trinkets and expensive clutter that served no purpose except to remind visitors of his status. There was a small, blue, porcelain kettle with steam wafting from the spout next to two matching cups. Hermione kept her attention on the man fully dressed in his Wizengamot regalia. Unnecessary outside of hearings and official events. He hadn't once looked up from his task since his door had opened for her admittance, calmly dipping his peacock quill in ink before scratching sounds filled the silence again. But there were little clues that gave away his true feelings: an impatience that told Hermione his silence was a power play. Unlike last time, she didn't have the advantage. But her experience with waiting out Theo's silences had prepared her for this. Hermione sat on a chair inlaid with patterned blue silk, and padded with matching damask. She kept herself poised and confident and waited with her hands locked on her lap. She learned more from his office than she had from their last conversation. Posturing and power plays. His attitude and brittle impatience. The way he made sure everyone knew who the leader was. Tiberius was doing his own work, which meant Malfoy was right. He wasn't as in control as he wanted everyone to believe; he was scrambling to keep his positionÑhis power. A king shouldn't have to prove who he is. Which made her want to pull Tiberius apart even more. Hermione eyed the row of portraits on the wall to her left, all of whom were watching her closely. Portraits of him in various outfits and poses and backgrounds. The arrogance of it was perversely amusing. Tiberius flipped from parchment to parchment, appearing to sign his name over and over. She could only wonder what he was doing, as she wasn't in the right position to read upside down. Pity. When he placed his quill on the holder next to the inkwell, she knew his impatience had won out. Hermione was ready. "Would you care for a cup of tea, Miss Granger?" "Yes, please." His wandless magic wasn't smooth, but he managed to pour two cups before replacing the kettle on his desk. She picked up one and wrapped her hands around the warming porcelain. The tea was dark enough to warrant the milk he poured into his cup, but she refused. "I bet you are wondering why you were summoned here today, Miss Granger." "The thought crossed my mind once or twice," Hermione replied with a fixed smile, small and anything but genuine. "I would have thought our last conversation would have set the tone and expectations of our future interactions. I don't like surprises any more than I like being summoned." "Apologies for that. I know you're a busy woman with a new assignment thatÑ" "Is frankly none of your business, Chief Warlock." Hermione looked down at the liquid. "As I am admittedly quite busy, I hope you don't mind if we bypass the small talk and get straight to the purpose of your invitation." "Yes, of course. Have you given any additional thought to my last offer?" "No, and I won't explain myself further. I've run out of ways to say no that you will comprehend." "Very well then, Miss Granger. That will be all. You are free to stay and enjoy your tea." Tiberius took a sip and dramatically placed it on the ornamental saucer. "In the interest of you declining my offer yet again, I've been hearing louder whispers aboutÑ" "Is this an official inquiry? Because if so, let me remind you that I'm allowed to have counsel present." She sat straighter. "And if this is indeed a friendly dialogue, I should remind you that the unlawful use of VeritaserumÑsay, outside of an official inquiryÑis a violation of Article Two, Part Three, Subsection D of the Unlawful Use of Potions Act signed into law on DecemberÑ" "Miss Granger, I don't know what you're inferring with your statement." "Your brew is colourless and odourless." Hermione sat the teacup on the desk. "But something I've noticed while brewing Veritaserum is that there's a sheen to it. Just a hint. Only noticeable if you know what to look for." "I assure you I would never do such a thing." "Hmm." Hermione folded her arms. "You wouldn't, but if you were looking to find out information that you were desperate enough to bend the rules for my own greater good to obtainÑperhaps that would be a risk you think you could reasonably take due to your station. I don't need Veritaserum to tell you what I know, because I don't know anything of interest." "But you do know something." "You're right. I know a great deal of things, Chief Warlock." Hermione stood up. "I've heard the public's rumblings. I've heard that there are those working to end the corruption that leads right up the ladder to you. I even know you've begun questioning those who might go against you." Tiberius looked nervous, agitated. "That is enough, MissÑ" "A word of friendly advice: pick your battles wisely or you will end up like your predecessors." She held her hand up when he rose to his feet.
