"It's well documented that they were more worried about the Ministry's reputation than actual security, that they abused their power to manipulate the media, and knowingly vilified the wrong people to give the facade of progress. In the end, it was their undoing." "Are you threatening me?" "No, but you should worry less about getting me under the Ministry's thumb, less about me playing the role you believe I should play, and less about the push to restore power back to the Minister. Try worrying more about what's happening out there to the people you want to lead so badly. You can interview everyone, dose them to find out whatever truths you seek, but you'll never be able to silence what is right." Hermione started for the door, but turned around. "Change is coming." She smiled. "You can't hide from something that's already begun." Time is on the side of change. Ruth Bader Ginsburg Chapter 8: A Silent Shout Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Eight A Silent Shout May 18, 2011 Narcissa looked like a ghost trapped between two worlds. Shrouded in darkness in the garden at five in the morning, her shadow shimmered against the grass. Cast by the flickering light coming from the lone outdoor lamp, she was highlighted by the moon itself. Her white silk nightgown hung below her knees, and the wind made it flutter like a silent, billowing flag. Her legs were exposed to the chill as her loose blonde curls blew around her shoulders. A robe lay forgotten around her feet. From the sliding glass door, Hermione could see it all. Narcissa's face was slack. Blank. Skin so pale it was almost translucent. Her eyes were milky, completely iced over. She was not present in her own mind. Thanks to early morning research and notes from her and Theo's discussion with Charles regarding possible reasons behind Narcissa's irregular readings, Hermione had been awake when the charmed parchment monitoring her patient's vitals started tremblingÑa sign of trouble. She had been through the Floo in seconds. The sight that had greeted Hermione was not one she expected. Malfoy. He stood facing the glass door, watching his mother with his hands clasped behind his back. Tall and imposing, the only thing that was off about him was something she didn't see until she approached: A bruise was forming on his cheek and he had a black eye. But Hermione didn't spare Malfoy another glance, already retrieving her wand and a potion to sedate her patient from her charmed bag. "How long has she been out there?" "I have no idea. I found her like that when I returned home." Ah, from his overnight work in Wales. She doubted he'd slept. Hermione dug a little deeper, her arm fully submerged in the bag as she rifled around for the last thing she needed. "And how long ago was that?" "Thirty minutes." "Did you tryÑ" "Granger, the state of my face should tell you exactly what I have and have not done." There was an edge to his tone that didn't land easy on her ears. Malfoy's bruised face gave nothing away about how he felt. He took a step back. "This isn't the first time this has happened. I'll leave you to do your job." When he turned, one hand was still behind his back while the other Ñhis rightÑwent to his shoulder, gripping as if trying to massage the tension away. Was he hurt? To his retreating form, she said, "I understand you don't want to be involved, but it's not just my job. It's her life and she's your mother. It would be helpful to know your side of her disease." Malfoy didn't stop, didn't react, vanishing from sight through the paned double doors of the study next to the staircase. The drapes went down and then he was gone, leaving Hermione to contend with Narcissa. Hermione sighed to the empty room, braced herself, and walked out. The predawn air was crisp; the breeze was cooler than she had anticipated, making her feel slightly brittle. In contrast, the grass beneath her feet was soft once she stepped off the cobblestone in her slow approach. There hadn't been many incidents, but enough for Hermione to learn how to handle Narcissa better. She knew to remain calm and keep her responses brief, no sudden movements, knew not to bend to pick up the robe, but use a spellÑwhich she did. Hermione was about to cast a warming charm on the robeÑNarcissa had to be ice coldÑ when the woman turned abruptly. Physically, she was unharmed, but her eyes were still vacant. Lost. Haunted. Her lips were trembling. Not from a fear response, but because she was whispering something under her breath. Hermione couldn't hear. Narcissa blinked a few times, which made Hermione think she was coming out of the episode, but no. She looked far too happy to see Hermione. The smile on Narcissa's face was slow, familiar. Fond. "Meda." Hermione's breath caught. Every shred of logic and research in her head told her that she looked nothing like Andromeda, but that was who Narcissa saw. A ghost from her past and a shade from her present. An image. A mirage. She knew what to do, what she needed to say, but the urge to correct Narcissa was strong. Hermione took a deep breath and went on a trip with her. Back to a time when her life was simpler, her mind was whole, and her sister was at her side. "Cissa," Hermione said affectionately, trying to mimic Andromeda's speech pattern as best as she could. "Are you cold?" She touched her bare arm and realised that no, she wasn't cold. The witch was impossibly warm. A charm. Instinctively, she looked over her shoulder, almost expecting a second presence. There was none. "It's beautiful out." Narcissa lifted her eyes to the sky, her tone light in a way Hermione had never heard from her before. "I think I'll stay. Just a little longer." "It'll be morning soon. You should come inside." Narcissa lowered her head slowly. There was a look that was both inviting and tinged with sadness. She touched Hermione's face with a tenderness that left her incapable of moving, staring into her eyes and stepping closer. "I know you aren't real." Narcissa's voice trembled. "I know you're a hallucination. Like the others." Like the others. The words sent a chill rippling up Hermione's spine. Tears formed at the corners of her eyes. "But I'm glad it's you here now. If only to see you again." Narcissa crumpled, leaving Hermione no option but to pull her close and lower them both to their knees in the grass. Narcissa's pain was loud in the morning's silence. It tugged and squeezed, carved and moulded, applying pressure to her fragile state until she shattered. Listening to her sobs was just as distressing as the knowledge that she likely wouldn't remember this episode. Hermione stroked her hair. "It's okay, I'm here now." An ache clawed at Hermione's chest, a heaviness that kept her from being able to breathe properly. Nothing terrible, just that this memory would stick with her for days. Weeks. Months. Her stomach quivered as she pushed down the swell steadily rising in her throat. This was the human element to an ugly, cruel disease that was unjust and painful to watch. Horrible to experience first-hand. It was a cold reminder that Narcissa's entire life was changing beyond her control and there were parts of her journey she would never remember. Like crying out for her sister. Striking her son. And gods, she was the most aggravating person Hermione had ever treated, but it was her duty to be patient. To be understanding. To be kindÉ even when Narcissa wasn't. And that was sobering. Grounding. Hermione held Narcissa until she calmed, until her grip loosened, until she had enough of a grasp on her own mind to do her job. The chill sank into her skin, and her fingers trembled as she manoeuvred until she could uncork the vial. "Cissa, w-who else do you see?" It was a question Hermione was scared to know the answer to, but nonetheless had to ask. Had to know. Narcissa lifted her head, and Hermione carefully dried her eyes with a whispered spell. Lacing her voice was an emotion she hid so well during the day: terror. "Those I know are dead. The Dark Lord, he was here, just as real as youÉ" Hermione swallowed. Malfoy. Distress and confusion started to work their way across Narcissa's features; what little colour was there faded fast. She knew what was next, had experienced it once before. The combativeness. The panic. Fear. "Drink. It'll help them go away." "You're not real. Why should I trust you?" "Because I'mÉ" Hermione trailed off, scrambling for reasons. She did not want to lie, but she also needed her to comply. "Just trust me. Please." By some miracle, Narcissa did, accepting the vial with hands that shook hard, and bringing it to her lips. The sedative worked quickly, and soon Hermione levitated her back into the house and tucked her into bed. By the time she closed Narcissa's bedroom door and asked Zippy to notify her when she woke, Hermione's exhaustion was bone deep. Both physically and emotionally. Her mind was whirring with several ideas about how to effectively utilise the Palliative Care team, who were due back at the start of next week. It might have been early in her disease, but Narcissa needed monitoring around the clock, and it couldn't be done with her enchanted parchment that monitored her vitals alone. There had to be someone there, someone who could coax her back and isolate the triggers for her episodes. Help her. One for the day and one for the night. Hermione was still thinking about logistics when she returned to the kitchen and found Malfoy placing his daily note by Scorpius' seat. "I assume your attempt was a success." He didn't look up, but he sounded as tired as she felt. "It was." Hermione paused. "Do you plan to sleep at all?" She had to ask because when she thought about it, he spent his days at the Ministry and his nights in Wales canvassing with a team for a possible Death Eater hideout. Malfoy was, by definition, burning the candle at both endsÉ and it showed. He was beginning to look drawn and unnaturally pale. His posture and face told Hermione he hadn't slept in days, if not longer. "That's none of your concern." Well, he sounded just as sharp as ever. "No, I suppose it's not." Hermione went into her bag and retrieved two vials that might help him through the dayÑInvigoration Draught and Girding Potion. She frowned and retrieved a third for pain before placing them all on the end of the table. "One's for pain. The other two are for you. They aren't substitutes for actual rest, but you'll become a danger to everyone and yourself without some sort of aid." Hermione would know. She'd wound up in St Mungo's after tempting fate too many times. "I don't want your potions, Granger. Nor do I want your pity." Her fist tightened at her side before she took a deep, cleansing breath and left them on the table as a standing offer. "I don't pity you and I definitely don't envy your life. Take them or not, Malfoy, I don't care. I'm trying to help and I don't have the energy for your attitude today. Your motherÑ" "What about her?" "She's resting and IÉ" "Who did she think you were?" he asked. Hermione sighed, scrubbing a rough hand over her cheek. "Andromeda, but I doubt she'll remember anything." "Lucky for her." His snort was bitter, grating, derisive. Malfoy's eye was worse nowÑangry and painful, the fresh bruise had spread across his cheekbone and blended away on his right temple. The other mark on the left side of his face had been the one Malfoy had, unsuccessfully, attempted to heal. The discolouration ran from his temple to his cheek, red and swollen, standing out against his pale skin. She felt bad for him, for what had likely happened when he'd gone to help his mother, for what her disease was doing to him. Not that he would ever admit it. "I've already said that I don't want your pity, Granger. And before you deny it, I don't need Legilimency to hear it loud and clear." Hermione waded through his defences as she took step after step until there was only a table between them. "Forgive me for feeling bad for you, I'll try not to. But if you take a seat, I can heal you." "I'm fine." Pursing her lips, Hermione rubbed her arm first then rested her hands on her hips. "You're terrible at Healing Charms, Malfoy. Just like you're not fine." He remained unreadable, a stoic mask if not for the small tick of his jaw. "She told me what she saw, who she thought you were. That must have beenÑ" "I'm not having this discussion with you of all people." He abruptly left the room. Following his departure, Hermione frowned at the empty space he had just occupied. That went about as well as expected. Two of the three potions she'd left were gone. Hermione hadn't seen him take either. The one that remained was for pain. Hermione went about making herself tea and breakfast in preparation to stay until Narcissa woke up. As she sat at the island, flipping through a recipe book for lunch ideas, she snuck glances at Zippy (as she had done every morning) while he crafted a fine breakfast for Scorpius. "Why do you make Scorpius such elaborate meals?" "It is what Mistress wants," Zippy answered automatically. "Mistress wishes to refine his palate." That was ridiculous; the little boy never seemed interested. Zippy snapped and the prepared meal floated over to where Scorpius sat each day, charmed to keep warm. Continuing his daily routine before his return to Narcissa's room, the elf vanished with a second snap of his fingersÑa bit flummoxed when she thanked him. Hermione wasn't alone for long. Malfoy appeared in the doorway while she sat her clean teacup back in the cupboard. Still bruised, he seemed calm and composed in that way of his. He had obviously taken the potions. His colour had returned, eyes brightened, posture straightened; he'd even had a shower and changed clothes. The only reason Hermione had been able to tell was because of the difference in the style, material, and cut of his trousers. And his hair wasn't completely dry. He'd abandoned his jacket for a black leather wand holster that he'd strapped to his right shoulder. The best position for a quick draw. Perhaps that was also the reason his glasses were tucked in the front of his shirt. He didn't put them on until he dropped a folder on the end of the island. "You left these." There was no telling what was inside. Hermione had research spread between two houses. "My mother's potions ingredients." Something she had misplaced two days prior. Malfoy opened the folder, and as she approached, she caught sight of his untidy scrawl. He'd made notes. Lots of them. In handwriting she could hardly read. Hermione glanced up at the impassive man, but he didn't move, so she took a small step out of his space. There were notes on her morning and afternoon potions. Suggestions, maybe? On her evening potions, Malfoy had circled two ingredients (knotgrass and dandelion root), underlined two more (Goat's horn and hopsÑmultiple times), and made some illegible comments beneath that. Hermione tilted her head to try and decipher the scrawl but came up with nothing. "Who created the potion?" His question made her blink twice. "I thought you didn't care to be involved." Barely concealed irritation appeared in a flash. "And I thought that, as my mother's Healer, you would be astute enough to know when something was wrong." "Oh, I knew something was wrong. I've known for weeks. The only reason you don't is because you don't care to know. Simple asÑ" "Your evening potion doesn't work." Hermione inhaled, readying her response when she paused. "Excuse me?" Single-minded, she brought the parchment to her face, squinting at his notes. Merlin, was that an A or a triangle? Or a D? "Has anyone ever told you that your handwriting is utter rubbish? Not that I understand your notes, but what makes you thinkÑ" The words died when she felt him at her arm, looming over her shoulder like a shadow. Malfoy pointed to the two ingredients he'd circled. "How did you make the decision to use this amount of knotgrass and dandelion root?" "Snowdrop would be too harsh on her stomach, and these two were recommended as replacements without diminishing efficacy." "Who told you that?" "I confirmed it with several Potions MastersÑ" "That's lazy, unlike you and, frankly, it annoys the hell out of me that I have to break it down this much for someone who is supposedly so bright." The final word was spat like it was vile. Hermione straightened her spine and set her shoulders, warm from being flustered and irritated and unanchored all at onceÑa state of being that, around him, was beginning to feel normal. She despised it so much that the question of why he bothered her had been locked away in a box, within a larger box, inside a metal cage, and shoved behind a spelled door inside her mind. She turned and found herself toe-to-toe with him. His solid chest was eye level, so she raised her head, looking at his strong jawline, his eyes behind the frames of his glassesÑwhen had he put those on? "As far as your mother's condition is concerned, I am limited to what I know, what I've read during research, and what I've been told. This is outside of all three. Obviously, a gap exists that I didn't know about. You can't blame me, but you can lose the damn attitude, Malfoy, and inform me so I can help her." "You want me to do your job for you?" "No, I want your help and cooperation. If you've figured something out, and it sounds like you have, either speak up or get out." "Your experts are idiots." The worst part wasn't Malfoy in her personal space criticising her work ethic, it was the part of her brain that latched on and catalogued the inconsistencies. His contrasts. She recognised the vague scent of mint, cedar, and something clean coming from him. She wished it smelled as horrible as he was acting. "My experts are at the top of their field for a reasonÑ" Hermione remembered who she was defending herself against and recoiled. "Actually, I'm confused as to why I'm explaining this to you. You've expressed time and time again that you don't care about any part of your mother's treatment." "I don't." Malfoy dusted the invisible lint off his shirt. From this angle the bruises on his face looked worse. There was a rough edge to his voice that made the hairs on Hermione's neck stand up. "I just thought you should know, Granger, that while the ingredients are not technically incorrect, the potion is rendered ineffective by my mother's allergy to Goat's Horn." What? "I'm sorry, what allergy?" Narcissa had none listed in her file. The fact that there wasÑwell, there was the anger rising in her that stemmed from the blatant disregard Narcissa had for her own health to keep something as petty as an allergy a secret. Real damage could have been done. Second, that could have been the key to everything: her irregular results and how she seemed to sharply decline in the evening. None of the potions were effective unless all three were being taken accordingly. Shite. A month's worth of work never happened, just like that. Hermione's mood further soured. She shut the folder. "What allergy?" Malfoy's expression shifted to something between a glower and a smirk, his eyes still hard. If possible, he stood taller. "I'm not surprised she didn't tell you. She scarcely remembers it herself, but it's not lethal. Goat's Horn has magical properties that don't work on her, which neutralises your evening potionsÉ and, from what I've read, likely all the others as well." There was a hint of something lurking underneath each word, each breathÑa sense of pride in his knowledge. Pride in knowing something no one else did. And how did she know that? Well, she recognised it in herself. "When did you figure this out?" "Last night before my Portkey to Wales. I found your ingredients list the day before and took a look. It wasn't hard to figure out the problem." "Because she's your mother and you know these things." "No, because I consult myself and my own knowledge when I want to figure something out. Not so-called experts," Malfoy snapped. "There are several alternatives that would serve as a replacement, but based on the other ingredients, you should add more arka and dandelion root. I've included the amounts on the parchment. Furthermore, you don't need hops in there at all. It's useless, not the binding agent you and your experts think it is. Might I suggest something common like shellac." Hermione was speechless. Malfoy had obviously put more thought into it than he would ever confess. Still, it was the most she'd heard him speak about anything, especially concerning his mother's treatment. A step forward. Change? He was open, however accidental that had been. Hermione forced back that giddy part of her that wanted to ask him a million things now that he appeared to be in a talkative mood. "How did you find out about her allergy?" "The ingredient was in a vanity potion of hers tÑ" Malfoy remembered himself, their proximity, and who he was speaking to, all but reaching out and snatching back the words he had spoken. One step forward, two steps back. Malfoy fixed his tie and ran a quick hand down the front of his shirt before taking another step back. Hermione allowed herself to follow each step as he closed himself up. "What does it matter how I know, Granger? Now you do, too. You have your parchment and your solution. Brew the potion with those suggestions and it should work." "Fine." She waited for him to say something else, but he didn't, long gone back into his fortress with high walls. "Thank you for your help, however reluctant." "I'm just trying not to get punched again." Hermione frowned. "How many times has something like that happened?" "Enough." Well, that was that. "Additionally, I found your potions book and left it upstairs in your designated area." "I'll need it to brew, along with the parchment, of course." His eyebrow lifted above his glasses. "You brew with books?" "Yes." "Because you're unfamiliar with the potion?" Hermione folded her arms. "I brew with books, and even parchment, no matter how many times I've created the potion. It's necessary for error-proof potions making." "Why?" "Why what?" "Why books or parchment? Why do you need directions when you already know what you're doingÑespecially when you've made a particular potion before?" "Why does it matter?" she asked defensively. "The potion is brewed correctly." He brought his hand to his chin and made a small hmm noise before pulling his wand from his holster and summoning a single vial of his mother's potions. Her afternoon dose. Malfoy caught it effortlessly while re-holstering his wand. Torn between watching his visual inspection of her work and just watching him, Hermione settled into some twisted hybrid of the two that had her watching every motion of Malfoy's hands, every movement of his eyes. She took the same breath he did when he uncorked the potion and inhaled a small whiff. Hermione couldn't look away. "Each week, I've looked at the potions you leave for my mother. Admittedly, your potions appear correct and the quality is quite good, given the lack of imagination." That made her bristle. "They areÉ better than some Apothecaries. Although, it could be better." She didn't take any criticism well, but his burned. "How could they possibly be better?" "If you experimented, they could be, but you obviously don't." An echo of the boy he'd once been coloured the deep timbre of his voice. "Something I find very strange." "And why's that?" "I remember you differently." Just like that, the flames of her anger extinguished. Hermione blinked in naked confusion. His step forward was as confident as her step back was defensive. "You've always annoyingly had the right answers. The Room of Requirement. Protean coins. Umbridge. The dragon at Gringotts. Your house-elf agenda. I'm certain there's more that you, Potter, and Weasley have managed to hide from the world, but as it stands right now, people will follow, should you ever choose to lead." "I have no interest in that." "So I recall." Malfoy's scrutiny was as heavy as a lead weight. "Not only have you changed careers, you also don't experiment. Not so daring anymore, are you?" Her fingers curled into a fist. At her lack of response, he probed harder, unreadable eyes searching for what seemed like years in the span of seconds. It wasn't the first time she'd heard those words, but coming from Malfoy, coupled with her exhaustion from Narcissa, they made her wilt. She no longer wanted to engage. Hermione kept her calm as she picked up her folder. "I'm going to check on your mother." She passed him on her way out, resolved to wait in Narcissa's sitting area until sheÑ "Interesting," he said, his voice loud in the silence. "For the last month, this is all I had to say to shut you up." He was trying to get a rise out of her. She wouldn't take the bait. "I'm as tired as you pretend not to be." Hermione turned around, using a last flare of energy to make her final point. Malfoy's arrogance had diminished into a grimace. "Don't underestimate me, Malfoy. I'm still quite daring, but I don't expend energy to experiment unless I absolutely have to. Until I have a reason. And right now, I don't. Also, you speak of who I was when the only reason I did any of it was because it was right. I did it all to help Harry. My job is done in that aspect." "Perhaps, but you're a Healer now. I should think that improving the potions you provide your patients warrants experimentation." "The potions work. Or they would have. Your mother's undisclosed allergy is the cause of all this, but that doesn't negate anything else. When that's fixed, they will work. Why would I try to fix something that isn't broken?" "Just because something isn't broken doesn't mean you've achieved optimal results. How would you even know?" Malfoy challenged. "You haven't experimented enough to ascertain if something is broken or not. I believe knowledge is about the pursuit of truth, rather than convenient applications. There is always room for improvement." "That can be said about people as well." His flinch was dramatic, highlighted by the bruises on his face, but he recovered. "Ah, yes. People. You think you know us all so well, don't you?" "One could argue with your assessment of my character that you're the same way." Malfoy scoffed. "Don't waste my time with the 'we're so alike' bullshit. We're not." "I never said we're alike. IÑ" "For the last month, I've listened to your rhetoric, your opinions and your deep-seated wish to make the world a better place one person, one interaction at a time. It's all idealistic bullshit, but I'll bite. On the original subject of experimentation, how can you strive for a better world when you won't experiment? When you won't let yourself try something new? The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over again, but expecting different results." His words lit a fire inside of Hermione, a call to arms to defend herself. All thoughts of abandoning the conversation vanished like smoke in the breeze. "First, I don't expect different results. I expect the correct ones. Second, it's not all bullshitÑ" "People aren't wired to care about anyone except themselves, their inner circle of family and friends, and anyone that serves a purpose to them. Humans are inherently self-centred and greedy. Every single thing we do, we do to serve the interest of our own." She took a step towards him. "People are not inherently anything but human. However, your pessimism doesn't surprise me." "I'm a realist, Granger, and you may say all the right things, but you're no different than anyone. Your work appears to be altruism, when you're driven by your desire to feel good about yourself, to look good in the eyes of others, and to remain consistent with your principles. Your selfishness may take a different form, but in the end, you're just like everyone else." "You have no idea who I am. Or the work I do." "I know enough about your work. Not about anything else, but your 'make a larger impact on a smaller scale' view is flawed by your behaviour," Malfoy argued. "In order to incite the change you talk about, you have to be willing to make alterations and modifications to existing solutions. You have to keep pushing. You can't be as complacent as you are, dependent on your existing knowledge." Hermione shifted her weight. "You quoted Einstein before when you saidÑ" "Your bias is showing." Malfoy looked more disappointed than annoyed. "No, it's not." She huffed, not in anger, but because Hermione found herself flustered and it aggravated the hell out of her. "As I was saying, if you want to quote Einstein, he also said that problems cannot be solved with the same mindset that created them. You think my beliefs are bullshit, but you haven't expressed your own opinion on the matter." "You don't care to hear my views." "I wouldn't have said anything if I didn't." She approached him like one would a wild cat, but Malfoy didn't back away. Not even when she stood right in front of him, close enough to touch. "It's so easy to criticise when you do nothing." "So we should all be like you, then? Solving the world's problems one at a time?" With a curt frown, he folded his arms, and for a second, Hermione's eyes drifted to the sleeve of his shirt, remembering the splashes of colour her curiosity wouldn't let her forget. "I'm not surprised you've misinterpreted my statement," she said. "We cannot solve the world's problems. I've never once put that responsibility on my shoulders. I'm only one person. As are you. Change always ripples out from something small. Furthermore, I believe it's our duty as humans to leave this world better than we found it, however we can, and that's what I intend to do. In my own way. Starting here." Hermione lifted on the tips of her toes and whispered a Healing Charm. Malfoy braced one hand on the granite and inhaled sharply, poised to argue, but his words were dead on arrival. Her fingers hovered over his healing skin as she murmured another spell, but the weight of his gaze remained heavy. Piercing. Hard to ignore. Hard to decipher. Tension bled from him as the magic took hold. She watched as the pain he'd never confess to feeling began to recede. Seconds passed, but felt far longer, before his bruises completely faded, healed, and vanished into nothing except flawless skin. "That is how I am wired," she said in a near whisper as she lowered herself back until the soles of her shoes touched the wooden floor again. His eyes followed her action. Followed her. They exhaled simultaneously. Hermione felt untethered, but not disoriented enough to stop her from making her point. "I'm not entirely altruistic, but I am wired to care about people. I'm wired to help anyone and everyone I can, even you. And for someone who doesn't give a damn, you've obviously put a lot of thought into my character." That woke him from his trance. "Just as I assume that you are trying to determine mine. Have you got your answers yet?" "No." He was so cagey, so defensive; it would take a thousand conversations to understand him and more energy than she had time to expend. Malfoy gave her an odd look before checking his watch. Hermione glanced at the clock behind him. It was just past seven. Time for him to leave. Malfoy didn't say goodbye, he never did, he simply sidestepped her on his way out. The only difference today was the fact that he repeatedly ran a rough hand through his hair. He was off to continue his schedule, working a job he never got paid for, while she remained in his home to do hersÑall the while noticing the little things that weren't her business. The flames of her curiosity continued to flicker. One in particular? "Scorpius looks for you every morning without fail." Malfoy paused just inside the arch of the doorway. She could see the wave of tension in the rise and fall of his shoulders, the flex of his hands, the rigid line of his posture. His audible breath. "I justÉ thought you should know." Hermione considered being there when Narcissa woke, but she wasn't. She needed the rest. As did the staff. After deciphering Malfoy's handwriting, researching alternatives all morning in her office, and checking everything over with her experts firstÑwho were impressed by Malfoy's suggestionsÑHermione reached out to Neville to see if he had the herbs in his greenhouse. He did, thankfully, already dried. Perfect. From there, Hermione fed the chickens table scraps and took her frustration out on the weeds in her herb garden. She watered everything in the greenhouse and made notes on how the arka plant was growing for Neville. It had grown. Not much, but just enough. With mostly everything done, Hermione treated herself to an early lunch and a book just before eleven. But after finishing her sandwich and two chapters, that mounting frustration returned, distracting her until she was reading pages twice. Which was how she found herself in her brewing room with a trusty book on the stand, making a batch of the evening potion for Narcissa. Pretty soon, everything was chopped, diced, minced, sorted, and added into the bubbling cauldron. The flames were low, just like they should be. Everything was just as Malfoy had directed in the horrid handwriting she was beginning to decode. It took Hermione a little longer than usual to focus. A little longer to settle. More effort to clear her mind. Brewing was as difficult as it could be calming, but today Hermione was undeniably agitated. It had something to do with the earworm that stuck with her from her conversation with Malfoy. It wasn't that she couldn't brew without booksÑshe couldÑbut there was comfort in the action, in the routine. There was familiarity in habit. Hermione wasn't passionate about potions or cooking. Despite having a room dedicated to each, she cooked and brewed potions mainly for others. But the joy she got in it wasn't just the fact that she could help them, it had to do with the residual part of the child in her that loved the act of following directions. Hermione liked the order in it, the stability; she liked the process of making something that, in truth, didn't require a lot of talent to produce. And the bit about not experimenting? There was no need for any other project. Everything had worked as it should through an extensive amount of research and the consultation of experts. It made no sense to change something that had been proven to work. No need to change the written word. That went for Narcissa's case, as well. The adjustment to her evening potion had taken little effortÑjust a tweak. Right now, the brew looked as he'd described on the corners of the parchment. What did he want her to do? Adjust the entire thing? That made no sense. Banishing the thought to the corner of her mind, Hermione inhaled and exhaled before allowing his words to roll off her shoulders into a heap around her feet. Then, she did things her way. The proper amount of time passed before the potion exuded its faint purple smoke, indicating completion. And after bottling it into seven vials, a glance at the wall made her frown. It was nearly one. Narcissa should be up soon. And they needed to talk. That mood followed her back into Malfoy's residence, where she stocked the new evening potions and disposed of the old ones. When Hermione went to check on Narcissa, Zippy was stationed outside her door. Watchful. "Has she woken up?" "Mistress continues to sleep peacefully." "Please get me when she wakes. And if she isn't up in an hour, please come get me anyway. I'll be here." "Yes, Miss." With a friendly nod at the house-elf, who seemed eager to follow her command, she left the way she'd entered, walking through Narcissa's private sitting area. The room was decorated in her ornate traditional styleÑthe only part of the house embellished in such a manner, a symbol of the room being strictly hers. Like Malfoy's office. Hermione had options to pass the time. There was a smaller study upstairs, right next to where Scorpius' tutor conducted lessons, which had been cleared after that disastrous first day. Hermione was headed there when she spotted something strange in the living room. Someone who had drifted off-course from his schedule. Scorpius. Standing by the glass door, with his back to her as he stared out into the empty garden, he pressed one hand on the glass that was sure to leave a smudge for Zippy to clean. It was the loneliest sight Hermione had ever seen. She went on the hunt for his nanny, finding Catherine in the library where his tutoring sessions took place with the tutor himself. She was helping him tune a stubborn piano for music lessons. Hermione asked if either of them had noticed that their pupil had left. "It's fine. I'll find him when we're ready to get started." It sounded like something she had done before, something she had great experience with. "Oh, I know where he is." Mr Graves pressed a key on the piano. Though not musically inclined, it sounded off to Hermione's ear. "Can you keep an eye on him until we finish?" Hermione almost said something much different, but acquiesced, "Of course." Scorpius was exactly where she left him, hand still on the glass. What he was looking at or for, Hermione had no idea. She stood next to him to see if she could figure it out, but all that did was alert him to her presence. He looked up, not startled or scared. Just blank. Staring had been their default for the last month. He did it at breakfast and the occasional lunch when Narcissa would request his presence. Scorpius looked on as she asked Narcissa her battery of questionsÑsomething she tried not to do around him, but Narcissa didn't care. It was innocent, if a little unnerving, but that had changed two weeks ago when Hermione started moving his glass of juice from his right to his left so he would stop accidentally reaching for Narcissa's. After that, Scorpius had watched her differently. It was hard to explain. Whenever he entered the kitchen, he would look around for his father, then after his spell of disappointment, his eyes would fall on her. Only her. And he would watch Hermione through his grandmother's initial directions to the point where she was certain, if tested, he would never remember what she'd said. Scorpius would watch her through breakfast, but wouldn't touch his juice until she moved it from the right to left. And after a few days of that, he even started looking back at her whenever he was sent to lessons. The first time Hermione waved, he'd nearly walked into a wall. Her second instinct had been to chuckle, which was quickly overridden by her first: to make sure he was okay. But Scorpius just blushed and left. She didn't hear Narcissa's first comment about his behaviour, but she had heard her aggravated sigh. "That boy." Today was different. Scorpius stared at her for so long Hermione started a conversation. "Do you want to go outside?" It was overcast, breezy, and would rain soon, but perhaps it would hold out long enough for Scorpius to get fresh air. The nod he gave in response was as hesitant as expected. That uncertainty extended even after she opened the door, allowing a gust of wind to blow his hair askance. In fact, Hermione had to walk out first before he tentatively followed. "Feels good, yes?" Scorpius didn't agree and it wasn't more than a few wind gusts later that Hermione followed him back inside where he sat in front of the window, fixed his hair, and watched; more content to observe than experience the weather for himself. Having nothing better to do, Hermione joined him, folding her legs just like his. The rain didn't stay away. Darkening clouds continued to roll in, and soon droplets hit the glass panes in a slow, rhythmic beat that quickened as the storm barrelled overhead. Hermione glanced at Scorpius and found him looking at her curiously. A soft smile developed where none had been before. "You really remind me of your dad. Only you don't scowl as much as he did." Scorpius perked up, scooting closer, eager to hear more. About his father. Hermione felt lightheaded. "Your dadÉ you want to know more about him?" Scorpius nodded, jittery in the way children got when they were stifling excitement. Dread rose in her chest. She rubbed the back of her neck. What could she tell Scorpius Malfoy about his father? In school, he had been a spoiled bully, a bigot, an ignorant blood purist who was intolerant and manipulative and believed himself better than everyone else. Malfoy had been his father's son. But everything couldn't be blamed on his parents. He'd made bad choices, done awful things, andÑKingsley's words came roaring back. While Draco Malfoy had been all of those terrible things, she didn't have the right to judge him. Not at face value, at least. Not at all, really. Not now. He was trying to atone in his own way. Though vastly different, Hermione had made choices for which she sought a similar version of atonement with her parents. Being on the winning side of the war hadn't justified every action, just like him being on the losing side didn't deem him eternally a villain incapable of change. It made them both human. Capable of both great and terrible things. It wasn't her place to determine what he deserved, but at that moment, it wasn't about Malfoy. It was about Scorpius. Still, it definitely wasn't ideal to give him a true account of the person his father had been, even as she sought answers to determine who his father was now. The pieces she had of Draco Malfoy's life made little senseÑthe notes he left contradicted the distance he kept, the time he'd invested to figuring out the problem with his mother's potions contradicted his overall apathy towards her diseaseÑbut something Hermione did know was that he had changed. And Scorpius deserved to know this version of his father. She knew it wasn't her place to tell him anything, but the open curiosity on his adorable face made Hermione try to find something she could say. "Your dad and IÉ we weren't friends. I don't know him that well, but what I do know is that he's smart and is good at fixing things." The more she spoke, the easier it got. "He was good at flying. He played QuidditchÑ" Scorpius tilted his head like a confused baby owl. "Ah, you don't know Quidditch. Neither do I, butÑ" Maybe one day Malfoy would teach him. "Your dad was great at potions. Still is, it seems." None of it was a lie, even if the truth was far more complex than the washed down version she gave. Scorpius hung on her every word, cheeks flushed pink as if he were holding his breath. He wanted to know him. The sight of his curiosity made Hermione's heart squeeze in her chest. She found herself grappling through the files in her mind just so she could help him. "H-he likes reading the paper and crosswords puzzles. He swims. He sets your place at the table every day and puts your note there himself." That made him freeze before producing not just one note, but a small handful, from the pocket of his trousers, dropping some like most kids did when they tried to grab too many things at once. What spilled from his pockets represented days and days of notes that Scorpius kept close as he tried to decipher his father from words too illegible for him to read. There was a sad irony that Hermione couldn't ignore. Couldn't help but feel the ache in her head and heart. What she'd told him wasn't much, but from the way his attention went from her to the notes, maybe it was enough. For now. After looking at each note and trying to decipher it, Scorpius returned them to his pocket that was clearly charmed to hold the sheer volume of things he kept there. He then went back to watching the storm, getting up and standing in front of the glass, just as she had found him before. He seemed contemplative and stoic in a way that made him look older than five. As though life had dealt him a bad handÑmaybe quite a fewÑbut he was bearing it. Even the way Scorpius held himself, like his father with both hands behind his back, made her feel both amused and sad. It was a strange mix. She could see that beneath it all, everything about him spoke of anguish. And melancholy. A roll of thunder and flash of lightning came and went, but he didn't flinch. His focus was on the raindrops that slid down the glass, distorting the world outside. He began randomly tracing the trail with a small finger. As she watched him, a question was called from the recesses of her mind. "Are you okay?" She felt the bottom drop out of her heart when Scorpius tensed and fell apart before her eyes. He flinched as if her words had physically struck him, the hand still behind him curling into a small fist. The action tugged hard on every string of Hermione's heart. She heard Scorpius take a sharp breath before he rested his head on the cool glass then took a step back, wrapped his small hands around his stomach, and curled in on himself as though he needed protection and the only place he could find it wasÉ in himself. Hermione moved on instinct rather than logic, placing a gentle, encouraging hand on his shoulder. He sidestepped. His message was clear. Don't touch. Keep away. And she listened, but remained close, helpless, hating that she'd unearthed his pain. Scorpius' cheeks reddened as he turned his back to her completely, taking ragged breath after breath as if he were struggling for air. Trying to keep something inside that desperately wanted out. "It'sÉ it's okay to not be okay." Scorpius didn't make noise, his hurt remained silent, but his pain? That was loud, vivid, and honest. It shook Hermione to the core. He stared at the ceiling as he struggled on, fighting it, breathing so loud it was deafening. Like the storm outside, the one in front of her was a force of nature all its own. Scorpius was all Hermione could hear. His devastation was all she could feel. But slowly, he began to realise that he wasn't alone. She was there. And he seemed to retreat further. Deeper. Fixing his face brick by brick. Getting his breathing under control. Scorpius did everything except cry. And she hated that he had been taught to control himself to that extent. Hermione crawled to him, putting herself back in his line of sightÑface to face. She had no idea what to say or what to do. But she knew she had to do something before he closed himself up again. Hermione didn't touch him, but she tried her best and offered some comfort. A word. The only one she could muster. "Scorpius." Whatever progress he'd made crumpled with the quivering of his lip. The forming tears in his eyes were scrubbed away too hard with small hands he then used to cover his face. Scorpius staggered back. "Can I help?" Gods, her hands were shaking so bad with her overwhelming urge to help him, to reach for him, to give a hurting child the comfort he so desperately needed. But the look he gave her haunted Hermione long after he calmed himself down enough to leave. A look that said one thing. No. She couldn't help. He had been quiet for too long. Ginny eyed the pie Hermione offered with the same suspicion she reserved for James whenever he tried to blame Albus and Lily for something they clearly hadn't done. Hermione held her breath until she accepted the offer. Who would turn down a pie? Blueberry, especially. It was Lily's favourite. But acceptance didn't smooth Ginny's furrowed brows or remove that pinched expression from her faceÑthe look that meant Hermione wasn't sure if Ginny was going to hold on for a meaningful lecture or let it go until next time. In truth, she didn't know which would be worse. "You only bake dessert under duress or for someone's birthday." Ginny looked at the second bag. "It's no one's birthday and you've baked two pies. What the hell happened to you today?" Actually, she'd made three. "I made that for Daphne. She loves pie." Hermione cleared her throat. "Where are the kids?" "Nice diversion attempt. I'll allow it. James is upstairs finishing his homework." She stopped and yelled for him to come down because Hermione was there. She could hear immediate movement. "Lily's with my parents, and Albus is out with Harry. He needed a break." Ginny sighed and joined her at the table. "School was rough today. He came home in tears and he's still eating lunch alone." Hermione hated to hear that. Al was so kind and generous, but he never knew what to say to the other children. He'd get so excited that he'd just freeze, much like stage fright. The children avoided him, called him names, andÑwell, he needed a friend who understood him. "I know it's not my weekend, but if youÑ" "Oh no, we couldn't." Ginny waved her off. "Harry took him to dinner and they're going to the Planetarium." Albus loved the stars. "I wouldn't mind." Thoughts of another little boy's pain and loneliness swelled and swirled in her memory, then receded, before coming back harderÑlike the tide. With it, Hermione felt the first signs of the emotional blowback from the afternoon rise in her chest. She felt tightness behind her eyes that matched the one in her chest and blinked furiously to prevent any tears from falling. Her thumbnail dug into her handÑhard. "It'd be nice to see him." Ginny leaned forward, placing her hand over Hermione's locked fingers, trying to catch her eye. Hermione looked past her friend to where another redhead entered the room. "Hi James!" she greeted him with a bright smile that quickly hollowed out. At seven, James wasn't a hugger; he never had been. He was more inclined to run or complain his way through one, but there must have been something on Hermione's face that made him approach with a tentativeness she had never seen from him before. Something that made him pause at her sideÉ Then wrap his arms around her. The hug didn't last long, just a few seconds, but it helped. James hopped over to Ginny's side with a wide smile. Too wide. Like a Cheshire cat. "MuuuuumÉ" Ginny gave him a long look. "Did you finish your assignments?" "Yes!" "Go get your shoes on and I'll take you over to the BurrÑ" With a whoop, he ran back out, and seconds later, they both heard him stomping around upstairs. Ginny chuckled to herself and Hermione couldn't help but join in. "George is doing fireworks at the Burrow. Angelina's in town. You should come. Take your mind off whatever it is that's troubling you." "It'll just be back tomorrow." "You never did say what happened today." "Not what." Hermione took a deep breath. "Who." Ginny's eyebrow lifted. "A Malfoy?" "Yes, but not the one you probably think. The smallest one isÉ" Hermione was at a loss for words. "Ginny." "Oh." Whatever her friend knew about the Malfoys' situation seemed to settle in. "OhÉ how long has it been? Six months?" Ginny winced, knowing too much about loss. "Not a lot of time. Not for any of them, even Malfoy." Hermione frowned. "From what I've gathered, it wasn't a marriage of love. Just duty." "Doesn't change the fact that he lost someone." The words stuck to her like a second skin, a film that no amount of scrubbing could cleanse. They settled in and made her itch. It was one thing to think of Draco Malfoy as father and son, but another to remind herself that he was in fact a widower. He'd lost someone. His attitude certainly never served as a cue; he never acted like someone who was in mourning. But that wasn't fair of her. How could anyone tell someone how to grieve? Much less someone like Draco Malfoy, whose pride and defensiveness had made him hard pressed to ask for help even in its simplest form. Even when he needed it. Especially then. After declining Ginny's invitation again, Hermione left for her second destination: Dean and Daphne's. When she showed up at their doorstep after dark, Dean took one look at her face, then at what she was holding, and stepped aside. "Daph! Hermione's here! Looks like she's brought a pie!" Daphne materialised at the top of the stairs. "Oh! What kind?" They locked eyes and knowing expressions passed. "Dean can youÑ" "Ron's invited me to the Burrow for fireworks, remember? But you said you wanted meÑ" "To work on the nursery, yes yes, but now you can go." Daphne had already started down the stairs, one hand on the railing and the other cradling her ever-growing belly. She was in her eighth month and starting to waddle, which was how she walked over to her husband, kissing him soundly before shooing him off. Dean left before she could change her mind, but not without a fond look back. "Want me to bring anything home?" "We're out of crisps. You ate them all." They all knew that was a lie, but Dean smiled. "Sure thing. Sorry, love." "The cheese ones, please." He chuckled, nodded, and left them alone in the foyer. "Smells fresh." Daphne accepted the pie with a raised eyebrow. "It is." Before she could ask, Hermione said, "Blueberry." "One of my favourites." Daphne led the way to their living room. Hermione took off her shoes and settled on the smaller of the two sofas, waiting while Daphne grabbed two forks. They ate in companionable silence. Daphne used her baby bump to balance the pie. They were halfway finished when she held the fork to her lips, and handed the pie off to Hermione. Not that she was finished, but she clearly had something to say. "As much as I love your pie, I know you didn't bake one just to come here and share it with me. You hardly like baking at all, unless it's someone's birthday or you're agitated." Daphne reached over and scooped up another bite. "You look like the latter. What happened?" When Hermione said nothing, Daphne sighed. "Obviously something happened, so don't lie." "Just a long day." "How is he?" The he Daphne was referring to was obvious. Ginny had asked her the same question, but for some reason honesty came easier now. Because she knew. Because she had experienced Scorpius' pain for herself. The father and son weren't the only ones who had lost someone. "IÉ" Hermione exhaled a rough breath. "I've spent the hours it took to prepare and bake this pie from scratch wondering how someone so small can feel so much. It'sÉ" Mind-boggling. Heart-breaking. Crippling. Long after he had left, Hermione struggled to stay within the boundaries she'd set up when she'd started working as Narcissa's Healer. Struggled to hold on to the belief, the idea, the fucking delusion that she could keep to the outside of the Malfoy family's storm. She hadn't done a good job. Hermione had stood outside the library earlier and listened to Scorpius struggle through music lessons, cringing as his nanny gently corrected him over and over until it was time for him to switch to another subject: dead languages. And he seemed to struggle through that as well, with the way Mr Graves kept directing him to focus. Hermione had only just been able to pull herself away. But her discomfort lingered, whispering the truth that she preferred to ignore because it really wasn't her place. As Malfoy liked to remind her, she had one patient, and that was Narcissa. But was it? The random question made Hermione remove herself completelyÑnot only from the room, but from the home. She'd let Zippy handle Narcissa's meals for the rest of the day, went home, pulled weeds, chased the chickens, reorganised her cupboards, and aggressively made three pies. The third one would be a gift for Scorpius' nanny. "I went over earlier, apparently right after you'd left." Daphne didn't look especially thrilled about the visit. "I didn't stay long. He was not having a good day." Of course not. Hermione bit her lip before asking, "Did he look at you?" "No." They continued eating the pie with new vigour, but Hermione could no longer taste the sweetness of the fruit or the richness of the crust. It all tasted like nothing, like dust. They stopped eating simultaneously. Hermione put the pie on the table while Daphne looked past her at the blank wall over her head. She grabbed Hermione's hand and held on without questioning any further. "I'm frustrated," Hermione confessed softly. "And I'm not certain I'll be able to remain objective." "With Narcissa?" "No." Hermione sucked in a deep breath while Daphne watched her, exhaling until she had nothing left. "With Scorpius. I'm not always clinical, you know. I do have a heart. I'm not blind, nor am I deaf to a child who is crying out for help. I have tried to keep my distance. I've watched this play out for an entire month, but I don't know how long I'll be able to ignore what's so blatantly happening in front of me. How don't they see?" "Draco is too busy to see. Too distracted trying to atone for his sins and fix everything to protect his family. Too overwhelmed by everything that's happened and everything that's coming at him incredibly fast." Which was an excuse, but also a reality. Hermione didn't know whether to empathise or criticise. So she did neither. "And Narcissa doesn't want to see." Daphne shook her head. "She's wilfully ignorant to the fact that she's turning him into Draco. Blessedly, not who he was when he was a child, but who he is now." "Apathetic? Pessimistic? Frustrating? Disconnected?" The list went on and on. "Ah yes, all of that. But moreoverÉ lonely." "How is Malfoy lonely? He has you all in whatever capacity he needs. He has Scorpius who carries around weeks worth of letters that are completely illegible to him, but does so because he's desperate to know his father." Hermione was more confused than ever. "How I see it, this is by choice: the distance he keeps and the loneliness you say he feels. He's only lonely because he chooses to be." "It's not an excuse, that's just all he knows." In solitude the lonely man is eaten up by himself, among crowds by the many. Friedrich Nietzsche Chapter End Notes A/N: Sorry for the wild ride of emotions and angst *hides* This was a complete insert chapter I'd forgotten to write, but boy was it fun to watch Draco and Hermione continue to be thrown off balance by each other. There's a lot of complexities at play with everyone, but in the center of it is Scorpius. Boy was I emotional when I wrote that scene. I had such a vivid picture in my head of it. But it's an important moment for Hermione to experience as she goes on...and the development of their relationship which is fast approaching. Art for this chapter: Scorpius Malfoy & His Window by inspectahdeck Embedded art by Jaxx-in-a-box [ Instagram / Tumblr / Ao3 ] Chapter9: Let TheWildRumpusStart! Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Nine Let The Wild Rumpus Start! May 20, 2011 Bridges. When Hermione spared a moment to consider them, she realised how strange they were. Outside of exquisite architecture and meticulous buildings, bridges were contraptions made of metal and wood, wire and thick rope, built to link places, people, and worlds kept apart by nature itself. Building one was complicatedÑimpossible without the right toolsÑbut travelling across a bridge involved an act of faith for someone like Hermione, who was terrified of heights. They posed a strange and sudden sensation of impending doom. Like flying. As Hermione sipped her mint tea with Narcissa's Palliative Healers, Sachs and Keating, she realised that bridges could be a metaphor for what she was trying to do: band all the pieces of Narcissa's life together to create a cohesive pathway of assistance, information, and progress where none had existed before. She had originally scheduled the meeting to take place at the Malfoy home, but changed her mind. Sometimes a change in scenery could shift perspectives. SheÕd need that to help create the foundation necessary to build those bridges. It was also away from Narcissa's presence and influence. The timing couldn't have been better. Fresh off holiday, their guards were down and they were relaxed. The backdrop of the world around her cottage was just as green and alive as the plants in her conservatory they'd examined upon arrival. Hermione provided tea and fresh apple pie she'd baked after another frustrating day. She forced a thin smile, politely listening to stories from their holidays. It was already going far better than their first meeting. Sachs had gone on a bucket list trip to Egypt while Keating spent time with family and welcomed a new grandchild she proudly showed pictures of. A girl named Helena. The bits she'd learned during their conversation would help in her ultimate quest to get to know them as individuals. It wasn't why Hermione had extended the invitation, of course, but she was finally understanding Theo's controlled silences. Words were powerful, but silence was just as important. As they chatted, she separated and analysed each as individuals rather than a pair. Sachs was a pale, greying brunette. She was bolder, more outspoken and confidentÑlike an extremely watered down version of Narcissa. Keating had a beautiful olive complexion that complemented her full head of grey. She was softer, more obliging and matronly. A nurturer and a follower. Both were traditional, but while she could win over Keating, Sachs would be her issue. But she could also be the key. Maybe they would both help her win over Narcissa. "Do you have any children?" Keating asked. She was clearly the sort that thought a good ice.breaker was a mildly invasive question about family. Hermione shook her head with a polite smile. "I have godchildren, and I am something like one to another, but none of my own." "Oh." Keating awkwardly patted her hand. "There's still time." It was something people said to placate a childless woman on the wrong side of thirty. What she meant was: your time is running out. Had Hermione's skin been thinner, had she not already had the same conversation with her mother, KeatingÕs words might have bothered her. But they didnÕt. Her lack of reaction caused the air around them to shift imperceptibly, awkwardness now tinged with discomfort. She needed to get things back on track. "If I'm meant to be a mother, I'll be one. If I'm not, I already have plenty of children to spoil." Both women agreed with wordless nods. It wasn't the best response, slightly too personal for her taste, but it was open enough to make her relatable. Hermione wasn't used to working with others, at least not at the level she would be working with them, but if the last month had taught her anything, it was that this was going to take a team effort. She needed their help. Their support. Their knowledge about the family. And, most importantly: their trust. Which wasn't going to come easily. "I'm glad you both had restful holidays. Have you had time to review everything I sent via owl?" The amended care plans had been thick. She'd given the delivery owls in Godric's Hollow two treats a piece to take them. They both nodded. "It was veryÉ detailed." Sachs was measured. "Do either of you have any preferences for morning or evening?Ó Hermione asked. ÒAnd do you understand your duties as they pertain to each shift?" "We've discussed it and I've agreed to take days as I've accompanied Narcissa to events in the past as an aide," Sachs spoke up. "Keating will take nights. Before you took over, we would alternate, but your manual didn't allow for such flexibility." Hermione couldn't ignore her tone. She brought her teacup to her lips and took a healthy sip of tea. Lemongrass and ginger. Just the tang she needed. "Before we go on, Sachs, do you have issues with my treatment methods, my presence, or just me ?" Sachs blinked, jarred by her brazen question and stunned into silence. Keating, seated across from her, went completely still. "I feel the key to a successful working relationship is to create one based on respect, trust, and open communication. I sincerely hope I haven't done or said anything that makes either of you feel as though you don't have a voice. Because you do. I consider myself to be approachable." Hermione made an easy gesture with her free hand. "Please, don't hesitate. Speak up." Sachs seized the opportunity. "I don't have a problem at all with anything you have laid out in your treatment package, but it seemsÉ strict. Narcissa will push back." Her treatment was no less strict than Scorpius' schedule, but that was another thing she kept to herself. The irony was astounding. "Be that as it may, it'll be your job to see that she doesn't. As she declines, she might become a different person, not the Narcissa you know. It's not an easy job going forward, so if you don't feel you can, please don't be afraid to let me know." They both, in their own words, accepted the challenge. Hermione took another sip. "Continuing on: the potions don't work unless they're taken both consistently and within a certain frame of time. She is allowed no other potions. Her condition, as well as her potions, require strict monitoring. I will also ask that you both make sure to keep notes in your notebooks. The parchment is spelled to appear on a master parchment so please monitor any fluctuations and be on the lookout for triggers to her episodes. While you trade off monitoring her days and nights, I'll be around during both, checking in, handling meals and making potions. Once we have a baseline, we can make adjustments." Sachs forked her pie. "I would have thought that after a month you would have been able to answer some of those questions yourself." Hermione sat her teacup on the saucer. "Due to an undisclosed allergy to Goat's Horn, her potions have been rendered useless until two days ago. Unfortunately, I'm still trying to create a baseline." Both looked confused. "She doesn't have any allergies." Ah, they hadn't known either. "That's not true, as I found out a few days ago." "From where?" "Her son." Keating and Sachs went from relaxed to sitting straighter, exchanging looks. The former looked flummoxed while the latter's eyebrows lifted slowly. "Draco helped?" "Reluctantly." Hermione wondered what they knew and how easy it might be to get them to talk about the Malfoys. Keating took her cues from Sachs, which made Hermione grimace. "He told me about the allergy and made the adjustments to her potion." So far, they were working well. Far from normal, but last night had been the first with zero disturbances. Hermione was as cautiously optimistic as Malfoy had been quietly cocky that morning over his black tea and daily crossword. Outside of his physical signs of exhaustion, her biggest clue to his lack of sleep had been Malfoy not reacting when she'd told him the answer to fifteen across. Vexatious. "Are they speaking again?" Keating asked. "Not particularly." Hermione bravely pushed the envelope. "You both have been working for the family for years. Do you know how long things have been like this between them?" Another look passed between the two women. "I only ask because I'm part of the team as well." Which was true. Something that was also true? The help knew everything. Keating and Sachs traded one last look before the former took the lead. "I was originally caring for Astoria, the poor dear." She paused for a moment. "Her parents spent an excessive amount of time and money trying to save her. She had just graduated from Hogwarts when I was hired, and they could barely afford my salary. When she married Draco, I was given a choice. Deciding to stay required me to move to France. I didn't think twice. I packed up my family and moved. I didn't want her to be alone and I didn't know what sort of man Draco wasÑthe rumours about him were awful." Interesting, but true. Malfoy's reputation in Wizarding London hadn't been the bestÑan understatement, despite his mother's beloved status. "And what do you think of him now?" Keating pondered her statement. "He's distant and guarded, but not unkind. They did the best they could under the circumstances." The statement was so loaded Hermione couldn't fathom interpreting it right then. ÒWas he involved?" "AstoriaÕs disease was incurable, Miss Granger, but there was a treatment that slowed it down. Not enough for her to have a normal lifespan, which wasn't good enough for her parents. They wanted her cured. Astoria spent every spare moment of her life being experimented on with dangerous magic and given harsh potions. By the time she married Draco, she was tired." Hermione couldn't fathom the pain sheÕd endured. "In a way, her marriage saved her. Draco respected her wishes for a normal life and gained more than a passing level of knowledge about her blood illness. I doubt she would have lived as long had he not made her potions himself." Hermione froze. His lack of involvement wasnÕt the norm. It was an exception. "When did you start?" Hermione asked Sachs. "Halfway into her pregnancy with Scorpius. Narcissa hired me exclusively for end of life care. No one expected her to survive childbirth. They were nearly correct about her and Scorpius." An awkward bubble swiftly rose to the surface before bursting, projecting the pure, vivid image of a man who had nearly lost everything in one dayÑin one instant. Hermione finished her tea, but it tasted like water. ÒShe was so frail after Scorpius was born but determined to be involved in raising him. Naturally, they had a Mediwitch and a nanny, but Astoria was very involved in his day-to-day care. And as he got older, she mustered the strength to teach him, despite being bedridden." Hermione had to ask. "Etiquette?" "No," Keating said. "The basics one would teach a toddler: colours, counting, letters, shapes. She hardly had the energy to take him outside, but she played with him, read to him all the time, and showed him everything she could. Her sister visited monthly, and she would take him places. Not around too many people, of course. There were a few incidentsÉ" Keating pressed her lips into a thin line. "Daphne stopped taking him out after the last one where Draco had to intervene. Instead, she tried to bring activities to him. I think that was around the time they put a telly in Astoria's quarters. Narcissa was upset." "Do they still have it?" Hermione found the mental image of Narcissa in a state about a telly appearing in her home hilarious. "I haven't seen it since the move back to London. It's probably put away along with everything else." All of Astoria's things. Keating looked wistful, like most caregivers thinking of a lost patient. Even when expected, it still hurt. "Narcissa would never allow it in the house. She only tolerated it because DracoÑwell, things were sour with them long before Scorpius was born. Partly had to do with Narcissa's treatment of her." "You're speculating." There was a hard set to Sachs' narrowed eyes. "Draco was hardly around." "He was around when he could be," Keating clarified after catching sight of Hermione's raised eyebrow. "He also helped when he could, butÉ" The woman's sigh was one of someone who had a lot to say, but didn't quite know how to phrase it. "I think he spent more time on security and warding than anything." "As he should have," Sachs said. "I still have scars on my hands from that poison." Hermione took a sharp look at Sachs' hands. The similarity between her scars and Molly's wasÉ "Narcissa's education didn't start until after Astoria reached the point of no return," Keating said. Sachs made a small noise after taking a sip of tea. "Had she allowed Narcissa to help with Scorpius earlier, she might have taken better care of herself and lived longer. But as it was, she dedicated every ounce of energy she could to raise him and shut Narcissa out until she had no choice in the matter." "Can you blame her?" Hermione wondered if she'd asked the question that had been on her mind. How embarrassing. But then she realised that no, she hadn't. It had come from Keating. There was a frown on her face and she was gripping her teacup with both hands; a complete opposite to Sachs who had nearly finished her pie. And there it was. The divide. May 21, 2011 Hermione woke up in stages. She was in no rush to start the day after a late night with Padma and Susan in the conservatory, drinking elf-made wine and chatting about the ins and outs of work at St Mungo'sÑsomething they couldn't do when everyone else was around. Hermione decided to lie there for a while and watch the sun creep across the floor towards the bed. Fortunately, she hadn't bothered to shut the drapes, and was catching a glimpse of a glorious sunrise. The promise of it pulled her from the bed and into her bath, where she showered before pulling her hair back into a purposefully messy bun. Opting for comfortable clothes, she laced up her Wellies and headed downstairs for tea. She also needed to check on Narcissa's enchanted parchment. A second night of stable readings was more than enough proof that the corrected potion was working. From the readings, it appeared she was still sleeping. Good. Today was the first day back for Keating and Sachs. Hermione made a mental reminder to visit Keating tonight and Sachs tomorrow. The phone rang. It was her mother. ÒGood morning, what are your plans for today?Ó "About to start in the garden." "Sounds lovely, dear." There was a noise in the background that sounded like her dad. "Oh, never you mind." He must have realised she was still on the phone. "Sorry, love. Your dad has opinions." Whatever that meant, Hermione knew better than to ask because she would never get an answer. Not from her dad, at least. "Anyway, I was calling to check and see if you were free for dinner Thursday. We'll be leaving for Greece in a couple of weeks and thought it would be lovely to see you before we go." Hermione blinked at the change in the schedule they had adhered to for years. A welcomed surprise that filled her with hope. "Oh! Of course." "Wonderful. See you then!" Farewells were exchanged before Hermione hung up the phone. With a pep in her step, she ventured around the conservatory, caring for the plants. By the time she started pruning the climbing roses, the sun had begun to make an appearance, brightening all corners of the room and further lifting her spirit. It was a lovely sight to behold. Both the conservatory from above and the world beyond the window. Peaceful and quiet. The morning sky was blue with streaks of orange, reds, and yellows, and cloudless for a change as the last couple of days had been grey, heavy and drizzly. Typical for the season, but today was a treat. Hermione looked around the orderly room. She'd done enough work. It was time to enjoy the view. After climbing down her ladder, she put it away and curled on her chaise by the full-length window with a fresh cup of tea and a battered copy of The Book Thief she'd been working through over the last week. She was lost in the story when she heard her Floo come to life and felt the tingling of her wards announcing the arrival of two people. After tucking her bookmark between the pages, Hermione went into the living room to find her guests patiently waiting. Well, not the smaller of the two. She had just enough time after hearing " Auntie 'Mione!" to shut the door behind her before a child-sized blur appeared wearing a Cannon jersey, jeans, and Velcro trainers. The blur named Albus Potter practically launched himself at her legs, hugging them tightly and almost knocking her off balance. "Oof!" Hermione laughed when he didn't let go. "Well, hello to you too, Al." "Hi!" Albus squeaked. Harry, meanwhile, chuckled from his spot in front of the fireplace, shaking his head as he sat his sonÕs bag on the sofa. "Hey." "It's only been a week!" She ruffled his soft but messy brown hair. "Missed me much?" "Yes!" He still held on. "He's not lying." His father crossed into the kitchen and approached them. "He woke us up at five, and was already dressed with his bag packed for the day. Quite determined." Harry gave Albus a fond look. The little boy raised his head, peering up at her with a big grin, flushed cheeks, and bright green eyes. "Sorry we're so early." "No bother at all." She looked down and smiled. "Did you eat?" Albus shook his head. She made an exaggerated face, pretending to think very deeply. "I might have more of the strawberry jam Deloris made." His eyes lit up more. "We can have eggs and toast with jam. How do you feel about bacon?" "Yes, please!" Hermione grinned. "Okay, go wash your hands and I'll let you help make breakfast." Off he ran up the stairs to the guest bath where his stool had a permanent home so he could reach the sink. They both watched him go, then Harry grinned. "He'll be gone for ten minutes, tops." "Yep." They both chuckled. "Thanks again, Hermione." "Stop thanking me, I love having Albus over. James and Lily, too." Even though all three together were chaotic at best. She had no idea how Harry and Ginny managed. Years of practice, she supposed. When she had all three, Hermione would sleep for hours after they left. "They're fun and a big help in the vegetable patch. What are you all doing today?" "Errands mostly, but we're taking the kids to the Aquarium and then to Diagon Alley this afternoon. I asked Al if he wanted to go, but when Ginny said he could come here, even though it wasn't his week, he was hell bent." Harry shrugged. "What's on your agenda?" "Weeding, but I've got to clean the chicken coop. We'll picnic in the pasture, too. Last time, he wanted me to read Where the Wild Things Are and Scaredy Squirrel, so I'll do that before we take a walk towards the forest." "Ah." Harry fixed his glasses and gave her a look as he leaned against the kitchen island. "He said he's ready this week. Let me know if he makes it, yeah? It's all he's talked about." "Will do." Hermione gave her best friend a knowing look as she folded her arms. "How's it going with Malfoy?" The question made him sigh. She had no idea if his response was good or not. "It's not going horribly, if that's what you're asking. We've started quietly training Teams D and E together. We let Hestia in enough for her to create the cover. Malfoy found a training room and warded the hell out of it. It's going well enough. Malfoy isÉ" Harry frowned. "We've scheduled a meeting with Team C on Monday." "Then why the sigh?" "Because it's Malfoy." Harry's statement was deeply relatable. "He's frustrating." "That he is." Harry was silent for a moment. "I will say he's been far more tolerable than usual. Also, he's not so horrible as a teacher. Yet for some reason, I still have the urge to hex himÑ repeatedly." "A natural reaction." Hermione patted his shoulder in mock sympathy. "There, there." Mending bridges wasn't easy. It would take time and a conscious effort from both. Whether it extended past the completion of their job of eradicating the threat of Death Eaters, Hermione had no idea. "Regardless, I'm just glad I was able to help." She tentatively touched on a subject she was curious about. "How has Malfoy been the lastÉ say, week or so?" "A bit off, but I can't tell how." He looked at her oddly. "Why?" "No reason." Harry didn't look convinced. Instead of retreating, she poked at the topic a little harder. Might as well; he was already suspicious. "He's doing the overnight canvass in Wales?" Harry's eyebrow disappeared into his hairline. "He told you that?" "Yes." HarryÕs look was oddly probing, but he was no TheoÑor even MalfoyÑso she returned his stare comfortably until he shrugged. "He volunteered to handle them. The Task Force isÉ" He sighed and scrubbed a hand over his face. "Malfoy's trying to wrangle them together before someone gets killed.
