Forever in infinite motion, they were full of chaotic fluctuations. Marked by each tick of the clock, seconds turned into minutes, which compounded into hours, constantly moving forward as they watched the stormÑfirst by the window, then on the sofa. The silence was as unsteady as the erratic beat of raindrops pelting the windows. When Hermione next looked at the clock, it was ten minutes after five. A time delicately balanced between the end of night and the beginning of a new day. It was also when Draco's hand released hers for the first time. Draco. Words of intent. A hand in hers. The startling amalgamation of moments and actions unearthed a full-formed name from the seeds of nothing. For all Hermione's denial, the digging and pulling at the pesky roots, her mental shift was seamless, in the same way the end of one chapter always sparked the beginning of the next. Natural progression. The inside of the Malfoys' home was dark and silent as the storm marched on, unabated and undiminished. Rain. Wind. Thunder. Lightning. Peaceful but unsettling. Atmospheric but stark. Exactly how Hermione felt with her back against the guest room door. Too late to sleep yet too early to begin the day. The only thing exchanged when they parted ways was a suggestion that they each shower, change, and meet in his office. To talk. Hermione arrived half an hour later with wet hair and two vials to get them through what was sure to be a long day. Draco was already there with tea and no arguments about the potion. Both of which she hadn't expected. They drank each vial before sitting on opposite ends of the sofa. Hermione felt better, more clear-headed and less tired almost instantly; her body was tricked into believing she'd had a full night's sleep. After shifting into a new position, facing him with a knee bent, she accepted the floating teacup with both hands. Black tea. Splash of milk. Simple but good. While enjoying the hot drink, Hermione observed the slight differences in his all black attire along with his wet hair. The rain battering the windows and the occasional crackle of lightning illuminating portions of his lowly lit office were the only sounds in the space. The air between them was cozy but tense. It defied description yet needed no explanation. Uniquely comfortable. Ever-present. Reclined on the cushions, Draco projected relaxation with his legs crossed and his arm resting on the armrest. Hermione silently wondered if he was restless, looking for cues to help answer the lingering question but found none. Still, he drank slowly, as if the worldÑ and by extension herÑwent unnoticed. Hermione had long since finished by the time he leaned forward to place his empty cup on the table. He broke the silence with a deep, resonant chuckle. "You look as though you're dying to speak." Draco returned to his seemingly casual position before turning, a curious quirk on his lips as he gave Hermione his undivided attention. "I'm not, actually." Hermione picked at the cuticles of her nails but she could still feel his eyes on her. She quickly dropped her hands and lifted her head. Ready to speak honestly. "I wouldn't know what to say nor do I have any idea how to begin navigatingÉ this." "You could start by moving." He rested a hand on the sofa next to him, eyes firmly on her as he patted the cushion once. Twice. It was a bold gesture, an indication that temporarily confused her until its meaning left Hermione both jarred and flushed in an aggravatingly adolescent way. Move closer. Once again, her nature pulled her in different directions. As if on a pendulum swinging between sensible and absurd, the former kept Hermione stationary, but the latter brought her out of her folded position. With a stubborn frown on her face, she slowly scooted until she was on the spot where his hand had just been. Any closer and Hermione would be sitting on him. Draco stretched an arm along the back of the sofa behind her head while the other maintained its spot on the armrest. For comfort? Hermione's new seat didn't afford her the space to dissect the reasons behind the sequence of actions; his proximity introduced an opportunity for observation that wasn't limited to sight alone. "Better?" Not at all. Draco was freshly shaven, his wet blond hair combed into place, but Hermione was drawn to subtle details. That was becoming a habit around him. Draco smelled clean, a subtle, woodsy scent that was almost heady from her place by his side. But that was nothing. Normal enough. What got her was something else. Heat. It radiated from him like a vibration. Hermione chalked it up to some sort of controlled energy that affected her as well. She ran tense hands down the front of her jeans, gripping her knees and curling her fingers into fists. It was a strange feeling. Their closeness. The storm. The solitude of his office. She felt silly for considering a sip of Calming Draught to unwind the knot in her stomach. The circumstances, time of day, and energy were different, but sitting under Draco's rigid scrutiny wasn't as new as his intent behind every action. Or was it? Hermione had realised her own attraction. She'd starved the stubborn thing and resisted giving it a name, but the feeling grew anyway. Terrible, really. Draco's attraction to her was unfathomable. Defied logic. Surprising, considering their push and pull from the very start, not to mention their history. The fact that they were now standing at the precipice of this impossible point proved the cosmos had a sense of humour. How? When? Why? What had she missed? Clearly a substantial amount, given the last few hours. The massive blow to her ego that stemmed from her lack of awareness of Draco's intent beforehand left Hermione irritated and scrambling for knowledge and answers, searching through tomes of comments and moments and looks she'd consistently tucked away to build a proper timeline. Analysing this would take all day. No, week. "What are we scheduled to work on this evening?" Draco's voice was low, edged in a fashion that churned the growing anxiety in her stomach. "I haven't looked. Why?" "We should add time to our schedules starting tonight, if you have it available." At both her sharp look and raised brow, Draco clarified. "To have conversations that don't pertain to work." "Oh. Like getting to know each other better." "Yes." "Okay." An awkward chuckle escaped Hermione before she could stop it. "But if your next suggestion is the addition of wine, I might think you're suggesting a date." "Not exactly." He drew his arm off the back of the sofa, resting his hand on his thigh and tapping his fingerÑa small quirk of his she associated with repressed unease. "I would call it a prelude of sorts." "What is the prelude to dating?" Confusion wrinkled her brow as she pressed her lips together in a thoughtful frown. "I'm hardly a delicate flower so courtship sounds Victorian and outdatedÑ" Draco went very still. As did she. "Oh. Okay, you're serious." "I am." Hermione tried to push past her initial judgment and into the foundation of his idea. From what she knew, courting involved getting to know a potential partner and deciding if they were worth pursuing. For her and Draco, it would be an opportunity to determine if their attraction and compatibility ran deeper than surface appeal. If it was sustainable. If it's not real or genuine, I don't want it. The familiar words echoed in her head. When Hermione gave it thought, itÉ Well, it wasn't entirely unreasonable. "I'm not sure exactly what your suggestion entails." She sat up straighter, slowly processing the idea. "Is there an officialÑ" "Nothing official. We will set our own rules and boundaries." Draco placed a careful hand over hers. The move was less bold and moreÉ understated. Perhaps that was why Hermione flipped her hand under his, flattened her palm, and laced their fingers together. Thunder rolled and their joined hands remained a warm weight on her thigh. The urge to distance herself from the moment surged then dulled, her muddied thoughts clearingÉ "I should review my contract with your mother to make certain I'm notÑ" "You can, if you'd like, but I've already done a thorough review." When Hermione recoiled in shock, Draco glanced down at their hands. "There is no clause in it that we're violating at this time." "When did you review it?" Draco didn't answer, looking instead at the bookshelves. Foresight was indicative of purpose and Hermione grew annoyed by the mounting clues that she was trailing behind. Slow on the uptake, she couldn't place the moment when childhood animosity and adult indifference had begun to shift in this unexpected direction for him. But Hermione didn't press, exacting a patience she didn't know existed. At least as it pertained to her pursuit of knowledge. Her mind was running rampant with what any potential answer could possibly mean for the whens and hows that kept creeping around in search of clarity. Part of her had to know, was desperate to ask, but Hermione strategically suppressed that urge. He didn't appear willing to discuss it, and she knew her questions would only lead to answers she wasn't ready to hear. "There are still questionable ethics involved in ourÑ" "Naturally." Draco's interruption was smooth, as if he'd expected the argument. "In the future, should we pursue anything further, I imagine there will need to be a different discussion. As of now, however, I trust this won't interfere with my mother's care." "Of course it won't. Nothing would change." Hermione touched her still-wet bun. "You and I will continue to work together on our current projects separate from our courtship." She really needed to think of a better term. Draco's fingers twitched in hers before he tightened his grip. "The rest we will sort through, but not right now." Fair. ButÉ As soon as Hermione tried to relax, as she had earlier in the conservatory, she winced at the new thought quickly sprouting above all the others. "What about your mother?" "What about her?" His inflection reflected his ambivalence. "Should we tell her?" It was a conversation she'd rather not have, but it was as inevitable as the spread of a wildfire. She could only imagine the one Narcissa would start if she knew. "Whether you like it or not, your mother has expectations of you that she hopes you will fulfil. She may respect me on some level, but I don't think she likes me. And I certainly don't think she considers me a worthy potenÑ" "It's not my mother's decision." Draco's tone was so firm she felt its pressure. His expression was one of perfect resolve. "I've said everything I plan to say on the matter, if you recall." "I do." She closed the door on that subject and opened the next. "What about Scorpius? Our friends?" If Hermione was being honest, she would confess her forming list of reservations, but they were still organising themselves, not yet ready for vocalisation. "I don't propose keeping secrets, but I also don't believe we should make it public knowledge unless we're absolutely certain." "No secrets." "Should someone find out, that's fine. We aren't hiding, but I'd prefer to keep this between us." Hermione absently swept her thumb in the groove between his fingers. "That goes for Scorpius as well. He is my biggest worry in all this. It hasn't been a year yet sinceÉ and I justÉ We don't know what he's thinking or how he'll feel. He's made too much progress to be set back by any major changes. I wouldn't want toÑ" "I agree." Draco's words were accompanied by a shift of their joined hands from her leg to his. "He is and always will be my primary concern in everything I do. He is also one of the reasons I'm affording you time to sort through everything you need to." "You should do the same." "I already have." Hermione blinked several timesÑrapidlyÑher brain speeding up to match the pounding in her chest. She jolted when a pop of lightning punctuated his loaded statement and a clap of thunder soon followed. But the exchange left her more rattled and unsure than the chaos beyond the walls. Needing space but unable to move to find it, she stayed still. Silent. Pensive. "Speaking of Scorpius, Skeeter's article wasn't in the morning Prophet when I glanced. I believe it will be in the evening edition for maximum effect, as it is distributed near the end of the work day. Nevertheless, I have time between meetings and wouldn't mind if you brought him to my office." It sounded casual, but the underlying weight of his statementÑof his trustÑwas there. "For lunch?" "Yes, to make up for my early departure yesterday. He appeared disappointed." Hermione smiled at the memory of the haul Scorpius had excitedly presented to his father, but it faded when she remembered how the high of his accepted offerings had ended. He had just wanted his dad to come back. Teddy had kept him distracted, but his smile hadn't quite reached his eyes the rest of the day. "He was, but he'll be thrilled to see where you work. If you don't mind, I have to stop by Neville's greenhouse beforehand and I think he'd love to see it." "He would." There was a clench along his jaw and a flash of paternal warmth Hermione associated with each time she caught Draco watching Scorpius greet his cactus. Quiet settled between them, one that stretched beyond comfort to something akin to growing intimacy. Hermione searched for a better word to describe the feeling but none fit. She tried to say something else, but her lips remained still. Slowly, she lost each prior thread of thought and worry in the warmth radiating from him. But she hung on to one. "Perhaps, Draco, since we're discussing rules, you should call me Hermione." "I could." He inclined his head as if considering it. "But I prefer Granger." She looked away with a smile, but caught the passage of time in a single glance. He wants to squash you and the attention you bring." "I don't understand why the Chief Warlock is wasting his time on a Healer instead of performing the duties of his job, but there are several things in life I can't make sense of." The next lift was nearly there. "Oh, and I didn't threaten them. You see, I'm in no official position to do so, but I did promise to hold anyone corrupt accountable." She rested a finger on her chin. "Now that I think about it, I suppose your initial statement was correct." With the arrival of the lift, the queue moved. There wasn't enough room for her and Scorpius so she waited and, unfortunately, McLaggen didn't take the last spot. In a show of faux kindness, he let the witch behind him take it. She rewarded him with a pretty smile that he held until the lift was gone. Then it vanished. And it wasn't until it was gone that he glanced over then down, one eyebrow rising. She followed his line of sight to Scorpius whoÑoh. Merlin. She'd never seen him look like that before. Brows scrunched. Mouth pursed. It was the closest impression of his father that she'd ever seen from him, but the vision was endearing when combined with his earmuffs and stained cheeks. "What's your name?" Scorpius' glare darkened as he stepped forward with a boldness that opposed how he'd greeted Neville. Children were an excellent judge of character, and this one sure as hell didn't like Cormac. "Oi there, are you her protector?" Cormac's teasing tone made Scorpius scowl. The next lift arrived and Hermione led the way. She put herself against the back wall with Scorpius in front of her, and placed her hands on his shoulders. He offered her the carnations he'd been holding for her and she accepted them. He almost smiled before he caught Cormac watching and it vanished behind a sneer. The lift was nearly empty after the first stop. Cormac wasn't the only person bemused by Scorpius' expression. A stranger broke the silence in the small space. "Aw, he's protecting his mummy." Bless his earmuffs, Scorpius didn't hear the comment. But Hermione did and she shook her head. "I'm not his mum." "Whose son is he?" Cormac's interest increased tenfold. "He's not Harry'sÑ" Blessedly, the doors to the lift opened on the right floor and Hermione guided Scorpius out towards Harry's office. Hermione needed Deloris' help to find the way to Draco's office and she wanted to get to their destination quickly should anyone else appear. Like Tiberius. She would have made it had Scorpius not slowed her down by looking over his shoulder and glowering. He appeared so distracted that Hermione turned as well. Cormac was following them. Great. No sense in hurrying, he would catch them in no less than twenty long strides. Hermione stopped and turned to face her problem head on, gently gripping Scorpius' tense shoulder. "Can I help you, Cormac?" He looked from her to the little boy at her side. "Whose kid, Hermione?" "I know you didn't follow me to ask that question. That information is none of your business." "No, I followed you to accompany you both to Harry's office, assuming that's where you're going." "I am, but no thanks. I have all the company I need." He stepped closer. "Might I remind you that you no longer workÑ" "I have a visitor's badge and a checked wand." She glared at him but kept her tone calm. "Any other excuses for stalking?" "You need a guide." "I can take it from here, thank you." A cool, perfectly even voice rose from behind them. Everyone turned and there stood Draco, looking every bit as in control as his voice sounded, but there was a purposeful edge in every step of his approach. For once, it wasn't reserved for Hermione. Only Cormac. "Malfoy," Cormac greeted once Draco stood at Hermione's side. The seconds ticked on in silence, each squaring their shoulders in a way that made them seem taller. Bigger. Draco was slightly shorter, but no one bothered to inform him of this. "I was just accompanying Hermione andÑ" Cormac looked down at Scorpius, who had stopped glaring in favour of staring at his father, before his eyes lifted back to Draco in recognition. "Is heÑ" "I said I can take it from here." There was a professional brusqueness to Draco's tone and appearance not many had perfected. Hermione paid no mind to the bit of heat she felt on her face and neck from the crispness of his words. "Hermione is going to Harry's office, so I'll accompany her there. I suppose your son isÑ" "They're both here to visit me." Cormac blinked several times. "Apologies." Draco was no longer speaking to him but to her. A smooth calmness that bordered on politeness. She was confused. They hadn't set a specific timeÑoh. Understanding dawned on her of what he was trying to accomplish. "Nothing to apologise for." Hermione angled her body towards Draco, mindful of Scorpius. "We were delayed by the fountain." "I also wasn't expecting you both this early and didn't open my Floo." "I forgot that I didn't have the option to Floo directly into your office from our first stop." She held up the small bundle of carnations as indication of where they'd been. After a single nod of acknowledgement, Draco made a polite gesture. "Shall we?" Cormac was still looking back and forth between them, his frown deepening with each pass. "Are you twoÑ" "What business do you have?" Draco's voice was chilly. "No business at all, besides my own personal interest. I will say that this is intriguing." Cormac gestured between the two of them, his frown shifting into something elseÑ something darker. "And explains a lot." "I think your presence is required elsewhere." And then Draco did something none of them expected. He picked his son up like he weighed nothing. The expression on Scorpius' face was one of shock and priceless confusion. Hermione almost laughed until she felt a hand on her lower back steering her in the direction of his office. Then his hand found hers as he led the way. Just as stunned as Scorpius, she quietly went along with it. "Is he gone?" Hermione looked over her shoulder. "Still watching." His hand stayed in hers until they rounded the corner, then it returned to his side. "Rumours will be all over the Ministry by the end of the day." "I'll handle it." A statement so firm Hermione didn't argue. She guessed Draco finally realised how far he'd abruptly deviated from his routine with Scorpius because his son was blatantly staring at him. Because they were face to face. Because he'd picked him up. While awake. For a moment, they just stared at each other. Scorpius' shock and confusion bled into something warm and kind. He dimpled a smile, and the frosty expression on Draco's face thawed slightly, then more when Scorpius smoothed the space between his father's brows, just like Hermione had done earlier. Fond amusement weaved itself into a genuine smile that made Hermione's heart swell. Oh. Then the moment ended. Draco's face evened back to his default. She was watching him, openly bearing witness to a moment she would never forget. After adjusting Scorpius on his armÑhis good one, she notedÑhe faced her. "I wasn't expecting you both this early." "The greenhouse trip didn't take very long. Are you ready for lunch?" "IÉ" Draco glanced at his hopeful son. "I can be." Draco's office was in the back and it seemed that they passed every member of the Task Force along the way. Conversations stopped abruptly. Work halted. Even a meeting behind a glass wall paused at the sight of a stern looking Draco Malfoy carrying an earmuff-wearing little boy. Everyone stared unabashedly. Hermione walked behind him, making certain to keep her flowers at her side and her expression just as impassive as his. No hint of awareness or embarrassment. Nothing to see. She did, however, see Scorpius awkwardly wave over his dad's shoulder at a small gaggle of witches from the French Task Force who waved back at him. He didn't smile, just buried his head in the crook of Draco's neck. Shy from the attention. The whispering conversations and ogling resumed as soon as they were out of earshot. Surely they had work to do. Hermione cleared her throat, and when she looked back, Draco was holding his office door open. Both father and son were regarding her with similar expressions. Wordlessly, she tucked her hair behind her ears and walked in. Draco's office was a blank slate, devoid of anything that would identify it as his. There was one lone bookshelf with a few books on each row and the larger ones crowded near the bottom. All it meant was this was a place he didn't think of as hisÑ not like his home office. The only comparison between the two spaces was his desk, which was respectfully cluttered. There was one oddity, though: a dirty potato perched on the corner. Hermione's face warmed from the token kept by a man who showed so little of himself. The door shut with an audible click and Draco's first order of business was to put his son down and try to sort a spot for lunch. Scorpius began to approach Hermione, but the vegetable called to him like a moth to a flame, and after touching it, he looked to his father for explanation. Only Draco had his back turned. "You kept it?" The question made Draco's head whip to her, then over to Scorpius, who was holding the memento. "It's spelled not to rot." Scorpius placed it back in its spot as carefully as he had the cactus not long ago. The bookshelf caught his attention next and he approached it while his father cleared away his work to give them a spot to eat. "Is there anything he can't touch?" Draco looked up from stacking parchments and set them aside. "They're regular books." Scorpius turned his head, asking Hermione a question with his eyes. "One book, but only after we eat." Hilariously, he considered the instruction before appearing to deem it fair with a nod and returning to her side. Once Draco's desk was clean, she pulled a chair around to sit next to him and unpack their lunches. Another problem presented itself. Draco's office was small, and he didn't have enough space for the extra chair they needed. Which was how Scorpius ended up sitting on his father's desk, earmuff-free, with his feet dangling off the side. He seemed pleased in a muted way that was all him. Lunch consisted of roasted chicken sandwiches for her and Draco, cheese toasties, sliced fruits, and vegetables that Scorpius went for first. Malfoy was puzzled. "I thought he liked cheese toasties?" "He's a picky eater, so he saves what he wants best for last." Hermione bit her own sandwich and lunch officially commenced. Scorpius ate slowly, happily, but halfway through, he did something unexpected. He began sharing with his father. First a sliced cucumber. Then a carrot. Then a strawberry. Scorpius ate one piece then offered the next to his father, who always accepted, but when Draco offered Scorpius a slice of his tangerine in return, the look on the boy's face changed, reminding her of a day he'd made a similar offer to Albus. Friendship. He accepted the slice and saved it for last. Even after the toasties. The best. Scorpius' first trip following the meal was to the bookshelf. He, of course, picked the largest book he could find. Hermione doubted it had pictures. When he wobbled once, the man next to her shifted in his chair, adjusting in the event he needed to make a quick grab to stop his son from falling over. Admirable, but Scorpius stubbornly remained on his feet and placed the book on the blanket Draco had transfigured for him. Before he sat to flip through the pages, Scorpius returned to her side, hands open for the earmuffs she'd taken off. He happily put them back on, finding peace in the silence. Unknowingly, he gave them the perfect opportunity to talk. "From McLaggen?" Draco stiffly gestured to the carnations he had been glancing at on and off during the meal. "Gods, no. As if I would accept such a gift from him." Hermione picked them up and noted how fresh they still were. Perhaps Luna had charmed them not to wilt. "These were picked by Scorpius at Neville's greenhouse. He gave them to me." "Oh." There was a small shift in his demeanour. Curious. "IÑdo you prefer flowers?" It wasn't just that she was visibly startled by his questionÑHermione very much wasÑher surprise was more about the way he watched her after, as was reading her for clues that might linger underneath her answer. It was easier to deflect, but defensiveness wasn't present in the small space of his office. Only honesty. "I'm no good at accepting gifts, or anything really. I can admit that." Begrudgingly, of course. "But I do believe gifts should be from the heart and have meaning." "Purpose?" His elbow was on the arm of the chair, his chin in his palm, watching her with clear interest. "Whether for brewing and consumption, caring for patients or those you know, nothing in your greenhouse or garden is there for aesthetics." "It's true." Hermione touched the flower gently. "The purpose of a flower is to attract pollinators to aid in fertilisation, but it doesn't make them any less beautiful or meaningful in their own right." She let the comment marinate as their gazes travelled to Scorpius, who seemed to be enjoying himself. Or so she hoped. He looked like he was studying for an exam, and the way he frowned, as if deeply concentrating, was both amusing and a bit strange. "The bookÑ" "Is in a dead language he can't read just yet." "Oh." At some point he would learn those things as part of his education. Hermione's opinions on that weren't as visceral now that she had seen Draco's translation efforts. Through Scorpius, she was also reminded of the importance of communication and preserving language. Languages were human creations. They were born, developed, declined, and perished. A life cycle that occurred fluidly throughout the course of history. Each person spoke in their own wayÉ Perhaps extending the lives of a few lost languages wasn't such a bad idea. With an internal shrug, Hermione stood and began packing everything back into her bag while Draco went to his desk and pulled out a note for her that she accepted once his desk was clear of any evidence of their meal. "These are the talking points I made with my mother during ourÉ discussion about her care." Argument was more apt, but Hermione wisely held her tongue. "You should review them and compare them to your own notes." "Good idea. When we talk to her together, we'll both be on the same footing." A better one. Hopefully. "Perhaps." His response was clipped, but also resigned and astonishingly placid. "When would you like to make another attempt?" "In a few days." "Very well." With nothing else to busy herself, Hermione took her seat again. Draco did the same shortly after. It was hard to tell what was on his mind; Hermione tried and failed. "It's unlikely I'll be home for dinner tonight. I imagine Skeeter's article will be out in the evening edition and it will be chaotic." Draco spoke of the impending media circus as if entirely unconcerned. "Percy and I will be meeting to discuss our strategy. Scorpius should be asleep before I return." "Is that why you asked me to bring him here?" He didn't answer, only moved to take her hand. Warm. Masculine. Easy to hold. Given this was the third time he'd done it, Hermione began to wonder about the significance of the gesture for him. Time passed as Hermione's understanding of him struggled under the weight of their recent developments. She had building questions, they stacked on top of each other, one after the next, but perhaps there was no need to push. Draco would express himself when he felt the need to. That had been his way thus far. And now wasn't the right time to push any boundaries, so Hermione embraced the silence of her surroundings, glancing at Draco as he watched Scorpius studiously flip through the pages of a book even she couldn't read. "Will you eat dinner?" "Likely with Percy." "Okay." Hermione shifted in her chair. "I'll be working in your office when you get back then. We canÉ" Talk? Court? Hermione gave an awkward shrug that earned her a raised brow. "Not sure what to call it. Spend time together?" "That sounds accurate." "How are you so rational about this?" "Your denial gave me time to think." The corner of his mouth twitched a littleÑonce, twice. The question of the hour was how much? She doubted Draco would answer, which was fine. The morning paper on his desk caught her attention. The headline sang the praises of the Ministry. Hermione sighed. The Prophet would be printing a different truth later. "Before knowing the extent of the fallout of the Prophet's article, I should really discuss my plans to approach Kingsley." "I'm certain Percy is curious. He didn't have much success." Or any at all. "I haven't exactly figured out what to say." She crossed her ankles. "Kingsley isÉ" "I'm fully aware of how he is." That wasn't what Hermione expected and she raised an eyebrow at the revelation. "Have you met him?" "Only once." Draco stared ahead. "After my trial." "What did he say?" Draco didn't speak for a long moment. "Forgiveness, repentance, and atonement are all different. If you work all three out for yourself, I have hope that the next time I see you, you will be a better man." That sounded like Kingsley. Wise words from a poignant man who saw things others couldn't see in themselves. "Have you seen him since?" "No." Source: Jaxxinthebox They made a quick stop by Hermione's home to find a vase for her carnations. Once she'd gotten Scorpius settled, she stepped outside for a short conversation with Goldstein, whose team was taking advantage of the small break in the storm and working under the watchful wands of Aurors. By the time she found Scorpius again, it felt far later than half past noon. He'd answered sleep's warm call by bundling himself up in a blanket on the chaise in the conservatory with his sock-covered feet poking out. He looked more than comfortable, too adorable to disturb. So she let him sleep. The day had already been eventful and there was still time before his afternoon lessons for him to reset. Scorpius left enough room for her to join him, and when she did, he unconsciously snuggled close, mumbling unintelligibly until she hummed and combed her fingers through his hair. In no time at all, he fell silent, breathing deeply. Comfort was found in the smallest of gestures. Hermione continued reading Persuasion until someone unexpectedly came through her Floo. Cho. Bookmarking her place and untangling from Scorpius without waking him was no easy feat. By the time she did both, her visitor was approaching with a gift bag and wearing a pleasant smile that slightly faded upon seeing the little boy. She stopped mid-step. "Oh, I didn't know youÑ" "I was just reading while he napped. He has about twenty minutes left before he needs to be back for afternoon lessons." Hermione picked up her wand and set a privacy charm, gesturing to the sofa close by. Her presence wasn't accidental. Cho never turned up without Padma or the promise of Girl's Night to de-stress from the week. After joining the other witch, Hermione took in her casual appearance, noting the display of her nerves in her curled fists and the way she looked everywhere except Hermione. "I'll confess, I'm surprised to see you here." "Sorry." Cho ran a hand through her dark hair. "Your Floo was open, so I came through thinking you were free." She picked up the bag she'd placed on the table. "This is for you." "What is it?" "A peace offering." "I didn't know we were in need of such a gesture." Hermione's acceptance of the gift was slow. "We're not in the middle of a war." Hermione folded her arms, hoping Cho would get to the point of her visit and put an end to the stilted air between them. She didn't. "Oh." Cho stared at her hands on her lap and the awkwardness continued. "I've heard a lot about him, but I've never met him." "I'm not saying this to be rude, but I'm curious about why you're here." "To apologise about what I said after brunch. In the loo." The sigh Cho exhaled was an unusual one, some strange mixture of sadness and frustration. "I've been feeling bad since it happened. I-I'm not that person, but I felt I had to say something. I realised as soon as I left that I shouldn't have said anything. I asked Padma for advice and she told me to talk to you directly. SoÉ here I am." When the silence stretched too thin, Hermione rested a hand on her arm. "Are you here to talk about it?" "Yes, but it's difficult." Cho focused on the world beyond the glass as she steeled herself. "I stand by my statement that I was trying to help you. I meant no harm." "Then why do you feel guilty?" "IÉ may have projected my own situation onto you. Cho seemed to settle into the impending conversation, crossing her legs and looking directly at her. "I don't know the extent of your relationship with Draco Malfoy. I only know what I've heard about you two getting closer. I might have misspoken, but in the event that I didn't, I-I wanted to make sure you were careful." "Why?" "He's lost his wife. The process of grieving is different for every individual. Not to mention, he has a son andÑ" "No one has the right to speak for him." "I'm not. I admit I don't know him well. I was speaking of my own experience with a man who was affected by the same loss." Emotions swelled in her voice like the rising tide, giving Hermione the opportunity to identify each one. Anger. Heartache. Defeat. Exhaustion. Feelings long suppressed sprang forth. Cho was such a practical person that Hermione had only seen her angry for the first time at brunch. Seeing the woman visibly upset startled her into moving closer, and offering silent reprieve and comfort in the form of an arm around her shoulder. Time continued as wordless tears slid down her flushed cheeks. Cho was embarrassed by her own feelings, batting the evidence away while apologising profusely. Hermione accepted none of her apologies, validating her feelings with support that grew into a hug. "I'll listen if you want to talk." "I should have known from the start it wouldn't end well." Numb stillness followed Hermione for the next set of hours, serving as a reminder that knowledge contained equal amounts of power and painÑas much a blessing as it was a curse. Thirty-seven silent minutes into her meeting with Theo, Hermione felt both damned and liberated by wisdom, but also convinced of Theo's awareness of her agitation. He'd cleared his schedule, but didn't disrupt the quiet. Perhaps he was testing her, waiting to see how long she would wait. For this, an eternity. Rather than allow herself to be consumed, Hermione sorted and arranged and compartmentalised. It was the only thing that kept her from hurling questions and demanding answers, the only activity that kept her mind calm. Serene. Exhaling a tiny breath, she continued her attempts to collect her thoughts. There were plenty. But this was a professional meeting and Hermione had brought along a stack of folders to review. Or simply stare at while replaying every word of a conversation that had lasted until Scorpius woke on his own. She had apologised for being late to return him for afternoon lessons, distracted instead by the memory of Scorpius staring at the stranger. Cho had only sobbed harder when he showed compassion in the small offer of one of Hermione's succulents. Cho. Her piece transported Hermione back to the beginning. Back to this quiet man. And now, he appeared to be less of a knotty enigma with cryptic advice and moreÉ human. Theo licked his fingertip before flipping to the next page in his book, glancing at her as if to give her an opportunity to break the silence. She opened her next set of folders, still staring blankly at the files in front of her. The move was met with an impressed eyebrow. He read on and Hermione appeared to do the same, but she might as well have been reading one of those dead languages for all she absorbed. All she could think about was the hindsight found in hints, clues, and bits of conversations. She wished she'd known. But what good would that knowledge have been? What would it have changed? Nothing. Hermione almost hated the word she'd spoken so fluidly before. "You've improved." "I'm always learning." Hermione closed her folder and began treating the conversation like a game of chess. Her first move was to let him set the tone. "I understand why you use silence as a weapon." "Or a means of defence." Theo gave her one of his knowing looks. "It depends on the situation, the person I'm speaking to, and what I intend to accomplish." "What do you intend to accomplish today?" Hermione waved her hand to pour them both a second cup of the peppermint and rosemary blend he liked so much. "A conversation, mainly, but also perhaps we can find answers to the questions I have." Funny. She had questions as well. Many. The collection of her questions grew larger, wider, deeper, but Hermione calmed her mind. Steadied herself. "I'm surprised you haven't answered your own questions." Her following chuckle was strategic and served to determine how relaxed he was. Very, judging by the steady hand holding his tea and the small smile on his face. "I have most of it figured out, true, but sometimes confirmation is key." Hermione took a sip from her teacup. "Where would you like to start?" "There is a security briefing on Monday at ten that you and the rest of the staff are required to attend." That wasn't expected. "Oh?" "Yes, there will be several topics of discussion." Theo sat straighter, sounding more official and less like a friend. "You aren't here much so this will be new information for you. The hospital has had three security breaches in the last month alone, so we've made changes to protocols as we investigate why these breaches keep occurring." "What sort of breaches?" "Unauthorised personnel attempting to gain access to restricted areas. Suspicious boxes found inside the hospital where they don't belong. Every bit of evidence leads me to believe there is a mole on the staff. As a result, we've taken to screening everyone." "Does the Ministry know? "No, but I've informed Hestia, Harry, and Draco. No one else. I've contracted the secrecy of those on important projects." The phrasing reminded her of a charmed parchment from her childhood. "Good idea." In that case, the ends justified the means. "Furthermore, we will be discussing everything we've learned about the airborne use of the poison, the proper charms to protect yourself, and distributing the new robes to protect against it should you come into contact with it." "How is the production going for the antidote?" "Top secret, but between you and I, it's progressing. With the Wizengamot attempting to assistÑ" "What's the caveat?" She already knew their involvement meant there was one. "They get a say in distribution and it will be available to all Ministry employees first." Hermione rolled her eyes at the corruption, counting down until a time when they wouldn't have to be in a resource competition with the people who were supposed to lead. "We should be fully stocked by October." "That's not soon enough, if Godric's Hollow told us anything." "I've been purchasing the antidote privately and St. Mungo's is stocked for a small-scale attack like the one at Godric's Hollow. But largerÉ" Theo shook his head. "How much have you brewed?" "Enough to cover everyone I know and myself, except Molly. She's already had the antidote so she's immune to being poisoned again." Hermione's mind kicked into overdrive. "I wonder if there's a way to take it prematurely, like a vaccine. Ideally, to test the theory you would need a sample of the poison. The powdered form should have been present at both the Ministry attack and Godric's Hollow." "You're right." Theo paused and sat his tea down. "I've been quietly having the research team attempt to replicate the poison with what was collected at both scenes. InÑ" "You're going toÑ" "I've had the idea for months. I just didn't have the poison available." Hermione sat back in her chair, letting everything sink in. "Threats are coming to the hospital, the Ministry, and any place under the Ministry's control." Theo's statement made sense, the list was long because the Ministry's reach was wide. "The full moon in August is on the thirteenth. Given what happened at your home, even though it happened outside your wards, I would err on the side of caution." "I'm still staying with the Malfoys at this time. If everything is finished by then, I'll be certain to stay home or with Pansy." Even with her party on an undetermined day, the group would likely kick-off the celebration of her actual birthday that day. "Speaking of Pansy, have you decided who you'll be going as?" "Colonel Brandon." A man with a tragic past who found love again. Hermione frowned, mouth tight. She exhaled. Now she was ready. "I need you to be honest with me." At the seriousness of her tone, Theo offered his undivided attention by sitting straighter in his chair. "It's not an easy topic to discuss, but Pansy and Ginny mentioned something that I found curious." "Oh?" "Why do you have a Pensieve?" Theo's signs of tension were subtle, but Hermione paid attention to something she might have otherwise filed away for later. The shift in his demeanour. The change in the energy between them. The slight gleam in his eyes. The levelled expression. She'd struck gold, but only because she knew where it was. "What do you know?" "One side of the story, but as your friend, I'd appreciate yours." Hermione folded her hands on the desk, catching the defensiveness she'd only seen when their topic drifted to things that mattered to him. This wasÉ personal. "I respect you enough not to make judgments before I know facts." "I appreciate that." He looked towards the window. "Cho?" "She confided in me under the guise of being worried about my relationship with Draco mirroring her relationship with you." Hermione refused to discuss the fact that she'd sobbed on her for the better part of an hour. "Does it?" Theo stared at her from across the desk. "No. As far as I know, his marriage was nothing more than a platonic partnership forged under a contract she took over after Daphne eloped with Dean." "She did it to protect her family, that's no secret." Theo stood and went to the window, looking out. Maybe it was for tactical purposes, but Hermione had seen him in a similar contemplative position in her conservatory and wondered if it was easier for him to speak when he could focus on the horizon or compare the different greys of the sky. "What did she give up?" "Nothing." "No." She didn't know why, but the word made her heart throb in pain. Hermione clenched her fists and exhaled. "I don't believe that. According to Daphne, Astoria couldn't muster feelings for Draco during their marriage. Was that because of you?" His lack of an answer was all the confirmation needed. "Does Draco know?" Before the question was even out, Hermione knew that answer, too. She felt sick. "It preceded their marriage and the war, if that's what you want to know. Not one-sided or unrequited. It was very much mutual." The quiet passion was evident in his tone as Theo told his truth. "It wasn't going to last anyway." "Why didn't you marry her beforeÑ" "Sins of the father." His chuckle was dry and filled with a type of sadness that burned. Hermione looked away, her stomach twisting with understanding of the generational nature of punishment. "The difference is that Draco and his family were exonerated. My father was not. It took years to bring my name out of the mud, not that it would have mattered. After Daphne, she was all they had left and they had already dedicated so much of their family's fortune to keeping her alive." It went unsaid, but Hermione knew. They couldn't pay the penalties of a broken contract. "Astoria knew she wouldn't live a full life. She was tired of their desperate attempts at curing her blood illness, so when Narcissa came along looking for penance, she saw an opportunity to fix all their problems at once." "Did she tell you beforehand?" "She did." Hermione couldn't imagine how that conversation went, but the hard look spoke only of pain. "Did youÉ" Hermione had to look away to ask the question. "Did you love her?" "Enough to let her go." Enough to later promise to care for Scorpius and Draco after she was gone. Hermione shook her head, feeling heavier as she put the painful pieces together to see the exact shape of duty. Love was a powerful motivator, but so was memory. That promise to her made Theo prioritise a person who had caused Astoria years of unhappiness as Draco's wife. How he did so without spite or malice, Hermione didn't know. "How are you friendly with Narcissa? HowÑ" "My promise to Astoria overrides my feelings about Narcissa's treatment of her. I leave the quiet anger to Draco and the louder spite to Daphne. I'm not a bitter man. I'mÑ" "How could you stand by and watch her marry someone she didn't love? Then have a childÉ" In another life, Scorpius could have beenÉ. Hermione couldn't fathom living with that sort of weight on her. The longing. "I told you once that there are several things required for two people to form a connection, and a relationship status isn't one of them." Theo glanced over his shoulder at her. "I meant that. We honoured her vowsÑ" "Draco knew from day one that he was committing to a life of nothing." And loneliness in certain aspects thatÑ Hermione took a deep breath and stared at her hands. They were locked together. Yes, he'd gotten a son, and a friendship, which afforded a certain degree of intimacy. Perhaps that was more than Draco had expected, maybe less, but she remembered Daphne saying Astoria knew he needed more. Perhaps love? Affection? The ability to take off his mask, shed his armour, and truly be vulnerable with another person? Partnerships should only be a facet of marriage, not the totality of it. But what did she know? Hermione wasn't well versed in love; her experience was limited and lacking. Maybe her inexperience inflated her views into the realm of idealism, but what she did understand was the reason why Draco was stern in his unwillingness to settle for nothing again. Real. Genuine. The core of his expectations. The reason Draco gave her time. Space. Why he took her hand. He was testing the simple luxury of reaching out and touching someone who might want to reach back. Touch, in itself, was a form of intimacy, a way to express emotions when words no longer held potency. Worry licked up the walls of her mind, flaring to life, but the flames were tempered by Theo and their unfinished conversation. "Cho doesn't know everything." "She knows enough, Theo. She told me you two were on and off for years in secret. She was there for you when you struggled and then when you mourned the loss of another woman. Someone else's wife. And you haven't stopped." Theo denied nothing. "Despite it all, she cares for you and thinks you're a good man. One who is unavailable because you're stuck in the past with a Pensieve of memories to keep you company. It's not that you can't let go, you just won't." "I'm not ready." Never had she heard him sound so earnest. So human. Hermione stood and joined him at the window. Now that Hermione's eyes were opened, Theo's pain was all she could see. She rested a gentle hand on his back for a moment of comfort. Support for a friend who had once done the same when she was lost. "I've realised that someone doesn't have to die in order for you to grieve their loss." Hermione peered up at her friend, who wouldn't look her in the eye. "I think you've been grieving for years in the name of duty. You need to let go, when you're ready, and when you do, remember that you aren't letting go of love or letting go of her. You're just letting go of the pain. You've been carrying it too long." Dinner was a quiet affair, other than Narcissa's attempts at conversation and engagement. Thankfully, she was feeling better from earlier, but in the same breath, she was trying to prod Hermione into conversation and not doing a good job at hiding it. She knew she should have diverted and encouraged more interaction between her and Scorpius, but she was drained, empty, and in no mood. Scorpius held on to her cardigan throughout dinner, but she thought it was more because of her silence than his nerves. Comfort. For her. "Are you feeling well tonight, Miss Granger?" She wasn't going to answer, but there was a concerned little boy hanging on to her every word. "I'm fine, thank you. Just tired. It's been quite a day." "I heard you took Scorpius to visit his father for lunch. Hopefully, it was before the unfortunate news broke in the Prophet's evening edition." Hermione had forgotten all about that in the wake of the day, but she couldn't muster the energy to wonder what had transpired. "He advised he would be having dinner elsewhere." She had told Scorpius already to ease his disappointment over Draco's absence. "I imagine that is why Draco is delayed." Narcissa made a contended noise that was clearly an attempt to draw Hermione into her snare of conversation. "It has been nice having him home for dinner as of late." Returning to stifling her words, Hermione finished her water as dinner concluded. Narcissa feigned tiredness after her third potion and bid them both good night. With just the two of them, Scorpius relaxed completely, signing to ask if she was okay. Hermione put on her bravest smile. "I'm fine." Cleaning after themselves passed in a blur with a helpful little boy rinsing dishes that easily could have been scrubbed with magic. Catherine handled his bedtime preparations, Hermione followed with a story, and once they finished Winnie the Pooh, she turned on the projector and picked out as many constellations as she could find until she noticed he'd fallen asleep. After that, time burned like a hidden fire that threatened to break out into a devastating conflagration. Hermione worked in Draco's office, or tried to. Thoughts and feelings poured through her fingers like sand. With Scorpius in bed, her work finished for the evening, and Draco running very late, there was little to distract her. She was tired enough to consider taking another potion to ensure she would be awake when Draco arrived home, but knew better. More than one potion would make sleep impossible for days so she took to the outdoors to clear her mind. Her thoughts followed anyway. There was no storm in the forecast, but the air seemed disturbed. Charged. Or maybe she was projecting. The garden wasn't as vast as the space surrounding her house, nor as open, but for tonight, it would have to do. Hermione tilted her head up to the dark sky and opened her mind, allowing herself to arrange the story of Astoria, Draco, and Theo. She didn't know everything, but what she did know made her heart ache for them all. Deep in thought, Hermione only realised she wasn't alone when she turned to go inside and spotted Draco by the glass door, the dull living room light illuminating him. Waiting. She didn't remember much about the walk across the grass, but he silently held the door open for her. They ended up in the living room. Draco settled into the armchair but Hermione was too restless to sit. "How long have you been home?" "Long enough." Draco's voice was as stiff as his body. There was caution in his narrowed eyes. "I imagine you have questions for meÉ pertaining to your earlier conversation." A breath escaped her. "He told you." "He did." Draco took off his glasses and placed them on the table, pinching the bridge of his nose between his fingers. "I was hoping to have this conversation at a later point, but I knew what I was getting into when I married Astoria. We both knew exactly what our marriage was and what it wasn't. I'm not a perfect man, nor was I a perfect husband. She deserved far better than what she got." "So did you." Hermione looked down at her shoes. "You still do." Her words pulled Draco from the chair into her orbit, but gravity kept Hermione grounded, making her stay instead of flying off at his approach. At his touch. The purpose in it. The quiet request that accompanied the fingers sliding between hers. Hermione unconsciously stepped closer, letting him lead her back to his office, where they sat together on his sofa. Ending the day just how it started. The touch was followed by a silence she couldn't sustain. "Daphne said she was your best friend." "She was." Draco remained quiet for a long moment as he seemed to carefully walk a rough road. "But I don't mourn for her like Theo does, if that's what you're concerned about. I do grieve her. It's not out ofÉ" He looked away, jaw working through the difficulty of each word. "If that's why you feel you need to know the details, then you're mistaken. Our marriage was one ofÑ" "I'm aware." Try as she might, Hermione couldn't slow her pulse enough to hide her own nervous tension. "You have a past, just as I do. Regardless of the circumstances of your marriage, Astoria was your wife. I respect that. You're allowed to process her loss and grieve, even as you try to navigate things with me." "Then whyÑ" "I-I want to know so I can understand better. It'sÉ a lot to absorb. All of it." Hermione's confession felt monumental, but from the look he was giving her, he knew it all too well. "But I don't want to discuss it tonight." "Agreed." It happened easily. Naturally. They went their separate ways to change into pyjamas, and upon her return, Hermione found Draco wearing familiar frames while reading a book that floated at his eye level. The Picture of Dorian Gray. Second edition. The pages looked worn. Used. He'd read it before. Apparently, many times. One finger swipe turned the page with fluid ease. Hermione initially leaned in to read over his shoulder, but one sidelong glance forced her to summon her own book and make herself comfortable beside him. A hand absently found hers with growing ease that transferred to her. Knowing the significance of his casual touches helped her settle. Relax. Well, somewhat. Soon, all was silent except the occasional turn of pages and their measured breaths. The weight and warmth of his hand served as a reminder of the shift between them. Just another in a series of what had already transpired and what was yet to come. Hermione mulled over her remaining time at the Malfoys' home while continuing to read about Anne's remaining time in Uppercross. Just when she was beginning to fall down a rabbit hole of thoughts, in a move that felt like another test, Draco brought their joined hands to his lips and brushed a kiss over her knuckles. A flash of surprised pleasure coursed through her, but his eyes never left the book. The clock on the wall chimed to note the new hour. Long after his lips were gone, the warmth remained. When words don't come easy, I make do with silence and find something in nothing. Strider Marcus Jones Chapter End Notes Thank you all for your love.
