It was time. He took her hand and they walked a familiar path through the darkness. The contrast from the cool night to the warm greenhouse had Draco shedding layers and rolling his sleeves while she ventured farther in. She was transfixed by how full and green everything was, how healthy, all while flowering and growing outside of season. Even the stubborn dittany was thriving. She had the urge to expand, but kept it at bay. Bigger meant more responsibility. More time. A commitment. "I'm torn." Draco's words pierced the silence. "I always am when it comes to you." "Torn between what?" "Coming closer and backing away." Hermione turned, despite all signs to look ahead. At the foot of the almond tree, they faced each other, close but distant. As always, he watched, but there was something in his eyes that felt dangerous; it both warned her away and lured her in. It seemed they were the same in that regard. Trepidation beat to the pounding rhythm of her heart. Fear etched itself in her actions. "Is that why you've beenÑ" "Partly." Draco took a step towards her and she did the same. "I told you I was nearly ready three months ago, that I was patient, but in the last month IÑit feels like a whiplash even I wasn't prepared for. IÑnever mind." "No." Hermione shook her head. "We have to talk about this." "I don't think you want that." Grey eyes looked off to the side before returning to hers. His face held a warning to tread lightly. So she did. Somewhat. "I think we're at a place where we can be honest with each other. So, I'll start by saying that I think you're upset by my comment, but I do like where we are right now. We're talking and spending time together. We're getting to know each other on a different level thaÑ" "I've told you more than anyone else, and sometimes I still don't think you understand me." The urge to charge forward and argue her point was only tempered by what she knew about him. "You're right and that points to my feelings about how we are now. We're learning each other." They were, much more than they had been. "You keep things from me, Draco, and there's so much you don't like talking about. Like finding me, for starters. I respect your silence because I know why it exists. I know it was traumatising for you." "It was, but I'm handling it. I'll admit that IÑ" Draco struggled for a moment then recovered. His hands ghosted the curves of her dress but ultimately retreated. "I'll admit the incident has a lot to do with my changing stance on us." Hermione frowned. "Changing staÑ" "I want you." The quiet ferocity in three words stopped her heart. "If you don't know this already, I'm not sure how many ways I have to say it or what I have to do to show my intent." Draco ran a frustrated hand in his hair. "I'm a fool because you clearly don't know what you want or how you feel." "IÑI know exactly how I feel about you, how quickly I've gotten to this point, and I'm trying not to be rash. It's everything outside of how I feel about you that's complicated." "I know that." "We have to think about Scorpius. If this doesn't workÑ" "Regardless of what happens, you've always kept his best interest in mind." "And I always will butÑ" "I heard what he called you in your memory." Hermione took a deep breath. She had been preparing for this moment. "I had no idea that was his name for me, but he's not confusing me with Astoria. He just sees me as a mother figure. There's a difference between being a mother figure and stepping into that role on a more permanent basis. There's more for me to consider, for you to consider. We need to know what he thinks and how he feels." "You're right." "There's also the fact that your mother is my patient, medical leave asideÑ" "I'm not accepting that excuse. I told you we'd cross that bridge when we got there." "We crossed that bridge in September in an ornate chair." She held her breath but Draco said nothing. "Injury aside, I have a job, a career, and a dutyÑ" "I have my own duties. In fact, you were the one who reminded me of them." "Failure for us isn't an option, and the list of expectations is high. Not just for us to succeed, but your mother'sÑ" "In some aspects, I would be fulfilling them." Hermione shifted her weight and looked away. "I'm not asking for your hand in marriage today, Granger, I'm asking you to weigh all these points and make a decision about us. I know what I want and what I do know is that I need someone who is certain. Who, after assessing the risks, thinks that I'mÑ" Worth it. Draco looked away and never finished. "I recognise it's not fair to have this conversation right now. It's been three months since I told you I was patient, one of which you've been focused on recovery. It's why I've been quiet, but you kept pushing for answers." "I only pushed because you've been distant and frustrated." "More with myself than you. I miscalculated my own patience." His fingertips scraped against hers. Resolve was found once again in a man who was quietly asking to be heard. "I'm ready." Hermione took several measured breaths in the silence that followed his declaration. "And a selfish moment has made me greedy." He released her hand. "I thought I could do this, I thought I could continue on like this, but I can't when I want more. Everything. I'm willing to give it. I deserve it and I won't accept less, but you're not ready to give it, and I'm ÑI'm not sure if you can." Hermione turned the words over and over in her head, not looking at him, but past him as she calculated risk versus reward in the following second. The risk was almost too great, but the reward wasÉ complicated. Unknown. "We could try." Draco's jaw was set stern. "Don't bullshit me, Granger. It's insulting." "I'm not sure what else to do or say." Her chest tightened. "I just don't wantÑ" "I don't want your attempts if you're not decided. I don't want you to placate me. I don't want you to play a role." The words burned. "You wanted honesty, and this is it." Draco was so close she could see his chest rise and fall with each breath. "You don't have the space for us yet, not the way I want, at least. Not the way I need. I'd rather you come to your own conclusions than to pull you along. I don't want to be a guess or a decision you make because you like where we are now. I know you well enough to know that if you say yes, it's at my behest and this decision has to be yours and yours alone. I want to be a choice. Your choice." "You are." Hermione reached for his hand. "It's just. You're asking for everything and IÑI'm allowed to think about it. I'm allowed to be afraid. I'm allowed to not be ready to make such a drastic change when I'm barely grasping scraps of normality as it is. You're giving me an ultimatum." "This isn't an ultimatum. Even if you said you were ready right now I wouldn't believe you. I've learned you need time and space. And I'm giving it to you." He squeezed her hand and let go again. "I'm laying down my hand and stepping back. I'm not coming any closer until you decide." Which shut Hermione up. "IÑ" Draco ran a hand through his hair. "I'd take everything as it is right now, like this, but I can't live another ten years wondering ifÑ" "Draco, that's notÑ" "I had three tasks." He paused for breath. "I was supposed to finish handling the threat against my family, eventually bury my mother, and raise my son. That was my future. Alone. I was fine not knowing the potential of what I could have, what we could have, but now I do and I want it enough to let you sort yourself out first." Hermione began to reach for him again and fell short. Once. Twice. Three times before resting her hands on his arms, ignoring the pain. Draco's attention settled heavily on her. "I'm notÑ" Hermione took a brave breath, voice thick with emotions as tears rolled down her cheeks. "This is more than attraction. It's real." A lifetime of emotions wedged in just a few months. Draco moved, but not in the way she thought he would. Instead of retreating, he loomed closer, cupping her face in a way that made her hands fall limp. There was a moment when she saw beneath the vulnerability in his words and into the place she had been trying to find all along. The core of who he was. There, she truly saw Draco Malfoy, and the man he was becoming right before her eyes. "You're not ready." His thumb stroked her cheek, wiping her tears, and all she could feel was her heart burning to ash. "I wish you were, butÑ" "I can be." "I'm not forcing you to be sure. You were right when you said that by making Scorpius a priority, I would have to make myself one as well." And this was his first act. His decision. Nothing she could say would change his mind. Not tonight. And though the words to stop him were fresh on her lips, Hermione kept her love close rather than giving voice to a thought her own hesitation and denial every step of the way wouldn't let him believe. Not yet. She touched his face and quietly acknowledged they had never been nothing. For there to be nothing, there had to have existed traces of something from which the null could grow. They were well past something. The starting point. The truth of what they were was wedged in the everything between the spark and containment. Silence stretched from one beat to the next before Hermione gave a silent nod and kissed him. There it was. That dangerous urge to speak her heart and melt into the shadows with him. But in that moment, all she could do was pour every unspoken emotion into her kiss. They held each other tight, drinking each other in. One last breath before they went under. It was urgent and organic. Honest acknowledgement of the depth and breadth passed back and forth with each breath. Need. Want. Warmth and emotion. So much of it she could barely see, but Hermione didn't care, she barely noticed, leaning into him and hugging him close in preparation to give Draco the space he needed. As well as steeling herself for her own task. His message had been loud and clear. Everything. Draco pulled away. The wall he'd been building was visible behind his eyes and stronger than ever. When he left, the cold set in. Go bravely. Go deeply - Or do not go. Komuha Chapter End Notes This week on callbacks and how Ina has been waiting for 18 chapters to make one from Hermione's conversation with Neville in Chapter 15: "You laugh now, but one day, you're going to meet someone and they're going to want more of you than you're used to sharing." ///"They're going to want it all: the good, bad, brilliant, and ugly. You'll be faced with a decision between forcing them out or letting them in."/// "I'm curious to see what you'll choose." Also: I had a lot of people really pissed that Hermione didn't tell him she loved him after 2 months and 25 days (the length of time between the Patient Man talk in chapter 23 to post-wedding shenanigans in chapter 32) and 3 weeks into trying to put her life back together after nearly dying. Merp. Impulsive, a 32 year old Hermione who has been single and independent for years is not. She has been careful this entire story, a logical thinker, (except for her emotional/anger outbursts and all things Scorpius related). She is not going to be impulsive about anything long term decisions. Lots of words, but the timeline is very compact due to highlighting several scenes and interactions. I use the dates to show how little time has passed for them, how quickly they progress, and while relationships are fully capable of moving fast.... theirs is actually moving quickly. A bit of a whirlwind given all the externals and we're still within the first year of Draco becoming a widower. Anywho, hope you enjoyed Draco speaking his peace, putting himself as a priority, laying it all out very openly, and stepping back. Her move now. Art for this chapter: Harry & Hermione on the Couch by bookloverdream-blessedindeed & Scorpius on a Broom by hpdrdontknow & Draco & Scorp Quidditch Lesson by pollux_ & Quidditch Kit Draco by bookloverdream-blessedindeed & Draco & Hermione Watching Fireworks by Oukyn & Astoria & Theo by bookloverdream-blessedindeed & Imagining of Draco Working on Translations by ectoheart Chapter 34: Eurydice Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Thirty Four Eurydice October 31, 2011 Her old friend's arrival was expected, albeit slow. Restlessness. Its vengeance knew no mercy. Too tired to sleep, too anxious to pace, Hermione laid in a bed that no longer felt like hers. Smaller. Darkness stretched in all directionsÑthick like a heavy smoke. It was suffocating, sparing nothing and engulfing everything. Silence lingered in her ears as she tossed, and painted shadows on her eyelids as she turned. Masking the walls and designing the void, the quiet of her mind created everything out of nothing. But then she turned on the light. Hermione felt herself grow and stretch too large for her own body while looking around the space that was once a fortress of comfort and finding it no longer was. Nothing would ever be the same. She was torn between grieving the loss and being grateful for the change. Like a lost soul, Hermione wandered from room to room. Thinking while running a finger over the spines of her research books. Ruminating while sitting on the stool in her kitchen. Reorganising everything but changing nothing. Calling forth memories as she touched the dry leaves of the hanging herbÑpart of a gift for the little boy who loved them dearly. Each one, new and old, drove her out to the conservatory, where she scanned the heavens in search of peace. Beautiful as always, the stars were silent. Empty. No comfort found. The crescent moon yielded the same reserve. Hermione looked down when she found nothing to ground her. She could feel every sensationÑfingers tingling, hand throbbing, muscles aching, head pounding. Draco's words and the question that refused to be ignored reverberated like the steady beat of a drum. What do you want? Those little whispered words reminded her of the gravity of each moment, of the choice Draco had given her, and the duty she owed to herself and them to sort her own demons. But every time Hermione took a step in the direction of an answer, she was brought face to face with the anxieties that plagued her. Hermione couldn't undo what had been done or what had been said, but the what ifs filled her lungs and squeezed her heart, making it harder to breathe with each punch of her pulse. It tore her apart. Her head and heart were at war again, and the walls were closing in on all sidesÑslowly but steadily moving to crush her, to make her scream. Heavy eyes fluttered shut and Hermione hoped they would remain that way, but that would have been too easy. There was no escape. Impossible to move or sit still, she watched night turn into dawn before wandering back inside. A curl of steam rose from a cup of tea waiting for her. Hibiscus. Bold. Vibrant. Alive. If her hands shook as she picked it up, or a residual tear slipped down her cheek as she inhaled the scent of berries and earth, there was no one there to judge her for it. Or for how something as simple as Draco's steadfastness in the form of tea released just a fraction of the tightness in her chest. Like last time, each sip called forth thoughts of its maker and what she needed to do. Her task. What she needed to find. The act of wandering without direction wasn't the most solid plan Hermione had ever come up with. It made matters worse, but she was aimless and roaming for something that had been lost to her, too. True calm. Peace. Hermione set out to find it. Unable to Apparate, she stepped into the floo and out in front of a confused Dean. Halia was with his mum, Daphne was still asleep, and he never probed when she asked him for a favour while Cheddar coiled around her ankles. He merely grabbed his car keys. "Where do you need to go?" The first trip took her to a familiar place. Her parents' home. It was a move as instinctive as birds flying south for the winter. Home in every literal sense of the word, it was where she was born and raised, and she'd kept it up for years before their return. The longing in her bones for steady words from her father was tangible. Being so close should have pushed her to move, but Hermione remained rooted to the spot and lost her nerve. This was her parents' home, its contents full of memories of a better time, but it was no longer her home. Hermione hadn't brought the utensils needed to properly dress the wound in the shape of her mother, and despite needing them both, she wasn't ready. Not yet, at least. The scars on her heart were still fresh. "Not here?" Dean had joined her beside the gate at some point during her reverie. "No." "Then where?" The second stop was to Harry and Ginny's, but Hermione never even got out of the car. She inhaled and rested her head against the seat. "Sorry for wasting your time." "You're not. Just ride." Dean put the car in gear. "I think I know a place." They walked aimlessly through the park until she got tired and found a spot under the tree where they could watch people jog and walk. It was a crisp morning, and autumn had a firm grip on everything. The well-beaten path had taken them on a picturesque journey during sunrise, but what she was looking for wasn't there either. The only things she found were memories of her many walks with Draco over the last few months, their conversation and debates, the edgy ease she felt in his presence, and a painful reminder of how contradictory her feelings for him had always been. Though the world around her was calm, and people were passing her by, a nameless face in the crowd, Hermione's emotions were still brewing. Restlessness had followed her here. Great. When they returned back to where they'd started their journey, and Hermione waved goodbye to Dean, instead of resuming her organising and cleaning efforts, she grabbed a fistful of powder. On instinct, she closed her eyes, exhaled, and called the first place she could think of. The destination wasn't what she expected. Hermione nearly changed her mind on principle, hesitating twice, but just when she was about to grab another handful of Floo powder, Kingsley appeared in front of the fireplace, dressed in preparation to care for the bees. "Do you plan to stay there all morning?" Despite his casual tone, concern was etched in his features. Understandable. She knew what she looked like. An exhausted mess with wild hair on the brink of a breakdown. She hadn't slept. Couldn't. Caught in the crossfire of her head and heart, she was left with a headache and heavy, tired limbs. "I don't want to intrude. I'll justÑ" "Stay." Kingsley drew her from the fireplace with an offered hand. "You're always welcomed here, Hermione. You know this." "It's too early." But Hermione accepted and allowed him to help her out. "I was justÑ" "Coming over." It was more of an order than an invitation. "For breakfast." "I'm notÑ" "So wonderful for you to drop by." His look was pointed yet gentle. Austere, in a way. "I was in need of company." Kingsley's cottage was always warm and inviting. A slight weight was lifted off her shoulders when she sat at the table while he prepared breakfast. Naturally, her focus drifted around the room to all the things that were the same, then zeroed in on the one thing that wasn't. There were two teacups already on the tableÑone still warm and the other half empty. Suspicion sparked like wildfire. "I'm not the first visitor you've had today." "No, you aren't." He looked up from adding salt and pepper to the eggs, making a small content sound before returning to his task. She had several questions, ones she thought about asking, but she lacked the energy necessary to pry. Breakfast was spinach omelettes with cheese and toast. It was always intriguing to watch him cook. He never measured out anythingÑall experience and intuition. It was inspiring enough to make Hermione want to help in order to learn the talent, but she remained still. Restless with barely enough dexterity to crack eggs without magic. As always, the food was delicious, but Hermione barely tasted it, deep in thoughts that had nothing to do with her current surroundings. Kingsley enjoyed his meal quietly, casting glances at her every so often as if waiting for her to make the decision to speak, but Hermione never did. When he finished cleaning the dishes, he returned with a fresh cup of tea for them both. Peppermint. Plain as she preferred, she didn't mind the bitterness, but it still wasn't right. She added a spoonful of honey and a squeeze of lemon, then spent the lengthening silence wondering why. Kingsley drank tea while radiating peace and power and rest. Nothing was required of Hermione except to relax without the need to fill the room with chatter. The longer it stretched, the more it transformed into a balm that eased her weary mind. By the time he suggested they venture outside with a second cup, she was agreeable. The air was more cold than cool today, but a thoughtful warming charm made her cast Kingsley an appreciative look before turning to the sight beyond his cottage. Autumn turned the world into various shades of red and yellow. The clouds were thin enough that the vague outline of the sun was visible. It should have felt more ominous, with the scent of rain in the air, but all Hermione could focus on was the sight. Different from her own outside her home or even the Malfoys', it was a reminder that seasons held a different magnificence in each location. "You are far quieter than usual." Kingsley joined her at the railing. "How was the meal?" "Lovely as always." Hermione focused on the warm cup in her hand, the wisp of steam, and the barest hint of its scent. "And what did you learn from it?" Hermione opened her mouth to answer, then shut it. "I didn't think about it at all." "The most important lesson of all. Sometimes, it is important to think things through, but sometimes it's equally as important to just be. You can't know the outcome." The statement gave her pause. "How is it that you know exactly what's wrong every time?" "I don't." Kingsley sipped his tea. "I only know that you seem heavier today, and restless, which is generally a sign that you're overthinking." "I am." Bringing her other hand to the cup for added warmth, she let it fall to the side with a sigh after realising it was far too stiff to open. "I know I am, but I can't stop." "Take back control of your thoughts, Hermione. It's the key to feeling peaceful again. Or you'll drown yourself in your worries." "I'm already drowning." Brittle and forced, her chuckle was hollow. "I believe that's why you're here. You're looking for something." Kingsley turned from watching the bees. There was a knowing look in his eyes that felt deeper than sight. "Guidance, perhaps." Hermione brought the cup to her lips but not before inhaling. Peppermint reminded her of the balm Draco rubbed on her for weeks after her attack, the memory of Scorpius holding it in front of the floo not knowing if she would return, and the different blends and combinations she'd had steeped and flavoured to her liking. And Draco's second note. Bring more honey. "If I'm troubled, I visit the bees and work. They are a comfort to me." "What are you troubled about? I very rarely see you rattled." "Human nature doesn't allow for perfection, and thus, I am capable of worrying, amongst other things. It's natural, especially when it concerns the future." Kingsley rested a comforting hand on her shoulder. "Exiting one phase of life and entering another is not easy, and often we want the transition to be perfect, but that's not possible, which makes us worry further because we want to try and make a preventive strike on situations." The impact of his words almost made Hermione drop her teacup. She clutched it tighter. "I've spent a fair amount of time in deep contemplation over this upcoming choice." The significance of his moment of vulnerability wasn't lost on her. "It is hard to find a balance between being a good leader and adhering to my own moral compass. I feel as though I'm always weighing one against the other. It is not an easy position to be in because I do not carry duty lightly. Tough decisions will need to be made eventually, and sometimes they will be unpopular." "But tough decisions don't equal ruthlessness." "No, they don't." The sigh that escaped was so faint she nearly missed it. "But that is an important thing that many leaders forget. It is easier to be feared than loved, but fear is not often a good motivator." "Respect is." Hermione finished her tea and placed the cup on the saucer. "I'm more likely to respond accordingly if I have respect for them." "Respect takes time to build." He wasn't wrong. "Any way I look at it, regardless of what happens with Percy's investigation into Tiberius, I would need to act accordingly and bring everything together quickly to stop history from repeating itself. We do not need another power vacuum. But I refuse to let fear be that motivator." "I don't think it would be. Everyone respects you enough to follow. I believe it's the role of your advisors to assist in that respect. Have you chosen?" "No, I haven't. I am taking the opportunity to get to know everyone. I expect I'll be asked to make a decision soon, but I'm not entertaining candidate options now with so much chatter on the subject." That was true, Draco had mentioned it during one of their nightly talks, and it brought to mind another question. She placed her teacup on the table and returned to his side. A thoughtful moment passed before she finally asked a question that had been in her heart for what felt like forever. "How do you make decisions with far-reaching effects without overthinking, without walking through each scenario or turning it over in your head?" "Interesting question." Kingsley blew a breath and his teacup left his hand, floating in front of him. "Making decisions isn't your issue, Hermione. Your indecision exists because you ponder every aspect of the choice, which takes time. This is why you file decisions away for a time when you have enough. But you never do." "That's what I've always done." "That isn't the issue, but the extent that you do it is. You don't let yourself follow the natural course of your thoughts. You compartmentalise. You put off everything until it's out of control and you're standing in front of a mountain that was once a hill." Which was what she'd done with Draco. Put it off. Thought it over in sections. And nowÉ "Talk to me." Genuine interest creased Kingsley's features and she was lulled into honesty by the concern evident in his next question. "What's troubling you, Hermione?" "I have a task." She took to staring at the evergreen forest in the distance to articulate her problems. "Something delicate." "Emotional, then." "Yes, but it's all a mess. My head and my heart fight all the time." On edge, she was vulnerable and susceptible to anything during her desperate search for peace. "It's exhausting. I'm exhausted. I've not slept, and I keep trying to intellectualise this, butÑ" "An age-old battle: the head versus the heart. Logic versus impulse. In the battle of wills, everyone forgets about the third party. The gut. Instinct. What do you want?" Every aspect of her life was locked in orbit around that singular question. "Think with your head. React with your heart. Listen to your gut." Running her finger along the railing, she sighed. "My gut doesn't know." "It does, but you don't speak its language." Kingsley's dark eyes turned more probing, albeit still gentle. Hard to understand how he managed it, or how he made her feel calm and safe when they were approaching the most fragile place in her heart. "Or perhaps you don't want to because then you'll have to decide, and when you decide, you'll have to commit to that decision. Have you considered that maybe that's what terrifies you?" Hermione couldn't dodge the direct hit. "I've known you for a long time. You don't move impulsively, but perhaps it is out of a certain fear you've never dealt with: failure." The word meant different things in different situations. Not an issue in some, but critical in others. Failure with Draco would create a ripple effect in every direction, and eventually spiral out of her control. Scorpius would take the biggest brunt of it, and that was hard to reconcile. "We were not born fearing failure." Kingsley's comment drew her attention from the troubling thoughts. "If we were, humans would still be crawling because we would have been too afraid to walk." "There's a lot at stake." "There always is, but that's not why you fear it. You're afraid of what your failures may say about you. When every step you take can lead to what you identify as your worst case scenario, you weigh each move with such scrutiny that you barely advance at all." "YouÑyou're right." Hermione worried her bottom lip with her teeth, looking off in the distance, trying to hold herself together. "I understand your struggles, but also understand that you have to decide what you're willing to sacrifice, and to do that, you're going to have to reconcile your own feelings. Sort through your emotions. Without thinking, tell me how you feel." "Lost. Torn." Tears gathered at the corners of her eyes. "Weary." "Are you all of those things because of your task?" Kingsley leaned a fraction closer. "Or because you've already found the answer you're looking for and you're scared of what will change as a result?" "IÉ" Hermione didn't have an answer. "I don't know. I've been standing still and looking for true calm." "You will look forever without realising it's not found in any particular place. There's not one spot in the world where it's located or one person you can find it in." A steady hand sat heavily on her back. "True calm is a choice you make by deciding not to let your fears keep you stagnant. Standing still is okay until you're ready to move. I think you're ready, or you wouldn't be wrestling this so hard, but you have to decide for yourself." She inhaled until she had no room for air, then released it all in a single breath that left her lightheaded. "Sorry, my emotions are all over the place." "Even in the best of times, emotions are tricky and hard to name. They masquerade as one another, they come and go, and they mutate like wisps in the wind." "I don't understand." "Ask anger what its name is, and it may tell you that it's fear. Ask fear to identify itself, and it may tell you its name is hate or sadness. It might even tell you that it's love." Hermione closed her eyes at the word trying to ease the growing pressure in her chest. "It's all relative. Sometimes emotions blur the lines between reality and belief." "It's not that I don't feel anything, it'sÑit's been slowly consuming me since I put a name to it. I've been in love before, that was easy, but this time I'm floundering." "I've never heard love described as easy. What if it were wearing a different mask before?" Kingsley chuckled ruefully. "Perhaps this is why you're struggling." "Why do you say that?" "Of all the emotions, love is not easy. It is natural, though you have to learn and differentiate its shape depending on each person. It's a fight. A conscious choice that requires constant effort." Kingsley paused as the weight of his words settled around them. "Love may not always be kind, but it is forgiving. Let it serve as your guide." "IÑ" Hermione swallowed the emotions that were clawing up her throat as she batted away tears before they could fall. "There's always a chance for error." "The learning process is riddled with them." "But the ramificationsÉ" "The stakes only grow higher the longer you stall. As much as you abhor them, mistakes are a part of life and a source of strength. If you learn something from an experience, can you ever really call it a mistake?" "IÑI can't say." "The only way you truly fail anything is if you quit, or worse, if you don't even try." Hermione stewed in silence, thoughts still swirling as tears kept welling from a spring he'd tapped during their conversation. For a few minutes, she remained perfectly still, eyes shut, tears still falling as she considered every word he'd said, mixed them with what Draco had said last night, and waited for her breathing to settle. "As you know, the beekeeping uniform is hanging in the closet in the guest room. Let the bees help you marinate with your thoughts." Twenty minutes later, Hermione was doing just that. Walking beside Kingsley as he worked, she took direction and completed each task he asked of her. Each one tested the new strength she found in her left hand. The buzzing muted the part of her mind that wanted to analyse the last four months and the part that wanted to bury it all and move. The sound was lulling, the tasks provided her with the usefulness she needed to feel, and the concentration it demanded settled her thoughts. Time helped her prepare herself. Breathe. Kingsley checked for mites, and made certain each hive was secured for winter, as there would be a drop in temperature in the next few days. He made certain all the charms were set in place, and refreshed each hive's stores of sugar water. Hermione checked the garden, studying the winter plants alongside the summer plants that had long since gone dormant. While she worked, Hermione noticed something out of the ordinary from her last visit. "You're missing a hive." "I am." He continued working, but waved her over. "A weaker hive lost its queen this summer. I considered a new queen, but I doubted she would produce enough eggs in time for them to survive the winter together." "What did you do?" "The best way to enhance the bee colony is to optimise each hive. This one was weak, so I combined it with a stronger hive that needed more workers. Now, there is balance." He touched the top of the pod, looking satisfied. "Now, they are thriving as one." Hermione couldn't help but apply the concept to her own situation. "How did you know that it would work and the new bees would mix with the existing bees?" "Call it a leap of faith, but in the end, they all need each other. Survival will always win." Hermione remained quiet. "It's not easy to reconcile with the idea that you're missing something, that you need something more. You have more needs than you're willing to consider or openly admit. And you aren't used to tending to your own needs. But maybe now is the time." "It's something I've known for a long time, but never had the opportunity to deal with it." "You've had opportunities, Hermione, but you haven't been pushed to truly tackle the issue before. You've never had someone rattle you out of your senses, someone who makes you step outside of your well-oiled machine and realise there could be flaws in the design." "If you noticed the flaws, why didn't you say something?" "It's always best when self-actualisation is natural and not forced. It's also not my place." He was right. "So how do I fix the errors? How do I combine these two hives into one?" "You decide if you want to survive or die in the winter's frost. And when you choose survival Ñit is instinct, after allÑyou determine what tools you need to thrive." "That doesn't sound easy at all." "It's not, but before I joined these hives, I made space for what they may need. I made room." Hermione looked to the sky that was more white than blue. "We become wiser through mistakes, just as we find strength we never knew we had in the support of another person. You think of it as having to start over, but you aren't. You're expanding, rebuilding, and reorienting your path. I told you months ago that it's okay to be afraid, but what isn't okay is letting that fear stop you." She remembered when he'd said those words. Back in March before any of this began. But unlike then, now his sentiment permeated her skin and sank to the core of her heart. "I kÑI'm used to being alone. I haven't had to make room for anyone in a very long time. Do I even know how?" "That's where the other half comes in, and you two work together to create a space that is neither his nor yours, but something you both share. Then you fill it. You're not alone in any of this. DracoÑ" "How did you know?" "It's been intriguing to watch what little I've seen." Kingsley walked on and she followed. "I would speak of your love for his son extending to him, but it came down to the fact that you wanted one thing in my return to the Ministry. Your only request was not for yourself at all, it was for him. I knew then what you seem to have only recently realised." At a loss for words, Hermione froze and brought her gloved hands to her chest. He turned around. "You are not the only one who seeks guidance. You aren't the only one who struggles with how fast this is all happening. The two of youÉ" Kingsley shook his head ruefully as the corners of his mouth twitched in faint amusement. "I look forward to seeing you two together. Truly together. It will be dynamic, with highs and lows, but I believe happiness and the type of intimate relationship he craves will open Draco up to a life of new possibilities and choices he's never been allowed to make. And you will, at long last, understand that letting people in isn't sacrificing your independence, it's allowing yourself a pillar and a source of support. You already know where you're going, Hermione. And I think that's why it all feels so out of your control." "IÉ" Her hand went to the compass around her neck. "Draco paces when he speaks of his exasperation and how maddening you are, but he is quiet with affection he often struggles to put into words. He is also humbled by everything you've done for his family and sometimes a little jealous about your relationship with Scorpius. Beneath his silence, I sense his unspoken worries and fears for you both and the unknown ahead, but also his dedication to becoming a better man. His determination." Hermione stared at him, heart hammering beneath her hands. "Ask any of those emotions to identify themselves, and what do you think they'll say their name is?" Love. The priceless sight of three screaming and laughing children running between two tentsÑor waddling in Albus the Penguin's caseÑwas made better by the abject horror on Narcissa Malfoy's face. Albus was hitting his brother with his wing, Lily was pinching Albus, who was all but dragging her around, and James was trying to handcuff his siblings together. For a second, she looked as if her entire world was collapsing in on itself. Her face shuffled through emotions and reactions, mouth opening and closing. But then she turned and dusted her hands. "Not my concern." Narcissa patted her coiffed hair, smoothed her maroon robes, and allowed the chaos to continue uninterrupted until Luna came out of the second tent and lured the children like pirates witnessing a siren's song. While dressed as a butterfly. The tent extravaganza was equipped to host thirty children instead of the three and an infant. One tent was full of every candy imaginable, spelled to dance around while the children tried to catch handfuls of the treats at a time. The other was a 'haunted house' featuring Luna, who had forgotten the theme and turned it into a discotheque with kid-friendly music and colourful fairy lights. The last time Hermione had gone in, she was showing them what looked like a Beltane dance. Halia was strapped to her chest, dressed adorably like a purple crayon and smiling around the fist in her mouth while cooing loudly at being bounced around. A strange sight, but the children loved it. Even Scorpius, who was dressed as Mint with the addition of a bright red bowtie. Less interested in dancing wildly with Luna, he gravitated towards Neville, who was dressed as the Whomping Willow to teach the children not to fear nature. The only time Hermione heard Scorpius' laughter above the music was when Neville spelled the branches of his costume to pick him up. "I see my grandson has picked up his father's predilection for heights." Hermione hadn't noticed Narcissa join her on the blanket. "It appears so." They watched in silence for a few long moments. "You look exhausted." Narcissa didn't ask for permission before taking her hand. Still too early to tell the potency of her new potion, she could recognise today was a good day. Minimal tremors provided the dexterity needed to massage Hermione's hand open. "Did you sleep?" "Not much." "Perhaps insomnia is contagious." Hermione trained her features to stay neutral, swallowing back the acid in her throat. Knowing icy blue eyes were watching her every move, she nodded from time to time, uttering random responses without much sense. Tension kept her shoulders hunched. If Narcissa noticed, she kept it to herself, only arching her brow in the tactful way she'd learned to bear her silence. "Miss Granger, I could not help but notice that you spent the night at your home." The observation was accompanied by a careful expression. "I cannot force you to stay here, but I would prefer that you not make it a habit to stay home until you are further along in the healing process. Come, let me braid your hair." Narcissa left no room for the arguments Hermione was too tired to give. Half an hour later, she was showered and changed, sitting at the vanity while Narcissa worked slowly and hummed a familiar tune that never failed to make Andromeda emotional. Normally quiet, today curiosity made Hermione speak. "What song are you humming?" "Oh." Confusion swept across Narcissa's features. "I do not recall. IÑ" "It has no words." Andromeda announced her presence from the doorway wearing a familiar, wistful look. "It's a tune our mother made up but hummed constantly while braiding our hair. Cissa first, because she was the youngest, Bella second, because she was bitterly impatient, and me last. But I never cared to have my hair braided." Always different, even then. "Mother would force you to sit each time." Narcissa chuckled as she finished up Hermione's braid, tsking at the curly wisps that escaped. "I am surprised you wear it braided now, or that you even know how to braid." "And I'm surprised you don't." "It is not proper." "Even before I ran away, I never cared for propriety." Andromeda shrugged. "But sometimes nurture wins over nature, and nostalgia was a good teacher, so I learned. NymphadoraÑ" She brought her hand to her mouth, swaying as if rocked by the memory of her lost daughter. Grief was sneaky. Like a thief, it waited for moments when it felt comfortable to talk about them, or even feel happy before jabbing in the knife. Andromeda bore the wound with grace, smiling through her tears. "I suppose she inherited her hatred of French braids from me." Through the mirror, Hermione watched Narcissa turn to her sister and reach for her, but hesitate in the end. She remained quiet. They all did. The silence lasted long enough for Narcissa to change her mind and approach her sister, hugging her close. "I did not know her or your husband, but I am sorry for your loss." "We both have lost, and it will stay with us forever, but we shouldn't waste time on nostalgia, especially whenÉ" I will lose you, too. Understanding her unspoken words, Narcissa pulled back and entwined their hands slowly. "I find myself missing the years we spent apart and the ones we will not have together, but I am grateful that you are here now, and for the memories we can make. I know it is not much, and it cannot make up for the yearsÑ" Andromeda scoffed, a tear escaping her eye. "You know I don't care about that, Cissa." "I do, but it needs to be said before I cannot." She lowered her head and tensed, suddenly realising they weren't alone. "You may go, Miss Granger." Hermione didn't move. "For what it's worth." She kept her eyes on Narcissa, open and truthful. "I'm happy I took your case. Happy that all of our discussions and arguments have brought us to this point of understanding. I'm happy to know you, and plan to see you through this in any capacity. Just as you have for me." Narcissa brought a hand on her necklace and cleared her throat. "Come now, Miss Granger." A slow smile curled her lips when Andromeda tightened her hold on the hand that still linked them. "You are far too sentimental on a day when my grandson looks as if he is trapped in Devil's Snare and happy about it. There is a dance party with a butterfly, a cat, a penguin, and a bat hybrid with tights." She touched her brow. "Madness. All of it." The serious moment dissolved with laughter, and when Hermione explained James' costume, Narcissa even cracked a smile. "I actually came to tell you both that Draco and Harry have returned and we're ready to eat." Hermione's smile vanished as the complex blend of emotions at seeing Draco again mixed with bits of dread and scraps of anxiety about being in his presence after how they parted last night. It didn't take a night alone to miss him or notice the blank space he left in his absence. It didn't take sleeping alone to be unable to stand the silence that served as a chance to hear his words play over and over in her head. Unable to escape them. Unable to escape him. The idea of going back to the polite yet reserved mannerisms, the idea of going back to how they once wereÑshe'd rather have nothing than that. Or would she? As they made their way downstairs to where everyone was arranged around the table lined with food, honesty tugged at Hermione's thoughts. Despite the lively conversation happening upon their arrival, everything blurred around the edges of her subconscious when Draco's eyes met hers He looked as tired as she felt, but she only noticed because she knew where to look. Not in his eyes, but in the pallor of his complexion, the rigidity of his attire, and the heavy sag of his shoulders. Hermione hated knowing it. Hated knowing that, like her, Draco hadn't taken anything for alertness. But, most of all, she hated knowing that the black he wore today was less of a fashion choice than a function. His armour. For her. Draco's attention moved to Scorpius, who was struggling with his fork because he refused to take his cactus hands off. Fondness creased the edge of his tired eyes as he picked up the utensil for his son, forked the mashed potatoes, and brought them to his lips. An awkward moment passed before Scorpius allowed himself to be fed. She took a seat on the other side of Scorpius after an inviting look, and grinned in return when he started to sign something but forgot, yet again, that he had limited use of his hands. After greeting everyone and offering Lily a meow that made her beam, the meal began in earnest and the chaos continued. The amount of subject changes made it easy to sit back and let her friends lead. The children were asking Neville to pick up different things with his branches. Luna and Susan encouraged him while Narcissa's visible horror returned. Andromeda and Daphne were talking while laughing. Halia was very much awake and opening her mouth like a baby bird with each bite of food Dean ate. Harry was a little quiet and Ginny's supportive hand never left his during the meal. Draco was just as quiet while he fed Scorpius, who looked happier and happier with each bite and was currently swinging his legs in his chair. His only comment came when Ginny mentioned that Ron was going to the heavily advertised Chudley Cannons match against the Falmouth Falcons on Saturday. "It'll be over in ten minutes." "The Cannons aren't so bad this year. The Ministry offered tickets to everyone. Rumour has it the Minister himself will be in attendance." "The Minister's presence won't change the fact that they're second to last place." Dean chuckled at himself and then at his daughter, who was now enjoying a bottle, even if she didn't look happy about it. "Actually, I just remembered the Arrows passed them, so they're currently last." Harry and Ginny exchanged looks. "Okay, they're a bit terrible," Ginny conceded. Disbelieving looks rained on them, and even Narcissa, who knew little about Quidditch, tilted her head. "Malfoy's right." Dean shrugged. "They're going to be destroyed. Ron asked me to go with but I told him I was teaching art lessons that night." Daphne gave her husband a look. "You don't start that until January." "I don't." His mischievous leer made his wife cackle. "Not even box seats will make a night of nursing his disappointment worth it." Harry didn't appear fond of the task either. "Shi"ÑGinny elbowed him in the ribs hard Ñ"lling. Shilling." The word came out in a wheeze. "Calling Molly on you for spousal abuse." "She would hit you harder if she knew you were swearing in front of the kids." Before Harry had a chance to rebut, they all caught the tail end of Neville asking James if he knew the difference between Halloween and Samhain. "No," James answered cheerfully. "Halloween's for candy and costumes, but we also celebrate our grandparents. They died saving dad." The blunt statement was concluded with a forkful of mashed potatoes. "Can I have more, mum?" Ginny was momentarily shocked, as was everyone else. "F-finish your vegetables." "Boo." But he forked the green beans anyway. Neville redirected the conversation. "Why'd you dress like a penguin, Al?" "So Mr Draco and I can match 'cause I'm his favourite." Dean nearly spit out his pumpkin juice. Daphne did. "I don't dress like that," Draco grumbled. Every eye shifted to him in varying shades of disbelief. "Of course not, darling." Narcissa smirked into her cup. Everyone else laughed. Albus reminded them all that Mr Draco hadn't said he wasn't his favourite, which only made the laughter grow louder, even from the kids, who mostly didn't know what they were laughing at. Except Scorpius. He appeared deep in thought, frowning in concentration so intense he'd stopped eating. His mood persisted, but when both Hermione and Draco asked if he was okay, he nodded with a cheerfulness that seemed hollow. Chaos returned after dinner when the kids made a game of taking turns running into the candy tent and grabbing everything they could before time was up. James came out with candy stuffed into his costume, Lily and Albus did, too, but after convincing Scorpius to remove his cactus hands in order to take his turn, he emerged with only a handful of candy: a single piece for each adult, even a puzzled Narcissa, and one for himself. "Thank you." Hermione offered support to fill the silence of everyone's confusion. "You share so well." Scorpius' soft smile brought out his dimples, made even more adorable by his costume. "You can get more than one piece of candy." Draco's voice was calm and his body relaxed, which prompted Scorpius to shuffle closer between his dad's legs and lean back on his knee. He opened his hand to reveal the individually wrapped lemon drop. "Go on." But Scorpius shook his head, signing for you and waiting with wide-eyed patience until Draco unwrapped his before his son did the same. Together, they ate their lemon drops, content in each other's presence in a way she hadn't observed thus far. And then he turned to her, waiting until she followed in kind, eating her piece as well. Jaw bulged in contentment, he reached for her hand while snuggling against his father's chest. It was always the little things that meant the most to him. You should think this through, her mind said. But this could be yours, her heart reminded her. Hermione cupped Scorpius' cheek and stopped, smile dimming as a profound sense of belonging shielded her thoughts. She lifted her eyes to find Draco watching her. And even as her heart and mind argued on, she tuned them out. The tired cogs in her mind slowed to a halt as the urge to cup his cheek and speak stretched and softened around her. Unplanned words were interrupted by Lily's high-pitched squeal at being picked up by Neville's branches. Scorpius abruptly left in Daphne's direction in a move that jarred them both, but before Hermione could say anything, Draco excused himself and went back inside, running his hands through his hair several times in what looked like frustration. She remained as she was until her heart stopped racing. The next few hours were spent drifting from conversation to conversation about any and everything. More than once, someone asked how she was doing, but Hermione couldn't bring herself to lie, so she changed the subject while trying not to watch Draco as often as she caught him watching her. She listened to Andromeda's news on Teddy, who was passing potions, while Susan, a late arrival, massaged open her hand. That didn't stop her from noticing the way Draco's grumpy scowl ebbed and flowed like the tide whenever he caught Albus imitating his mannerisms with penguin wings before he rolled his eyes and taught the little boy how to properly scowl. Something he tried out on everyone. "I didn't expect that at all." Andromeda's eyes were alight with amusement. "Poor Harry." Poor Harry indeed. Her best friend looked as if every misfortune had rebounded on him all at once. Ginny sympathetically patted his shoulder and laughed at her husband's misery. Too humoured by the sight, Hermione didn't notice Scorpius and Daphne's approach until they were standing next to her. Then she saw the camera. "He wants a picture." Hermione's smile was true when she posed with the little boy dressed as his favourite plant, but things became more difficult when Scorpius scampered off, returning with his father and a signed request that left them both awkwardly staring at each other. A picture together. All three of them. Like a family. Hermione tried to rub away the strange feeling that only swelled with each passing moment. "I shouldÑ" "It's what he wants." Apparently, Draco hadn't been able to tell his son no. Not even for his own sake. Which was how they ended up standing beside each other with Scorpius grinning between them. Draco's hand was a ghost on her back as they posed. She could feel the periphery of his presence, but it never manifested in a solid sensation. Close but distant. Boundaries up. Gods, Hermione had never been more tempted to fabricate a reason to lean into him. But she didn't. Couldn't. To love was to respect, and she did so without hesitation. But still, it was unnerving to be so used to someone. To be aware enough to notice the littlest changes and shifts in them. To be used to their silence and moods, the dichotomy of messy handwriting versus meticulous ways. Wearing a smile was as difficult as processing the realisation that it was possible to miss a routine, be lonely for a time, and be homesick for people. When Scorpius and his appointed camerawoman left in the direction of a waiting Albus, she found herself alone with Draco for a second time. He didn't immediately leave, nor were they interrupted, and despite the need to speak, it was difficult to swallow uncertainty and find something to say. Conversation had never been this hard. Not even in the beginning. "Have you slept?" Draco tensed at the question. "Not well. The sofa wasn't as comfortable as I remember." Despite his honesty, grey eyes went in all directions except hers. It was as intentional as the hands he'd slid into his pocket. She wondered if he did it to keep himself from touching her. "Did you?" "A little this morning in Kingsley's guest room. IÉ" Hermione folded her arms and looked around. The strange sight of a cactus and a penguin hugging while Daphne snapped multiple pictures wasn't enough to keep her silent. "I don't know how to do this." "Neither do I." Noise from music and conversation persisted long enough for the photo session to conclude between the best friends and they watched together in silence. Scorpius' smile faded once again as the thick fog of his mood rolled back in. Her instinct was to go to him, but she remained where she was, watching to try and figure him out. Scorpius approached Harry, who was talking to Neville, and signed something that appeared to confuse both men. Hermione hadn't caught it either, but beside her, Draco tensed in realisation. He said nothing, but his eyes followed his son as he practically fled in Albus' direction. And past him. Albus became a waddling blur of flapping wings as he took off after his best friend, caught up, and remained at his side, mouth moving in words Hermione couldn't hear over the music. "Excuse me." Draco stepped back and turned away before walking casually in the direction of his son, who was unknowingly on a path that led straight to him. Approaching both boys, a knee in the grass and beckoning gestures brought them to his side. He spoke calmly to Albus, who saluted with a wing and ran off, past her, and to his dad's side, bouncing from foot to foot impatiently until Harry addressed him. A wing was thrown in their direction and Harry approached, but Albus bounced over to where his mother was talking to Daphne. The universal sign of needing to go to the bathroom made Ginny chuckle and take him inside. Draco was still talking and signing to Scorpius, who looked a bit distressed. What was wrong? "Albus said you wanted something, but then he announced he had to use the loo before he could say what." Judging by Harry's level of amusement, those hadn't been his exact words. But then he sobered and nodded in Draco's direction as he opened his hand to his son. "Did he tell you?" "Tell me what?" "Malfoy mentioned you stayed at home last night, but I don't think you should. Not yet. Wards aside, the area has had more sightings. News of an animal attack the night of the last full moon even made the Muggle news. We're investigating." Her blood went cold. "I did stay at home last night, but I won't again until I'm well enough to defend myself should I need to." "The objective is to see that you don't get to that point." He would have said more but thumbed in the direction of the two Malfoys. "What's going on there?" "No idea. He's been in an odd mood off and on since dinnerÉ" Hermione trailed off when whatever Draco had said was enough for Scorpius to slowly place his hand into his father's. An oddly touching sight they made. Rising to his feet, he led the wary little boy back to them. Beneath the party atmosphere and the sound of James laughing, serious blue eyes peered up at Harry as he let go of his father's hand. When Draco pressed an encouraging hand on his son's back, he finally signed. Are you okay? Harry knew a few words, but not that question. History made him turn to Hermione for answers. "He wants to know if you're okay." She sounded as perplexed as Harry looked. "What is it, Scorpius?" The next two words he signed served as a reminder that Scorpius internalised everything until whatever was he holding in had to come out. Mother. Father. More confused looks came from Harry, but before Draco could interpret, Hermione decided it would be best for this conversation to take place away from the party. Draco made an odd sight, sitting on the glass table with Scorpius dressed as a cactus standing beside him, squarely focused on Harry next to her on the sofa. "SoooooÉ" Harry stretched the word as much as he could with an inflection that would have made any one of his three kids giggle. But not Scorpius. After exchanging looks with Draco, Hermione bridged the gap. "He remembers what James said at the table about your parents. He wants to know if you're okay." Realisation dimmed Harry's smile, but he didn't answer right away, instead smoothing a hand over his beard. It was hard to determine if Draco's tension came from Harry's silence or his own helplessness. "That shocked you, didn't it?" Scorpius nodded slowly, finally expressing just how troubled he was by what he'd learned so bluntly. "James has heard stories of their bravery, but I imagine you see it differently, right?" Loss. Harry's question was accompanied by a gesture inviting Scorpius closer. It wasn't until the little boy was standing in front of him and staring at his hands that Harry continued. "I am okay, thank you for asking. I don't remember my parents because it happened when I was a baby." If at all possible, the new information made things worse. Deeply affected, Scorpius curled his fingers and blinked several times in the sort of distress that made Draco shift close enough to place that reassuring hand on his back. Still, his hands shook and his eyes blurred with distraught tears as he signed another question Harry looked to Hermione to interpret. But she was too lost for words. Draco took over. "Do you miss them?" "Some days more than others." Harry chose his words carefully. "But on days like today, I'm okay because I know they aren't gone. Not really. They live here." He brought a hand to his heart, so focused on Scorpius, he didn't notice the tears in his own eyes. "And as long as they're there, I'll never forget." After a moment's hesitation, he leaned closer to a child who knew a similar sort of loss and bore a pain that would never truly relent. Bound by the onset of a grief that shaped them into children who knew too much too soon about death's permanence and how it shaped each person differently. Harry's had once amounted to bitterness and anger, but Scorpius' made him compassionate and empathetic. Determined to help anything that struggled as he once had. "As long as your mum is here." Harry picked up Scorpius' hand and placed it against his heart, covering his little fist with his palm. "You'll never forget her, not even when you're as old as I am. She will always be with you." Scorpius smiled through falling tears. And when he sobbed openly and freely, Hermione knew it wasn't out of sadness. It was relief. November 1, 2011 In a bed her body recognised, sleep was even more elusive. It was hard to ignore what was missing in the familiar place. The lack of presence at her back or warmth in the sheets. All the things she never needed before but now couldn't seem to close her eyes without. Hours had passed since they silently fell back into their normal routine of reading Scorpius a bedtime storyÑa silent agreement made that nothing with him would change, even if everything between them had. She hoped they were better actors, but when Scorpius had insisted on holding both their hands while Draco read about a lost pygmy puff, she worried he had noticed the shift in equilibrium. She certainly had. Draco disappeared the moment Scorpius was sleeping deeply enough for him to pry his hand away and answer a call made from Harry that had come through an hour before. That left Hermione with her normal task of tucking him in and turning the projector on. Counting the constellations, she stopped at thirteen and ventured back to the room where she found she was only able to sit on the bed. She couldn't lie down. Not without him. It was nearly midnight when Hermione left the room, wandering aimlessly until she stood outside of Draco's closed office door. The wards didn't reject her, so Hermione ventured in slowly, peeking around for signs of life. She found none aside from the map on the table, where the diamonds were slowly spinning. And the Pensieve. More than ever, it called to her, and Hermione answered. Drifting to the onyx bowl, she ran her fingers along the waxy rim then down to the rune marking. A wisp of silver swirled in the bowl. The truth was too built up in her mind, and she was ready to see for herself. This was a terrible idea, but Draco's reticence and her own mounting curiosity made her reckless in a way she hadn't been for a while. Like navigating her feelings, she had to walk this careful journey through her memories. And she had to do it alone. Hermione shook the nerves from her hands, lowered her face into the bowl, and plunged into the darkness, through ice, then landed at the start. The alarms. In the chaos that followed, Hermione remembered little, but now, as an observer, she was horrified by what Scorpius had heard in those initial moments. She watched each of his fearful breaths. The way he clutched her tight. She couldn't focus on it, but a visceral exhale of relief swept through her when Scorpius vanished. She was heartbroken by the image of him desperately reaching for her. Without the little boy in the room, her attention was drawn elsewhere, and this vantage point gave her the perfect sight of Theo's palpable relief just before the door shattered to pieces. Heart hammering as each step brought her to the inevitable. He's looking for you. The explosion still made her jolt, and she winced at the sight of her body hitting the wall. Trapped in the moment, Hermione waved a hand in front of her face to clear the vision of dust from her memories as she watched herself struggle to her feet. Slowly. After that, she was lost. Blurring together, reality and memory collectively swept her into the horror of each moment. Tagging Death Eaters with letters. Running into Padma and rolling with each following moment. Panic pounded in her veins and fear roared in her ears as she watched them run from Greyback. For the first time, she truly looked at him. More animal than man, it was evident there was intelligence in his predatory focus. A terrifying gleam lit his eyes. Padma had been right to worry about them splitting up. The chase that led to the second explosion made Hermione clench her sideÑthe memory of pain from pulling the glass shard out all over again. Dizzy from anxiety by the time Greyback caught her, tears fell freely as she watched him shatter her wrist with effortless force before he licked her tears. It was hard to watch herself struggle, kick helplessly, then offer herself in exchange for Padma's safety. A phantom pull left her nauseous and disoriented. Time raced by in swirling patterns that painted the sides of her vision, but then it slowed. A room. A shack. Faces. Everything stopped long enough for her to focus on each blur. Heart and head pounding, sick from the sensation, Hermione closed her eyes for a second then looked again. Harder. The first face was one she didn't recognise. The second was the blurred image of a person she'd seen up close for the first time at fifteen: Rodolphus Lestrange. And the third was someone she'd last seen in her Summons. A familiar face. Cormac. Hermione launched herself from the memory, gasping and choking as she collapsed on her hands and knees. Pain deafened everything. She choked on air and dry heaved so hard her muscles clenched with an unforgiving strength. Beneath the roaring in her ears, she heard someone cursing. Tears fell without care or permission. Her wrist buckled and gave, but instead of hitting her head on the floor, hands surrounded her. Her ear pressed against the chest of someone whose heart was pounding hard enough for her to feel it against her cheek. "Breathe." She did just that, reorienting herself to reality through the firm ground and the hands on her. Concerned grey eyes searched her face for evidence of a trauma he could not see, a raw wound bleeding beneath her skin. "I told you not toÑ" "I saw everything." November 3, 2011 With the addition of the Apothecary's Rose, there was officially no more space in the greenhouse. Hermione walked the pathway with Neville that led through the rich foliage as she absorbed this realisation. Potted plants covered the once empty tables, overcrowded trellises, and enough of them hung from the ceiling to block a portion of the plants from direct sunlight. Everything was still bursting with colour and life during a time of year most were usually dormant. Full tables served as a reminder of March when she'd daydreamed of this moment. "You should strongly consider expanding." "Or you could stop storing all your plants from school here." Neville tilted his head to the side. "None of these came from me." "Sorry, what?" "I thought you knew, but clearly you don't." His expression shifted to faint amusement. "Interesting." "What's interesting? And you thought I knew what?" "I thought you had coordinatedÑokay, never mind. I'll just tell you." He gestured around the room. "All the new plants came from the Malfoy Manor's greenhouse. Malfoy adjusted the blood wards to include me. We're related through my great-great grandmother's sister. He had explicit instructions to begin moving everything here." A gasp escaped. "When?" "End of August." Neville looked more and more curious. "I really thought you knew." "I didn't." "I didn't know things between you two were serious enough for him to move in." "He's not. He'sÑhe knows I wanted to fill this place with everything beneficial to brew. I told him as much." Once during his first visit, then again in the last month of sharing bits and pieces of herself she hadn't shared with anyone else. Hermione was not only stunned by what or how much Draco had retained, but also by how long he'd been quietly piecing the bits of her together. "He certainly made that a reality." Neville looked around, lips curling into a gentle smile at the life around them. Excitement grew the more he spoke. "I've taken cuttings of plants not seen in nature for well over a hundred years, and I've let my students study how to propagate them. He even gave me a book of seeds and asked me to figure out how to grow each. Some are from plants that haven't existed in generations." Hermione was speechless. "IÑthe Head of Herbology has taken an interest in the project of reintroducing the plants into nature and studying their magical and mundane properties, but we don't have a dedicated space yet to cultivate them." The more they walked, the more she turned over the new information in her head. "Why would he do all of this?" "Perhaps he's preserving his legacyÉ or maybe he's making one of his own." Now's your chance to grow something all your own. Hermione pressed a hand over her heart. Her words and thoughts ached to spill forth, but they never got a chance because an adorably goggled Scorpius peeked his head around the corner, then waved a mitted hand. Still an adorable alien, only now Narcissa was with him holding Mint. The way he greeted Neville showed a warm respect, but the way Neville took a knee spoke of his own fondness for the boy. Neville held his hand up, giving Scorpius an expectant look that made him scrunch his little nose and hesitate for a second before placing his mitted hand against Neville's open palm in the slowest high five she'd ever seen. Neville grinned like they'd crossed some invisible bridge Hermione didn't know existed. "Apologies for our tardiness," Narcissa said by way of greeting before handing the cactus to Neville once he stood back up. "My grandson insisted that Meda put on his mittens and goggles before we left her to make lunch so he couldn't carry this." "Safety first." Scorpius dimpled. "Are you working today?" Neville asked. "No, just delivering Scorpius. I am returning home to lie down." Only when she brought her hands together did Hermione see the tremble. "It has not been a good day." Neville took that moment to ask Scorpius if he wanted to check on the daylily, which had sprouted just before the wedding, much to his excitement. He took every opportunity to see how it had grown and changed since his last visit. When Neville and Scorpius turned the corner, Hermione looked back at Narcissa. "Do you want to talk?" "Not particularly, but make certain Mr Longbottom does not prune today. He has improved in that regard, but I would like him to use a different method on the trees." "Okay." "When do you plan to expand the charms?" The question was directed at Hermione. "I know you are hesitant to do much in your current condition and expanding it further would make it difficult to tend to alone, but you are no longer alone, Miss Granger." "IÑ" She folded her arms. "Neville has more responsibilities than tending my garden. IÑ" "You are good at making assumptions without being in possession of the facts. Mr Longbottom isn't the only person who tends to your plants." "Draco?" She'd already been surprised so many times by him. "He is far too busy." Before Hermione could ask anything else, Narcissa said she was exhausted and took her leave. Momentarily dazed, Hermione shook it off and found her way back to where Neville and Scorpius were huddled around her work table in the corner, the latter on a stool so he could observe. When she joined them, Neville stepped away to find the proper soil. "Do you know what we're doing with Mint today?" Hermione asked Scorpius. "Repotting," Scorpius answered softly. Each word he gave her meant the world, and observing the progression of his verbal confidence was nothing short of amazing. He tapped the new pot with his gloved hand. It was old and vaguely familiar, but she couldn't put her finger on where she'd seen it before. "Exactly." Hermione grinned at him. "Do you know why we do this?" "To make room." "You're right." Hermione paused. "Did Mr Neville tell you that?" He nodded proudly. "First, we have our new potÑwhy did you pick this one? I thought you would want a blue one." Blue was his favourite colour, after all. "Daddy picked it." "Oh?" Neville returned wearing dragonhide gloves, interrupting any further inquiries with a bag of coarse dirt. He got to work, breaking up the soil with a trowel along the edges before moving it aside, and explaining everything as he went. Neville let Scorpius assist with preparing the new home for Mint, scooping out the new dirt and filling the pot with the perfect amount. There was more dirt on the workstation than inside the pot, but despite his blush at the accident, he only looked happy as he pressed on. "Is this an old pot?" "From the Malfoy greenhouse actually." Neville peered inside. "Runes at the bottom make it change colours when it's time to water or when it needs care. They also protect it when needed. I've never seen anything like it, but every pot from there has these charms." The fact that Draco had chosen to put Mint in it above anything else was exclusively for Scorpius' sake. "When you repot, make sure the cactus is at the same depth it was in the old pot." Neville walked him through each step as they worked. "You don't want to change it too much. It can scare it." Scorpius watched closely as Neville extracted the cactus from the pot with care. He looked more nervous than she felt. Neville was mindful of his actions, dusting the excess soil from around the roots. "Normally, we wouldn't repot this early, but you've done such a good job taking care of him that he's outgrown his home." Hermione couldn't ignore the parallels. "You should be so proud of yourself." And he was. His smile was lit with confidence. When Neville placed it in the dirt, Scorpius got right to work filling the space around it. "Next we'll put it back on its stand and let it get used to its new home. Then, in a week, he'll need to be watered. Sounds good?" Scorpius looked at the old pot, frowning as he held it the same way he had so many times before. It was half-full of old dirt but Hermione could understand the attachment. "Just because we change pots doesn't mean that we get rid of the old one in favour of the new. We keep it so we can use it again. This will become a new home for another plant." "Maybe one of your rehab plants, then." Scorpius brightened at Neville's suggestion, and accepted Hermione's offered hand, making sure he stepped off the stool carefully. Scorpius jumped and looked like he wanted to do it again before smiling bashfully at her, but it was okay, her smile matched. He was more playful in small actions than she'd ever seen him before. "All finished." "I'd have to move things around andÑ" All heads turned when Padma and Luna walked in, shaking the chill from their bodies as they quickly acclimated to the warmth. Hermione called to the three that she was going to walk Padma back to the house and to bring Scorpius in to eat when they finished. The walk back was quick. The brisk air served as a shock to Hermione's system after being in the warm greenhouse, but it didn't last. The conservatory was warm, too. Daphne and Pansy were seated on the sofa, and when Andromeda walked in, she backed out. It took exactly one glance and two seconds for Hermione to figure out why. Pansy looked mutinous. And Hermione knew better than to charge headfirst into that storm. "Wasn't expecting a visit." "We actually just got here," Daphne said pleasantly, but there was a warning in her tone. "Have you both eaten?" "No, but I'm not hungry." Pansy didn't bother to hide her ire as she looked around. "Are you staying here now?" "No." Even if she were at the point when she could, a growing doubt existed where none had before. Home was nice and familiar, but at its core, things were different. Felt different. There was a piece missing, and no matter how many nights she laid awake, she couldn't blot out the truth. "What's wrong, Pansy?" Because it made little sense to mince words. "You're in a mood." "I told you once that in a fight between you and Draco, I would pick him every time. Well, today I asked him how things were going with you, and he shut down and refused to talk to me. So we're going to discuss this orÑ" "We're not going to discuss this because what happened is between the two of us. "Actions always speak louder." November 5, 2011 While Padma's first shift had gone smoothly, this one had been one difficult case after the next where they had to work together to figure out each diagnosis.
