The decor was changed from the ceiling all the way down to the floors. She wondered if he'd found it just as difficult to sit in there as Hermione thought she would, but there was no time to reflect. Teddy was stiff, but his emotions were on display through the wild array of hair colours as Andromeda carded her fingers through the strands to comfort him. Scorpius was harder to read, looking anxiously at his cousin when his father signed to ask if he was okay. Andromeda stood and gestured to the other side of the office for them to follow. Draco led the way, but Hermione carefully stooped before the boys. Scorpius scooted closer to Teddy in clear concern for his cousin, whose silence contrasted the chaotic cycling of his hair. When Hermione cupped Teddy's cheek, a rebel tear finally slid free as Teddy closed his eyes and leaned into her touch. "IÑ" He swallowed thickly. "I tried to help. Nan said I did, butÉ" A few more tears broke free before he whispered, "I was scared." "Just like there's happiness in sadness, there's bravery in being afraid." Hermione soothed the teenager struggling to hold back a sob, swiping a thumb over his cheek. Her eyes slid to Scorpius, who was studying his hand, opening and closing his fist. She started to speak to him, to check how he was feeling, but Scorpius looked up. "Mum told meÉ" Scorpius' small voice broke the silence. "It's okay to be sad." "That's right, love." Hermione reached for Scorpius' hand. "We all get scared. I'll tell you a secret: I'm scared, too. It's normal. I don't know what happened yet, but I'm really proud of you both because you're here right now and you're being really, really brave." Teddy looked at his cousin, then her, before he wiped his face and caught his breath as his hair began to settle. Hermione didn't understand the context behind the teenager's fear just yet, but she gave each boy a piece of candy and left to join the others. When she looked back, she found Theo in Andromeda's former seat next to his godson. Only then did she notice the reverence on his face, likely for the same reason a flush pinkened Scorpius' cheeks. She often forgot the gift of the little boy's voice was still new for so many in his life. Offering them the privacy they deserved, Hermione turned her attention to those waiting for her. "This morning didn't start out well." Andromeda ran a shaking hand through her hair. "Narcissa was a bit listless after dressing for brunch, so Teddy kept her company while I got Scorpius ready. Charles and Keating were discussing the incident over breakfast when Teddy called for us all in a panic." "Did anything unusual happen?" Draco asked. "Oh. She had some difficulty swallowing tea." Which was both a new issue and a hint of the disease's progression. The tension on Draco's face was a reminder of the research he'd done on his own. He knew. "When we came in, Teddy said she'd alerted him." Andromeda's voice was thin yet steadyÑ the embodiment of her inner war between ingrained restraint and devastating emotion. "Narcissa couldn't speak, couldn't respond, and then she-she fainted. Charles said her heart rate was low-too low. So he brought her here. She's stable for now, but completely disengaged. She doesn't remember anything or anyone. Not even me." Narcissa always remembered Andromeda. "Charles asked me to wait here while he and Roger ran tests. He found a bruise and a little blood on the back of her head. At some point this morning, she must have hit her head on something and didn't tell anyone. He said a concussion would explain her symptoms." Hermione looked at Draco, who seemed to be listening with rapt attention. Like a sponge, he was absorbing her words with little reaction. "I'd like to see her." Hermione recognised the storm. She wanted nothing more than to calm it. "What room is she in?" "Private Suite One." They peered over at the two boys and Theo, who was speaking very calmly to Teddy. Scorpius' head was barely visible over the back of the sofa, but what they could see of it was leaning on his cousin's shoulder. Hermione was the one to break the news. "We'll be right back, okay?" When Scorpius began to move, she placed a hand on his knee before signing as she said, "Stay with Teddy. Keep him company. He's better with you here, right?" Teddy nodded. Scorpius agreed slowly. Hermione kissed both of their foreheads then headed for the door, turning just in time to catch Draco ruffling Teddy's hair when it flashed Draco's shade before finally settling into a deep turquoise. He signed to Scorpius, gave Theo and Andromeda looks, and joined her. Their walk was slow as Hermione stifled her own growing thoughts and tried to focus on steadying him. She clipped her identification badge onto her dress just in time for a Mediwitch to come around the corner. When Draco was asked for identification, Hermione stepped in with ease. "That won't be necessary. I'm escorting Mr Malfoy to Private Suite One." The witch allowed them to pass. The door to Narcissa's room opened just as Hermione was poised to knock. "I'm glad you're both here." Charles didn't seem surprised to see either of them. "How is she?" Draco asked. "Stable and resting, but refusing to comply. Roger is finishing and Sachs is trying to convince her to eat something so we don't have to take alternative methods to address her low blood sugar." Charles tucked his parchment and wand into his robe and looked back and forth between them. "Do you both have a moment? We need to talk." Hermione didn't like the sound of that, but she followed as Charles led them both to his office. Next to her, Draco was stone-faced, devoid of emotions except the slight shake of his hand when their fingers brushed. They were in public and he was Occluding, but she wanted him to knowÑshe needed to remind himÑthat she was still there. Beyond the wall built for self-preservation, she was standing by his side. She was on his side. With him. Always. Their eyes met, and for a moment his cleared, focused, before he gave her a little nod. Something inside of her unfurled before they all entered a smaller room. An office. Charles' office. There were boxes packed in the corner, things he'd brought to start one position but had no time to unpack or sort before beginning another. His desk had a few personal effects, and six neat stacks of patient folders, but little else filled the space. Charles retrieved the parchment from earlier and floated it between them where they could all see. "These are her scans from today." A moment of searching his desk later, then a second parchment joined the first. Draco squinted while Hermione dug around for a spare pair of reading glasses she'd started carrying in her bag. Draco seemed surprised when she offered them to him. "These are from October when she started the experimental potion." A few more parchments joined the first two. "These range from November through December." Several more lined up in a neat row. "And these track each month since March, when Miss Granger did her baseline tests." Hermione had to step closer to see the differences, but they were there. "These results areÉ unexpected." "How so?" Draco asked. Charles opened a folder on his desk, charming another set of results to blur the patient's name before they joined the hovering display and arranged themselves beneath Narcissa's. Hermione looked at each, not seeing the difference in the scans untilÑ Well, the scan for November was different. As was December's. And today's. Hermione's interest was piqued. "When we began the experimental potion at the end of October, I said she was at the midway point of the disease. Her scans were identical to the patient I used as a baseline in my research. The new symptom today confirms my belief. It's normal to oscillate between phases, but when comparing results, IÑ" He kept up September's scan through the most recent for both patients and waved off the others. "See here. If everything had continued along similar trajectories, all of these should look identical." But they didn't. Not exactly. Hermione held her breath. "Does that meanÑ" "I'm not sure what this means exactly, only that she was progressing as anticipated until November. Now, the rate of decline doesn't align. In fact, she's trending a little over a month behind, give or take a week in some scans." Charles pulled today's scan over to the scans of the patient that should have trended in line with Narcissa's November. Those were identical. "In factÑand I'll have to confirmÑbut I don't believe her results match any patient at her current phase. Additionally, I have noticed Narcissa doesn't have delusions. By this point, it's rare if a patient hasn't had at least one delusional incidentÑpersecutory, mainly, but your mother only has hallucinations." Draco took off his glasses and pinched the bridge of his nose. "What does this mean?" "Today was the first incident of this kind. We'll keep monitoring her, and I don't plan to alter anything because it can be directly attributed to a physical cause. Keep brewing the experimental potion and propagating the ingredients. I don't want to speak too soon, but I have a suspicion that something in this potion is slowing the progression of her disease, even if only slightly. I don't know what, why, or how, but the scans are rather convincing." "Theoretically, if we isolate the components, and figure it out, we couldÉ" Hermione was surprised by the swell of emotion at the thought as it all came together. "Slowing it down is just the first step. Eventually, we might be able to find a way to reverse it." "Only in theory right now." Charles tapped his wand on the parchment. It glowed, highlighting the parts of Narcissa's scan where her brain was progressing differently than the other patient's. "We'll continue to perform routine scans, but this potion is more promising than anything else I've encountered in my years in this field." "We can ask the research team to begin studying the potion, and isolating the compound effects of each ingredient. Draco, what do youÑ" All of Hermione's racing thoughts screeched to a halt when she noticed Draco's hands clutched behind his back. He was comparing the two scans that had sparked the initial excitement, yet he seemed troubled. Hermione said his name, but Draco looked away and down, turning his back to them and the scans. "It's too late for her." His voice was toneless, defeated, as if the truth of something he'd known for weeks and months was sinking in all over again. "Even if we isolate the cause of the unexpected results, she's progressed too far." Hermione and Charles exchanged looks. More experienced with comforting the families of patients, he took the lead. "It is easy to focus on what will be lost, but your mother is still here, and she needs you now more than ever." Draco exhaled a slow breath, nodding solemnly. "We will need to discuss care going forward," Charles said after a moment of silence. "I believe the move to Andromeda's home has been for the best. The area is smaller and there are less ways for Narcissa to injure herself, but her fall today means she can no longer be alone. Some new safety measures must be enacted as well. No travelling by Floo alone, not even to familiar places. We will charm the floors to stop her from falling and take other preventative measures. Believe it or not, Occupational Therapy has been helpful. She knew to alert Teddy that something was wrong." "What does she want?" Draco's question surprised them both. "She's not giving up," Charles said with conviction. "Her mood today is the result of what has happened and not her overall feelings on the matter. She wants to continue working to maintain her quality of life for as long as possible, and I intend to continue working with her." "How long do you think she has?" "Until today, I would have given her another year." Charles pulled down two of the scans and looked them over. "However, should she maintain this slightly slower rate of decline, I could safely add a few more months. It's hard to say anything definitively right now. I'll need time to sit down and compare these cases, quantify the changes. Knowing the potion is slowing the disease is one thing, butÑ" "It's still a progressive disease." Inevitable. "That's correct." Charles approached Draco, carefully placing a hand on his shoulder. "I've admitted Narcissa. She'll need to stay for a few days for observation, as she's refusing to eat. You two should go be with her, help her relax, and try to get something into her system." They left to aid in those efforts, and Hermione reached for Draco's hand as they stood in front of the door to Private Suite One. "Do you need a moment alone?" "No." They entered together to find Narcissa alone and awake, staring in the direction of the window with the shades pulled back. She was far paler than Hermione had ever seen her, looking frail in the hospital bed. Hermione peeked at the parchments monitoring Narcissa's vitals while Draco took the lone seat, then his mother's hand. His touch sharpened Narcissa's focus, but her eyes were still glassy. "Mother." "Draco." She tried to sit up, but he stopped her. "You are here." "I am." He seemed to exhale the same relief Hermione felt at Narcissa recognising him. "Rest." The snap of Hermione's heels on the floor made Narcissa turn to her, taking in her attire first. "Are you a Healer?" Apparently, Hermione's identity was temporarily lost. "I am, but I'm off duty. I was just reviewing your vitals." She offered a patient smile, feeling as mixed as she often did when Narcissa forgot her. "I'm Hermione Granger. It's a pleasure to meet you, Mrs Malfoy. I'm going to sit in the corner and monitor you, if that's okay?" "Yes." Hermione swiped the folder Roger had left behind and took a seat, reading his notes while Narcissa's tired blue eyes focused on her only son. "I wish you were angry with me, Draco," she whispered sadly. "I wish we were still fighting." "Why?" His voice cracked with a torrent of emotions that battered the walls he'd built so high. "So you would not look at me like you are now." "I was angry, for years, even after Astoria told me to let it go for my sake." Remorse reappeared. Narcissa closed her eyes and bore its weight. "We are doing better, but I fear you think it is too late for us." Draco only looked down in response. His mother let go of his hand, bringing hers to his cheek and slowly caressing it. "I am still here, Draco." Narcissa spoke as firmly as her frail body would allow. "Try not to look at me like I am already gone." Draco leaned into his mother's touch as if it were all he could do. His jaw tensed just enough to seal in his thoughts, but two whispered words slipped through. "I'm trying." The arrival of Padma and Theo could be explained, but Parvati being between them meant only one thing. Trouble. They entered the room one by one. Arms folded, Draco's stance spoke of preparation for anything, but his eyes lacked the same fortitude. He was tired. Hermione remained where she was, but closed Roger's notes; she'd been too distracted to read them. The way Padma looked at Parvati to begin made even Narcissa take notice and sit up In the end, it was Theo who cleared his throat. "I've already terminated the source of the leak, but the information is out." Draco stiffened. "What information?" "I was called into work to write a major story that's set to break." Parvati didn't hesitate. "Skeeter wants it released in the evening edition to beat the rest of the papers set to release in the morning." She stepped closer. "It's about you, Mrs Malfoy. As of tonight, your illness will no longer be a secret from the public." Narcissa said nothing. "How much will it take to kill the story?" Draco looked ready to hand over as many Galleons as it took. "The leaker was paid four thousand Galleons for the information in a bidding war that The Daily Prophet won. The other papers still know enough to publish their own version of the truth." "What information leaked exactly?" Hermione left the folders behind and stood at the end of the bed. "Is it something that can be disproven?" "We received copies of the records of today's incident. IÑ" Parvati shoved her hands in her pockets. "I can say no to writing the article, but I have no access to steal the information back or erase it. There are copies and too many people know." It had already spread beyond the point of containment. "The hospital will take legal action on the leak for releasing confidential medical records." Theo was seething. "Their licence will be suspended, and they'll be forced to pay back what they made plus more in penalties." Hermione's mind was racing. "As far as stopping the news from getting out, you would have to move quickly and draw papers for approval toÑ" "No." Narcissa's lone word silenced the room. "That will not be necessary." "ButÑ" Theo's argument fell on deaf ears. "Pride cometh before the fall, but more than that, it is time to face this." Poised in spite of her undone state and current condition, Narcissa's resolve was as strong as her spirit. "I have hid this for a long time. For what reason? To save face? Fear of pity or stigma?" "It's none of their business," Draco argued. "No, it is not, and TheoÑdo what you must to punish the person who violated my privacy, but I cannot run from this. I cannot hide. And I will not." "You don't have to do this." Hermione moved to Draco's side. "We can cover this up. We can Ñ" "Who am I to you?" Narcissa's eyes narrowed at her. It wasn't rude, only curious. "You fight like I mean something to you." "You do." Hermione placed a hand on her frail leg covered in blankets. "You should be allowed to accept your disease without the world knowing, without the public making profit off of your life, withoutÑ" "Why? When I die, rumours will swirl and bury the truth. Right now, I have just as little control over the muscles of my body as I do over how I will die. But this I can control. I have already told the story of my life, now I would like to tell the story of what will ultimately cause my deathÑat least while I still can." Blue eyes shifted from Hermione to Parvati. "Will you help me?" "I will." Parvati ignored everyone's looks except Narcissa. "This is your story to tell, not theirs." "Thank you." Narcissa inhaled. Draco seemed torn, but he nodded. "What do you need, Patil?" Parvati patted her bag. "I have everything, but once I finish interviewing her, I'll need a quiet place to work on the article. It's due in two hours." "You can use my office," Padma offered before leaving to update the others. Draco began to leave with Hermione and Theo, but Narcissa stopped him with one request. "Stay." Some stories were easy to tell, others were not. Hermione always considered Narcissa's dementia one of the most complex, but still, she thought Scorpius should have been told of it months before. It was a battle she had reluctantly recused herself from, though now she learned it was also one she didn't need to fight. Leaned against her old office door, she watched a father and his son connect. After Narcissa's interview, Padma had brought both Malfoys here and summoned them lunch trays, but while Scorpius was happily working his way through the options, Draco had yet to unwrap anything on his tray. He focused on cutting slices of apples for his son as he talked in plain terms about what happened earlier that day. Much like he had about Astoria and the playground attack, Draco had explained it to Scorpius in his own time, in his own way. And here he was, doing it again. "Do you remember when I said your grandmother was sick?" Scorpius nodded, accepting a slice and biting into it while Draco cut another. "Do you remember what you're supposed to do when something's wrong?" Get Mum, he signed comfortably. "That's right." Warmth swelled in Hermione's chest as she thought back to August when he knew to alert her that something was wrong. She remembered his lack of reaction each time Narcissa called him Draco or forgot who he was entirely. Maybe he'd been talking to Scorpius longer than she'd realised. The little boy couldn't possibly understand everything, but he knew enough. Which was more important than ever. "I can help." It was always Scorpius' first instinct. A tired smile curved the corners of Draco's lips. "You already do." "Like Teddy?" Draco nodded slowly. "Exactly like that." Scorpius was nearly finished with his tray when he signed a question instead of accepting another apple slice. Dad, are you sad? Momentarily taken aback, Draco blinked, but he never got a chance to answer. Hermione had been spotted. Not by him, but by Scorpius as he rushed to her side. All finished, she signed. Yes. Are you ready? Yes. For all of his eagerness and all the conversation they'd had about hospital rooms on the way to Narcissa's, Scorpius lingered by the doorway when they actually got there. Draco went to his mother's side, next to Pansy and behind where Teddy was seated on the edge of her bed. Hermione put the tangerines Scorpius had insisted on bringing from his tray in her bag for safe keeping and stayed with him. The little boy wasn't ready to enter and no one would force him. Draco was the epitome of patience. He waited until his son met his eyes before extending his hand as both a bridge and a lifeline, all in one simple gesture. Face devoid of everything except a careful contemplation, she now recognised it as an expression reserved for Scorpius alone. Each little step was heavy with hesitation, and his hand tightened in hers, but Scorpius bravely made his way to his father's side before taking his hand, too. Linked through the common thread of touch, with one little hand in each of their own, Hermione had another unexpected epiphany. Draco wasn't just being strong for his son, he was leaning on him, too. They were all drawing strength from one another. "Scorpius." Narcissa's voice was gentle. "I am fine." She offered a weak smile to the little boy who worried his bottom lip with his teeth before getting his father's attention and signing a question. Surprise flashed across Draco's face, but he didn't let it linger. "He wants to know if he can get on the bed with you." Narcissa didn't deny him. Scorpius joined Teddy and Draco filled the space between his mother and Pansy while the teenager peeled tangerines Hermione retrieved from her bag at Scorpius' request. The first slice offered to Narcissa was rejected. "I am not hungryÑ" She paused at the sight of an insistent pair of blue eyes. "Maybe I will try one." One turned into two. Then three. Pretty soon, Narcissa was accepting the cup of warm broth she'd previously turned down while Teddy prattled on from one topic to the next and Scorpius held her frail, petal-veined hand. It was nearly midnight when Hermione left Charles' office after reviewing countless case files, and she was greeted by Andromeda asleep in a reclining chair with her hand in Narcissa's. Teddy and Scorpius were curled up together on the sofa. Hours before, Ginny and Percy had come with dinner and left with Pansy. Kingsley and Draco were speaking by the window, the former placing a hand on the latter's shoulder. Before leaving, Kingsley fixed the blanket covering Andromeda, which roused her enough to notice him, and he placed a kiss on her hand as they exchanged soft words. At Hermione's first yawn, Andromeda insisted that she would stay, and they should take everyone home for the night. She woke Teddy first, then dragged Draco from the window to carry Scorpius, leading the way back to Theo's office, where the whirlwind had started. She made certain both boys were comfortable in their beds then checked on Draco, only to find him in the shower. She joined him and they bathed in silence. After, as they laid together, she let Draco Occlude without question, in search of the peace he so desperately needed. When she was nearly asleep, he got up and said he'd be right back. Half an hour passed. Then double that. Hermione discovered him by accidentÑlittle more than a figure in the middle of the pasture she'd noticed while searching the house. A barely noticeable breeze stirred trees and bushes as she walked out to join him. The moonlight highlighted what she'd seen rolling off him all day in waves. Weariness. Draco took a sharp breath when she wrapped her arms around him. "Talk to me." "I don't know what to say." The unexpectedly honest answer caused Draco to clear his throat. "I'm tired." "Then let it all go tonight. You should rest." Hermione laid her head against his chest. Soon, they were matching breaths. She was offering peace in the form of shared silence and the balm of quietly reminding him of something he'd taught her in one of her lowest moments. The bravery in fear. The strength in surrender. The catharsis found in trusting another. "You're tired and I don't think you've let yourself feel anything about this." She knew his silence was confirmation. "But you can't keep this inside." Not when it had been clawing its way out. Still, Draco stubbornly tried to fight it while looking to the skies, then again once they were inside on the ottoman covered in blankets. But, in the end, a lifetime of cracks connected and he shattered. Healing was a cyclical journey of destruction and repair. "I'll be here. I'm not going anywhere." January 2, 2012 Percy looked surprised when Hermione stepped out of the Floo. "I know you were expecting Draco, but he's resting." Hermione watched Percy settle back in his chair. "I saw your name on his Magi-Scheduler and thought I'd come in his place before I start my first day as temporary Undersecretary." But not before making breakfast for Teddy and Scorpius and taking the former to Harry and Ginny's to visit while the latter crawled into bed with his father and a fed puppy. She'd left Draco a note, along with a cup of tea, charmed to stay warm. "I'm glad to hear that." Percy waited until she took the seat in front of his desk. "I was prepared to argue if he'd come through. How is Narcissa?" "Better today, it seems." Hermione had made a quick stop by the hospital before meeting Percy. Narcissa still looked tired. She'd recognised Hermione, enough to mention, with a quirk of her lips, that their breakfast had been delivered by Kingsley himself, making Andromeda fuss while she smiled into her tea. "They're keeping her for a bit to monitor her concussion." "I read the article last night. It was poignant, if a little bittersweet." Hermione had read it this morning when Padma had delivered a copy. "They've re-printed it in the morning paper." Percy nodded to a stack of different publications on his desk. "Outside of Tiberius' trial in two weeks for treason before the Council of Magical Law, it's all anyone is talking about." "Theo's fielding everything." The news. The media. The requests for comment. And he would continue to do so, even after she was back at Andromeda's. Until she said otherwise. "What were you and Draco supposed to meet about?" "Today's agenda." "Which is?" "I've had to take a different route with Draco's translations and the law research, as there is no single law to restore order in one declaration. I was going to go in there with everything I had, ready for war, but Draco suggested another approach." "Let me guess." Hermione smirked. "A softer touch." Percy narrowed his eyes. She'd gotten it right in one. "Let's forget about a particular law and focus on the root of the Wizengamot's power." Hermione frowned. "This entire system isn't built to last on a long-term basis. They've pieced together odd self-serving laws as patches. It's restrictive, inhibits growth, encourages inequality, and ultimately creates puppet leaders like the one sitting in a cell next to Tiberius." "The purpose of the law that stripped the Minister position of its power was intended to be a temporary solution, but they've kept it in place and manipulated it." Percy's jaw tensed. "When the Wizengamot took control of the government, the reason why made sense. Initially, at least. Kingsley was the fifth Minister in four years. A group was thought to be more trustworthy than any individual. Until faith was restored in the head of government again, it was logical for the Wizengamot to step in as an impartial oligarchy." "They are anything but impartial." "Exactly." Percy stretched his neck from side to side. "The law was for ten years of Wizengamot intervention, but two-thirds had to agree to restore the Minister's power after that, and then the Chief Warlock had to sign off. But when ten years were up, things were worse than ever. We began pressing for a vote, but they refused. Which is why I started the restoration." Hermione let his words sink in. "I still find it all interesting." "What?" "Why has no one on the Wizengamot dug deeper into the laws that keep them in power? Why has no one thought to use their perceived power to access the law books at the Cambridge Magical Library, or even the archives in the Hall of Records? They're either unbelievably arrogant orÑ" "It likely has to do with the lack of an overt challenge. They got comfortable and complacent." When Percy folded his hands, Hermione knew she had his full attention. "But go on." "The continued existence of these law books and scrolls Draco found point to their failings. If they were smart, they would vote to abolish each measure of opposition as they're discovered and presented." "True, but they can't. They don't understand the implications of each law or their translations, nor do they know the foundations of the system they are sworn to uphold, no matter how terribly they seem to be at their duties. In the process, they could destroy themselves, and by extension, the government." "Self-destruction at will." Hermione toyed with her compass necklace. "Might not be such a bad thing." "No, maybe not. But we don't have time for a Ministry-wide scorched earth policy." "This is what you would have forced them into doing, had you decided to go in there and challenge everything all at once." She dusted a stray bit of puppy hair off of her pinstripe blouse. "The shutdown taught us they are more than willing to sacrifice to avoid losing." Percy's frown deepened in acceptance. She wasn't wrong, but there was more to consider. Hermione spent several minutes looking over his work, skimming the translated scrolls in Draco's terrible handwriting, and thinking about it while Percy sipped his water. "We have two options." Hermione stood up and paced the room. "We could subdue them with their own corruption or fight it by strengthening the laws that reduce the capacity for such corruption to happen again. We have the ability to establish a clear definition of the government's reach." "How would I do that?" "By giving them a reason to return the balance to the Ministry." Percy tapped his fingers against the desk. "If it becomes more of a burden than a benefitÉ" "Exactly." "They still won't agree. It would be easier to just walk in with demands andÑ" "We were successful once with that method, but only because we made a spectacle by bringing in the people. It was a power move, and I will admit it worked, but we can't keep making power moves." They had to be smarter, more subtle. "What is it that you need to accomplish first?" "I have a list, but right now I'm working on a way to reinstate the Public Information Services department. The last Minister dissolved it and turned public relations into something the Wizengamot Administration Services handled." Giving control of what the public was told to those corrupting the Ministry from the inside. "It would make the fight against the Death Eaters or any future threats more efficient, especially when it comes to issuing public warnings." Which had not been done for as long as Hermione could remember. If ever. Officially, the threat level was still listed as light green. But it was quite the opposite. Changing the official designation would raise budget allowances for security and public events would be handled differentlyÑlike Quidditch games. Which went against Tiberius' objective to eliminate his problems. "Okay." Every new piece that fell into place only served to reignite Hermione's anger, but she had to focus. "Let's start small, with something we can tackle immediately. What could circumvent this particular rule?" "That's the thing, this department is modern. There are no translated laws that apply." Hermione sat down and crossed her legs, leaning her elbow on the arm of the chair as she pondered through every option. "You could list out everything this department would be in charge of in excruciating detail like a First Year's Potions essay. If it's anywhere near as backed up as the rest of the Ministry, it would overload themÑespecially if they're all urgent. They'll reach a point where they have to agree to pass it back or risk being exposed for negligence under the new leadership." "That'sÑmorally grey. Not to mention manipulative." Percy pursed his lips. "But it might work." All for the greater good. "From there, you systematically peel away their power one unenforced law at a time. Draco's translated laws can reverse or override the measures they've put in place. Technically, you don't even have to do anything for them to work like that. All you have to do is point out the flaws in the laws they established without bothering to look to see what existed beforehand. By rooting out and exploiting the technicalities in the legal language that were never properly explored, and just pushed through for their own personal gain, you can use what they changed against them." Percy pinched the bridge of his nose. "It'll be a slow process." "But a systematic one they won't understand how to stop." Hermione shifted in her seat. "I think you know as well as I do that restoration was never going to be a single act." "I'm aware, but I'm also under a time restraint." "Oh?" Percy took a deep breath. "I'm resigning effective the first of February." "What?" "It wasn't my plan. Not something I thought I'd ever do." Percy looked around his office. "But sometimes in order to weed out corruption you need a person on the inside." "Oh." Hermione inhaled sharply. "You're joining the Wizengamot." "Yes." He nodded and it was hard to read his thoughts on the move. "Kingsley asked the night he took over. I'm assuming one of the fallen member's seats, and so are a few other members of the restoration." Hermione winced, still unimpressed with most of them. "I know what you're thinking, but I believe what we've uncovered has made them all more determined to correct the injustices from the inside." The additions, plus the change of heart from the weaker members, would be enough to make real change and fulfil their ultimate goal: restore balance to the Ministry. "What I'm doing now is preparing my successor to take up the cause once I leave. I plan to have everything I need from McLaggen before the trial begins." "And until then?" "I'm going to peel away the rot, piece by piece." Percy chuckled low. "And using your method, I might even enjoy it more than I thought." Hermione laughed with him, but caught sight of her watch, rising to her feet. Her first meeting was in ten minutes. When Percy stood as well and picked up his wand, she tilted her head. "I believe you need an escort, Madam Undersecretary." Hermione's arrival was met with a silence too deferential for her liking. After introducing herself to the twenty or so new faces and hugging Deloris, she shook Kingsley's hand and followed him to the office door already plated with her name. "I made certain no one removed anything that belonged to the prior Undersecretary," Deloris said with a smile. "Oh, and thank you for the balm. How did you know I was nearly out?" Hermione paused. She hadn't given DelorisÑoh. The level of competence required had one explanation: Draco. "Anytime." Hermione's slow smile took credit for something Draco would never admit to. "How have you been?" "Much better now that Kingsley is back and change is underway." "Deloris and her secretaries have been vital to the transition, leaving the offices preserved with locking charms and finding information." Kingsley gave her a patient look. "I will leave you in her capable hands. I have a full schedule today putting out bureaucratic fires. Welcome back. I believe there was something delivered for you." Delivered? When Kingsley was gone, Deloris took out her wand and aimed it at the door. "Outside of clearing the desk of documents and moving them to the chair, everything has been kept the same, as requested." Hermione pushed the door open and looked around. The office was larger than expected and cluttered to the point of obstruction. Parchments, scrolls, and folders commanded almost every surface, including the sofa, chairs, and side tables. There was a wall of bookshelves hidden by a line of folders stacked almost as high, but she couldn't focus on any of it. Not when her delivery was sitting in the middle of the only clean surface in the room. The desk held only a fresh roll of parchment, a few quills, and a note folded next to one last thing Hermione knew wasn't courtesy of the secretary standing next to her. A steaming cup of tea. "How long ago was this placed here?" She wandered to the desk and picked up the note. You left without this - DM Her suppressed smile faded when she noticed Deloris' curious stare. "Your house-elf Zippy delivered it five minutes before your arrival. A note from someone special, I take it?" "Yes." There was no sense in hiding it. "Well, I'll leave you to enjoy your tea and get set up. I've made certain no one will bother you today." She frowned in confusion. "Why not?" "You need time to settle in, dear." "No, I don't." Hermione picked up her tea and drew a long inhale of bold hibiscus. "There's work to do, people to meet, and things to figure out as I begin to navigate this role. Do you have a list of the candidates for various staffing positions?" Deloris looked rattled. "Y-yes. Of course, I do." "Could you get those for me?" Hermione drifted over to the chair, picking up a folder on the top of the stack. "Also, can we spare anyone to go through these with me?" As it turned out, they couldn't, but Hermione took the task between meeting individually with the staff, learning their names and roles, and creating an ever-growing to-do list for the coming days. The first few files weren't very interesting. Aside from being personnel files specifically requested by the Minister that hadn't been delivered prior to his arrest, she didn't notice anything alarming. She briefly wondered if they were suspected restoration members, but if that were the case, the old Minister was either an especially bad detective, or the restoration's influence was much broader than she knew. Honestly, either option was more than viable. Moving on, she drifted across the room to the first set of bookshelves and plucked a file from eye level. The whole world opened with one look. The prior Undersecretary might have been silently complicit, but they recorded everything. Every correspondence between them and the Minister or Tiberius. Every requestÑdenied, approved, or strategically ignored. Every deal made. Officially and otherwise. All in black and white. Right in her hands. Complaints from every department were recorded and compiled together, too, filling up an entire bookshelf on their own. Most were against Tiberius, Cormac, and the MinisterÑ everything from harassment to negligence to wrongful termination. Several members of the Wizengamot had formal complaints mixed in the stacks. Flipping through file after file, Hermione's heart ached for the people victimised for so many years by a broken system and the power hungry tyrants allowed to run it without oversight. It would take time to create a trend, but each piece of information would be invaluable in the coming months as PercyÑand then his successorÑdismantled them piece by piece and exposed their incompetence just as they deserved. By the time Hermione noticed the clock, hours had passed and she had a healthy stack of evidence that needed to be hand delivered to the legal department for review. While attempting to put them in some semblance of order, there was a knock on her door. Two raps. Then a third. Hermione hadn't heard that knock in a long time, but she knew who it belonged to. "Come in." In the doorway was Draco, as expected, but it was Scorpius who came bearing a small box of gifts. Everything was put on pause, and Hermione barely pushed her chair back before the excited boy was at her side. "Hi, love." She pressed a quick kiss to his temple and he preened. Draco did the same, albeit more subtly, when she rose to her feet and offered him the same greeting. It was new, not something they'd done in front of Scorpius, but natural nonetheless. She turned back to Scorpius, who wasn't paying them any attention in favour of finding an empty spot on her desk to put the box. "What's all this?" "A few things for your new office." Draco leaned against the side of her desk and peeked at the empty teacup. A smile ghosted his lips. "Scorpius insisted." His accusation was confirmed with an enthusiastic nod. Hermione cleared a spot for him and glanced over at Draco. "Better today?" "Yes." Satisfied, Hermione proceeded to give her attention to Scorpius, who was bouncing from foot to foot. He really wanted her to see everything. A cutting of bamboo. A picture of them from Christmas. Artwork chosen from the wall of their home office. And a familiar glowing potato. Hermione set each item in their new place and thanked Scorpius with a hug. When they were all safely secured on her desk just to his liking, he finally broadened his focus to the office at large. Her very disorganised office. Scorpius' eyes grew three times their size. "I have a lot of work to do, huh?" He nodded. "I can help." "Not today." Draco chuckled and scooped up his son. "We're going to visit your grandmother." "I was just about to take these folders to the legal office. I found some additional evidence against Tiberius and the Minister. You can come withÑ" Hermione stopped when Draco's eyebrow began to climb. It dawned on her just what he was thinking. "I'm pretty sure Deloris saw you and Scorpius." The resemblance to his father was strikingly adorable. Draco coughed. "In that case, you two wait here, I'll be back." January 10, 2012 Hermione would have given him the world, but Scorpius only asked for one birthday gift. For everyone in his family picture to celebrate with him. He didn't care how or where, just that everyone was together. And though they had to make an exception for Teddy, who'd already boarded the Hogwarts Express back to school, he'd been granted permission to Floo call from the Headmistress' office. According to Draco, he was staying with Theo during the search. Hermione hadn't been around during each of his prior visits, so when he stepped out of the Floo, she had to orient herself quickly to the foreign concept of an adult Gregory Goyle. Standing in front of the fireplace, it was easy to see that he'd grown into his once hulking figure, now taller than Draco but not quite as tall as Blaise. Goyle's hair was still bristly and his eyes were small, but he wore glasses now and seemed strangely reservedÑa word Hermione never would have associated with the child she'd known at school. Draco's hand warmed the small of her back as he introduced her. "She's myÑ" Forever passed in the span of a single breath. "Mine." The glint in his eyes spoke silently of something she already knew. He was hers, too. "Good to see you." Greg offered his hand with ease. Once upon a time, this simple action had been beneath him; influenced by bigotry and scorn, he hadn't considered Hermione someone worthy of such civilities. But today, they were starting over. Sure, they had met before, but they were different peopleÑboth then and now. "It's nice to see you again, too, Greg." Knowing everything Draco had told her about Gregory Goyle prior to his arrival, his struggles during the war, after watching Crabbe die, moving to America, meeting the witch who eventually became his wife, and how much she bettered him along the way by helping him heal and emerge from the darknessÑit made it easy for Hermione to accept his olive branch. Their paths had been similar, no matter how different. No two journeys of growth and change ever looked alike. "Welcome." She leaned into the warmth of Draco's palm and gestured for Greg to join the others. Hermione sent Draco with a displaced houseplant in danger of Lily's wrath and a request to clear the children and puppy out of the conservatory. James and Albus bounded out of the house ahead of Scorpius, who was holding A. Shock began to numb her as adrenaline ebbed, slowing its manic hum. Susan made her sit and breathe through the withdrawal. "Anti-Apparition wards are set up at the Ministry. I had to use my necklace to get out. Someone should check all the wards here to be safe and make sure the Floo is secured. Anyone who needs to get here already has access or another way in." Everyone stayed together, locked down following Blaise, Ron, and Dean's arrival to wait out the storm. Hermione forced a smile as she looked around the room. "I'm sure they'll be here soon." Time passed in virtual silence. Seconds stretched to minutes which added up to hours until they resumed some semblance of normality for the children who only knew something was very, very wrong. Narcissa went to her room at her sister's behest and fell asleep shortly after from the stress. Most of the others nodded off wherever they sat when their eyes could no longer stay open. Andromeda stayed awake as long as she could, asking questions about Kingsley, and Hermione told her as much as she knew: he had been alive and uninjured the last time she'd seen him. It would have to do. Midnight came and went. Hermione had stopped herself three times from going back. Scorpius' head was a steady weight on her thigh when a crash from the conservatory made her jump. As quickly as possible, she settled Scorpius on the cushion and rushed to the source of shattering glass that marked someone's arrival. The only ones awake, she and Susan skidded into the conservatory to find Draco. Alive. Dirty with wet hair and smelling like decontaminate. But alive. His eyes were swollen shut and blood dripped from his fingertips. One shoulder was clearly wrenched out of its socket. Hermione was already ignoring Susan's attempts to treat his wounds when Draco wrapped his good arm around her and hugged her close. Draco leaned on herÑtoo heavily. And he was panting like he'd run all the way here from the Ministry. "They're dead. My uncles. They're dead." His disbelief was palpable. "It's finally finished." "You're bleeding, Malfoy." Susan approached his other side, summoning a parchment and casting the first diagnostic charm. "Fuck, it's a Wasting Curse." Much like the one he'd been hit with the night his father died. Draco barked out a delirious laugh while Susan yelled for help. Padma rushed in. Hermione was frozen, but not for long. In the midst of the growing cloud of fear, determination cut through like sunshine. "I can fix this, I can fix this." Susan Vanished his jacket and shirt and found the source of the curse eating away at the muscles of his back. She gagged at the sight. "Shite, it's spreading. The only way to contain this is to slow his heartbeatÑCalming Draught. Get me a Calming Draught." "Won't work fast enough. Or long enough," Padma argued. "Draught of Living Death." Hermione looked at her shocked friends. "It'll buy time." "It's risky." Susan blinked. "And we can't do it here." Padma ran to retrieve the vial. With Blaise's help, they stretched Draco out on the floor on his side. Hermione laid with him, keeping him calm as his internal temperature dropped despite repeated warming charms. Each breath grew more and more ragged. His eyes swam in and out of focus, taking on a glassy sheen. Hermione swept her fingers across his forehead. "I have toÑ" "T-trust you," Draco mumbled. He was going into shock and fading fast as his pallor dimmed to a sickly grey. Twisting in agony, he grasped thin threads of consciousness with what little remained of his strength. "Are you sure?" Susan handed Hermione the uncorked vial. "It'll work." There was no other option. Hermione held the vial to his lips. In trust, there was no fear. "Drink." Pain will leave you, when you let go. Jeremy Aldana Chapter End Notes Well, well. I am a sucker for a cinematic ending. Surprise! This is the penultimate chapter of Measure of a Man. The next chapter will be the final chapter and 42 will be an epilogue. Chapter 41: Out OfThe Shadows Chapter Notes *NSFW art See the end of the chapter for more notes Forty One Out Of The Shadows January 20, 2012 Like shadows eclipsed even the most vibrant colours, the curse held Draco tight within its grip. Ash white cheeks framed pale lips. His forehead was cool to the touch. Stubborn to a fault, Hermione refused to cede the fight. She followed him to the edge of darkness, summoned her strength, and dragged him back. Blood painted an abstract masterpiece across the floor. Lines and spotsÑsmall, large, and a range of sizes betweenÑcoalesced to tell the tale of the night that had yet to end. The unfinished portrait of Draco's pain stained Hermione's senses. Everyone held their breath. Time ticked by in an endless sequence of suffocating seconds as they waited for the curse to stop feeding off Draco's flesh, rotting his muscles, and melting his bones. When it did, Susan jolted. "It worked!" Padma slackened from relief. "You were right, Hermione." The knot in her stomach loosened slightly. "We need to move him, preferably to St Mungo's now that he's stable enough." Susan got to her feet. "All my books are there and we need zero interruptions." "There won't be room." Given everything Hermione had seen before escaping, the hospital would be overrun. "Maybe I can convert my office intoÑ" Her mind went to the sleeping boy on the sofa they'd have to pass. "ScorÑ" "Dean took him upstairs." Daphne stood in the doorway, her eyes on Draco. "H-he's still sleeping." "Can youÑ" "Of course." Daphne regained her composure. "We're all going to be with him and Narcissa for as long as it takes. You just focus on Draco." Hermione carefully lowered Draco onto his side on the makeshift bed in her old office while Padma put up a silencing charm to mitigate any interruptions. Susan opened the spellbook and flipped to the right page, filling the stilted silence. "The reversal process takes five spells. He's been through this before, but it's still dangerous. We'll need to monitor everything. His vitals should alert us to any sign of trouble, but the timing has to be preciseÑdown to the secondÑor the counter-curse won't work." "Okay." Hermione exhaled. "Ready when you are." Susan conjured an hourglass, and Padma waved her wand to enhance Draco's vitals, studying the proof of life laced in the parchment's faint lines. Then they both looked at her. The hourglass embodied time, the ever-present reminder of man's mortality. "His vitals are holding." One grain equalled a second. One turn marked the top of a new hour. "Susan, go ahead and start." Time was as finite as the sands in the hourglass. As was Hermione's patience. "No." Hermione tightened her grip on her wand. "I'm taking the lead." Draco's wound was festering. The rot was suspended in animation across the surface of his skin. He looked ravaged. "You can't do this," Padma argued. "Each one you start, you have to finish. You're too invested to think straight. Susan looked across the bed. "Keep monitoring his vitals and I'll assist Hermione." The hourglass turned over. Liquid power flowed through Hermione's veins like electricity. It flared from the tip of her wand in sharp slices; precise incantations filled the silence. The air smelled like nature. Like earth. Like magic. The ends of her curls lifted and twisted when the first counter-spell finally bound with the curse. Padma's relieved exhale cut through the rustle of Susan's panicked page-flipping. "Steady." Susan brought the book to eye-level and peered at the next incantation. "We're just getting started." Hermione centred her focus. The hourglass flipped. One. A trickle of sweat slid from Hermione's hairline and caught in her eyebrow. Susan dabbed it away. "Do you need me to repeat the next incantation?" "No." Hermione's pulse thumped hard as her next spell sharpened the translucent stream pouring from the tip of her wand into bright light. "I can take it from here." The hourglass turned. Two. The curse fought back. Susan's voice was frantic. "You're running out of time." The festering sores began to spread again. Inky black rot crept across pale skin. Susan returned to the book with renewed determination. The parchment in Padma's hands glowed bright. Draco's vitals teetered on the verge of critical. Ebony tendrils roped wider. The cursed rot spilled like acid across his flesh. Hermione pushed the fear from her mind and repeated the counter-curse with more force. Then again. And again. She said it until her mouth grew dry. Until the edges stopped spreading. Until the parchment's frightening pulse faded. "He's stable." They all exhaled. The hourglass turned end over end again. Three. "Hermione." Susan dabbed her face with a cloth. "You're shaking and exhausted. The book says, if any point, the curse begins to fight back, we have to stopÑ" "I'm not stopping." The only way out of hell was through. "What's the next spell?" The hourglass turned. Four. The next incantation was complicated at best, exhaustive at worst, but there were no other options. Hermione remained just as focused as she had been from the start. White hot magic adorned Draco's deathly pale skin, layering over the rot and trapping it in a pocket of intense energy that isolated the curse. She was nearly finished when her thoughts began shifting. They needed to determine the extent of the damage, save what they could, and grow back the rest. Her hands shook as her anxiety spiked; her precision slipped and her spellwork wavered. "Stay the course," Susan said. "You're almost there." The last grains of sand drained from the top of the hourglass to mark the end of the fifth hour. It flipped end over end once again. Another successful spell made Hermione sag against Susan, weak from the exertion. Relief was the terse expectancy of a new day's light. The eager wait to once again feel the pull of Draco's presence was only beginning. The objective was far from finished. They may have stopped the curse, and the wounds were no longer festering, but now it was time to heal. To repair. "Let us take it from here." Vulnera Sanentur. It sounded like a medical melody. Haunting and healing at the same time. The black sky beyond the window gradually dimmed to a dark grey. It was easier to watch than the scene in the room. All attributed their symptoms to age, but in light of Parvati's in depth article on her illness, people are now asking to come in and have tests run. Some had other issues that warranted different treatment, but these are the ones we've diagnosed with the same form of dementia." The media had been supportive of Narcissa in the last few weeks, commending her on her poise and grace in the face of mortality. Her bravery was a badge of honour for how well she'd managed to keep herself together while living with her disease. Still, the amount of new cases was staggering. Hermione stared between the stacks. "This is too much for one department to handle." "We have two new Healers coming to the research program," Roger said. "Healer Smith has recruited a few members of his team to join our department in the coming months as well. And from what I understand, you and Draco are brewing more of the experimental potion." "That's correct." Hermione sat back, arms folded, curiously looking across the desk at Roger. Draco had bottled a batch the night before and she'd assisted because he'd gotten tired in the middle of the process. "I don't understand why I'm here exactly." "We just wanted to discuss your plans and keep you informed about ours." "I'm going back to Healer Academy for a shift in specialties. Charles gave her a look, then a smile that she returned. "I didn't want to assume." "It would be an honour, Healer Granger." A quiet moment passed that left Hermione humbled, thinking back to their very first Floo call that had led to many more. All at different hours, different days, sometimes in the middle of the night. He never turned her away. Not once. And he'd been the first to truly help, to teach and guide her. It was nice that their relationship wouldn't change in that aspect. Charles was a wealth of knowledge she was eager to learn from. "In the meantime," Roger shifted in his seat. "I think it would be wise for you to attend bi.weekly meetings like this to keep you up to date. Theo tells me you're assisting Kingsley in the Ministry part-time." "I am, but only until we find a candidate for Undersecretary." There were a few applications from overseas beginning to filter in. Hermione had yet to narrow the field, but there were enough options to begin considering who would be the best fit. She hadn't been to her office since the attack; the Ministry was shut down to be decontaminated with only essential personnel allowed on siteÑpersonnel that had to be vaccinated before entering. Theo's teams had been working around the clock producing vaccines and antidotes. "Draco and I had begun discussing a theoretical approach to add to the existing potion. "He has made several doses for his mother in the coming months, but I know little of his plans to propagate the ingredients." "We're growing most of them in our greenhouse, but we're in the process of forming a partnership with the University that would be beneficial. Draco's family has seeds and plants from extinct species that the University will begin to propagate in nature. One being the Silphium plant we use in the potion. It'll be a couple of years in the making, and until then our capacity is limited by how much we can safely harvest, but in the future, we'll expand." "He's already started," Charles said. "He gave me the patent and plans to teach me how to brew it so that I may teach others." Roger scratched his chin. "Will there be enough to start treating a few others with it now?" "I doubt it. The new patients would need to receive the normal treatment course first, and longevity is a concern, not to mention theÑ" Hermione frowned as a thought sprang forth. "Wait, are we taking on all these patients?" "Yes," Theo answered slowly, almost as if he knew what she'd sayÑor how much she would argue against it. "I know your opinion on this. Your care with Narcissa was extremely hands on, but given all we've learned, we can lean heavier on the therapy aspect while facilitating Floo calls with their assigned Healers rather than in person care. Narcissa has volunteered to attend when she's able." Hermione inhaled sharply, startled by the news. "Why would she do that?" Roger asked. She wanted to know the answer to that as well. "Narcissa isÉ " Charles chuckled. "This was all her idea. She's also allotted a large sum to support the families who cannot afford the potions or make the changes needed in their homes, and a substantial amount more towards the research for the cure." Hermione was stunned. "She's convinced Draco to allow a company to use his charm to create other tracking objects specifically designed for dementia patients, too, like the one he placed on her necklace. It proved useful more than a few times during her episodes." Theo settled back in his seat. "I'm working on that. Companies are sending proposals." So much was changing. Even if it was too late to save herself, Narcissa would fight to the end while making peace with her circumstances. Still, Hermione had to wonder if Draco wasn't the only one creating a legacy. She had to wonder if Narcissa had taken a quiet note from her son and began to cement her own, even in the face of mortality. She had to wonder if this was another memory Narcissa wanted to leave behind. A single act that could save many. Something stronger than her place in society. Something unforgettable. Hope. January 31, 2012 Even though it wouldn't happen until at least after Easter, Scorpius was practising for their first day of real school. He chose to wear his uniform of grey trousers and bowtie, a button down white shirt, and a navy jumper. "Greyback will be impossible to find after nightfall." He might not know these woods, but his senses were sharper than theirs. "Maybe not." Harry shrugged. "But I managed to get a tracker on him before he fled." Draco was vaguely impressed. "How did youÑ" "I hit him with a tracking charm. It wasn't part of the plan butÑ" Harry waved in the general direction of the charred ruins. "Fire arrows, tree bombs, and blinding light weren't really part of the plan either." "You've always had a gift for thinking on your feet, Harry." Hermione smiled before looking over to Draco. "Shall we?" They both placed a hand on Harry's shoulders and allowed the spell to take them to their target. Or close to it. They landed near where they'd started, but the forest was dense, old, and damp, despite it not recently raining. Unease settled in the marrow of her bones as they listened for any sign of their targets. Light steps led Harry over rotting wood stumps. Hermione followed a safe distance after, and Draco covered them from behind. A low snarl and the snap of twigs barely registered before Greyback sprang from his hiding spot. Harry went down with a shout. His wand flew from his hand. Hermione aimed the strongest blasting spell she knew at Greyback. A jet of magic struck the centre of his chest and flung him back. One tree clipped his shoulder, a second caught his leg, then a third stopped his momentum as his hulking figure slammed against its trunk with a crack. But it wasn't enough. Hermione could hardly believe her own eyes. What would have immobilised even the most competent man only fuelled Greyback's fury. He looked up, locked eyes with her, and the earth slowed on its axis. For a moment, Hermione was back in the hospital hallway all those months ago. His hands tightened her throat andÑ It only lasted a second. This time, she was not at his mercy. This time, the tables had turned. He must have known it, too, because his roar of frustration shook the birds loose from the tree limbs in a mass exodus. Rage rattled in the air around them. Injured, still bleeding from the unhealed curse, and with fresh burns from Harry's arrows singed in his matted fur, Greyback looked every bit the monster of her worst nightmares. But they had a plan. Hermione and Draco took turns binding him with gold cords, then wound them around the tree to keep him in place. The tree hadn't been part of the plan, but the gold cords were. At least one thing had worked in their favour. Harry got to his feet and found his wand when they finally had Greyback properly restrained. There was barely any fur visible beneath the golden threads when they stopped chanting. "What do weÑ" "Malfoy, the incantation to turn him back?" Harry favoured one side, looking sick and flushed, but Hermione didn't have time to heal him yet. "I know it." Draco was slightly out of breath. "But I have to get close enough toÑ" Greyback's snarl sent them all stepping back in caution. He was straining against the binds, but they held. For now. "How close do youÑ" A sharp crack preceded an explosion as the top half of the tree snapped free from the pressure. Draco shoved Hermione out of the path of the falling evergreen and it landed with a resounding crash between them. Splinters sprayed like shrapnel in a wooden display of war. One look passed before the fight was on. At least this time they had better odds: three on one. For the first time, Greyback seemed confused by his own instincts, unable to tell which scent was truly hers in his critically injured state. All three of his opponents smelled the same. But then he charged. At her. She was in the middle, after all. Hermione's severing charm pierced his flesh and blood painted the forest floor. But it only slowed him momentarily. She braced for a blow that never came. Harry and Draco sent blasting charms from opposite sides. The opposing forces crushed him in the centre. Disoriented, Greyback stumbled. "Now!" Draco took off towards Greyback in a dead sprint while Hermione and Harry used every bit of magic to keep him off balance. Lights. Explosions. Anything and everything to disrupt his senses. Draco's first attempt failed, a swipe from Greyback sent him flying backwards. Unfettered, he ran towards danger once again. "Bind him!" Hermione went for something stronger than ropes and aimed her wand at the fallen tree. "Incarcifors!" It split apart and transfigured into a makeshift iron cage that creaked and groaned as Greyback bent it with a wet snarl. Harry hit him with a blasting charm from behind that sent him into the hole he'd created in the bars. Draco made it to the cage just in time, pressing his wand against Greyback's neck. In the middle of the chaos, she didn't hear the incantation, but she knew it worked when fangs and fur began to recede. They only needed a few minutes at most. Padma's spell wasn't meant for longevity, but neither was Greyback, who took another swipe. That time, Draco caught his arm. They both vanished with a pop. "Did you seeÑ" "He'll change back. It's temporary!" Hermione was already pulling at her compass necklace with shaking hands. It didn't just take her home. It took her to him. "You're not going anywhere without me." Harry limped to her side, gasping from the pain of what she could now see were likely broken ribs. "Can that take us both?" As it turned out, it could. The landing was hard and they both stumbled into the middle of the Manor's rose garden. Dusk left an ominous glow over the estate. It still reminded Hermione of a graveyard. She looked around for Draco and heard him coughing before she found him lying beneath an overgrown bush that was blooming much too soon. Harry pulled Draco to his feet. They stood around Greyback, who was seizing as the wolf left behind the broken body of a naked man with half his skin ripped apart. Dead eyes locked with hers and he tried to speak, but words were impossible while choking on blood and bile. Still, one managed to escape. "Finally." It paralyzed her mind but beckoned her body closer. "Don't." Harry stopped her. "He'sÑ" The spell began to fade. Greyback snarled and spat blood through elongating teeth that were sharpening before their eyes. Bones cracked like splintering wood. Screams of pain morphed into howls. Humanity gave way to the whims of the monster. The diseased wolf was returning to lay claim to the mangled man. There was no time to spare, and only one thing to do. Put them down. With measured fear in his eyes, Draco gave her the choice. It was her chance. Her opportunity to take back the peace Greyback had stolen from her. Hermione raised her wand, held it steady, and stared down the vinewood at the figure knowing what needed to be done. She took one breath. Then two. For all her anger and fear and hate, she still couldn't cast the spell. The emotions were there, aching in her bones, but they were masquerading as one another and mutating like wisps in the wind. "You can't." Harry lowered her arm with a shake of his head. "You have to mean it." "No." Draco's words were forged in the fire of determination, pain etched in every muscle it took for him to raise his wand. "No, you don't." Emotions blurred the lines between reality and belief. Draco dressed his fear as hate. "Avada Kedavra." February 12, 2012 A sea of people stretched in all directions, steeped in a reverent silence. Every eye in the crowd fell on one man. It was nothing short of magical. No one spoke. No one moved. They just waited to hear the words of their new Minister, who'd just had Interim officially removed from his title. Kingsley stood alone on the edge of the fountain in perfect silence for so long Hermione had no idea if he would speak at all. Until he raised his wand against his neck to amplify his words. "I was reminded by a friend that the measure of a man is what he does with power." From the hidden room overlooking the crowded Atrium, Hermione went so still that Scorpius looked at her in concern. Standing in a chair to get a better view of the sight below made them the same height; all he had to do was lean against her in an act of comfort. She pressed a quick kiss to his temple. "I'm fine." And she was. Even as they waited for the news. "While it can be used, misused, and abused, power can also transform the course of history. But this depends on both the character of the one wielding it and the choices they make. And while we are gathered here with the belief that I am the person who shall decide such things, I'm here to say that I am not. Under my leadership, that power rests with youÉ all of you." The door opened, and soon Draco was a presence beside her. An internal investigation had kept him away all day in a meeting that even her temporary position as Senior Undersecretary hadn't granted her access to, but what she did know was both she and Harry had spoken on his behalf. Hermione hadn't made it more than a few minutes into her interview before berating the panel for even hinting at the possibility of charges. Draco's expression remained devoid of emotion, but Hermione knew he'd been entertained by her defence, especially when she'd boldly stated that had Harry delivered the Killing Curse, there would be no investigation. They'd stuttered and stumbled over their reasons, and to Draco's eternal amusement, Hermione had made them pay for every word uttered out of place. They'd all looked properly shamed. She'd been just as happy as the panel to be excused. But now they were waiting. "My job as Minister is to lead and empower, to facilitate and guide, to pursue and encourage progress." "Verdict came back." Hermione froze. "And?" "My use of the curse was deemed justified." Relief made her lean against him. "Then I immediately tendered my resignation to the DMLE and notified them of my intent to accept a seat on the Wizengamot." "I am preparing us not for the predictability of the past, but for the realities of today and the unknown that is tomorrow." "How are you feeling?" "IÑ" Draco released a fraction more of the tension he held in a heavy exhale. "Free." She believed him. "I am not a perfect man, but I am determined to lead this imperfect institution into a better future, and this future will not be a continuation of our past. The Ministry isÑjust as we all areÑcapable of change. It is up to all of us to be the change we seek." They each placed a hand on Scorpius' shoulder and looked at each other. Everything wasn't perfect. There were more battles to fight, more victories and losses they would face together, but today's success was enough for now. They had time to breathe and experience a happiness that wasn't fleeting. Together. As a family. The future was finally here. It was now. The ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in moments of comfort and convenience, but where he stands at times of challenge and controversy. Martin Luther King Jr. Chapter End Notes Well, here we are at the end of the story, but we still have an epilogue to go. Lastly, a MASSIVE THANK YOU to my sensitivity reader Sri1997 for helping me with the Bladma wedding scenes.
