A damp breeze made all the flowering bushes lining the fence dance, including the new white daylilies. She had only one destination in mind. Home. The sun peeked above the treeline to greet the fading moon as it crept into the shadows of the sleeping world. When Hermione reached the gate to the garden, intent on stopping by the greenhouse to check on the olives that were showing signs of ripening, a curious sight caught her attention. Floating next to the swing held up by magic were her favourite teacup and saucer. A wispy curlicue of steam rose from the cup. Hermione approached, and a growing smile lit her face. It seized her soul. She knew what blend Draco had chosen before she took her first sip: red raspberry and nettle tea with a bit of honey. Her preference as of late. Settled on the swing, she enjoyed the view of nature preparing itself for one last burst of colour. The brisk undercurrent in the air warned of the autumnal ritual that signalled the impending change of seasons. It reminded her of sitting out here with Narcissa. They'd spent a fair amount of time together in the garden untilÑ As always, it caught Hermione unaware. Grief never arrived or left on a particular schedule, she'd always known that, but even three years wasn't long enough for its ebb and flow to grow predictable. Still, there was no more sense in questioning when or why the waves would arrive than there would be in questioning the ocean's tides. She simply closed her eyes, allowing the feelings to flow through her. Much like water, grief came in many forms. Some days it felt like an aching emptiness, but not today. Between the waning moon and the rising sun, today grief was a gentle wave that let her breathe and honour Narcissa's memory. She was grateful for both the good and challenging times they shared right here. Flashes of memories warmed Hermione from the inside out as she sipped her tea. Roses and gardening gloves and planting pansies and forget-me-nots. Absurd to think anyone could forget Narcissa. It made her smile. Even laugh. Dahlia and Cho wandered out of the tent first, arm-in-arm, with matching grins, and Susan followed at a more leisurely pace. Cho was off for brunch with a group of her Muggle professor friends, Susan was set to spend her first day off in two weeks with Ron and their daughter, and Dahlia was going home to Greg to do the same before he left for the Apothecary. "Be careful." Cho couldn't keep a straight face. "Padma's cooking in the tent." They all left after delivering the warning. Well, it wasn't on fire. That was promising. Somewhat. Hermione finished her tea before Daphne emerged and joined her on the swing. She had a plate of what looked like spinach quiche. "Breakfast." Daphne began forking the leafy lump. "It looks right, but I'm not sure if it's edible?" "You're about to find out." Hermione's snicker earned her the same scowl she often gave Draco. Daphne pouted. "You're not going to try it with me?" "I'm having breakfast with Scorpius before we go to Diagon Alley as soon as heÑ" "Oh shite." Daphne unceremoniously shoved a forkful in her mouth while Hermione looked at Padma approaching. "Tastes like mushy egg custard." Pansy took the tent down with a few waves of her wand. When Daphne took a second bite, Hermione grimaced. "Don't look at me like that. I know what she's going through. I'd rather avoid a repeat of last night's hormonal breakdown about how she's already failed as a mum because she can't cook." Fair. The closer Padma got to them, the more excited she looked to see that Daphne had at least given her cooking attempt a try. One of Padma's arms cradled her small bump as she squeezed between them. When Pansy wandered over, they made space again. It was a tight fit, but they managed. The silence was short, broken when Daphne dared to take a third bite. Padma's hope was palpable. "Is it good?" "Not bad." It was the same tone she employed with her two children: gentle if a little cautious. It tended to come in handy particularly well when Lucas, her four-year-old, offered her a mud pie as big as his grin after spending time in Hermione's garden. "It hasÉ texture." Padma narrowed her eyes. "Pansy said it looked toxic." "I don't have children, so I don't have to pretend to like anything. I'm as shite of a liar as Padma is a cook." Pansy shrugged. "But who am I to judge anyway? I don't cook either. That's why I have Percy and you have Blaise. At least they both know how to order in. Added benefits include no dishes or expectations." "OiÑ" For a flash, Padma was ready to argue, but she paused, finger in mid-air. "Actually, that's valid. No matter how much my mother tries, I'm no cook. Texture's not quite a compliment, but thanks, Daph. I am, however, the leading Werewolf Healing Specialist in the country. Honestly, by demand, I'm the rarity. Chefs are practically pedestrian by comparison. There's one at every restaurant andÑ" "That's the spirit." Pansy's eyes sparkled. "Looks like someone's back." Ginny emerged from the house in workout clothes. She had been called home early the night before to tend to Lily's sick cat. Reluctantly, Harry had followed. "Is everyone back?" Hermione asked. "Yes." Nearly tipping the swing, Ginny fell across Pansy's lap. It earned her a dirty look she ignored. "Harry took Lily to Judo, and Dean's swimming with your two plus Al and James, which left me time to come back and kidnap Daphne and Pansy for a morning run." "Too hungover to run," they said in unison. "I know for a fact you both took hangover potions. No excuses. I'd drag Hermione, too, at least for walking, but Malfoy told me you're having breakfast then going for Scorpius' wand appointment in Diagon Alley." Hermione nodded. They'd been talking about this day for monthsÑyears, evenÑbut she was still trying to wrap her heart and mind around the truth that Scorpius was leaving. Time passed in a trickle, then a stream. A series of small moments in the pasture, hands held, stars counted, pies baked, and potions brewed. Chickens raised marked the years, and the rock wall on the side of the house grew along with Scorpius. There were no dramatic swells, just peaceful moments in the valleys between; it was the cycle of life. Highs and lows. Joys and pains. Life and death. A captivating canvas of memories. Every moment had brought them here in what felt like an instantÑHogwarts shopping with Scorpius. It wasÉ surreal. Hermione's breath caught. Chest tight, she looked over at Daphne. A knowing sadness passed between them. If only they could see him today, too. Daphne's smile curved into something wistful and proud. "How's he feeling? He was worried one won't choose him." Daphne dragged Pansy up with her as she stood, undeterred by Pansy's groan. "He'sÉ" Hermione smiled softly, thinking back to the conversation with him. "But what if there isn't one made for me?" "Just as there is someone for everyone, there is a wand for each wizard." Hermione pushed the fringe off Scorpius' forehead. "We'll all be there with you." "He's quiet. Every step towards September first has made him both excited and nervous." While Scorpius had changed in many ways, time preserved a fair few traits, too. He was still an intuitive, kind, and quiet child who preferred to talk with his hands and had moments of surly stubbornness that tested even Draco's patience. They could now spot incoming spikes of anxiety hours before they peaked. He didn't shuffle anymore, instead slowing and holding on a little tighter, or breathing a little deeper whenever he felt overwhelmed. And for the last two weeks, Scorpius had started to do that with increasing frequency. Only the promise of Albus and the others had convinced him to go over to Daphne's last night. Hermione started to say more, but it dissolved into a smile when the glass door opened. A looked enormous between Scorpius and his ever-present shadow. Leo. The little brother Scorpius had drawn into existence. A perfect blend of both Hermione and Draco, Leo's blonde hair was curly, but his eyes were identical to Hermione's father's: hazel, and the little boy had her softer features. With Draco's propensity for dramatics and Hermione's anxiety-inducing tendency to charge headfirst into everything, even troubleÑespecially troubleÑlife was never dull with Leo around. A long-suffering but proud Draco stood beside her as she healed everything from Scorpius' cuts and bruises from climbing to Leo's broken finger after falling off his broom the first time. Draco was there for everything, in every way, and he never once wavered. "I'm still confused about how Draco's children are nothing like him. Scorpius is kind and good and Leo isÉ" Padma tilted her head at the sight of Leo jumping up and down, eager to be let out. Scorpius put a steadying arm on his little brother's shoulder to calm him down. "Leo is just Leo. I'm surprised Draco hasn't put him in a bubble yet." "Only because I won't let him." Hermione shook her head. "Also, I wouldn't say they're not like him. Especially Scorpius, but Leo is just as direct as Draco." Much to their embarrassment and amusement. "I'll confess, it's funny watching him get a taste of his own quirks." And heart-warming watching him in moments of pride. Scorpius was Draco's redemption, but Leo was his salvation. Living, breathing proof that he was, indeed, a better man. When Scorpius finally opened the door, a dog and a boy shot out like light beams, heading in different directions. He followed them at a leisurely pace that was very much Draco, but got distracted by the small plot of rehab plants that had been moved outdoors. Always just shorter than Albus, last summer he'd grown past his best friend. Now he was a head taller and stood at Hermione's chin. They'd be face to face soon, and she didn't know how that made her feel. But she didn't let herself focus on that, welcoming the energy only a four-year-old could provide. "Mummy! I'm free!" Leo opened his arm proudly. "Miss Susan said I can take it off." It was the sling he'd been in all week after dislocating his shoulder when he fell out of the olive tree Lucas dared him to climb. It wasn't his first fall, and considering his affinity for climbing and heights, much like his brother and father, it wouldn't be his last. "That's good, love." Hermione got off the swing and drew him in for a hug. "And what are you going to do next time?" "Not fall." Leo was extremely proud of his answer. Every adult nearly choked to hold back their laughter. Daphne coughed right around the time Scorpius made it to the group. He signed his concern, which she responded to with fluid ease that came after years of signing, assuring her nephew she was fine before handing her plate to Pansy and accepting his hug. If they held on a little longer, Hermione knew why. She touched his face, whispered something only they could hear, and Scorpius touched the ends of her hair. A fraction of the tension he carried seemed to settle. Fade. With Astoria on their minds and hearts, the sadness didn't last. He came to Hermione's side, leaning against her, and she wrapped an arm around him, pressing a kiss to his temple. "Hi, love. Did Harry drop you both off?" Scorpius nodded with a little smile. "Did you have fun?" Yes. Leo peered at the quiche on the swing next to Padma. "Is it alive?" Out of everyone, Padma laughed the hardest. "It might be." Padma pinched Leo's flushed cheek then glanced at her watch. "I have a Floo call with Parvati who is already planning my Dohale Jevan." Daphne made a face. "She's planning your baby shower from Bucharest?" Parvati had been living with Charlie and running a local wizarding paper for the last two years after a string of acclaimed work catapulted her into stardom. Draco kept a copy of the article she'd written after Narcissa died three years ago in his top drawer. Hermione knew the days he pulled it out to read were the days he missed her most. "Yes, but it'll be in December. They're staying until the baby's born." "Maybe Molly will use the long visit to convince them to add to the Weasley hoard since you and PercyÑ" "Refuse to, thankyoverymuch." Pansy ruffled Leo's hair. He didn't hate it nearly as much as Scorpius, who had already stepped back pre-emptively. "Besides, you lot have the parenting thing all covered. Luna and I will be the fun aunts." Hermione reflexively patted down the back of his hair. "Can I feed the chickens, Mummy?" Leo stuck his lower lip out and widened his eyes. "You can, but don't chase Thirteen and Fourteen. Oh, and don't play in the duck pond." He was notorious for it. They'd added it to the coop after Draco and Scorpius found a duckling caught in twigs in the stream. "Yay!" The little boy cheered. "I won't. I promise!" He most definitely would. Without being asked, Scorpius went with him. He was always so protective of the brother who looked up to him as if he'd arranged the very stars in the sky. When they were far enough down the cobblestone path, Scorpius said something that made Leo laugh, and soon they were entering the coop together. A familiar warning whistle rang out from the doorway, and despite nearing the trees, A started running back in blurry waves of fur. Draco. In black joggers and short sleeves, he looked like he'd just woken up. Hermione let her eyes linger on the flashes of red and orange in the dragon's brilliant scales that piqued her curiosity so long ago. Now openly displayed, that part had changed over the years as he filled in the blank spaces with new meaning. Right now, Draco looked as tired as she felt. The reason for their lack of sleep in the last six weeks was cradled in his arm. Rose. Named for the flowers her grandmother loved most. Judging from one moving mitten and the large steadying hand on her back, she was awake and the only one ready for the day out in a pale blue romper. The matching headband dangling from Draco's wrist meant Hermione would have to attempt to put it on. Draco apparently hadn't been able to. Once everyone awed over the baby, they said their goodbyes. A finally made his way over to them, with his tongue out and tail wagging, and waited patiently for Draco's attention, which he got with a scratch behind the ears and short command to go watch the boys. They trusted him in the coop; the dog hadn't once eaten a chicken. He was as good with them as he was with anything smaller than himselfÑwhich was a long list. Hermione lifted on the tips of her toes to press a greeting kiss to Draco's lips before laying a hand on her daughter's back to do the same. "How was your night?" Last night was his first alone with Rose and Hermione's first away from them both since the birth. "Good." Hermione's voice made Rose's grey eyes open, and after slipping the headband off Draco's wrist and onto her daughter's head, she smiled. "It's been a while since we were all able to get together like this." Life was busy before Rose, but now the word had taken on an entirely new definition. "How was she?" "She slept all night except for feedings and changings." Draco was good about her schedule Ñabout most things, really. And she never had to check behind him or worry. He was her partner. Husband. She'd been right. It was the name Hermione liked best of all. Surrounded by flowers and Apothecary Roses in full bloom, they'd been married in the greenhouse rose garden at dawn on the Summer Solstice five years before. Both dressed in ivory, their smiles never faded while Kingsley bound them together for life with warm gold cords of magic. Witnessed by her parents, Narcissa, Andromeda, Scorpius, and the puppy, it was a peaceful day full of love. A day to remember. Of course, nothing was ever quite that simple with their friends. Narcissa and Pansy threw a reception disguised as Narcissa's society farewell partyÑa solstice party with more guests than they could name; music and food and activities were plentiful. Last Light was spent at the Stonehenge replica, leaning back on Draco's chest. The people they were the year before felt a lifetime away. When the fireworks started, Draco took her hand and they left with a pop. The party was all anyone could talk about for weeks. It made for the perfect distraction as none of their guestsÑsave their friends and familyÑfound out they'd actually attended their wedding reception until Hermione's official name change went public during their honeymoon. Magic was efficient to a fault. Hermione Granger became Hermione Malfoy on every correspondence and publication, every paper and form. Even her office door. News spread like wildfire. The media erupted with theories about the length of their relationship. Speculation was rampant, and even amongst their closest friends, there were questions about why she'd changed her last nameÑespecially after being so adamantly against it. While Hermione's personal opinion hadn't changed, and Draco didn't care one way or another, Scorpius had been her deciding factor. He was and always would be a Malfoy. And they were a family. She refused to create any division with hyphenated names where none needed to exist. "We should get everyone ready," Draco said when Rose began to doze to the rhythm of his heartbeat. "Your parents are meeting us in Diagon Alley in an hour." Hermione grimaced at the thought of keeping her mother waiting. Not that she would say anything to their faces, but the irritation would be felt. The thawing hadn't happened until Narcissa took a turn she never recovered from. She'd passed when Leo wasn't much older than Rose. Draco's grief hadn't changed Hermione's mind about the level of civility with her own mother, but her mother's perspective began to shift. Maybe it had to do with the little space she had in Hermione's life and thriving family, maybe it was witnessing her dad's bond with Scorpius intensify, or maybe it was because of her mother's own changing heart. Hermione could never be certain, but whatever it was, her mother began to reach out more. To try. Hermione agreed to weekly dinners, but only expended as much energy as her mother gave. Even now, they were civil enough to maintain a status quo, but neither of them challenged it. "But that means now it's up to you to take care of Mum and Dad and Rose." "But I'm four!" "You can still brew with Dad and garden with Mum andÉ" Odd. Hermione leaned forward but before she could figure out why it was suddenly silent, hands settled at her waist, pulling her back from the doorway. "A two way silencing charm?" Her gasp would have alerted the boys to her eavesdropping, but Draco was nothing if not thorough. "Clever." "Leave them to it." Draco kissed her collar in greeting. "I asked him to talk to Leo. I figured it would help them both." "IÑthat's a good idea." She watched them pull weeds in the flower bed together, chatting back and forth with growing animation. Leo was chaos incarnate, but he somehow brought a sort of peace to Scorpius, too. Their brotherly bond had been beautiful from the beginning. Where Scorpius went, Leo followed, and he never turned him away, even when Albus griped about his consistent presence. Holding Rose for the first time seemed to kickstart Scorpius' open hesitation about leaving for school, but watching him with Leo now made her realise it had little to do with leaving home, and more to do with missing them. Missing out on anything and everything that defined home. His family. "We'll send pictures for his scrapbook." Hermione worried at her bottom lip. "I think that'll help." "It might." Draco exhaled before pulling her over to the sofa in the conservatory. She sat on his lap and wrapped an arm around his shoulders. "How was that meeting you weren't supposed to take?" "How did youÑnever mind. " Draco chuckled. "I figured you wouldn't sit still for long." "The meeting went well, if you must know. Everything is going as planned. Potions team is still breaking down the components. IÑ" A sharp cry pierced the conversation. "I left a few bottles in theÑ" "I saw. I'll be right back." Draco returned ten minutes later with an armful of Rose. She didn't look particularly happy about the bottle hovering next to her head. "I know, sweetheart." Draco's deep voice tempered her. "Not your favourite way, but we've done this before. Remember what I said last time. It comes from the same place, I promise. Mummy just needs to rest." There had been a noticeable shift in Draco since her birth. He loved Scorpius and Leo fiercely, but there was something different between him and Rose. Something different in him. Softer. And it came out in moments like this, when he smiled at her simply for existing. Or when he talked to her in low tones while she fussed. Hermione could hear him during night feedings telling her stories. He'd done the same with Leo, but back then he'd also confessed secret worries. Now, he spoke only of hopes and wishes. Not just for her, but for them all. Draco's smile faded when they heard the Floo activate. Then it curved down into a frown at Albus' greeting to Catherine, which was loud enough for his namesake, who had been napping in the sun, to lift his head. "Only two more days!" "Shut up." James' comment earned Ginny's admonishment. "James Potter, you're not too old for the get-along shirt." "Mum!" "I remember you being this loud before you left for Hogwarts the first time," Harry said. "Weren't you justÑ" "Dad!" Like always, Albus appeared first, grin fixed on his target, who sighed like every misdeed was returning for its reckoning all at once. A normal greeting for Albus Potter. "Hi, Uncle Draco." At least he had the decency to lower his voice after seeing Rose in Draco's arms, but that didn't stop him from approaching his favourite adult after he waved at the baby. "Hi, Rose." "Albus." Still, for as sour as Draco's expression was, Hermione knew better. "Did youÑ" "I did!" The eleven-year-old proudly pulled out his new wand. Hermione choked back a laugh; she would recognise that wood anywhere. "Hawthorn wood and dragon heartstring core." Even from the doorway, Harry sounded put out. Ginny patted his back. Albus hugged Hermione tightly and she ruffled his already wild hair. "Congratulations." "Where's Scorp? I want to show him my wand." "Outside with LÑ" Albus bolted for the door before she could finish, quickly followed by James who was rolling his eyes. Lily came over in a blur, hugged them both quickly, then ran behind her brothers. Hermione's eyes didn't even have time to adjust to her vibrant pink shirt with dancing black cats before Lily was gone. Hermione turned her attention to Harry and Ginny. "I see you both survived Diagon Alley." "Barely." Harry let out a dramatic sigh. "People were watching." "They always watch." "Doesn't mean I like it or will get used to it." He scratched his beard. "I could use a whisky." "Not a chance, Potter." Draco scowled. "You don't appreciate anything that costs more than a pint of Butterbeer." It was true. The last time the two had nearly come to blows was when Draco offered him his own version of friendship in the form of two fingers of his favourite whisky. Harry took a sip and promptly spit it back into the glass. Hermione could tell her husband was recalling the incident by the way his glare intensified, but then Rose reached the end of the bottle. He ignored Harry's existence in favour of shifting his daughter to his shoulder to burp her. Ginny and Hermione laughed. The rest of the day was leisurely wasted. They went to the treeline treehouse with the children, and sat around while they played. From there, they picked tangerines and mangos in the greenhouse. Lily gorged on blueberries fresh off the bush before anyone could stop her. Scorpius surveyed the greenhouse like he always did until Hermione made him put down the book and hold his sister. They sat under the olive tree while Hermione and Ginny looked on in amusement as their husbands made a competition of apple picking. Albus and James were just as competitive as Scorpius was not, but it was Leo, who was sitting on Draco's shoulders, that won. At twilight, the kids caught fireflies in the pasture. When they all came inside, Scorpius introduced Lily to Hugo, who'd finally come out of hiding. She had two cats of her own, but there was an odd look on her face when she glanced at Scorpius while scratching behind the cat's ears. Draco squinted in suspicion. Hermione didn't think of it again until she emerged from the office where she'd read reports while nursing after everyone left. Scorpius had long since fallen asleep hugging A, his face buried in fur while a nature documentary played on the telly. Hugo was curled behind him. She found Draco in the conservatory, relaxed on the chaise reading a book, with Leo passed out on his chest. "Taking him to bed?" "He'll wake up the moment I move." Draco adjusted his glasses before running an absent hand over their son's curls. He looked over, catching sight of Rose in her arms. "You?" "Not yet. I wanted a little time with Scorpius, but he's asleep." Draco scooted over a bit and she joined him on the chaise, where they sat in peaceful silence under the starry sky. The quiet didn't last long. Leo started snoring in long, deep breaths. Draco laughed as Leo's snores grew louder. "So, LilyÑ" "No." Draco didn't even blink. "You have zero control over that." "They're children." "I know, but in seven or eight years, don't be surprised ifÑ" "I'd rather not think about tying myself to Potter any more than I already am." Draco grimaced. Hermione's laugh startled the baby, but she hushed her back to sleep while her husband looked on with familiar heat in his eyes. "I think you want another." Draco's comment was so quiet she nearly missed it. She lifted her eyes to him. "I think you do, too." He was looking at her the way he had each time he stated, "I'm ready." The first time was their first Christmas in Chamonix as husband and wife. The second while they vacationed at the barely used Malfoy home in New Zealand. And tonight in their own home would be the third. "You're right." He carefully shifted closer. "I do." "Not yet." "No. Not yet." His kiss was gentle, neither moving enough to wake their sleeping children. "But practise makes perfect." "You might be onto something." August 31, 2017 Kingsley accepted her basket of fruits and vegetables while she smiled graciously at his gift of honey. And after he made a vegetarian lunch, they went outdoors to enjoy a sweet treat on the porch. Armed with his favouriteÑliquorice wandsÑthey enjoyed the humid warmth that always came at the end of summer. There were more hives than in years past. Hermione chewed on her wand while Kingsley held Rose, who took turns blinking up at him and the world around them. "She's very serene." "I know." Hermione shook her head. "We aren't sure where she gets it from." While their serenity was hard fought and earned, Rose's was natural. She didn't fuss much, only when she was hungry or her nappy needed to be changed. Daphne suspected she was still getting used to the world and would roar to life in the coming months. Hermione couldn't wait to see if that was true, but for now, she enjoyed her daughter. Rose had fit seamlessly into their lives from the moment she was born. "Scorpius all packed?" "Yes. We took him to the cemetery to lay flowers for Astoria this morning. Then to Narcissa's to do the same. He wanted to say goodbye to the volunteers." After all, he was one of them during the weekends when he wasn't playing sports or spending time with the Muggle and magical friends he'd made over the years at school. They'd been sad to see him and Albus leave. "Good. Scorpius and Albus will do well at Hogwarts." "Or Al will become the driving force that convinces McGonagall to retire once and for all, and Scorpius will be there to make sure nobody dies in the process. Or worse." They both laughed. Kingsley sobered slowly and turned his probing attention on her. "You know I did invite you here today for more than a routine visit." "I assumed as much. Draco was cagey when he mentioned it. He was taking the boys to the Apothecary and asked me to bring you and Andromeda vegetables." Hermione realised she'd fallen into a trap, but she was far more curious than defensive. "I figured she'd be here, but she isn't." "She's with Teddy at the Apothecary." He filled in the blank. "By this point, I imagine she has convinced Draco to let her take Leo and Scorpius for ice cream and a trip to the joke shop to visit George." That sounded accurate. Hermione took a bite of her liquorice wand. "Why did you invite me today?" "To seek your counsel." Something he'd kept up regularly despite years since she'd last held any official title in his office. Every few months his name appeared on her Magi-Scheduler, and they'd take tea and talk about everything. Kingsley asked for her thoughts on the sweeping changes as the newly formed Wizengamot Ñnow led by Percy as Chief WarlockÑrolled them out. The power they released back. The order they replaced. He asked for her opinions on foreign and domestic policies. They debated. They chatted. They agreed. They disagreed. At first, the conversations felt like tests, but each tea caused the feeling to fade. She was now comfortable giving her opinion freelyÑsometimes without being asked. She opened up and offered the counsel he sought. Sometimes her advice made it into speeches or laws the Ministry passed, other times he kept her thoughts for himself. "There will be a time I decide to retire permanently." His words, like him, were careful and deliberate. "I do not intend to remain Minister forever." He'd won re-election by a landslide against an established puristÑa sign that those days were well and truly over. Change had come to stay, and it was taking root everywhere, in everything. They were weeding out the corrupt members of the Wizengamot, either through retirement or formal charges. The kickbacks ceased and pardons based on wealth were a thing of the past. Early on, Draco had even been the deciding vote needed to overturn an archaic law. One voice had brought about lasting change. The Ministry's actions were seen as aggressive to some who benefited from the nepotism and racist laws, but to the majority, things were improving. They weren't where they needed to be, and the fight was far from over, but Kingsley had fought hard and given power back to the people who had been voiceless for so long. His thoughts of retirement weren't a surprise. First and foremost, Kingsley was a beekeeper. "Are you planning to retire soon?" Hermione asked. "No, we are still in flux. We need stability at the top or the entire house will fall." "Then whyÑ" "Be it five years or fifteen, I will stay and lead as long as it takes for you to decide." The quiet strength of his statement resounded in her. Hermione fell silent as her pulse kicked up with an instant and clear understanding of what he was asking. She felt as if she were speeding down a mountain rather than sitting outside with her friend and the bees lingering in the air. "KingsleyÉ" "I'm not appointing you today or even asking you to decide by the time Rose begins to show you the fierceness of her serenity." "I justÑ" Hermione shook her head. "I'm a Healer, not a politician." "Just as Draco didn't need to become a Potions Master in order to invent, the Ministry doesn't need a politician to lead. It has a beekeeper guiding the course of its current journey." Kingsley turned to her. "Who's to say that there is no need for a Healer? I believe all you would need to do is exactly what you're already doingÑhealing." Despite the overwhelming instinct to run from the conversation and what he was asking of her, Hermione bit her liquorice wand and listened. She stayed. "Not all wounds are physical, as you know. Some are so deep that even when they are healed, the systematic damage has already taken its toll. I cannot heal them all in my time, but perhaps a Healer can continue what I have started. There are many who will support you. I will support you." His words were familiar, a distant echo of something she'd once said to him. Too restless to remain still, Hermione stood and walked to the edge of the porch, leaning on the railing and looking out at the apiary. The garden started there by Neville and his students had grown and flourished ever since. "Hermione, I told you before that you would make an excellent Minister for Magic one day, and it had little to do with your brillianceÉ I mean that more than ever now." She turned to find Kingsley standing close with her daughter cradled in his arms. Rose blinked up at Hermione when he handed her back. He didn't return to his chair, instead joining her at the railing. "Time has made you a better version of the woman you once were, Hermione." His voice was low and gentle. "You understand your own power, but love has humbled you with wisdom. Rather than holding yourself back, you now move forward with a purpose. Your family grounds you." Hermione smiled down at her daughter. "Pain has made you stronger, and taught you how to trust others. You are better prepared now to face tough situations. Your work has made you more open-minded. Dare I say innovative. You see the world in a new way and better understand its potential." "Yes, but Minister? I don't know. I-I'm not finished with my work. My kids are young, and Draco likes the quiet peace we've managed to carve out despite leading very public lives. I like making large changes on a smaller scale, and this would beÑ" "Quite the opposite, I know. But you set a precedent in my office within a couple of months, I can't imagine how you would change the Ministry. I trust you will lead by exampleÑshould you choose to." He placed a hand on her shoulder. "As with everything, this is your choice. I meant it when I said I would wait, and if you ultimately decide not to run, I will respect your decision." "Thank you. I-It's a lot to think about." They stood in silence, listening to the bees humming in harmony. Thunder rumbled in the distance. "But I do wish you felt the same strength of conviction you have provided Draco. I wish you saw the same potential in yourself that you and I recognised in him. Potential that, might I add, has come to fruition in more ways than one." Kingsley returned to his seat and pulled another liquorice wand from the box. "I see such promise in you, Hermione. I always have, and I'm not the only one." Hermione's hackles rose with suspicious realisation. "Did youÉ" "Draco plays to his strengths." Of course he did. And while the knowledge that he'd even consider life as a Minister's husband left her reeling, it was Kingsley's parting words that lingered long after they went their separate ways. "You told me once that there are leaders and then there are those who lead. The former holds authority while the latter inspires. I believe you, like me, can be both." September 1, 2017 Scorpius was exactly where Hermione expected to find him. In the greenhouse peering up at the tangerine tree, with his gloved hands behind his back. It was so early the grow lights were on, and the moon was still high in the sky. He didn't startle when she approached him from behind, resting her hands on his shoulders. When he leaned back, her embrace turned into a hug. Hermione pressed her cheek against his temple, and they stood in a comfortable silence. "We'll see her before you leave." Narcissa's portrait hung in the add-on libraryÑforever asleep, she never woke up, and no one knew why, but they placed her with Astoria's mundane painting. Two women who made the most of what little time they were afforded. Not all wounds were healed, but Scorpius kept her gifted memories close. They were of him. Memories of her firsts. Holding him. Seeing him smile. Hearing him speak. "I know." His response was soft. "Can we stay like this for a little longer?" "Of course." Hermione inhaled as Scorpius closed his eyes and did the same. "Are you nervous?" "Yes." Gloved hands covered her arms. "But also no." "I felt like that, too, but I didn't have a lot of friends when I was your age. I went to Hogwarts alone. I read and read and tried to learn as much as I could to fit in. It didn't work at first because I was a swot, but you're going to have a great time. You've read all the books so now it's time to experience it for yourself. You're not going alone." She squeezed him gently. "It'll be an adventure. Make more friends. Be brave. Make your own magic." "What if I'm not Slytherin like dad?" "He'll love you regardless, but between you and me, we have a bet that you won't be." Scorpius turned his head, eyes wide with surprise. "HeÑ" "Wants you to forge your own path. This is your life to live, and he wants you to make your own choices. And that starts today when you leave, and continues no matter what house you're sorted into. Your history and legacy are important, they tell you where you've come from, but they don't determine where you're going. There are a lot of bad things that have happened in your family's past, things that people might judge you for, but what does your dad say about that?" "The past doesn't define me." "No, it doesn't." Hermione smiled, recalling the many times she'd heard Draco repeat it to his son. "You are better. You have always been better. You have your mother's heart, her spirit. And you'll show the world by being exactly who you are. There is only one Scorpius Malfoy, and the Sorting Hat will know which house you belong in." Scorpius was silent for so long that dawn began to break. "Mum?" "YÑ" Hermione swallowed around a lump in her throat. She would never get over the twinge of emotion every time he called her that. "Yes?" "Thanks for being my Mum." "Thank you for choosing me." Platform 9 . was exactly as Hermione remembered. Alive with the thrum of returning Hogwarts students and nervous First Years crowding towards the train with their baggage. Parents clogged the walkways, not yet ready to say goodbye. They'd arrived an hour before the train's scheduled departure, leaving them plenty of time for farewells and final checks. Theo's goodbye came first, Daphne's second, then Blaise and Padma third. Hermione's parents brought Scorpius treats to share, and her dad playfully reminded him to brush his teeth after eating. Susan, to even Ron's surprise, got a little choked up during her goodbyes. Cho, who once kept her distance out of resentment, hugged him and wished him well. Teddy teased him with a smile before going to find Victoire. Pansy ruffled his hair while Scorpius frowned. "Being irritated with me is better than being sad. So I'll take that as a win." For a second, Scorpius looked at her as if realising something he should have already known, but the moment ended when Percy drew him away to check everything against the extensive list they'd meticulously compiled one last time. Percy and Scorpius shared an affinity for organisation, and he'd been all too happy to help facilitate. Additional extension charms were needed on Scorpius' school trunk. He'd never quite grown out of his love for keeping the things he loved close to him, and when asked what he wanted to take to school, he'd responded with nearly everything. Stuffed with clothes, books, his notebooks, and the scrapbook they'd filled both the new and old pages of over the years, plus the cauldrons and everything required for a First YearÑwell, a feather-light charm was necessary, too. On top sat Hugo's cat carrier, and next to that was Scorpius' satchel Hermione had packed with a container of snacks and a piece of sad strawberry pie. Next to that, his forever companion Mint stood tall and proud. Larger, too. He had everything. He was ready. But Scorpius didn't board. All eyes were on them until the Potters came rushing through the barrier, one after the other, with Harry and Ginny pushing through last. Albus had been bouncing with excitement all week, but like Scorpius, right now he stood still. Nerves had finally struck him. James hugged his parents and ran off to put his trunk on the train and find his friends. Ginny and Lily stood back while Harry talked to a hesitant Albus with both hands on his shoulders. Scorpius reached for Leo first, who looked close to tears. He kissed Rose on the cheek and whispered goodbye to his sister who was wide awake in his father's arms. Then he stood before her. He was still the same, yet so, so different. A hint of the nervous little boy he used to be emerged, but she tempered it the only way she knew how. Hermione fixed the bowtie he insisted on wearing, dusted the invisible lint off the sleeve of his robes, and took him into her arms. She held him close, closing her eyes when he squeezed the ends of her cardigan. For comfort. A reminder. "You're ready, love. Leo, your dad, and I will take care of everything. We'll send you pictures. We'll write notes. It'll be different, but not all change is bad." Hermione brushed the fringe off his forehead and Scorpius relaxed a fraction. "I love you, my brave scorpion. You know that, don't you?" He nodded, too emotional for words. His eyes were watering just like hers. "Distance means nothing when we're all looking up at the same sky." Hermione pressed her lips to his forehead until he settled enough to take a breath without shaking. Leo hugged her leg and she rubbed the little boy's curly hair to settle him. Scorpius held her a little tighter before letting go and turning to his father. A quick change from Draco's arms to hers didn't bother little Rose at all. Scorpius wasn't the only one surprised when Draco took a knee in front of his son on the crowded platform. Every eye was on the pair. I'll make you proud. Scorpius signed slowly. I will. You already do. Draco covered his hands before he could sign again. He reached into his pocket and pulled out one last note, placing it in his son's hands. "You always have." Scorpius scrubbed away a rogue tear, clutching the note. "Dad." His voice was but a whisper. Draco's smile was slow as he hugged his son, whispering three words into his earÑ sentiments Scorpius knew and trusted. Still, Draco held on, held tight, and didn't let go until Al came rushing over with Harry, Ginny, and Lily. Less nervous after his talk with Harry, Al hugged Hermione while Scorpius did the same with the Potters and Lily. When Albus got to Draco, he smiled. "Don't miss me too much, Uncle Draco." "I won't miss you at all." "Sure you won't." He tackled Draco with a hug, and the moment it was returned, Al pulled back, grinning victoriously. "I knew I was your favourite!" Before Draco could argue, the train whistled in warning. It was time to go. They walked Scorpius and his luggage to the train. With Mint in one hand, Hugo in the cat carrier in the other, and a satchel strapped across his chest, Scorpius was ready to board behind Albus. "Send our love to Neville," Ginny called as she waved. Both promised they would. Scorpius looked back three times before vanishing through the train door. Ten minutes later, when the Hogwarts Express pulled off, no one moved until it was long out of sight. Draco picked up a still-waving Leo, then took Hermione's hand and kissed it. Only then did her first tears fall. Not from sadness. No. She was happy. All was well. The quietest people have the loudest minds. Hermione hadn't thought of the quote in years. While it had initially pertained best to Theo, upon seeing Draco alone on the charmed hammock in the middle of the pasture, with only the moon and stars lighting the world around him, she was struck by just how applicable the statement was to him as well. His pensive nature used to frustrate Hermione, but it had also kept her alert, drew her attention, and constantly made her wonder who he was and what was on his mind. But now she didn't have to guess. Didn't have to ask. Now she knew. Hermione joined him on the hammock, curled up at his side, and waited for the mood he'd been in all day to settle. Being quiet was his choice, but not a permanent state. "What do you think he's doing now?" His question gave her pause, as did the waver in his voice. "It's eight o'clock. Scorpius should be sorted into his house and stuffed full from the Welcoming Feast by now. Maybe he's in his house's common room meeting his roommates." Hermione brought their joined hands to her lips and kissed his wedding band. "But knowing Scorpius, he'll be in the corner with a book, watching the sky." Draco, who never cared for the stars as much as his son, looked up. Hermione joined him, searching the skies in the place she'd found her home. Peace settled over them when he pressed his lips to her forehead and breathed her in, tethered to each other and the life they'd created. For what felt like hours, they looked with the silent hope that Scorpius was taking in the same sight: the same moon, the same stars. "I miss him already," Hermione confessed. It was something they both knew. "I told him today that distance meant nothing when we were all under the same sky." Seconds and minutes passed before Draco tilted her chin up and kissed her long and slow, smiling against her lips. "I agree." "We could go in and check the notebook. Maybe he's written." "In a minute." Draco shifted his hold on her. "I wanted to talk about something." His tone made her curious. "What?" "Kingsley told me he'd talked to you, but you didn't give him an answer." "I was too busy being surprised that you were the one who'd suggested the conversation." "Because I know you." And he did. Who she was before, who she was now, and who she could become. Hermione couldn't speak. Not at first. "I-I happen to know you just as well, Draco." Hermione ran a thumb across his lips. "You're on the Wizengamot and have the Apothecary. I'm on the trajectory to cure magical dementia in my lifetime. You don't care for a life in the public eye, especially not in that capacity, and despite being good at it, I don't care for bureaucracy or politics." "All true, but I've always wondered why you were a Healer until I realised this is who you are now, not necessarily who you'll always be." Draco stopped and brought their joined hands to rest on his chest. "I've been atoning. You were right about being part of the solution rather than the problem. I have no regrets, but maybe in ten years you'll be ready. Or not. Just something to think about." "DracoÉ" "I know, which is why the question comes back to an old one. What do you want?" For all her thoughts on the subject since her talk with Kingsley, there was no clear answer. Hermione pressed her ear to his chest, listening to his heartbeat as they relaxed beneath the stars. A comfortable silence settled over them. With his hand running up and down the length of her back, Hermione's thoughts floated through days, months, and years of moments with Draco's unwavering presence tethering her to Earth. Drifted through the creation of everything they'd built from the ashes of nothing. And circled back to the happiness they fought for each day. Life was fluid, it took twists and turns, and each choice made was a form of energy. Hermione kept this in mind when making hers. "I'll keep an open mind, I will. I'll keep advising him, but I'm in no rush to make a choice." "I understand." Draco made a low noise when her lips brushed his knuckles. "And until then?" "Let's stand still. Let's continue as we are." This life they'd created wasn't perfect or ordinary, but it was everything. "I may not know what direction to take just yet, but I think that's the best part of the adventure." The journey, after all, was the destination. THE END The End is not only an End. This is the Beginning of a new Start. Gaurav GRV Sharma Chapter End Notes I hope you have enjoyed this journey. I can finally say the entire intent and purpose of Measure of a Man was to read as both something of a love letter from Draco to Scorpius as told by Hermione (who pieces the story of a father's love together while falling in love with said father AND son) as well as the story of two people who begin as nothing, form something, and become everything. It was also written to fit neatly inside the 19 year jump post-DH and become something of the multiverse, leading up to the epilogue, all while making it a Dramione, with changes of course-Rose as the sister and Hugo as the cat. Al and Scorpius beginning Hogwarts as besties. All themes of family and choice, sacrifice and growth, the many facets of love and hard lessons aside--all other themes aside, it was intended to give Draco an arc that exists both within Scorpius and outside of him, and leaves open the possibility of Hermione turning to canon and becoming Minister or...not. Also, no matter how much I trolled everyone, yes, I always intended for Draco and Hermione to have more children. I would never spoil the end of my own story by answering definitively either way. Also, fitting that this story ends on Earth Day for our narrator who is Earth to Draco's moon and Scorpius' sun. I did not plan that LOL. I wish I had. Lastly, thank you to everyone for the love and appreciation for this little giant of mine. All the reviews and feedback, the kudos, the DMs, the recs, and every single artist who was/is/will be inspired by this story to create gorgeous artwork. I am flustered by it all and incredibly humbled and honored. I'd make a self-deprecating joke here but my mind is blank. As always: To K, the literal yin to my yang, you are incredible. Dunno what you expected late May 2020 when you said "do it" when I said (loosely) "I want to write cottagecore Hermione and incorporate a very old idea into it that I didn't think I was mature enough 10 years ago to write" But thank you for sticking with me for the 22 months it took me to conceptualize, plan, write, us to edit, and post this monster. You're the real MVP. Bailey, too, for stepping in as Alpha and being a sounding board. To Jaxx for simply being your talented self and forgetting all the spoilers I dropped on you during this process. LOL. To friends, you are incredible. To the Discord group (and even the Facebook group and twt peeps): thank you for keeping me sane with your THEOries (both crack and legit), positive energy, friendships formed, and just everything, you guys made what can be a lonely process of writing something for so long so, so much better. Much love. Ina. Art for this chapter: MoaM Manifestation by pollux_ & 1 Year Post-Epilogue by mcbibon & Hermione at Peace by ectoheart End Notes Disclaimer: I own nothing. Any mistakes are my own.
